subtitle: ならまち · ベジ・グルテンフリー

places.name: ならまちヴィーガン 菜美（nabi） (Naramachi Vegan Nabi)
places.gf_detail: Fully dedicated gluten-free kitchen. Every item on the menu is completely vegan, gluten-free and nut-free, and the kitchen deliberately avoids wheat, soba (buckwheat) and peanuts entirely. Umami is built from kombu, shiitake and vegetable stock rather than wheat-based seasonings. Because no wheat enters the kitchen at all, this is one of the rare genuinely celiac-friendly options in central Nara; diners with severe restrictions are still asked to contact the shop in advance.
places.vegan_detail: 100% plant-based. Every dish, dessert and drink is fully vegan with no animal products, eggs or dairy. Offerings include vegan ice cream, gluten-free muffins, parfaits, lunch sets and an omakase course, with seasonal limited-time dishes.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-17:00; closed Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday. Hours may change — confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (near Kintetsu Nara / JR Nara Station)
places.cuisine: Vegan, gluten-free and nut-free organic cafe
places.notes: The cafe was born from a mother's personal struggle: the owner's daughter had severe egg allergies and childhood asthma, and the family constantly found it hard to share meals while traveling because everyday foods like bread, cake and ice cream contained eggs. Watching her daugh

chef_bio.background: The cafe was born from a mother's personal struggle: the owner's daughter had severe egg allergies and childhood asthma, and the family constantly found it hard to share meals while traveling because everyday foods like bread, cake and ice cream contained eggs. Watching her daughter be handed fruit or sorbet while everyone else enjoyed ice cream inspired her to build a place where that exclusion would never happen. Surveys also showed overseas visitors to Nara felt there were too few vegetarian-friendly restaurants. After originally operating in the Mochiidono shopping arcade, the cafe relocated and reopened on July 1, 2023 in a spacious traditional wooden machiya in Higashishiodo-cho.
chef_bio.philosophy: Vegan, delicious and healthy — a table where anyone can eat the same food together whether or not they are vegan, vegetarian or have a food allergy, so no one is left out.
chef_bio.specialty: Fully vegan, gluten-free and nut-free fare: vegan ice cream, gluten-free muffins, parfaits, lunch sets, an omakase course and takeout bento, with seasonal limited dishes. Flavor is built from kombu, shiitake and vegetable stock and fermented seasonings.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is run as a family operation, described locally as run by sisters, and is known for delicious food and warm hospitality that draws a loyal base of repeat customers.
The founding motivation came directly from the owner's daughter's egg allergy and the frustration of never being able to share the same desserts on family trips.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is wheat-free, buckwheat-free and peanut-free — these allergens are deliberately not used anywhere, so cross-contamination risk from wheat is minimal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Umami is drawn from kombu, shiitake and vegetable stock rather than wheat-based seasonings, and the kitchen avoids wheat entirely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All menu items are 100% vegan with no animal products in the kitchen, so vegan cross-contact is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with dietary restrictions are explicitly asked to contact the shop in advance so the kitchen can accommodate them.

positives.text: Every single dish is completely vegan, gluten-free and nut-free, founded specifically to serve people with food allergies.
English-speaking staff and an English menu make it easy to confirm ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, so a foreign student can order and ask about ingredients comfortably.

places.name: ヴィーガンカフェ 楽夢菜 (Vegan Cafe Ramuna (Rakumuna))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. While rice dishes use natural-farming brown rice, the kitchen makes its own bread, cakes and tarts from 100% whole-grain wheat flour, and the menu features soy-meat dishes (karaage, nuggets, burgers) and a shoyu (wheat-based soy sauce) ramen. Wheat is used throughout the bakery and savory menu and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation, so this is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance. Ask staff directly if pursuing any GF option.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan (100% plant-based, whole-foods style). No animal products at all — no meat, fish, dairy, egg, gelatin or honey — and no white/refined sugar. Bread is made with organic whole-grain flour and natural yeast, rice is natural-farming brown rice, vegetables are largely pesticide-free, seasonings are traditionally-made organic, and coffee is self-roasted organic Colombian. Signature items include soy-meat karaage, VEGAN burger/hamburg plate, okara nuggets, curry sets, egg-style sandwiches, shoyu ramen, and dairy-free cakes/tarts. The owners are committed to animal welfare, making this a genuine vegan destination. Will accommodate gokun-nuki (no five pungent alliums) on request.
places.hours_raw: 木・金・土・日 12:00～20:00（月・火・水 定休）。営業日・時間は変更されることがあるため要確認。
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち東側・奈良ホテル近く） / 近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約16分
places.notes: A small, owner-run vegan cafe tucked into a quiet lane in the Takahata district on the eastern edge of Naramachi, just across from the historic Nara Hotel and within easy reach of Kasuga Taisha and the Daijo-in garden. Everything is made by hand in-house.

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run vegan cafe tucked into a quiet lane in the Takahata district on the eastern edge of Naramachi, just across from the historic Nara Hotel and within easy reach of Kasuga Taisha and the Daijo-in garden. Everything is made by hand in-house.
chef_bio.philosophy: 100% vegan whole-foods cooking using as much organic, additive-free and traditionally-made ingredients as possible: natural-farming brown rice, organic whole-grain bread with natural yeast, largely pesticide-free vegetables, traditionally brewed organic seasonings, organic beer/sake and self-roasted organic coffee. No animal products of any kind (including gelatin and honey) and no refined white sugar. The owners care deeply about animal welfare and keep the cafe a sincerely vegan space.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based comfort food and sweets: a juicy soy-meat karaage that surprises diners with how meat-like it tastes, a VEGAN hamburg plate and burgers, okara (soy pulp) nuggets, curry sets, egg-style sandwiches, a shoyu ramen, enzyme/natural-farming brown rice dishes, and dairy-free, refined-sugar-free cakes, tarts and ohagi.

anecdotes.text: Diners single out the soy-meat karaage as so juicy you wouldn't believe it's vegan.
Described as a genuinely vegan eatery whose owners are kind to animals and keep animal-welfare leaflets and vegan comics in the shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread, cakes and tarts are baked in-house from whole-grain wheat flour and the savory menu includes soy-meat items and a shoyu (wheat) ramen, so wheat is present across the kitchen with no dedicated GF setup.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu includes a shoyu ramen; standard soy sauce contains wheat. Seasonings are traditionally-made organic but not stated to be wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen with no animal products used (no meat, fish, dairy, egg, gelatin or honey), so cross-contact with animal ingredients is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Will accommodate gokun-nuki (preparation without the five pungent alliums) on request.

positives.text: 100% vegan, whole-foods kitchen using organic, additive-free ingredients and no refined sugar, so vegan diners can order anything on the menu with confidence.
English menu available to help international visitors order.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, welcoming small vegan cafe that offers an English menu, so a foreign student can manage with a few words of Japanese and friendly gestures.

places.name: 粟 ならまち店 (Awa Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a Japanese kaiseki-style restaurant where dishes rely on soy sauce, miso, and dashi, and the menu includes tempura (wheat batter) and wagashi made from millet/grains. There is no statement about gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment, so a celiac traveler would need to ask in advance and explain restrictions in detail. Not currently celiac-safe without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: There is a dedicated vegetable course, "大和と世界の野菜" (Yamato and World Vegetables, around ¥4,800), which is vegetable-centered and vegetarian-leaning, and the restaurant's whole identity is built around Yamato traditional vegetables. However, no fully vegan course is advertised: standard preparation in Japanese cuisine uses fish-based dashi (bonito/kombu blends), and the kitchen also handles Yamato beef and chicken. A vegan diner should reserve ahead and explicitly request no fish dashi, no katsuobushi, and no animal products; the vegetable focus makes accommodation plausible but it must be confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (last entry 13:00, L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:30-21:00 (last entry 19:00, L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesdays. Reservations accepted 10:00-21:00 by phone or online. (Tabelog lists slightly different L.O. times; official site times used here.)
places.neighborhood: ならまち（勝南院町）/ 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 郷土料理・大和伝統野菜の創作和食
places.notes: Awa occupies a townhouse in the narrow lanes of Naramachi that has stood for around 140 years and was carefully renovated to keep its old machiya character, with counter seats and tatami rooms looking onto an interior garden over two floors. It is the flagship dining room of a Na

chef_bio.chef_name: 三浦雅之 (Masayuki Miura) — owner/representative; 真興 大輔 (Daisuke Shinko) — chef and manager
chef_bio.origin: Nara City — Naramachi historic district, with its own pesticide-free farm in the nearby Gokaya hills
chef_bio.background: Awa occupies a townhouse in the narrow lanes of Naramachi that has stood for around 140 years and was carefully renovated to keep its old machiya character, with counter seats and tatami rooms looking onto an interior garden over two floors. It is the flagship dining room of a Nara food-culture project based in the Gokaya (former Gokaya Village) area of Nara City, which pairs restaurant work with farming and research into the region's heritage crops.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team treats small-scale heirloom varieties as 'family vegetables,' grown with care for personal use and sharing with neighbors rather than mass production. The name Awa expresses 'a' (beginning) and 'wa' (harmony), and the project's motto is to make, eat, discover, and pass on the region's food culture — preserving Yamato traditional vegetables as a way to revitalize the community.
chef_bio.specialty: Course menus built around Yamato traditional vegetables (such as Yamato yam, flat turnips, himo peppers, traditional spinach, Uda golden burdock and Yamato mustard greens), alongside Yamato beef — a heritage breed raised in the region since the Kamakura era — and Yamato local chicken. Signature offerings include the Harvest Festival (収穫祭) lunch with baskets of just-picked vegetables, heirloom-vegetable tempura, Yamato beef ribeye, and millet-based 'awa' wagashi sweets.

anecdotes.text: Owner Masayuki Miura is a pioneer in cultivating and promoting Yamato traditional vegetables, and chef-manager Daisuke Shinko takes part in the farming himself, treating his role as much as an educator about local produce as a cook.
Staff bring out the day's featured vegetables and describe each one individually, turning the meal into a kind of storytelling about Nara's agricultural heritage.

The restaurant is run as part of a collaborative model joining 株式会社粟 (sixth-industry farming and dining), the NPO Kiyosumi Village researching traditional vegetables, and a local cooperative farming council.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes tempura (wheat-flour batter) and millet/grain-based wagashi, and Japanese seasoning relies on soy sauce and miso; no gluten-free handling or separate equipment is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese kaiseki-style kitchen the dishes use standard soy sauce and miso, which typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares Yamato beef and local chicken alongside the vegetable courses, and Japanese stocks generally use fish-based dashi; shared preparation and dashi are likely unless arranged in advance.

positives.text: A dedicated vegetable course ('Yamato and World Vegetables', about ¥4,800) is offered, and the entire restaurant is built around vegetables, so vegetarian/vegan accommodation is plausible if requested when reserving.
Vegetables come from the restaurant's own pesticide-free farm near Nara, with seasonal heirloom varieties as the centerpiece of every course.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, fully reservation-based course restaurant where staff present each day's vegetables individually, so a few words of Japanese and advance booking (online via TableCheck) will make a foreign student feel welcome, though English support is not guaranteed.

places.name: 柿の葉ずし 平宗 奈良店 (Kakinohazushi Hiraso Nara-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Signature kaki-no-ha sushi is vinegared rice that is fine in itself, but the menu centers on wheat-heavy dishes (にゅうめん/三輪素麺 somen, tempura) and soy sauce, and fish/dashi-based seasonings are used throughout. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The signature kaki-no-ha sushi is topped with fish (mackerel/saba, salmon), and set meals include sashimi (お造り), fish, and dashi/煮麺 broths that are typically fish-based. Items like 胡麻豆腐 (sesame tofu) and 茶粥 (tea rice porridge) may be plant-based but are not served as a verified vegan menu, and no vegan accommodation is documented.
places.hours_raw: 本館（販売）10:00-20:30 / はなれ（食事）11:30-20:00 L.O.、（販売）11:30-15:00。新館（食事）11:30-14:30 L.O.。定休日 月曜（祝日の場合は翌日）。柿の葉ずし等の販売は年中無休。
places.neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池近く・今御門町）
places.cuisine: 柿の葉寿司・茶粥・にゅうめんなど奈良の郷土料理
places.notes: Hiraso traces its founding to 1861 (Bunkyū 1), when Hirai Sōhachi opened a shop dealing in sushi, river fish, and dried goods in Kami-Ichimura, Yoshino, deep in Nara's mountains. The house name 平宗 fuses the founder's surname Hirai (平) with a character from his given name Sōhachi

roles: 老舗（しにせ）柿の葉寿司店
chef_bio.origin: Yoshino, Nara Prefecture (奈良県吉野・上市村)
chef_bio.background: Hiraso traces its founding to 1861 (Bunkyū 1), when Hirai Sōhachi opened a shop dealing in sushi, river fish, and dried goods in Kami-Ichimura, Yoshino, deep in Nara's mountains. The house name 平宗 fuses the founder's surname Hirai (平) with a character from his given name Sōhachi (宗). The Nara branch opened in 1968 in the Naramachi district beside Sarusawa Pond, where guests dine within earshot of Kōfuku-ji temple's bell.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house describes its work as carrying on the dedication of its predecessors and the care demanded when preparing food fit even for imperial offering — a craftsman's reverence applied to a humble regional dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaki-no-ha-zushi (persimmon-leaf sushi) wrapping vinegared rice and salted fish such as mackerel and salmon in aromatic persimmon leaves, served alongside Nara local fare like cha-gayu (tea rice porridge), Miwa somen / nyūmen, and seasonal set meals.

anecdotes.text: Persimmon-leaf sushi began as a summer-festival home dish in Yoshino and was first served commercially by Hiraso as a restaurant around 1902 (Meiji 35); its leap to nationwide fame came in 1962 when it debuted at Osaka's Tennoji Station building.
In 1951 the house was honored to present ayu (sweetfish) sushi to Emperor Shōwa during his tour of Nara.
Novelist Tanizaki Jun'ichiro praised persimmon-leaf sushi in his essay In Praise of Shadows, even ordering persimmon leaves and salmon to make it at home.
Beyond meals and souvenirs, the Nara branch offers a hands-on kaki-no-ha sushi making experience.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu pairs sushi with wheat-based somen/nyumen and tempura and uses soy sauce; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used with the sushi and dishes; no tamari/GF soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use fish (mackerel/salmon) and fish-based dashi broths; sesame tofu and tea porridge appear on set menus but are not offered as a verified vegan course.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-established, tourist-friendly restaurant in the heart of Naramachi where a few words of Japanese help, but staff are used to visitors and there is an English menu presence; pointing at the picture menu works fine.

places.name: カナカナ (Kanakana)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly and not suitable for celiacs. The daily set meal and dishes rely on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso) and the café also serves homemade cakes, cheesecake and baked pudding. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no celiac accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan destination. The signature daily カナカナごはん is a home-style Japanese set built around fish and meat (e.g., pan-fried cod, pork-and-tomato hotpot) plus miso soup that typically uses fish dashi, so it is not vegan by default. There is no published vegan/vegetarian menu; a plant-eater would have to ask in person about that day's dishes, and options cannot be guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–19:00 (L.O. 18:30), Tuesday–Sunday and public holidays; closed Mondays (if Monday is a holiday, open that day and closed the following day). Obon/summer closures and occasional extended hours during Naramachi festivals.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（公納堂町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanakana is a café set inside a roughly century-old Nara townhouse (machiya) in the historic Naramachi district, near the former grounds of Gangoji Temple. The owner, Ueshima, came across the long-abandoned house and—after carefully cleaning and restoring it, keeping latticed win

chef_bio.background: Kanakana is a café set inside a roughly century-old Nara townhouse (machiya) in the historic Naramachi district, near the former grounds of Gangoji Temple. The owner, Ueshima, came across the long-abandoned house and—after carefully cleaning and restoring it, keeping latticed windows, exposed wooden columns and the remains of an old hearth—realized a dream she had held since her student days to open her own café. It is widely regarded as one of the pioneering townhouse cafés that sparked the Naramachi café boom, and even on weekday mornings a line often forms before opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen works to a simple idea of 'warmth in cooking'—warm dishes are served hot and cold dishes cold—and the room is furnished with vintage pieces and folk art to create a nostalgic, welcoming space; the coffee has long been sourced from the same Gifu roaster (milou) specifically to keep the experience consistent for regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the all-day 'Kanakana-gohan' set: a daily-changing home-style spread of a main (such as pan-fried cod in tomato sauce or a pork-and-tomato hotpot) with several small vegetable, tofu and konnyaku side dishes, rice with greens, and miso soup, finished with a choice of coffee, American coffee, black tea or one of seven herbal teas—offered at the same price all day. House sweets include a cheesecake steamed slowly for about three hours (neither rare nor baked) and homemade baked pudding.

anecdotes.text: Owner Ueshima first found the abandoned townhouse unsettling, but saw potential in its individual parts and restored it by hand to open the café she had dreamed of since she was a student.
The café keeps its coffee unchanged from the Gifu roaster 'milou' so that long-time regulars always get the taste they remember.
Considered a trailblazer of the Naramachi townhouse-café scene, it regularly draws a line of around ten people right at opening even on weekdays.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The home-style Japanese set meal and house-baked cakes/puddings imply standard wheat-containing seasonings and flour; no gluten-free preparation is described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily set is built around fish and meat (e.g., cod, pork) with miso soup, so shared preparation with animal products is the norm and no separate vegan handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, walk-in-only neighborhood café where a few words of Japanese and patience for the queue go a long way; the daily set meal is simply pointed out, so you don't need to navigate a complex menu.

places.name: カフェこよみ (Cafe Koyomi)
places.gf_detail: The entire menu is gluten-free — no wheat flour is used anywhere. Sweets and cakes are made with the owner's homemade rice flour (米粉), and the savory lunch (馳走膳 / Koyomi bowl) is built around home-grown vegetables, beans, and rice rather than wheat. Because the whole kitchen is wheat-free, the cross-contamination risk is far lower than at an omnivore cafe, though no formal celiac/dedicated-fryer certification is documented. Celiac travelers can ask directly about miso and any seasonings.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan (oriental vegan / plant-based). All dishes use seasonal home-grown vegetables, beans and bean products; miso soup is made with homemade miso and a vegetable-based stock (no fish dashi). Sweets are made without dairy, egg, or white sugar. Coffee is ground per order and brewed in a French press.
places.hours_raw: 水・木・金 11:30〜14:00（L.O.）／15:00 close。月・火・土・日・祝 定休。事前相談で営業日・営業時間外も柔軟に対応。
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺
places.notes: Cafe Koyomi sits on the old-fashioned Funahashi shopping street (船橋商店街) just east of Kintetsu Nara Station, in a calm space lined with Japanese paper (washi) and cypress (hinoki) that the owner likens to a cozy living room. The owner grows much of the produce and rice herself, th

chef_bio.background: Cafe Koyomi sits on the old-fashioned Funahashi shopping street (船橋商店街) just east of Kintetsu Nara Station, in a calm space lined with Japanese paper (washi) and cypress (hinoki) that the owner likens to a cozy living room. The owner grows much of the produce and rice herself, then cooks lunch to match the season.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name KOYOMI means 'calendar/almanac': just as the seasons turn (季節に移ろい暦があるように), the owner believes food has its own season, and prepares each dish slowly and 'ひとつひとつ丁寧に' (with careful attention to every element) using vegetables grown over time.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature lunch is the seasonal 馳走膳 (chisō-zen) set and the 'Koyomi bowl', built around home-grown vegetables, beans and pot-cooked rice with a homemade-miso soup. Sweets are vegan, gluten-free cakes and weekly-changing treats made from the owner's own rice flour, with no dairy, egg, or white sugar; coffee is ground to order and pressed in a French press.

anecdotes.text: The cafe doubles as a small venue for music and art events and can be booked for private hire.
Hours are deliberately limited (Wed-Fri, midday only), but the owner notes she will flexibly accommodate other days or times if you consult in advance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat-free and gluten-free, so there is no wheat flour in the kitchen to cross-contaminate; sweets use the owner's homemade rice flour. No formal celiac protocol or dedicated-fryer statement is published, so confirm seasonings directly if you are highly sensitive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso soup uses homemade miso and a vegetable-based (non-fish) stock; the site does not specify whether any soy sauce used is wheat-free tamari, so celiac diners should ask about soy sauce / miso ingredients.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The whole kitchen is plant-based (no meat, fish, dairy, or egg on the menu), so animal cross-contact risk is minimal.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner offers to flexibly accommodate requests if consulted in advance, suggesting individual dietary questions can be discussed before visiting.

positives.text: Every item on the menu is both vegan and gluten-free, made with home-grown vegetables and homemade rice flour and miso, making it one of the safest single-stop choices in central Nara for travelers needing both diets.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, home-style cafe on a nostalgic shopping street where the owner grows the vegetables herself; it has an English welcome page and online reservation, but in person it's a quiet local spot, so a foreign student will be most comfortable bringing a little patience and a translation app.

places.name: Pleased To Meet Me（プリトミ） (Pleased To Meet Me)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no GF claims are documented. The signature dish is served as a pita sandwich (wheat), and falafel is deep-fried with no documented dedicated fryer. A falafel plate/set without the pita could be a possibility, but flour coating, frying oil shared with other items, and curry roux are unverified. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners must ask in person before ordering; nothing here is confirmed safe.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore-leaning kissa (café/bar) that also serves fish, but it clearly markets vegan and vegetarian options. The falafel pita/plate is built from chickpea fritters with deep-fried eggplant, tomato and lettuce and is explicitly described as containing no meat; the weekend vegetable curry is also flagged vegan-friendly. Because the kitchen handles fish and dairy (ice cream floats use dairy), vegans should specify their needs when ordering and confirm the curry batch and any sauces are animal-free, as the curry sells out and recipes can vary.
places.hours_raw: Tue/Wed/Fri 15:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sat/Sun 14:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Mon & Thu. Irregular closures are common — owner posts updates on Twitter/X. Some listings show wider/earlier hours, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（法蓮町・北町／やすらぎの道沿い）
places.cuisine: ファラフェル・カレー
places.notes: Pleased To Meet Me — known affectionately as Puritomi — is a tiny, owner-run kissa tucked along Yasuragi-no-michi in the quiet Kitamachi side of central Nara, about a ten-minute walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. Opened in 2017, it blurs the line between café, bar and record sh

chef_bio.background: Pleased To Meet Me — known affectionately as Puritomi — is a tiny, owner-run kissa tucked along Yasuragi-no-michi in the quiet Kitamachi side of central Nara, about a ten-minute walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. Opened in 2017, it blurs the line between café, bar and record shop: the room carries a nostalgic Showa-era mood, with vintage collectibles on the shelves and analog vinyl for sale alongside the food and drinks.
chef_bio.philosophy: Music and hospitality are part of the experience as much as the food; the owner works largely solo and keeps the place personal and unhurried.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the falafel — chickpea fritters fried with spices, Middle Eastern style — served either as a pita sandwich or as a plate, layered with deep-fried eggplant, tomato and lettuce for a dish of contrasting textures and no meat. On weekends the owner makes a vegetable curry (which often sells out), and the bar side pours craft beer, homemade ginger ale and fruit juices.

anecdotes.text: One visitor from Manchester recalled that, after the owner learned where she was from, he put on some Joy Division for her.
Regulars describe the owner as chill and friendly, running the small space himself with minimal staff.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Falafel is deep-fried and typically served in a wheat pita; no information on dedicated fryers or flour use is documented, so gluten cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen also serves fish and dairy items (e.g., dairy-based ice cream floats), so a shared cooking surface and utensils are likely; vegans should specify their needs when ordering.

positives.text: The falafel dish is explicitly described as containing no meat, and the venue openly lists vegan and vegetarian menu options.
The owner is friendly and approachable, which makes it easier to ask about ingredients in person.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner is described as friendly, chill and music-loving (and has chatted with foreign visitors in English), so a foreign student can manage with a few Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu, though the space is tiny and cash-only.

places.name: そうめん処 スルスル (Soumen-dokoro Surusuru)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Sōmen and nyūmen are wheat-flour noodles, and one signature dish (tai-dashi tomato sōmen) is made on whole-wheat noodles. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no celiac accommodation. Unsafe for celiac and gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The kitchen is built around a sea-bream (tai) fish broth — the owner adapted the French fish-stock technique fumet de poisson made from simmered fish bones — so the core dishes are fish-based. No documented vegan menu or plant-based dashi option.
places.hours_raw: 平日 11:30〜14:30LO、土・日曜・祝日は11:00〜14:00LO。定休日 月・火・水曜（時期により異なる）。電話予約は営業日の15:00〜18:00のみ。
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (ならまち), central Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station /
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner Takeda Kaori grew up in the Kantō region (Kanagawa) and first worked as a company employee while earning a cooking license, then spent about two years training at a French restaurant in Osaka. On a trip to Nara she tasted Miwa sōmen from Sakurai and was so struck by how dif

chef_bio.background: Owner Takeda Kaori grew up in the Kantō region (Kanagawa) and first worked as a company employee while earning a cooking license, then spent about two years training at a French restaurant in Osaka. On a trip to Nara she tasted Miwa sōmen from Sakurai and was so struck by how different it was from any sōmen she'd had that she resolved to move to Nara and open a shop devoted to it.
chef_bio.philosophy: She builds the broth on the French fish-stock technique fumet de poisson — simmered from sea-bream bones — and pairs it with sōmen because, as she puts it, the beauty of sōmen is its simplicity: it is the finest of noodles and goes wonderfully with a good dashi.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is tai-dashi sōmen (about ¥1,000): hand-pulled Miwa sōmen from a Sakurai maker served warm in a clear sea-bream broth, finished with a secret salt sauce and aromatics like ginger-garlic oil and rosemary. A French-and-Italian twist, tai-dashi tomato sōmen, uses whole-wheat noodles, and seasonal versions include cream and chicken keema styles, often served as a tasting set with small appetizers, dessert and a drink.

anecdotes.text: She had no family ties to Nara — a single bowl of Miwa sōmen on a visit was enough to make her relocate from the Kantō region and open her own sōmen specialty shop in a renovated Naramachi townhouse in April 2019.
Drawing on her French restaurant training, she reimagined humble sōmen with a fumet de poisson sea-bream broth, and even created an Italian-leaning tomato version served on whole-wheat noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat sōmen/nyūmen, including a whole-wheat noodle dish, so wheat is central to the kitchen and there is no gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are made from sea bream (fish) using a French fish-stock method, so dishes are fish-based and not suitable for vegans or those avoiding dashi/seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run machiya sōmen shop with a short Japanese menu of a few set dishes — a smile, a little Japanese, and pointing will carry you, and reserving online ahead helps on weekends.

places.name: onwa（オンワ） (onwa)
places.gf_detail: 100%グルテンフリーを掲げる専門カフェ。小麦粉を一切使わず、スイーツ・食事ともに小麦不使用で構成されている。アレルギーを持つ子どもでも食べられるケーキを作りたいという思いから始まった店で、ケーキ・タルト・バスクチーズケーキなどもすべてグルテンフリー。専用設備に関する明記はないため、重度のセリアック病の方は来店時に醤油やソース等の原材料を確認すると安心。
places.vegan_detail: 100%プラントベース（完全ヴィーガン）。動物性食材・卵・乳製品・はちみつ・魚介を一切使用せず、出汁も植物性。大和野菜など奈良の有機・無農薬野菜と有機茶を中心に使用。看板の「Vegan Delight」プレートはタコス、唐揚げ（大豆ミート）、ハンバーグ、チリコンカン、大根もちなど約10品を盛り合わせた完全菜食の一皿。
places.hours_raw: 11:00～17:00（土曜は11:00～20:00）。火曜定休。桜・年末年始・お盆などは夜間延長営業の場合あり。
places.neighborhood: 三条大宮町（JR奈良駅西口から徒歩約10分、なら100年会館近く）
places.cuisine: プラントベース＆グルテンフリーのヴィーガンカフェ
places.notes: JR奈良駅から徒歩約10分、住宅街の細い路地にある古民家を改装したヴィーガンカフェ。2017年12月にオープンした。

chef_bio.background: JR奈良駅から徒歩約10分、住宅街の細い路地にある古民家を改装したヴィーガンカフェ。2017年12月にオープンした。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「全ての人が同じテーブルで、Happyでヘルシーなおいしい食事を楽しめる」ことを目指し、100%プラントベース＆グルテンフリーで料理とスイーツを提供。動物性食材・卵・乳製品・小麦を使わず、奈良の有機・無農薬野菜（大和野菜）と有機茶を中心に、化学調味料を使わない無添加の調理を行う。
chef_bio.specialty: 看板メニューは約10品を盛り合わせた「Vegan Delight」プレート（タコス、大豆ミートの唐揚げ、ハンバーグ、チリコンカン、大根もちなど）。スイーツでは大和高原の自然栽培抹茶を使った抹茶チョコレートケーキや、乳製品不使用のヴィーガン・バスクチーズケーキ、美しいタルトが人気。

anecdotes.text: オーナーは「アレルギーをもつ子どもたちにもケーキを食べさせてあげたい」「アレルギーの子だけでなく、その家族や友達もみんなでおいしいと楽しんでもらえるものを作りたい」という思いからこの店を始めた。
古民家を改装した店内では折敷や抹茶碗など和の器を用い、大和高原の自然栽培抹茶や奈良県産の有機認証茶（TE=CHA）を使う。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 店全体が小麦粉を一切使わない100%グルテンフリー方針のため、店内に小麦を持ち込まない調理体制。専用フライヤーや厳密なセリアック対応の明記はないため、重度のセリアック病の場合は来店時に確認すると安心。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油・タレ類の原材料（小麦含有の有無）についてのウェブ上の明記は確認できず。グルテンフリー専門店だがソース類の確認は来店時に行うと確実。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 店全体が100%プラントベースで動物性食材を扱わないため、ヴィーガンの交差接触リスクは低い。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 「アレルギーをもつ子どもにもケーキを」という理念で創業しており、アレルギー対応への配慮が店のコンセプトの中心。英語スタッフ・英語メニューもあり相談しやすい。

positives.text: 100%プラントベース＆グルテンフリー、無添加・化学調味料不使用で、ヴィーガンもグルテンフリーの旅行者も全メニューから安心して選べる。
卵・乳製品・小麦・動物性食材を一切使わないため、複数のアレルギーを持つ人も同じテーブルで食事を共有できる。

cultural_comfort.note: 英語メニューと英語対応スタッフがあり、完全ヴィーガン＆グルテンフリーで全品が植物性なので、留学生やアレルギーのある方も安心して食事を選べます。

places.name: Big Mountain Cafe＆Farm（ビッグマウンテン カフェ＆ファーム） (Big Mountain Cafe & Farm)
places.gf_detail: The cafe is built around gluten-free cooking: all desserts/sweets are gluten-free, and they have developed gluten-free versions of burgers, buns/bread, fried chicken and fries. The signature lunch is a "Gluten-free Beauty Set Meal" (グルテンフリー美容定食). However, no dedicated-fryer or documented cross-contamination protocol is published, so strictly celiac diners should confirm handling directly. Note their house-made fermented seasonings include soy koji / soy-sauce koji (醤油糀) and standard soy sauce can contain wheat — ask which seasoning is used on a given dish.
places.vegan_detail: Substantial vegan offerings: koji curry set (using soy-based "meat"), vegan pulled jackfruit / jackfruit burger, soy-milk-based fruit smoothies, and a vegan variety lunch plate (advance reservation required). Not an exclusively vegan kitchen — the menu also includes Hawaiian dishes with meat/egg (loco moco, garlic shrimp, oxtail soup), so vegan diners should order from the marked vegan items. English menu clearly identifies vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Lunch & Cafe: weekdays from 11:00, weekends/holidays from 11:30; Happy Hour 16:00–18:00; Dinner 18:00–20:30 (L.O. 19:30, reservations only). Cafe closing approx. 20:30 weekdays / 21:00 weekends per aggregator listings. Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Wednesday of each month (open if a holiday).
places.neighborhood: 奈良きたまち（西新在家町）／近鉄奈良駅周辺
places.notes: Big Mountain Cafe & Farm is a health- and beauty-focused cafe in the Nara Kitamachi area, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Its philosophy is to treat health not as a passing trend but as a constant: serving food that is gentle on the body, safe, delicious, low-calorie and

chef_bio.origin: 奈良市西新在家町（奈良きたまちエリア）
chef_bio.background: Big Mountain Cafe & Farm is a health- and beauty-focused cafe in the Nara Kitamachi area, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Its philosophy is to treat health not as a passing trend but as a constant: serving food that is gentle on the body, safe, delicious, low-calorie and high in protein. The kitchen leans heavily on fermentation, making its own shio koji (salt koji) and shoyu koji (soy-sauce koji) seasonings in-house and serving sprouted, fermented brown rice. Much of the produce comes from the cafe's own farm and contracted farmers around Japan, and organic vegetables are sometimes sold on site.
chef_bio.philosophy: Health and beauty as a daily constant, not a trend — food that is body-gentle, safe, low-calorie and high-protein, powered by koji fermentation and farm-fresh produce.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the monthly-rotating 'Gluten-Free Beauty Set Meal' (グルテンフリー美容定食, around 2,800 yen with tea or coffee), built around seasonal vegetables and house koji seasonings, with only about 15 servings made each day. All sweets/desserts are gluten-free, and the cafe also makes gluten-free burgers, buns, fried chicken and fries. Vegan highlights include a koji curry set with soy-based meat and vegan pulled jackfruit.

anecdotes.text: The owners drew their Hawaiian-inspired dishes — loco moco, garlic shrimp, oxtail soup and pancakes — from 15-plus years of annual trips to Hawaii, adapting them to Japanese palates, and source Kona coffee directly from Muramatsu Small Farm in Kona along with partners like Manoa Chocolate Factory.

The cafe makes its own salt koji and soy-sauce koji fermentation seasonings and cooks with sprouted fermented brown rice, using vegetables and fruit from its own farm and contracted farms across Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free menu document or cross-contamination / dedicated-fryer protocol is published; HappyCow's listing notes no specific celiac safety information is provided.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cafe makes house soy-sauce koji (醤油糀) and uses fermented seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so confirm which seasoning is used on a given dish if strictly avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is not exclusively vegan — it also prepares meat, seafood and egg dishes (loco moco, garlic shrimp, oxtail soup), so vegan diners should order from the clearly marked vegan items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodations are arranged at the time of reservation against an allergen list; same-day changes cannot be accommodated.
The vegan variety lunch plate requires advance reservation.

positives.text: Built as a gluten-free kitchen: all sweets are gluten-free and they bake gluten-free burgers, buns, fried chicken and fries.
English menu available with vegan and gluten-free items clearly identified, aiding diners with dietary restrictions.
Listed by Find Me Gluten Free as a dedicated gluten-free restaurant in Nara and recommended for those with dietary restrictions.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available with vegan and gluten-free items clearly marked, and the aloha-themed, casual cafe atmosphere makes it very approachable for a foreign student.

places.name: 玄 (Gen)
places.gf_detail: This is a juwari (100% buckwheat) soba specialist, so the noodles themselves contain no wheat flour as a binder. However, this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free or celiac-safe kitchen: soba is served with tsuyu dipping sauce made from soy sauce (which contains wheat), and there is no indication of allergen control or dedicated equipment to prevent cross-contamination. Not safe for celiacs. No published allergy-handling information.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional soba restaurant. The signature ume-tataki mizusoba (water soba dressed with plum paste and salt) appears plant-based, and the inaka soba with grated daikon may also be, but standard soba tsuyu is made with fish (katsuo/dashi) and the menu is set/omakase-style with limited choice. Vegans must inquire in advance; not a vegan destination. No published vegan/ingredient details.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Fri: lunch 11:30-13:30 (last entry 13:00), dinner 18:00-21:00 (entry at 18:00 only). Sat: lunch 11:30-13:30 only (no dinner). Closed Sunday and Monday. Roughly 40 portions of soba made per day.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / near Kintetsu Nara Station (Fukuchiin-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gen occupies a quiet early-Showa-era house tucked down a narrow Naramachi lane, a building that once served as a villa connected to the Imanishi family, brewers of the local 'Harushika' sake. Chef Shimozaki Hiroyuki came to soba from an unusual path: he previously worked as a cra

chef_bio.background: Gen occupies a quiet early-Showa-era house tucked down a narrow Naramachi lane, a building that once served as a villa connected to the Imanishi family, brewers of the local 'Harushika' sake. Chef Shimozaki Hiroyuki came to soba from an unusual path: he previously worked as a craftsman making reeds for woodwind instruments, and that precision shows in his noodles.
chef_bio.philosophy: Shimozaki stores carefully selected domestic buckwheat in a low-temperature warehouse and grinds only that day's portion fresh each morning. He cooks each guest's serving individually rather than several at once, which is part of why only about 40 portions are available per day and why reservations are essential.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-ground, stone-milled juwari (100% buckwheat) soba with no binding agent, made unusually thin. Signature plates include ume-tataki mizusoba (water soba dressed with plum paste and salt, eaten in several flavor stages) and inaka soba, a coarser country-style soba served with spicy grated daikon.

anecdotes.text: Despite being hidden down an alley, Gen draws devoted fans from far away, including monks from Todai-ji, musicians, and kabuki actors.
Chef Shimozaki was a woodwind-instrument reed craftsman before opening the soba restaurant, and grinds his buckwheat by hand on a stone mill each day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No published allergen or cross-contamination policy. Although the soba is 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder, the dipping sauce and kitchen are not gluten-free controlled.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with traditional tsuyu, which is based on soy sauce that normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard soba tsuyu uses fish-based dashi (katsuo), so default preparations are not vegan; no vegan accommodation is documented.

positives.text: The noodles are juwari (100% buckwheat) with no wheat flour used as a binder.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a fully reservation-only, cash-only soba destination where some Japanese is expected and dinner is an expensive set course, so it suits a confident visitor rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 旬彩ひより (Shunsai Hiyori)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no documented GF accommodation. The vegetable courses still include koromo-fried items, soup/nimono made with standard dashi and soy sauce (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), and pickles — none confirmed wheat-free. The homemade warabi mochi is starch-based but cross-contact is undocumented. Celiac travelers should not assume safety; call ahead (0742-24-1470). No dedicated fryer or allergen handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The base lunch course "野菜びより" (¥2,750) is built around Yamato vegetables, ancient-grain rice, pickles and a single hot pot, so it is more vegetable-friendly than most Japanese restaurants. However, it is NOT vegan: the kaiseki and higher lunch tiers offer fish (iwago/shrimp), Yamato beef and Yamato chicken, and the soups/simmered dishes almost certainly use fish-based dashi and wheat-containing soy sauce. No vegan menu or plant-only dashi is documented. Vegans must call ahead to confirm dashi and seasonings.
places.neighborhood: ならまち／近鉄奈良駅
places.notes: Shunsai Hiyori sits at the entrance to Naramachi, on the first floor of the Uzuraiya Club building next to the World Heritage temple Gangoji, with a Nara rakugo storytelling hall upstairs. From a modest storefront it opens into a surprisingly spacious dining room of tables, count

chef_bio.background: Shunsai Hiyori sits at the entrance to Naramachi, on the first floor of the Uzuraiya Club building next to the World Heritage temple Gangoji, with a Nara rakugo storytelling hall upstairs. From a modest storefront it opens into a surprisingly spacious dining room of tables, counter and terrace seating.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen celebrates Yamato vegetables as Nara's answer to Kyoto's kyo-yasai, produce raised in nutrient-rich soil and Yamato water and served at its seasonal peak, letting the vegetables themselves carry the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato traditional vegetables are the heart of every plate. The signature lunch, Yasai Biyori, pairs an array of seasonal Yamato vegetables with ancient-grain rice, house pickles, an individual hot pot and homemade warabi mochi; dinner is the Yamato vegetable kaiseki, a vegetable-led course with optional Yamato beef, Yamato chicken and Suzuka-range rock fish.

anecdotes.text: Lunch runs as fixed seatings (noon and 1:30) and reservations are essentially required, especially on weekends.
A local reviewer frames the concept simply: Kyoto has kyo-yasai, and Yamato vegetables are Nara's equivalent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soups, simmered vegetable dishes and pickles use standard Japanese seasonings; soy sauce in Japan typically contains wheat and no wheat-free soy sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu offers fish, Yamato beef and Yamato chicken alongside the vegetable courses, and soups/hot pots are likely fish-dashi based; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: The cuisine is built around Yamato vegetables with a dedicated vegetable lunch course, making it easier than most restaurants to eat largely plant-based if you confirm the dashi and seasonings.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, reservation-based Japanese restaurant where lunch runs in fixed seatings; staff likely speak limited English, so a foreign student should reserve in advance and have key dietary questions written in Japanese.

places.name: カフェマル (cafe maru)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The headline items are pancakes/French-toast-style dishes built on flour (the pancakes use rice flour for chewiness but are not described as wheat-free, and the kitchen handles wheat heavily). The spice curry's roux/seasoning is not documented as gluten-free and soy meat products commonly contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is advertised. A celiac traveler should not rely on this place.
places.vegan_detail: A signature "Healthy Spice Curry" (originally named "Vegan Curry", ¥1,700) is made entirely from plant-based ingredients, using soy meat instead of animal protein, served with turmeric rice. This is a genuinely animal-free dish. However, most of the menu (the signature Maru pancakes, French-toast-style items, drinks/desserts) is not vegan — pancakes contain butter/dairy. So this is an omnivore café with one solid, well-known vegan main, not a vegan destination. No published statement on whether other vegan options exist or on separate prep for the vegan curry.
places.hours_raw: Mon 12:00-17:00; Tue closed; Wed-Fri 12:00-18:00; Sat & holidays 10:00-18:00; Sun 10:00-17:00. One-drink minimum per guest (age 6+). Reservations possible for morning visits only (before 12:00). (Hours vary slightly across listings; occasional irregular closures.)
places.neighborhood: ならまち（脇戸町）/ Naramachi, near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: Cafe maru opened in April 2014 inside a Naramachi townhouse over 100 years old, a short walk from the World Heritage temple Gangoji. The owner carefully renovated the historic machiya — keeping its high ceilings and interior courtyard — into an open, calm space with table, sofa a

chef_bio.background: Cafe maru opened in April 2014 inside a Naramachi townhouse over 100 years old, a short walk from the World Heritage temple Gangoji. The owner carefully renovated the historic machiya — keeping its high ceilings and interior courtyard — into an open, calm space with table, sofa and tatami seating that welcomes families and travelers alike.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner set out to create the pancake as 'a new genre' rather than chasing fleeting trends — describing it as combining the best of pancakes and French toast through a final pan-frying step.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 'Maru Pancake' (¥1,300) is served sliced rather than whole, finished with a butter pan-fry that gives a savory aroma and a crisp-outside, chewy-inside texture (rice flour is worked into the batter). Other draws are the plant-based 'Healthy Spice Curry' (¥1,700, soy-meat, turmeric rice) and creative summer shaved ice using Nara's Yamato ice-house tradition, such as Dragon Fruit Milk Ice.

anecdotes.text: When foot traffic collapsed during the pandemic, the owner expanded the menu beyond pancakes — adding the plant-based spice curry and creative shaved ice — to broaden appeal to health-conscious and international visitors. The curry, first sold as 'Vegan Curry,' was later renamed 'Healthy Spice Curry' to better convey its character.
The café occupies a renovated townhouse over 100 years old (described elsewhere as over 110 years) along Naramachi's main street, preserving the district's traditional charm.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free menu or allergen handling is advertised; the kitchen centers on wheat-based pancakes and French-toast-style items, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A genuinely plant-based spice curry (soy meat, turmeric rice) is offered, but no information is published on whether it is prepared separately from dairy/egg-containing pancake items in the same kitchen.

positives.text: A clearly plant-based main — the 'Healthy Spice Curry,' originally sold as 'Vegan Curry,' using only plant-based ingredients with soy meat instead of animal protein — is a reliable option for vegan diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly machiya café where a foreign student can comfortably order with basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu; staff are used to international visitors, though detailed allergy conversations in English may be limited.

places.name: 塔の茶屋 (Tou no Chaya)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. The chagayu (rice tea porridge) itself is rice-based, but the bento and kaiseki include many accompaniments such as nama-fu (wheat gluten), simmered dishes, and clear soups that typically use soy sauce and fish/kombu dashi. Soy sauce in Japan contains wheat, and nama-fu is pure wheat gluten, so this is not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free protocol or dedicated handling is documented; a strictly gluten-free diner would need to ask and accept significant uncertainty.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. While chagayu has roots in Buddhist monastic meals, the menu here is omnivorous: the chagayu bento includes items such as chicken wrapped in burdock, egg crepes, and dishes prepared with fish dashi. The plain chagayu and some vegetable side dishes may be plant-based, but no vegan menu or guarantee exists. A vegan would need to call ahead and confirm what can be accommodated.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:30 (may close earlier if ingredients run out); dinner is reservation-only kaiseki. Tabelog lists overall hours as 11:30-21:00. Closed Tuesdays (if Tuesday is a holiday, open that day and closed the following day instead).
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Kyobate area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded around 1963 beside the five-story pagoda of Kofukuji Temple, this long-running family-run teahouse took its name (literally 'the pagoda teahouse') from that landmark setting. When the temple grounds were renovated, the shop moved in 2016 to a quiet old house in the Narama

chef_bio.background: Founded around 1963 beside the five-story pagoda of Kofukuji Temple, this long-running family-run teahouse took its name (literally 'the pagoda teahouse') from that landmark setting. When the temple grounds were renovated, the shop moved in 2016 to a quiet old house in the Naramachi/Kyobate district, where it continues today after more than half a century.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder set out to offer hospitality through Nara's regional cuisine, preserving the ancient local tradition of chagayu as one of the characteristic flavors of old Nara. That founding spirit still guides the kitchen, which leans on seasonal Yamato ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Chagayu (tea rice porridge) is the signature. Unlike the common roasted-tea version, here the porridge is simmered with tencha, the same leaf used to make matcha, giving it a delicate young-grass green color, a light non-sticky texture, gentle sweetness and a refined bitterness. It is served as a chagayu bento in a black lacquered box with a dozen or so accompaniments such as handmade sesame tofu, simmered seasonal vegetables, egg crepe, and chicken wrapped in burdock, with multi-course chagayu kaiseki available by reservation.

anecdotes.text: Chagayu was long the everyday breakfast of Nara, so much so that people said 'the Yamato morning breaks with chagayu'; this house keeps that custom alive with its distinctive tencha-green version.
The porridge is made by simmering rice and then adding tencha tied in a cotton cloth bag, stopping the heat before the rice turns sticky to keep it light and fluffy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bento and kaiseki include simmered dishes and clear soups that conventionally use Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and fish/kombu dashi; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The chagayu bento includes animal items such as chicken wrapped in burdock and egg crepe, and dishes commonly use fish dashi, so plant-based diners cannot assume the set is vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, traditional old-house restaurant where staff likely speak limited English and the experience is formal; a foreign student would feel most comfortable bringing a guide or Japanese-speaking friend, especially to reserve the dinner kaiseki.

places.name: 月日亭 近鉄奈良駅前店 (Tsukihitei Kintetsu Nara Ekimae-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The kitchen serves sukiyaki, tempura (wheat batter), and dishes built on fish/soy-based dashi and soy sauce, all of which contain wheat. The signature chagayu (rice cooked in roasted tea) is rice-based, but it is served as a set alongside tempura and other side dishes, with no documented gluten-free handling or dedicated preparation. No celiac accommodation is advertised.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on Yamato beef, pork and chicken (sukiyaki, teppanyaki, kaiseki), and even the lighter chagayu set is accompanied by items such as prosciutto-wrapped cheese and is cooked within a fish-dashi-based kitchen. No vegan menu or plant-based set is documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki, Kintetsu Nara Station area
places.notes: 月日亭 (Tsukihitei) traces its name to a celebrated ryokan-style restaurant, 春日奥山 月日亭, set deep in the UNESCO-protected primeval forest of Mount Kasuga and built in 1901 as a prefectural guest house that has hosted dignitaries for over a century. This Kintetsu Nara Station-front bra

chef_bio.origin: Nara (central Nara, with roots in the Kasuga Okuyama mountain ryokan)
chef_bio.background: 月日亭 (Tsukihitei) traces its name to a celebrated ryokan-style restaurant, 春日奥山 月日亭, set deep in the UNESCO-protected primeval forest of Mount Kasuga and built in 1901 as a prefectural guest house that has hosted dignitaries for over a century. This Kintetsu Nara Station-front branch is its approachable downtown sibling, bringing the same lineage of Yamato regional cooking to a spot just a minute from the station in the Higashimuki shopping street area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing local Yamato ingredients and Nara's regional food traditions in a relaxed setting, making the heritage cuisine of its mountain parent restaurant accessible to everyday diners and travelers.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the Yamato chagayu (tea rice porridge) set: Nara-grown Hinohikari rice cooked from raw grains in fragrant roasted Yoshino tea, brought to the table in a teapot-shaped pot. The grains stay intact for a light, savory porridge long beloved as Nara's traditional breakfast. The kitchen also serves Yamato beef, Yamato pork and Yamato chicken prepared as sukiyaki, teppanyaki and multi-course kaiseki.

anecdotes.text: A diner trying chagayu for the first time here described it as refreshingly light, with the fragrance of roasted tea and a gentle rice umami, the texture pleasantly shifting as the rice drank up the tea over the meal; the set came with side dishes like kelp rolls and vegetable tempura.

The branch is noted as the casual, station-front counterpart to the historic 春日奥山 月日亭, an early-20th-century guest house tucked inside the Kasuga primeval forest where eight-course seasonal kaiseki is served.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen routinely prepares wheat-batter tempura and soy-sauce/dashi-based dishes; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki and kaiseki dishes rely on standard soy sauce and dashi, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around beef, pork and chicken and fish-based dashi; even the chagayu set includes non-vegan sides such as prosciutto-wrapped cheese.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished but welcoming downtown Japanese restaurant one minute from Kintetsu Nara Station; staff are used to visitors and an English/photo menu helps, though deep allergy discussions may need a translation app or guide.

places.name: よつばカフェ (Yotsuba Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on wheat-based items — homemade pizza, sandwiches, toast, cakes, cookies, and muffins — and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No explicitly vegan menu is documented. The husband grows and supplies his own vegetables, and there are vegetable-forward and dessert items, but signature dishes use dairy and animal products (butter/cream/yogurt-marinated chicken curry, Camembert and sausage pizza, kinako panna cotta likely containing cream/gelatin, cheesecakes, coffee). A vegan diner would need to ask what can be adapted; nothing is confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00); Saturdays, Sundays & holidays 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:00). Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（紀寺町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yotsuba Cafe occupies a roughly century-old machiya (traditional Nara townhouse) in the quiet Kidera-cho area of Naramachi, with its original beams, ceilings, and earthen walls left largely intact. Yoshie, the founder, fell in love with the old building and opened the cafe on her

chef_bio.background: Yotsuba Cafe occupies a roughly century-old machiya (traditional Nara townhouse) in the quiet Kidera-cho area of Naramachi, with its original beams, ceilings, and earthen walls left largely intact. Yoshie, the founder, fell in love with the old building and opened the cafe on her own in 2002. She later met her husband at the shop, and the couple have run it together as a husband-and-wife team for around two decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: The couple emphasize handmade warmth in everything from the hand-painted signage to the food. The husband grew up on a farm and supplies vegetables from his own garden; during the pandemic they set up an unmanned roadside vegetable-and-rice stand ('nokisaki ichiba') out front, born from the question they posed themselves — '自分ひとりでもできることはなんだろう？' (What can I do even on my own?) — which keeps them connected to the neighborhood.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature items include the Yotsuba half-size homemade pizza shaped like a cat (using homegrown Manganji peppers as whiskers, Camembert ears, olive eyes, a fresh-tomato nose and sausage mouth), a Nara-themed kinako (roasted soybean) panna cotta playfully named 'Shika no Otoshimono' (deer droppings) served with black-bean candy, a tomato-and-butter butter-chicken curry with heritage black-rice blend, plus cheesecakes (orange, framboise, cafe mocha), fruit-and-chiffon sandwiches, parfaits, coffee jelly and handmade cookies.

anecdotes.text: Founder Yoshie discovered and fell for the old townhouse, opened the cafe alone in 2002, then met her future husband at the shop; they have run it together for about 20 years.
The husband, raised on a farm, grows vegetables in his own garden and sells fresh produce and rice at an unmanned stand in front of the cafe.
Guests remove their shoes and sit on tatami across two floors; bookshelves of picture books make it welcoming for families with small children.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-heavy (pizza, sandwiches, toast, cakes, cookies, muffins) with no gluten-free preparation documented; cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use dairy and meat (butter/cream curry with chicken, Camembert-and-sausage pizza, cheesecakes); no vegan-dedicated preparation is documented, so cross-contact with animal products should be assumed.

positives.text: A small, handmade-focused family cafe using the husband's own homegrown vegetables, so individual requests may be accommodated if discussed directly with the owners.

cultural_comfort.note: A homey, family-run machiya cafe where you remove your shoes and sit on tatami; staff likely speak little English and there is no English menu, so a foreign student is most comfortable with a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend.

places.name: 喜菜亭 (Kinatei)
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The standard ramen and udon dishes use wheat noodles, and soy-based seasonings are used throughout, so celiac diners should not assume the regular menu is safe. However, the kitchen advertises that gluten-free and allergy accommodations can be arranged with advance reservation, and rice is offered (brown rice / seven-tenths-milled rice). Travelers needing strict GF should call ahead (0742-20-6188) and confirm noodle substitutions and cross-contamination handling directly.
places.vegan_detail: Fully (100%) plant-based. No meat or fish in any ingredient; oils and seasonings contain no animal protein; dairy-free and egg-free. Broth/dashi uses only kombu and shiitake — no bonito/katsuo. The kitchen states its cookware and tableware have never been used to prepare meat or fish. The restaurant also avoids the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, etc.) in a Buddhist 五葷-free style, reportedly the only restaurant in Nara Prefecture to do so. Entire menu is suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays: lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. ~14:00). Dinner by reservation only (18:00–22:00 / 17:00–21:30 depending on source; advance booking required). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: JR奈良駅エリア (Sugikacho, near JR Nara Station)
places.notes: Kinatei opened on July 1, 2011, near JR Nara Station. Its owner has been vegetarian for around 45 years and was originally a musician fluent in English who taught himself Chinese in order to talk with the international guests who find their way to the restaurant. He runs the plac

chef_bio.background: Kinatei opened on July 1, 2011, near JR Nara Station. Its owner has been vegetarian for around 45 years and was originally a musician fluent in English who taught himself Chinese in order to talk with the international guests who find their way to the restaurant. He runs the place as a 100% vegetarian kitchen and has since opened a sister vegan guesthouse and restaurant, Kina House Sesoko, on an island in Okinawa.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner cooks to offer food that is anshin-anzen (safe and reassuring) and to build heart-to-heart communication through meals as a small contribution to world peace. Ingredients are organic and pesticide-free where possible, free of additives and chemical seasonings, and the kitchen avoids the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion) in the Buddhist tradition — reportedly the only Nara restaurant to do so.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature plant-based dishes include a tofu hamburger studded with diced lotus root for texture and boosted with soy chips, a chinjao-rosu (pepper-steak-style stir-fry) made with non-GMO soy meat, and a vegetable minestrone built on celery, zucchini, potato and concentrated tomato. Lunch is a set of four vegetable dishes, a miso soup blending four kinds of miso, and a choice of brown or seven-tenths-milled rice. Vegan ramen and udon round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The owner, vegetarian for about 45 years, was a musician who learned Chinese on his own to welcome the international visitors who come to the restaurant; a world map inside marks where guests have traveled from.
Broth never contains bonito — only kombu and shiitake — and the cookware and tableware have never been used to prepare meat or fish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a gluten-free kitchen; standard ramen and udon use wheat noodles and soy-based seasonings are used throughout, so the regular menu is not celiac-safe without arrangement.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-based seasonings and soy-meat analogues are central to the menu; assume standard soy sauce contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen states its cookware and tableware have never been used to cook meat or fish, minimizing animal cross-contact.
Broth uses only kombu and shiitake with no bonito, and dishes are dairy-free and egg-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free, vegan, and allergy accommodations can be arranged with advance reservation; the owner himself has lifelong allergies and screens ingredients carefully.

positives.text: Fully 100% plant-based with no animal protein in any ingredient, oil, or broth, plus avoidance of the five pungent vegetables.
English-speaking owner and multilingual support make it easy to discuss dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner speaks English (and self-taught Chinese) and welcomes international guests, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English and ask about vegan and allergy needs.

places.name: 喫茶ツバキ (Kissa Tsubaki)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a traditional kissaten whose menu centers on coffee, sweets (custard pudding, meringue chantilly, coffee jelly), hot cakes, and Neapolitan pasta — the pasta is wheat, and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan options. The menu is coffee-house fare built around dairy and eggs (custard pudding, cream soda, meringue chantilly, hot cakes) and the Neapolitan pasta is topped with hamburg steak and a soft-boiled egg. No plant-based dishes or vegan accommodation are advertised. A vegan could likely order black coffee or tea only.
places.neighborhood: ならまち北西・椿井町（椿井市場近く、近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約7分）
places.cuisine: 純喫茶・レトロ喫茶店
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Tsubaki is the cafe wing of Kagirohiya Shibata Iryoten (かぎろいや 柴田衣料店), a long-established Nara kimono and clothing shop founded in 1912-1913 (Taisho era). The family carved a nostalgic, retro-modern kissaten out of part of the historic shop, opening it in late 2024 and holdi

roles: family-run cafe operated by a 1910s-founded kimono shop
chef_bio.origin: Nara — Tsubai-cho, in the northwest of the Naramachi old town near Tsubai Market
chef_bio.background: Kissa Tsubaki is the cafe wing of Kagirohiya Shibata Iryoten (かぎろいや 柴田衣料店), a long-established Nara kimono and clothing shop founded in 1912-1913 (Taisho era). The family carved a nostalgic, retro-modern kissaten out of part of the historic shop, opening it in late 2024 and holding a grand opening on April 18, 2025. Staff serve in kimono, and the space is filled with sofa seating and antique-style chairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners conceived the cafe as a bridge between the shop's traditional kimono heritage and contemporary life — a calm, spacious retreat where locals and travelers alike can slow down and feel they have stepped back through Nara's history. Ingredients are sourced from neighboring local vendors as a deliberate community-minded practice.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-dripped house self-roasted coffee, custard pudding, melon cream soda, coffee jelly (with an optional whiskey syrup), meringue chantilly with seasonal fruit sauce, and a hearty Neapolitan pasta topped with hamburg steak and a soft-boiled egg. The shop also pours Japanese black tea grown on Nara farms.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies part of the historic Shibata kimono shop and offers authentic kimono rentals plus vintage-furniture photo spots, so guests can dress in kimono and shoot photos as part of a Naramachi stroll.
The coffee jelly was noted as 早くも人気 (already a hit) shortly after opening.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, walk-in-welcome retro coffee house where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though staff are used to tourists since it doubles as a kimono-rental photo spot.

places.name: ボリクコーヒー (bolik coffee)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free offering. Menu centers on house-baked sweets, scones, and hot ham-and-cheese sandwiches (all wheat-based), plus coffee. No information indicates gluten-free options or celiac-safe handling, so a gluten-free diner would have to ask, and options are likely very limited.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented café. Documented items include dairy- and meat-containing dishes such as borscht with yogurt, ham-and-cheese sandwiches, and Western-style cakes (Mont Blanc, tiramisu, carrot cake). Black coffee is fine, but no plant-based milk or vegan food is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask and expect limited choices.
places.hours_raw: 12:30～18:00（L.O. 17:30）、月曜・火曜 定休日。展示開催時以外はお休みになる期間もあり、営業状況はInstagram（@kanakanabolikcoffee）で要確認。
places.neighborhood: ならまち（庚申堂のすぐ隣）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒー・古民家カフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] bolik coffee is the hideaway-style sister shop of the long-loved Naramachi machiya café Kanakana, both run by Miho Ioka and her husband. Tucked into the old merchant quarter right beside Koshin-dō, it occupies a quiet townhouse that doubles as a shop for Russian and Eastern-Europ

chef_bio.background: bolik coffee is the hideaway-style sister shop of the long-loved Naramachi machiya café Kanakana, both run by Miho Ioka and her husband. Tucked into the old merchant quarter right beside Koshin-dō, it occupies a quiet townhouse that doubles as a shop for Russian and Eastern-European goods and books, and a gallery that hosts rotating artist exhibitions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ioka has traveled to Russia and Eastern Europe nearly forty times to hunt for the cute homewares and folk objects she loves; bolik channels that enduring curiosity for travel and craft into a relaxed adult café where coffee, handmade food, and a rotating display of art and imported goods come together.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee using beans roasted by the affiliated Kanakana, paired with house-made baked sweets (cakes such as Mont Blanc, tiramisu and carrot cake, and scones). Lunch sets rotate every month or two and pair a Japanese soup set with an international one — the signature 'Bolik soup set' features a pink chilled borscht made with beets and yogurt, served with a hot ham-and-cheese sandwich, salad with garlic-ginger dressing, and pickles.

anecdotes.text: Owner Miho Ioka has made close to forty trips to Russia to buy decorative goods, and has authored several books about her finds, including 'かわいいロシアのA to Z' and 'カナカナのかわいいロシアに出会う旅'.
The café sits right next to Naramachi's Koshin-dō and its shelves are lined with Russian matryoshka dolls and world goods, with irregular exhibition events shaping when it opens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around wheat-based baked sweets, scones, and bread-based hot sandwiches in a small shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes include yogurt-based borscht, ham-and-cheese sandwiches, and dairy cakes, so vegan and animal-based items share the same kitchen with no documented separation.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, individually run machiya café where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way; English support is not guaranteed, and you should check the shop's Instagram for whether it is open before visiting.

places.name: 喫茶52 (Kissa 52)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The menu is built around toast and other wheat-based light fare in a shared small kitchen, with no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Drinks like coffee or fruit squash are naturally wheat-free, but there is no allergen control documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Showa-style cafe centered on coffee, dairy cream sodas, custard pudding (eggs and milk), and toast — not a vegan destination. Black coffee and some fruit-based squashes (lemon squash, melon soda without ice cream) could be ordered, but most signature items contain dairy and/or egg. No documented vegan menu; you would need to ask.
places.hours_raw: 火～日 10:00-20:00（L.O. 19:00）／定休日 月曜（火曜不定休あり）
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, south of Kofuku-ji on the slope toward Sarus
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on 2 May 2021 on the slope linking Kofuku-ji and Sarusawa Pond in central Nara, Kissa 52 is a small owner-run pure kissaten built as a love letter to the Showa-era coffee shops the owner remembers from childhood.

chef_bio.background: Opened on 2 May 2021 on the slope linking Kofuku-ji and Sarusawa Pond in central Nara, Kissa 52 is a small owner-run pure kissaten built as a love letter to the Showa-era coffee shops the owner remembers from childhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's concept is a 'crossroads of culture and history' — a neoclassical, vintage space meant to feel nostalgic to older guests and fresh to younger ones, with vinyl records playing from a jukebox.
chef_bio.specialty: Adult-twist melon cream soda (around 950 yen), supervised in collaboration with the well-known Nara bar scene and inspired by absinthe, with melting ice cream and a clouded effect; house creme caramel pudding (around 600-700 yen) made with specialty eggs from Tokushima, with an optional brandy-caramel; and coffee brewed with beans from Nara roaster ROKUMEI COFFEE.

anecdotes.text: The compact neo-classical interior is filled with retro touches — a jukebox spinning vinyl records, vintage game-table cabinets, and Showa-era curios — turning childhood treats like melon soda and pudding into a calm, grown-up experience.
The cafe collaborates with Nara's craft drink makers and roaster ROKUMEI COFFEE, and has experimented with drinks featuring Yamato Tachibana, a traditional Nara citrus.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based toast is a core menu item prepared in a small shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items (cream sodas, custard pudding) contain dairy and egg; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, design-conscious retro cafe where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, quiet manner go a long way; menus are picture-friendly (cream sodas, pudding) so ordering is easy even with limited Japanese.

places.name: アーガイル (Argyle)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop whose menu centers on toast, sandwiches, hot dogs, pasta, and curry — all wheat-based. There is no indication of any gluten-free offering or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A retro kissaten built around morning sets (toast, boiled egg, yogurt, ham and eggs, omelette rice) and siphon-brewed coffee, with pasta and curry. The food is egg-, dairy-, and meat-heavy with no documented vegan items. A plant-based diner would have very little to eat beyond black coffee or tea; you would need to ask in person about anything else.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 6:30–18:30; Sun 6:30–18:00. Opens early at 6:30 AM, with morning sets a popular draw.
places.neighborhood: Omiyacho (between JR Nara and Kintetsu Shin-Omiya stations)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Argyle opened in 1981 on the ground floor of a large apartment building and has held onto a deeply Showa-era atmosphere ever since. Worn wooden tables are surrounded by standing and wall clocks whose slightly out-of-sync ticking has become part of the room's charm, and an aquariu

chef_bio.origin: Nara City, in the Omiyacho area between JR Nara and Kintetsu Shin-Omiya stations
chef_bio.background: Argyle opened in 1981 on the ground floor of a large apartment building and has held onto a deeply Showa-era atmosphere ever since. Worn wooden tables are surrounded by standing and wall clocks whose slightly out-of-sync ticking has become part of the room's charm, and an aquarium of fish sits near the counter alongside shelves of books on all sorts of subjects.
chef_bio.philosophy: A gentle, unhurried place: soft background music, the murmur of clocks, and calm conversation between the master and regulars give the sense that time slows down inside. Coffee is drip-brewed by siphon only after you order, balanced between acidity and bitterness so it stays clean and easy to drink.
chef_bio.specialty: Early-morning morning sets and siphon-brewed coffee. The 600-yen morning lets you choose toast (with salad), a sandwich, or a hot dog, served with a boiled egg, yogurt, a seasonal fruit plate, and coffee or tea. Beyond mornings the kitchen makes ham and eggs, omelette rice, pasta, and curry.

anecdotes.text: The shop opens at 6:30 in the morning, and the freshly made, generously portioned 600-yen morning set has long been one of its biggest draws.
Visitors describe an atmosphere where even a first-timer is made to feel like a regular, the entrance bell announcing each arrival into the retro room.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on toast, sandwiches, hot dogs, pasta, and curry — wheat is pervasive throughout the kitchen, so cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core offerings rely on eggs, dairy (yogurt), and meat; no plant-based menu is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet neighborhood kissaten run by a master who chats with regulars in Japanese, so basic Japanese phrases and a relaxed, respectful manner will help a foreign student feel at home.

places.name: 喫茶デイジー (Kissa Daisy)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a retro kissaten whose signature dishes are wheat-based: Napolitan spaghetti, sandwiches, and ketchup-rice omurice. No dedicated GF menu, no celiac accommodation documented, and a shared kitchen. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Core items contain animal products (Napolitan with bacon, chicken omurice, braised-pork lu rou han, cream soda with ice cream, pudding with egg and cream). Cream sodas without ice cream or plain drinks might be the only plant-based options; a vegan would need to ask, and choices would be very limited.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00, closed Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Mochiidono shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara S
places.notes: 喫茶デイジー (Kissa Daisy) began as a tenant of もちいどの夢CUBE, an incubator for new shops within Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade, opening there on November 20, 2020. After 'graduating' from the incubator, it relocated about ten seconds' walk away to its own larger permanent space and re
