chef_bio.background: 喫茶デイジー (Kissa Daisy) began as a tenant of もちいどの夢CUBE, an incubator for new shops within Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade, opening there on November 20, 2020. After 'graduating' from the incubator, it relocated about ten seconds' walk away to its own larger permanent space and reopened on July 23, 2023, roughly doubling its seating.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Izumi wanted to create 'a gathering place for people' and 'a place where visitors' hearts can reset' over a leisurely tea or meal, with an interior so distinctive that 'no matter where you photograph it, you instantly know it's Kissa Daisy.'
chef_bio.specialty: Classic kissaten fare with a retro-pop twist: Napolitan spaghetti (thick noodles with onions, carrots, peppers and bacon), chicken ketchup-rice omurice, jewel-named cream sodas (Ruby/pomegranate, Sapphire/blueberry, Emerald/melon) served in vintage glassware, and a dense retro 'Runrun Pudding' with whipped cream and a cherry.

anecdotes.text: The café is wall-to-wall 1980s-90s nostalgia: a Michelin Man and Tetsujin 28-go robot mark the entrance, CRT televisions, a Super Famicom and tamagotchi sit among the décor, and over 200 Famicom cartridges are available for customers to actually play while 80s-90s pop plays overhead.
The venue takes its name and look from the daisy-patterned tablecloths, and the relocated shop updated its recipes — for example the omurice now folds chicken into the ketchup rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling documented; signature dishes are wheat-based (Napolitan spaghetti, sandwiches) prepared in a shared small kitchen, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan menu or cross-contact handling documented; most items contain meat, egg, or dairy and are cooked on shared equipment.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, casual retro café where a foreign student can comfortably order with simple Japanese and pointing; staff are unlikely to speak much English, but the relaxed, playful atmosphere (retro games, cream sodas) makes it welcoming.

places.name: 萬御菓子誂處 樫舎 (Yorozu Onkashi Atsuraedokoro Kashiya)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a traditional wagashi (Japanese confectionery) shop. While many wagashi are rice/bean based (mochi, warabimochi, namagashi), the kitchen is not gluten-free and there is no documented allergen control or dedicated gluten-free handling. Dry confections, monaka-style items, and shared production surfaces mean wheat cross-contact cannot be ruled out. Not suitable for celiacs; a sensitive eater would need to ask in detail in person.
places.vegan_detail: Many of the sweets are plant-based by nature (sugar, adzuki bean paste, glutinous rice, kinako), and matcha/Nara bancha tea are vegan. However, the menu is not designed for vegans and there is no documented vegan labeling. Some items can contain egg or dairy, and the omakase course is chef's choice, so a vegan would need to ask which specific sweets are free of animal ingredients. Plain seasonal namagashi with matcha is the most likely vegan-friendly option but must be confirmed in person.
places.hours_raw: 物販（菓子販売）9:00-18:00。茶房・喫茶 11:00-16:30（予約優先）。和菓子コースは11:00開始の完全予約制。年中無休。
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約10分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kashiya was founded in 2008 in a renovated traditional townhouse in the historic Naramachi district near Gango-ji, by head wagashi craftsman Kita Seiichiro. The shop both sells handmade Japanese confections and offers a sit-down experience: a first-floor counter where sweets are

chef_bio.background: Kashiya was founded in 2008 in a renovated traditional townhouse in the historic Naramachi district near Gango-ji, by head wagashi craftsman Kita Seiichiro. The shop both sells handmade Japanese confections and offers a sit-down experience: a first-floor counter where sweets are made in front of guests, and a second-floor tea room.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kita's guiding belief is that the ingredients are everything: as he puts it, a craftsman can only absorb water into, heat, and sweeten ingredients, but cannot create flavor itself, so the materials are the life of the sweet. The shop's stated principle is to make confections that do not defile the finest ingredients nurtured by heaven and earth.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal jo-namagashi (上生菓子, fine fresh sweets), a chef's-choice four-item wagashi course served at the counter, and summer kakigori (shaved ice) presented in Nara lacquerware. Items include dry confections made with Shiga kinako and mochi using Omi-Habutae rice paper.

anecdotes.text: The owner sources premium ingredients from specific regions to showcase them at their peak, and the counter course (around 3,300 yen, reservation required) lets guests watch each sweet being hand-formed before them, paired with a choice of Nara bancha, matcha, coffee, or roasted barley tea.
Summer kakigori is served in Nara lacquerware bowls, and the shop pairs fresh seasonal namagashi with matcha for around 1,210 yen for less formal visits.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol is documented; the shop produces a range of traditional confections, some of which contain wheat, in a shared kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan handling is documented; the omakase course is chef's choice and some wagashi can contain egg or dairy, so vegans must confirm specific items in person.

positives.text: Many core offerings are naturally plant-based (adzuki bean paste, glutinous rice, kinako, sugar) and tea service includes vegan matcha and Nara bancha.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, traditional family-run wagashi house where the counter sweets course is fully reservation-based and seating is on tatami, so a foreign student should ideally reserve ahead, expect a calm and somewhat formal atmosphere, and be ready to communicate basic needs in Japanese.

places.name: 柿の葉すし本舗たなか なら本店（柿の葉茶屋） (Kakinoha Sushi Honpo Tanaka Nara Honten (Kak)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The core product, kakinoha-zushi (persimmon-leaf sushi), is vinegared rice topped with fish, and the eat-in sets are served with miso soup containing fu (wheat gluten). Sushi and sauces use standard Japanese soy sauce (made with wheat), and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation, separate handling, or staff allergy accommodation. Celiac travelers should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature dish is fish-topped sushi (mackerel/saba, salmon, sea bream, conger eel, red tuna), and the menu centers on seafood. Miso soup and sauces are not confirmed dashi-free or animal-free. While persimmon sweets (jukushi) and some side dishes exist, there is no vegan menu or accommodation documented.
places.hours_raw: Main shop 9:00-19:00 (Tabelog lists 9:30-19:00); Kakinoha Tea House (柿の葉茶屋 eat-in) 11:00-16:00. Hours may change without notice.
places.neighborhood: 東向商店街入口／近鉄奈良駅すぐ (Higashimuki shopping street entrance, ~1-2
places.notes: Kakinoha Sushi Honpo Tanaka traces its roots to the late Meiji era in Gojo, Nara. The founder, Tanaka Tokumatsu, was a master carpenter whose wife Yoshi cooked for his many craftsmen; the family later ran a diner in the shopping street by Gojo Station, where the persimmon-leaf su

chef_bio.background: Kakinoha Sushi Honpo Tanaka traces its roots to the late Meiji era in Gojo, Nara. The founder, Tanaka Tokumatsu, was a master carpenter whose wife Yoshi cooked for his many craftsmen; the family later ran a diner in the shopping street by Gojo Station, where the persimmon-leaf sushi they made only in summer became the origin of the company's product. Tokumatsu's grandson Shuji took over, and the kakinoha-zushi made by his wife Taka grew into a local specialty that drew customers from Osaka and Kobe. Deciding to make it the family's main trade, Shuji formally established the company in 1973. This Nara flagship sits at the entrance of the Higashimuki shopping street just outside Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Operating under the concept of 'tradition and innovation' (伝統と革新), the company aims not only to preserve the regional dish of kakinoha-zushi but to keep adding something new, researching persimmon-leaf salting techniques and expanding nationwide shipping.
chef_bio.specialty: Kakinoha-zushi (persimmon-leaf sushi) wrapped in salted persimmon leaves, with mackerel (saba), salmon, sea bream, conger eel and red tuna; eat-in sets pairing the sushi with seasonal sides and soup; and jukushi, a creamy frozen dessert made from Nara's Gojo persimmons.

anecdotes.text: When the Showa Emperor and Empress visited Nara Prefecture in 1979, the company's kakinoha-zushi was presented to them via the Chuguji temple abbess.

The flagship's eat-in 'Kakinoha Tea House' lets visitors enjoy the persimmon-leaf sushi and Tanaka's original sushi and Japanese sweets on the spot, and the building also offers a Nara-themed library, free umbrella rental, and luggage storage for travelers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Eat-in sushi sets are accompanied by miso soup containing fu (wheat gluten), indicating wheat is present in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi and accompanying sauces use standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat; no gluten-free soy alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around fish-topped sushi (mackerel, salmon, sea bream, conger eel, tuna), so shared surfaces and tools contact seafood; no separate vegan handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tourist-friendly flagship shop right at the station with English menus, takeout, and casual eat-in sets, so a foreign student can comfortably order pointing at displays and pay by card.

places.name: カフェ コジカ (Cafe Cojica)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The menu is built around Western-style yoshoku — braised Yamato beef and pork hamburger steak with demi-glace, smoked pork loin, pancakes, and pudding — all of which typically contain wheat (flour, breadcrumbs, demi-glace roux). No dedicated fryer, allergen menu, or celiac handling is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and fish-forward Western cafe. Signature dishes are Yamato beef/pork hamburger steak, hojicha-smoked Yamato pork loin, and dishes described as particular about fish. Sweets (pudding, pancakes, tea latte) use dairy and eggs. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented. A vegan diner would have very little to eat here; ask in advance, but assume nothing is reliably vegan.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-16:00 daily it is open; dinner 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00) Wed-Thu, 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00) Fri-Sat and holidays; Sunday lunch only (no dinner). From November 2025 closed Mondays and Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: Sanjo-cho, central Nara (walkable from Kintetsu/JR Nara Stat
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe Cojica (the name plays on kojika, a baby deer, echoing Nara's famous deer) opened in January 2010 inside a wooden townhouse said to be more than 120 years old. The owners renovated the old machiya while keeping its tatami rooms, wooden beams, and step-up entry where guests s

chef_bio.background: Cafe Cojica (the name plays on kojika, a baby deer, echoing Nara's famous deer) opened in January 2010 inside a wooden townhouse said to be more than 120 years old. The owners renovated the old machiya while keeping its tatami rooms, wooden beams, and step-up entry where guests slip off their shoes, giving the place a nostalgic, lived-in calm just a short walk from Nara's station area and the Naramachi quarter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe describes itself around an 'all for mams' idea — cooking the kind of nourishing, locally grown food a mother would happily feed her own children. Nearly every dish leans on Nara-grown ingredients, from Yamato beef and pork to Yanagyu-district tea and Kurokawa Honke kuzu, and the space is built to welcome families, with tatami room, child menus, and facilities for parents with babies.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature plate is a braised hamburger steak made from Yamato beef and Yamato pork under a rich demi-glace sauce; other favorites include a Japanese-style ankake hamburger, hojicha tea-smoked Yamato pork loin, fluffy skillet pancakes with rotating seasonal toppings, and the firm, old-fashioned 'cojica pudding' finished with a little deer-shaped cookie. Drinks lean local too, like a deep-steamed latte made with Nara prefecture tea.

anecdotes.text: Deer motifs and small deer ornaments are tucked throughout the interior, a nod both to the cafe's fawn name and to Nara's free-roaming deer.
The signature 'cojica pudding' is a firm, nostalgic-style custard served with a deer-shaped cookie as an accent.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, family-friendly old-house cafe where a little Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; English support is not advertised, but the picture-friendly Western menu (hamburger, pancakes, pudding) makes ordering easy.

places.name: 竹の館 (Take no Yakata)
places.gf_detail: This is an oden and udon house. Udon is wheat noodles, and the oden broth is a Japanese dashi that almost certainly contains soy sauce (wheat) and bonito/fish stock, with many items (fishcakes, etc.) containing wheat binders. There is no documented gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional oden/izakaya. The oden simmering broth is built on fish/bonito dashi and the menu centers on eggs, fishcakes, octopus, sausage and other animal items, so it is not vegan. No plant-based broth or vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:00～翌2:00（深夜営業）。定休日は火曜日。（食べログでは月・火休と記載される場合あり）
places.neighborhood: 南魚屋町（近鉄奈良駅から南へ徒歩約10分、やすらぎの道沿い）
places.cuisine: おでん・うどん・居酒屋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 竹の館 is a long-loved oden and udon house tucked along Yasuragi-no-michi, a short walk south of the heart of Nara. The interior is dressed throughout in bamboo, which gives the room its name and a quiet, woody warmth, and a red lantern reading 'おでん・うどん' marks the door.

chef_bio.background: 竹の館 is a long-loved oden and udon house tucked along Yasuragi-no-michi, a short walk south of the heart of Nara. The interior is dressed throughout in bamboo, which gives the room its name and a quiet, woody warmth, and a red lantern reading 'おでん・うどん' marks the door.
chef_bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a gentle dashi, with everything from eggs, daikon and roll cabbage to octopus, mushrooms, sausage and a recommended chicken tsukune skewer, each priced from around 100 to 400 yen. The local custom here is to finish your oden with a bowl of udon; the salmon-flake onigiri is also a favorite.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as the ambiance of a longstanding, unpretentious eatery: counter seats look straight onto the bubbling oden pot, the prices stay friendly, and solo diners are made to feel at home, which is why it is beloved by locals and travelers alike.
It keeps deep-night hours, staying open until 2 a.m., making it a go-to for a late, comforting bowl of oden and udon after the rest of central Nara has quieted down.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on oden and wheat udon; the shared simmering dashi and many oden items contain soy sauce (wheat) and fish stock, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden broth in this style is seasoned with regular soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All oden items share one pot of fish/bonito dashi alongside eggs, fishcakes and meat, so any item carries animal-broth cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, unpretentious late-night local spot that warmly accepts solo diners, but it is a Japanese-speaking neighborhood haunt, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the oden pot) will go a long way.

places.name: 大廣 (Taikou (Oohiro))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a classic Chinese diner whose core menu — ramen, tanmen (野菜そば), wonton, gyoza, fried rice and stir-fries — is built on wheat noodles, wheat-skin dumplings/wonton, and wheat-based soy sauce seasonings. Broths are simmered with soy seasoning. There is no documented gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or celiac handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The kitchen centers on pork (housemade chashu), chicken stock, and meat/seafood stir-fries; the signature ramen broth is chicken-based simmered 5+ hours and the tanmen comes loaded with seasonal vegetables but is not documented as meat/dashi-free. A vegetable-heavy 野菜そば (tanmen) exists, but no dish is confirmed vegan and broths/seasonings are animal-based, so a vegan would have to ask directly and verify ingredients. Assume not vegan-safe without confirmation.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺・椿井市場内（東向商店街近く、奈良最古の商店街）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 大廣 is a beloved neighborhood Chinese diner tucked inside Tsubai Market, said to be Nara's oldest market, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It was opened in 1971 by founder 村上義廣 (Yoshihiro Murakami), who ran it side by side with his wife 三和子 (Miwako) for 54 years. The vinta

chef_bio.background: 大廣 is a beloved neighborhood Chinese diner tucked inside Tsubai Market, said to be Nara's oldest market, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It was opened in 1971 by founder 村上義廣 (Yoshihiro Murakami), who ran it side by side with his wife 三和子 (Miwako) for 54 years. The vintage market storefront — flowers set out neatly by the counter — has been likened to a scene from Spirited Away. In September 2025 their eldest son, 力 (Chikara), took over as the second-generation owner.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything homemade and made to last: across an enormous menu of over 100 items, the core components are prepared by hand. The new generation has leaned back into the originals — reviving housemade menma and wakame after a 30-year absence and returning the chashu to its traditional twine-tied method.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-tied housemade chashu and a clear, gently ginger-scented broth. The chashu ramen (チャーシュー麺) is the signature: pork is bound with twine to form a beautiful round roll and simmered with Kochi ginger for a moist-yet-refreshing balance, in a chicken stock cooked more than five hours. The tanmen / 野菜そば piles on seasonal vegetables — eggplant, broccoli, daikon and more — in a light, clean soup. Gyoza, menma, wakame and chili oil are all made in-house.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1971; Yoshihiro and Miwako Murakami ran the counter together for 54 years before their eldest son Chikara became the second-generation owner in September 2025.
Under Chikara, housemade menma and wakame returned to the bowl after 30 years, and the chashu went back to being hand-tied with twine for its signature round shape.

A couple-run counter shop inside historic Tsubai Market with flowers by the counter, whose nostalgic storefront has been compared to Spirited Away; long-time regulars come as much for the proprietors as for the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-noodle ramen/tanmen, wheat-skin gyoza and wonton, and wheat-based soy seasonings are all prepared in a single small shared kitchen, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and stir-fries are seasoned with soy-based (wheat-containing) sauce; the chashu ramen uses a soy-seasoned chicken stock.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on pork (housemade chashu) and a multi-hour chicken stock; even vegetable dishes like the tanmen are cooked in the same broth/seasoning system and are not documented as animal-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, old-school family-run counter diner in a historic market where ordering and chatting happen in Japanese, so it is most comfortable if you can manage a little Japanese or point at the menu; staff are warm and welcoming.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick crimped (chijire) noodles supplied by a local noodle maker (Inoue Seimen). Chewy and mochi-like with a natural wheat sweetness; soak up the gentle shoyu broth well. House-makes its own menma and chili oil, but the noodles themselves are not handmade.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu Ramen (チャーシューメン) — the classic shoyu chuka soba piled with the shop's signature roast pork; the regular Ramen is the everyday version of the same bowl.
ramen.chashu: The heart of the bowl: pork loin tied with twine and braised so it slices into neat firm rounds, cooked with Kochi-grown ginger for a clean, refreshing lift against its moist fat. Recently returned to the shop's original 1971 braising method.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; a counter-only town-Chinese kitchen with no documented rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on long-simmered chicken bones and feet, and the bowls are dressed with pork chashu and naruto. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented. The vegetable-forward tanmen still uses the animal soup.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl of old-Nara nostalgia: the kind of gentle, twine-tied-chashu chuka soba that anchored Showa-era town-Chinese kitchens, its soul a chicken-bone-and-feet shoyu broth simmered five-plus hours into something quiet and comforting rather than flashy. You taste the lineage of the neighborhood Chinese counter that has fed locals since 1971, where the roast pork — not a fad — is the star.

places.highlight: A bowl of old-Nara nostalgia: the kind of gentle, twine-tied-chashu chuka soba that anchored Showa-era town-Chinese kitchens, its soul a chicken-bone-and-feet shoyu broth simmered five-plus hours into something quiet and comforting rather than flashy. You taste the lineage of the neighborhood Chinese counter that has fed locals since 1971, where the roast pork — not a fad — is the star.

places.name: 寧楽菓子司 中西与三郎 (Nara Kashitsukasa Nakanishi Yosaburo)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no celiac accommodation is documented. While some wagashi here are rice- or arrowroot-based (warabi mochi uses warabi/kuzu starch; kuzu mochi uses arrowroot), the Naramachi dango is made with uiro (rice-flour based, but no allergen disclosure) and other namagashi and seasonal items may contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, ingredient labeling, or cross-contamination control is published. A celiac traveler should not assume any item is safe without confirming directly with the shop.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan certification or labeling is published. Several signature items are plant-based by tradition: warabi mochi (warabi/kuzu starch with house-made azuki red bean paste), kuzu mochi, and azuki-based sweets typically contain no animal products. However, sets are served with matcha (vegan) but some seasonal items, namagashi, or zenzai could contain non-vegan ingredients, and no ingredient disclosure exists. Vegans should ask item by item. The shop is happy to take questions; this is a traditional sweets house, not a marketed vegan venue.
places.hours_raw: Tea room (Rokubouan): weekdays 11:00-17:30, weekends/holidays 11:00-18:00 (last order). Shop / product sales: 9:30-18:00 daily. Official site states the tea room is closed Mon & Tue; Tabelog lists irregular closures announced on the official site, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Wakido-cho), near Mochiidono shopping arcade, abo
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1913 (Taisho 2) in the heart of Naramachi, this long-established confectioner began as a mochi shop and has carried its craft through generations while continuing to invent new sweets. The shop sits in a quiet townhouse just off the Mochiidono arcade, with a tatami tea

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1913 (Taisho 2) in the heart of Naramachi, this long-established confectioner began as a mochi shop and has carried its craft through generations while continuing to invent new sweets. The shop sits in a quiet townhouse just off the Mochiidono arcade, with a tatami tea room called Rokubouan tucked behind it where guests slip off their shoes and sit overlooking an inner courtyard.
chef_bio.philosophy: To offer an unhurried moment over seasonal sweets and matcha in a traditional machiya, while keeping wagashi rooted in local ingredients and Naramachi's culture.
chef_bio.specialty: Warabi mochi made with high-grade warabi/arrowroot starch (offered plain and in a Yamato-tea version) served with house-made azuki paste; Naramachi dango, a strolling snack kneading Yamato tea, miso and azuki into uiro; Yoshino kuzu mochi; the playful 'Koshin-san' sweet shaped after the protective monkey talismans that hang from Naramachi houses; and seasonal namagashi named for flowers. In summer the tea room serves kakigori shaved ice with Yamato-tea and house-made syrups.

anecdotes.text: The 'Naramachi dango' was devised as a snack to eat while walking and exploring the old Naramachi quarter, kneading Yamato tea, miso and azuki into uiro.
The 'Koshin-san' sweet is modeled on the red monkey amulets traditionally hung from the eaves of Naramachi townhouses as protective charms.
Behind the shop, the Rokubouan tea room is an intimate tatami space you enter shoeless, looking out over an interior courtyard.

positives.text: Several signature sweets are plant-based by tradition: warabi mochi (warabi/kuzu starch with house-made azuki paste) and Yoshino kuzu mochi are starch- and bean-based rather than wheat- or dairy-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, traditional townhouse tea room where you remove your shoes and sit on tatami; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably, even without fluent English service.

places.name: ロクメイコーヒー 奈良店 (Rokumei Coffee Co. Nara)
places.gf_detail: This is a specialty coffee café, not a gluten-free destination. Drip coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the signature food is freshly baked croissants made with Nara-grown wheat flour, and other café snacks are wheat-based. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, no celiac protocol, and a shared kitchen handling wheat pastries. Not suitable for strict celiacs beyond ordering plain black coffee. Ask staff if you have concerns.
places.vegan_detail: Black hand-drip and specialty coffee is plant-based, and plant-milk substitution may be possible for milk-based drinks (confirm with staff). However, the headline food items are butter croissants and other dairy/egg-containing pastries, so there is no vegan food program. Tabelog lists a "vegetarian-friendly / particular about vegetables" tag, but no documented dedicated vegan menu. A coffee-only vegan visit works; a vegan meal does not.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（西御門町・東向商店街エリア）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー／ハンドドリップ・カフェ
places.notes: Rokumei Coffee Co. began in 1974 as a traditional Nara kissaten (the former 'coffee beans ROCOCO') and was reborn as a specialty-coffee roaster-café in 2010. Its flagship sits just a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, with a deer (鹿/鹿鳴) motif nodding to the city.

chef_bio.background: Rokumei Coffee Co. began in 1974 as a traditional Nara kissaten (the former 'coffee beans ROCOCO') and was reborn as a specialty-coffee roaster-café in 2010. Its flagship sits just a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, with a deer (鹿/鹿鳴) motif nodding to the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'seed to cup' / 'from production to the cup' approach with rigorous quality control, plus a local-production-for-local-consumption ethos expressed through Nara-wheat croissants and an everyday-drinkable house blend.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-dripped single-origin and house 'Rokumei Blend' specialty coffee, roasted in-house, served alongside croissants baked daily with Nara-grown wheat.

anecdotes.text: Owner-roaster Koji Ida won the Japan Coffee Roasting Championship (JCRC) in 2018 and is a CQI-certified Q Grader, personally traveling to coffee farms to source and then roasting the beans in-house.

The house Rokumei Blend is designed to be enjoyed daily 'without fatigue,' with woody fruitiness, a clean finish and sweet lingering notes, while shallow-roast hand drips draw out fruity sweetness and acidity.
Croissants are made with Nara-grown wheat as an expression of local-production-for-local-consumption, chosen to pair with the coffee's acidity and sweetness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat croissants and pastries are baked and handled on-site, so gluten is present in the kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter croissants and dairy/egg pastries are central to the menu; no separate vegan preparation is documented, so plant-based items risk dairy contact.

positives.text: Plain black hand-drip and specialty coffee is naturally gluten-free and vegan, and the baristas brew each cup to order.
Tabelog lists vegetarian-friendly and 'particular about vegetables' tags for the shop.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, modern specialty-coffee café used to tourists and students; an English Tabelog page and walk-in-friendly counter make it easy for a foreign student to order coffee and a croissant without reservations.

places.name: おばんざい HANARE (Obanzai HANARE)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The set lunch centers on deep-fried wheat-breaded mains (tonkatsu, tempura, fried chicken, fried horse mackerel, cream croquettes, spring rolls) and the obanzai sides and miso soup rely on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso) and almost certainly fish/kombu dashi containing wheat-based soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The buffet-style lunch lets diners pick four obanzai sides, several of which are vegetable-based (dried daikon, vegetable tempura, tofu salad, simmered vegetables), but the kitchen is omnivore and the main dish is a meat or fish protein. Sides are seasoned with soy sauce/miso and are highly likely to contain fish dashi, so they are not reliably vegan. A flexible vegetarian could assemble a vegetable-leaning plate, but vegan safety is not confirmed and would need to be asked about on-site.
places.hours_raw: 火～金 11:30-15:00（ランチのみ）／月・土・日・祝 定休
places.neighborhood: 奈良市中心部・三条通り（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
places.cuisine: おばんざい・日本料理
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] おばんざい HANARE opened in June 2025 on Sanjo-dori, the main shopping street running toward Kintetsu Nara Station. It is a daytime offshoot of A'su (アッス), a Korean-cuisine restaurant near JR Nara Station, and was created as a lunch-only concept by the same family.

chef_bio.background: おばんざい HANARE opened in June 2025 on Sanjo-dori, the main shopping street running toward Kintetsu Nara Station. It is a daytime offshoot of A'su (アッス), a Korean-cuisine restaurant near JR Nara Station, and was created as a lunch-only concept by the same family.
chef_bio.philosophy: Gentle, comforting home cooking made with care; the daily obanzai are described as having a flavor that 'gently soaks into the body.'
chef_bio.specialty: A single ¥1,800 set lunch in which diners choose one daily main protein (such as tonkatsu, tempura, fried chicken, skewered cutlets, cream croquettes, spring rolls or fried horse mackerel) and four home-style obanzai sides from a rotating selection (egg rolls, tofu salad, dried daikon, vegetable tempura, simmered dishes), served with rice, miso soup and pickles.

anecdotes.text: All of the obanzai are made by the owner's mother, who has experience teaching cooking classes, giving the menu a genuine home-kitchen character.
The shop opened on Sanjo-dori in early June 2025 as a lunch-time sister concept to the Korean restaurant A'su, drawing on A'su's supply of quality pork and vegetables.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The mains are mostly deep-fried wheat-breaded items (tonkatsu, tempura, fried chicken, fried fish, croquettes) sharing fryer oil, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Obanzai sides and miso soup use standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso) that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable obanzai are prepared in an omnivore kitchen alongside meat and fish and are likely seasoned with fish dashi; vegan separation is not documented.

positives.text: The buffet-style choose-your-sides format makes it easy to build a vegetable-leaning plate, and an English menu helps with asking about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the format is simple (pick one main plus four vegetable sides), so a foreign student can order comfortably even with little Japanese.

places.name: 鹿の舟 竈 (Shika no Fune Kamado)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Rice is cooked in a traditional wood-fired kamado and the meals center on local Nara rice, vegetables, tofu and miso, but soy sauce and miso typically contain wheat, and some dishes include wheat gluten (麸/fu, e.g. the Yamato beef hot pot with fu). The venue lists "allergy accommodations upon consultation," so a gluten-restricted diner should ask in advance; there is no documented gluten-free menu or dedicated handling for celiac safety.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-forward but not vegan by default. The kitchen leans heavily on Yamato (local Nara) vegetables, tofu, and a kombu-based dashi for the miso soup, which makes a vegetable-centered, partly vegetarian meal realistic. However the rotating set menus also include meat (e.g. Yamato beef hot pot) and fish-based items, and soy sauce/miso are standard. A vegan should ask which sets are free of animal ingredients; accommodation may be possible on request but is not guaranteed or formalized.
places.hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（食事は～15:00、ごはんがなくなり次第終了）、水曜定休
places.neighborhood: ならまち南端・京終（奈良市井上町）
places.cuisine: かまど炊きご飯と奈良食材の定食
places.notes: 竈 (Kamado) is the dining hall of 鹿の舟 (Shika no Fune), a three-part complex on the southern edge of Naramachi near Kyobate that opened in 2015. It was produced by Yukiko Ishimura, the founder of the long-beloved Nara café and lifestyle shop Kuruminoki (くるみの木), which opened in 1984

chef_bio.background: 竈 (Kamado) is the dining hall of 鹿の舟 (Shika no Fune), a three-part complex on the southern edge of Naramachi near Kyobate that opened in 2015. It was produced by Yukiko Ishimura, the founder of the long-beloved Nara café and lifestyle shop Kuruminoki (くるみの木), which opened in 1984. Alongside the Kamado dining hall, the complex holds an information center (繭 Mayu) and a café (囀 Saezuri).
chef_bio.philosophy: Hyper-local sourcing built around Nara: low-pesticide/organic local rice, Yamato vegetables, neighboring artisan condiments, and Yoshino cedar firewood, served in a calm, light-filled room that pairs rustic kamado cooking with quiet modern design.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice cooked the old way in a wood-fired kamado built into the counter, served as a seasonal set (teishoku) with miso soup, tofu, a vegetable side, and pickles. Documented dishes include nasu dengaku (eggplant with miso), seasonal tempura, nameko-and-greens miso soup, and a Yamato beef hot pot with tofu and wheat gluten.

anecdotes.text: The kamado sits right at the counter so guests watch and smell rice being cooked over firewood; reviewers describe the local rice as having an unpretentious sweetness and umami that comes through as you chew.
The space was conceived by Yukiko Ishimura of Kuruminoki as part of the 2015 Shika no Fune complex, designed around a 'laid-back yet beautifully modern' worldview in southern Naramachi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Meals are built on miso, soy sauce, and tofu, and at least one set (Yamato beef hot pot) includes wheat gluten (fu), so wheat is present across the standard menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus rotate and include meat (Yamato beef) and fish-based items alongside vegetable and tofu dishes, so a strictly vegan meal is not guaranteed and would need to be confirmed per day.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The venue lists allergy accommodations available upon consultation and offers an English menu.

positives.text: The miso soup and base preparations use a kombu-based dashi rather than fish dashi, making a vegetable-and-tofu-centered meal realistic for those avoiding fish stock.
Cooking centers on local Yamato vegetables, tofu, and kamado-cooked rice, so plant-forward options are a core part of the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the staff can discuss allergies on request, so a foreign student can manage comfortably with a friendly greeting and a few words of Japanese.

places.name: 鹿の舟 囀 (Shika no Fune Saezuri)
places.gf_detail: This is a café built around wheat: signature offerings include seasonal toasts on campagne bread, cakes, and brûlée-style desserts with homemade custard. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs; a gluten-sensitive visitor could potentially have a fruit dessert or a pot of Yamato tea but should ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Desserts feature dairy (homemade custard cream, vanilla ice cream) and the toasts use bread that may contain dairy/egg. A vegan could likely order a pot of organic Yamato tea, and possibly a plain seasonal fruit item if available, but cakes and signature desserts are not vegan. Ask staff about specific ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–17:00 (last order 16:30). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays per the official Saezuri page.
places.neighborhood: ならまち南端・京終（鹿の舟内）
places.notes: Saezuri is the tearoom of Shika no Fune, a complex housed in a renovated Taisho-era building near the southern edge of Naramachi in the Kyobate area. The whole project is produced by Kurumi no Ki, the beloved Nara café-and-crafts company founded by Ishimura Yukiko; Shika no Fune

chef_bio.chef_name: Ishimura Yukiko (founder of Kurumi no Ki)
chef_bio.background: Saezuri is the tearoom of Shika no Fune, a complex housed in a renovated Taisho-era building near the southern edge of Naramachi in the Kyobate area. The whole project is produced by Kurumi no Ki, the beloved Nara café-and-crafts company founded by Ishimura Yukiko; Shika no Fune also includes a tourist information space (Mayu) and a kamado-cooked rice restaurant (Kamado).
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder describes the wish behind the name as everyone — visitors and locals alike — riding together in one big boat heading toward the future; the room is designed so that outdoor greenery flows naturally into a bright, unhurried space for enjoying seasonal, Nara-grown ingredients at leisure.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit desserts that use whole fruits, signature plates like a fresh banana and nuts brulee with homemade custard and torched caramel, roasted whole apple with vanilla ice cream and caramel in season, seasonal toasts on Kyoto YENTA campagne bread (e.g. zucchini-and-plum, fresh ginger), and organic Yamato tea from the Tsukigase region of Nara.

anecdotes.text: The architecture was designed by Nakamura Yoshifumi and the logo by artist Mochizuki Michiharu, part of the carefully curated design identity of the space.
Desserts are built around whole seasonal fruit — 'using whole seasonal fruit to make delicious desserts' — a hallmark of the Kurumi no Ki kitchen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the menu (campagne-bread toasts, cakes), so heavy gluten presence and cross-contact in a small café kitchen should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature desserts use homemade custard cream and vanilla ice cream (dairy/egg), so dessert prep surfaces are not vegan-isolated.

positives.text: Organic Yamato tea from Nara's Tsukigase region is available and is naturally gluten-free and vegan as a drink.
Desserts emphasize whole seasonal fruit, so a fresh-fruit option may be available for restricted diners — ask on the day.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, design-conscious tearoom where pointing at the menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get you through; English support is not documented, so learning the names of a couple of desserts or teas helps.

places.name: 吉野本葛 天極堂 奈良本店 (Yoshino Honkuzu Tengyokudo Nara Honten)
places.gf_detail: Kuzu (本葛 / Yoshino arrowroot starch) is itself naturally gluten-free, and several signature items are starch-based: kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri, and kuzu yu. However this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The signature 葛うどん (kuzu udon) is made with thin wheat-flour noodles that have kuzu worked into them, so it is NOT gluten-free, and many savory dishes use dashi and soy-based seasonings. A celiac diner would need to ask carefully and stick to the pure-starch sweets; no documented allergen protocols or dedicated equipment.
places.vegan_detail: Some sweet items appear plant-based in principle (kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri and kuzu yu are made from kuzu starch and typically served with kinako/brown-sugar syrup), but this is not a vegan establishment. Savory kuzu dishes such as kuzu udon and kuzu porridge are commonly finished with fish dashi, and no vegan certification or ingredient disclosure is documented. A vegan would need to confirm dashi and toppings dish by dish.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:30 (L.O. 19:00); closed Tuesdays (open and closed the following day if Tuesday falls on a national holiday)
places.neighborhood: Oshiage-cho, beside the former Todai-ji West Gate (Saidaimon
places.notes: Tengyokudo is the dining and confection arm of Inoue Tengyokudo, a Nara kuzu maker established in 1870 that has produced Yoshino honkuzu (traditionally refined arrowroot starch) without interruption ever since. The company controls everything from raw material to finished product

chef_bio.background: Tengyokudo is the dining and confection arm of Inoue Tengyokudo, a Nara kuzu maker established in 1870 that has produced Yoshino honkuzu (traditionally refined arrowroot starch) without interruption ever since. The company controls everything from raw material to finished product. This main store sits in Oshiage-cho beside the former West Gate (Saidaimon) site of Todai-ji, with a second-floor dining room that looks out over the park — ginkgo in autumn and cherry blossoms in spring.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company expresses its outlook as never forgetting gratitude for the blessings of heaven and earth, and wishing to deliver delicious, enriching everyday living to its customers — now and in the future.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature offerings include freshly made kuzu mochi (made to order and served within about ten minutes, cool on the outside and warm within), kuzu kiri, kuzu yu, and Yoshino udon — smooth thin noodles with kuzu worked into them, finished in a kuzu-thickened sauce. Pure kuzu products and kuzu yu are also sold to take home.

anecdotes.text: Kuzu has been prized since ancient times as both medicine and nourishing food; the traditionally made form is called Yoshino honkuzu and is held to have special value.
The kuzu mochi is prepared fresh to order and arrives within about ten minutes, with a contrast of a cool exterior and a warm interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen; the house specialty 葛うどん (kuzu udon) is made with wheat noodles, so wheat is handled on site.

positives.text: Pure kuzu (Yoshino arrowroot starch) is naturally gluten-free, and several signature items — kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri and kuzu yu — are starch-based sweets rather than wheat-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming traditional sweets house and cafe near Todai-ji where you can order by pointing at picture menus; a few words of Japanese help, but the relaxed cafe setting makes it easy for a foreign student to visit.

places.name: 洋食 春 (Yoshoku Haru)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese yōshoku (Western) restaurant whose core dishes are inherently wheat-based and breaded: hamburger steak with demi-glace sauce (flour-thickened), breaded tonkatsu and shrimp fry (panko/wheat coating), and hayashi rice (roux-based). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no celiac accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-focused Western diner; signature items are beef hamburger steak, pork tonkatsu, Yamato beef/pork dishes and shrimp fry. House pudding uses dairy and the tartar sauce/dressings contain animal products. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (last order). Lunch only — evening service is temporarily suspended. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday (open on public holidays).
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (ならまち), near Sarusawa Pond / Mochiidono, about a 1
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yoshoku Haru opened in October 2011 inside a townhouse said to be more than 120 years old in the historic Naramachi district, just off Sarusawa Pond. The dining space keeps the old machiya character, with Western table seating on one side and a large tatami room that looks out on

chef_bio.background: Yoshoku Haru opened in October 2011 inside a townhouse said to be more than 120 years old in the historic Naramachi district, just off Sarusawa Pond. The dining space keeps the old machiya character, with Western table seating on one side and a large tatami room that looks out onto a Japanese garden.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes local Nara ingredients (Yamato beef, Yamato pork) and aims for comforting food that three generations, from grandparents to grandchildren, can enjoy together with peace of mind.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade nostalgic Western dishes: a juicy double-ground hamburger steak served sizzling on an iron plate with demi-glace sauce, often paired with a large whole-head shrimp fry that is crisp enough to eat head and tail; also tonkatsu over hayashi rice and Yamato-beef rice (gyūmeshi). House-made pudding uses milk from Uemura Ranch in Nara, and the kitchen makes an original tartar sauce with carefully chosen Nara pickles and a house soy-based dressing.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a townhouse over 120 years old, with a garden-view tatami room and a corridor leading through the garden to the restroom, giving it the feel of dining in an old family home.
The most talked-about plate is a whole-head shrimp fry so crisply fried that the head is crunchy and even the tail can be eaten, served alongside the iron-plate hamburger.
House-made pudding is made with milk from Uemura Ranch in Nara, and the tartar sauce uses local Nara pickles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded and deep-fried (tonkatsu, whole shrimp fry) in shared oil, and the hamburger and hayashi rice use flour-based sauces, so heavy gluten exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant uses a house soy-based dressing; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so soy-derived seasonings here should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around beef, pork and seafood with dairy (house pudding from Uemura Ranch milk) and egg-based tartar sauce; no separated vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood lunch spot in an old townhouse where ordering is simple by pointing at the popular hamburger or shrimp-fry sets, but staff likely speak limited English and it is cash-only, so bring yen and a little patience.

places.name: 吉野葛 佐久良 (Yoshino Kuzu Sakura)
places.gf_detail: The signature kuzukiri and kuzumochi are made from 100% Yoshino hon-kuzu (arrowroot starch), which is naturally gluten-free. However, this is a traditional sweets shop with no documented allergen handling, and some kuzu confections elsewhere are blended with wheat or served with sweetened soy/kinako that may carry gluten. There is no information confirming dedicated equipment or cross-contamination controls, so a celiac diner must ask directly about ingredients and preparation before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The core kuzukiri and kuzumochi are plant-based (arrowroot starch served with kuromitsu black-sugar syrup and/or matcha), which can be vegan. But honey, kinako, or dairy/cream may appear in some seasonal items and accompaniments, and ingredients are not documented for vegans. Confirm syrups and toppings before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00; closed Wednesdays and Thursdays (open on holidays)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Takamikadomachi), about 10 min walk south of Kint
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sakura is a kuzu sweets shop (甘味処) opened by a kimono merchant (呉服店) whose business dates to the Meiji era, set in the historic Naramachi district near Kintetsu Nara Station.

chef_bio.background: Sakura is a kuzu sweets shop (甘味処) opened by a kimono merchant (呉服店) whose business dates to the Meiji era, set in the historic Naramachi district near Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Because true hon-kuzu turns cloudy and changes flavor as time passes, every order is prepared fresh by hand only after the customer orders, preserving the clarity and delicate texture of the arrowroot.
chef_bio.specialty: Desserts made from 100% rare Yoshino hon-kuzu (true Yoshino arrowroot): kuzukiri (translucent arrowroot noodles) and kuzumochi (arrowroot mochi), served plain or with matcha and kuromitsu syrup.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a traditional machiya townhouse roughly 150–160 years old, lending a calm, historic setting in the Naramachi quarter.
The confectionery grew out of a kimono shop founded in the early Meiji period, tying it to Nara's textile and craft heritage.

positives.text: Signature dishes are made from 100% Yoshino hon-kuzu (arrowroot), a naturally gluten-free and plant-based starch, prepared fresh to order.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional family-run machiya teahouse where English is unlikely, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with a guide or a translation app and a willingness to point at the simple kuzu menu.

places.name: 奈良町豆腐庵 こんどう (Naramachi Tofu-an Kondo)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen is tofu- and soy-centered, and set meals include explicitly wheat-based items such as fu (wheat gluten) and dengaku miso, plus dashi and soy sauce that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or celiac-safe protocol is documented. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: Tofu-focused and plant-forward (yudofu, oboro tofu, fresh yuba, okara, ganmodoki), so there is genuine vegan potential, but it is not a vegan restaurant. Set meals are built around dashi and soy sauce that are commonly fish/bonito-based, and items like hirousu/ganmodoki and clear soup may contain fish dashi. No documented vegan menu; a vegan diner must confirm the dashi and broth ingredients before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Sat, Sun 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00, dinner by reservation). Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Nishi-shinya-cho (near Kintetsu Nara Station and
places.notes: Naramachi Tofu-an Kondo is the sit-down restaurant of Kondo Tofu Shop, a tofu maker based in Tenri, who opened this place to let visitors taste their tofu cooked many ways. It occupies a roughly 180-year-old townhouse in the old merchant quarter of Naramachi that is registered as

chef_bio.background: Naramachi Tofu-an Kondo is the sit-down restaurant of Kondo Tofu Shop, a tofu maker based in Tenri, who opened this place to let visitors taste their tofu cooked many ways. It occupies a roughly 180-year-old townhouse in the old merchant quarter of Naramachi that is registered as a Tangible Cultural Property, complete with an interior courtyard garden.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cherishing the natural flavor of the ingredient: tofu is made only from carefully selected 100% domestic soybeans (such as Kyushu Fukuyutaka and Hokuriku Enrei) set with mineral-rich Izu Oshima sea nigari, to bring out a deep, mellow bean taste.
chef_bio.specialty: Tofu set meals built around yudofu (hot tofu in a soy-milk style broth), oboro tofu eaten with sea salt, dashi soy sauce or yuzu pepper, fresh yuba and oboro sashimi, agedashi tofu, dengaku, okara and hirousu, often finished with a sweet.

anecdotes.text: The historic machiya was used as a filming location for director Naomi Kawase's 2003 film Shara (Sharasoju).
The site notes that tofu first reached Japan via Buddhist monks traveling to Tang-dynasty China during the Nara period, tying the dish to the city's history.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Set meals are documented to include fu (wheat gluten) and dengaku with miso, so wheat is present in the kitchen and in standard courses.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oboro tofu and other dishes are served with dashi soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented, so standard soy sauce (typically containing wheat) should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses include items such as ganmodoki/hirousu and clear soup; the dashi base is not documented as plant-based, so fish/bonito dashi should be assumed unless confirmed.

positives.text: Tofu is made from 100% domestic soybeans and sea nigari with no documented additives, and many courses are plant-based tofu, yuba and vegetable preparations.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional tofu-cuisine townhouse where English is unlikely, so a foreign student will be most comfortable arriving with a guide or translation app and a willingness to point at the set menu.

places.name: 春日荷茶屋 (Kasuga Ninaijaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Core offerings contain wheat: the summer 春日素麺 (somen) is wheat noodles, the kuzumochi and other cakes contain wheat, and the persimmon-leaf sushi involves soy. White miso and the seasoning are typical wheat-containing Japanese condiments. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and a celiac visitor cannot eat safely here. Even the rice porridge risks cross-contact in a shared kitchen serving noodles and cakes.
places.vegan_detail: The signature 万葉粥 (manyo porridge) is made with Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso — no fish dashi — so it can be a plant-based option, and there are sweets like Yoshino kuzumochi and matcha. However this is not a vegan kitchen: the popular set meals include saba (mackerel) persimmon-leaf sushi, white miso may not be confirmed dashi-free in every preparation, and there is no formal vegan menu. A vegan would order the plain manyo porridge and sweets and should confirm ingredients when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–16:00 (L.O. around 16:00). Closed Mondays in Jan–Mar, Jun–Sep, and Dec; open daily in Apr, May, Oct, and Nov. (Some sources list 10:30–16:30, L.O. 16:00.)
places.neighborhood: 奈良公園・春日大社境内
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kasuga Ninaijaya is a garden teahouse set right along the wooded approach to Kasuga Taisha, near the Manyo Botanical Garden, where pilgrims and park visitors have long stopped to rest. The name 荷茶屋 ("carried teahouse") recalls the old custom of vendors who carried tea and refresh

roles: teahouse / 甘味処 on the Kasuga Taisha approach
chef_bio.origin: On the grounds of Kasuga Taisha, 奈良公園, central Nara.
chef_bio.background: Kasuga Ninaijaya is a garden teahouse set right along the wooded approach to Kasuga Taisha, near the Manyo Botanical Garden, where pilgrims and park visitors have long stopped to rest. The name 荷茶屋 ("carried teahouse") recalls the old custom of vendors who carried tea and refreshments to shrine-goers, and the shop carries that tradition into a quiet spot among the seasonal flowering trees of the Kasuga forest.
chef_bio.philosophy: A seasonal, place-rooted menu that lets diners taste the changing year — vegetables and dishes rotate month by month, served in a garden where the forest seasons are part of the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is 万葉粥 (manyo porridge), a gentle rice porridge simmered in Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso with monthly-changing seasonal vegetables inspired by the Man'yoshu poetry anthology, made with carefully chosen Hinohikari rice. It anchors the 大和名物膳 set (porridge, two pieces of saba persimmon-leaf sushi, seasonal kuzumochi, a side and pickles), and in summer the shop serves traditional Kasuga 春日素麺 eaten the old way with umeboshi and mustard. Sweets include Yoshino-kuzu kuzumochi, matcha with Yamato tea, and seasonal zenzai and kakigori.

anecdotes.text: The monthly manyo porridge is made with Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso and premium Hinohikari rice, with handmade in-house accompaniments that rotate each month.
Guests dine in garden seating surrounded by the seasonal flowering trees of the Kasuga forest, making it a favorite rest stop after visiting Kasuga Taisha.
A visitor praised the manyo porridge as 'absolutely delicious,' especially the chilled summer version, and described a welcoming setting where they shared a table with an international couple.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen serves wheat somen noodles and wheat-containing cakes alongside the rice porridge, so shared-equipment cross-contact is likely; there is no gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The set meals include saba persimmon-leaf sushi (contains soy), and the menu notes wheat in the noodles and cakes; standard Japanese white miso and seasonings apply.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish (saba) sushi is prepared in the same kitchen as the vegetable porridge; while the porridge uses kombu dashi rather than fish dashi, shared preparation means a vegan should ask about cross-contact.

positives.text: The signature manyo porridge is made with kombu dashi and white miso rather than fish stock, so its base broth contains no fish.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed open-air teahouse on the shrine approach where you can point at the porridge or sweets on a simple menu, but staff likely speak limited English, so a foreign student should be ready to gesture or use a translation app.

places.name: ほうせき箱 (Houseki-bako)
places.gf_detail: A kakigori (shaved ice) specialist. Plain shaved ice with fruit syrups can be naturally gluten-free, but the signature dishes use milk/yogurt espuma and varied seasonal syrups/toppings, and there is no documented gluten-free protocol or allergen labeling. Anyone with celiac disease should ask about each component before ordering, as ingredients change seasonally and cross-contact in a small kitchen cannot be ruled out.
places.vegan_detail: The signature creations are built on dairy: milk espuma and yogurt espuma are central to the menu, so the headline dishes are not vegan. A simple fruit-syrup shaved ice without the dairy foam might be possible, but there is no documented vegan option. Ask staff whether a dairy-free version can be made.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-13:00, 14:00-16:50 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri); 10:00-13:00, 14:00-17:20 (Sat, Sun); closed Thursdays. Web reservation only, opening from 21:00 the day before.
places.neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街・餅飯殿町
places.cuisine: エスプーマかき氷専門店
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Houseki-bako opened in March 2015 inside the Mochiidono Yume CUBE, helping to launch Nara's now-famous kakigori (shaved ice) boom. Demand grew so intense that the original shop had to restrict entry, and in March 2018 it relocated to a roomier space further along the same Mochiid

chef_bio.background: Houseki-bako opened in March 2015 inside the Mochiidono Yume CUBE, helping to launch Nara's now-famous kakigori (shaved ice) boom. Demand grew so intense that the original shop had to restrict entry, and in March 2018 it relocated to a roomier space further along the same Mochiidono shopping arcade.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner, Okada-san, shaves the ice for every order himself, believing that the hand doing the shaving directly affects the texture of the finished ice.
chef_bio.specialty: Espuma kakigori: finely shaved ice crowned with airy foam ("espuma") made from milk or yogurt, layered with seasonal fruit syrups and toppings. The contrast between the delicate ice and the creamy foam is the shop's signature.

anecdotes.text: Okada-san insists on personally shaving the ice for each serving because, in his view, the person shaving changes the texture of the finished dish.
The shop pioneered Nara's kakigori boom after opening in the Mochiidono Yume CUBE in 2015, and was so popular it relocated to a larger 36-seat space within the same arcade in 2018.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dishes are built on dairy-based milk and yogurt espuma foams, so the core menu is not dairy-free.

positives.text: As a shaved-ice shop, the base is simply ice with fruit syrups, so a non-dairy fruit version may be possible if requested, though this is not documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, beloved local shop that runs entirely on a same-day-eve web reservation system in Japanese, so a foreign student should book ahead online and be ready to communicate mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 喫茶バルドー (Kissa Bardot)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are wheat-based — the Kitamachi Napolitan is spaghetti and the dry curry is served with rice but the menu centers on retro café cooking with no documented gluten-free options or allergen accommodation. A celiac traveler should not rely on this kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Signature items include sausage, fried egg, parmesan, and dairy-based ice cream (Uemura Farm milk), and there is no documented vegan menu. A flexible vegetarian might find a drink or a simple item, but anyone strict should ask in advance and not count on it.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi / Naijihara-cho (a roughly 5-minute walk north fro
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi district along the Yasuragi-no-Michi promenade, near the south gate of Nara Women's University, Bardot is a small owner-run coffee house that wears its Showa-era nostalgia proudly. Antique furnishings, 1980s magazines left out to read, and a Frenc

chef_bio.background: Tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi district along the Yasuragi-no-Michi promenade, near the south gate of Nara Women's University, Bardot is a small owner-run coffee house that wears its Showa-era nostalgia proudly. Antique furnishings, 1980s magazines left out to read, and a French touch — red awnings, French flags, and a wall of Brigitte Bardot photos that gives the shop its name — set the mood, while jazz and bossa nova drift through the room.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners aim to offer guests a leisurely, unhurried stretch of time in an intimate setting, leaning into old-fashioned, nostalgic flavors rather than chasing trends.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the Kitamachi Napolitan: al dente spaghetti tossed with sausage, onion, bell pepper, and mushrooms in a rich tomato sauce, crowned with a fried egg and finished with parmesan and Tabasco. The café is also known for its soy-sauce ice cream — Uemura Farm ice cream drizzled with a special soy sauce from Oita (with a salt-topped version using Uyuni salt-lake salt) — and a Nara-only 'Kofun' (burial-mound shaped) dry curry. The cafe ole is made with unhomogenized Uemura Farm milk.

anecdotes.text: The shop doubles as a curated little boutique of one-of-a-kind imported French goods, sold alongside the coffee.
Its soy-sauce ice cream has been featured in magazines including SAVVY.
The name and decor pay homage to French actress Brigitte Bardot, with her photos lining the retro interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on wheat pasta (Napolitan) and shares a small space with no documented allergen separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used as a featured ingredient (the signature soy-sauce ice cream), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes include sausage, fried egg, parmesan, and dairy-based ice cream, so vegan separation is not maintained.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, owner-run retro coffee house where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed pace go a long way; menus and signage are mainly Japanese, so a foreign student should be comfortable pointing and trying simple phrases.

places.name: 喫茶南風 (Kissa Nanpu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a classic kissaten whose core menu — omurice (often served with wheat-based demi/ketchup sauces), sandwiches, cakes, and morning toast sets — is wheat-heavy. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. The signature dishes center on egg (omurice), dairy (homemade cakes, custard pudding, coffee with milk), and sandwiches. Nothing on the documented menu is plant-based, and no vegan accommodation is mentioned.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30) per official shop page; Tabelog lists 9:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Sunday morning service until 11:00.
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai, Naramachi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Nanpu opened in 1998 inside the covered Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a historic shopping street running south from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Naramachi. It is a classic, family-style Japanese coffee shop that has become a reliable rest stop for visitors exploring the old

chef_bio.background: Kissa Nanpu opened in 1998 inside the covered Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a historic shopping street running south from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Naramachi. It is a classic, family-style Japanese coffee shop that has become a reliable rest stop for visitors exploring the old town.
chef_bio.philosophy: Welcomes tired sightseers to pause and refresh while wandering Nara's historic streets.
chef_bio.specialty: Known for fluffy egg omelette rice (omurice, including omu-pilaf variations), homemade cakes, custard pudding, and morning service sets (from around 520 yen, with Sunday morning service offered until 11:00).

anecdotes.text: The shop describes itself warmly on its arcade profile: 'This is Kissa Nanpu, which opened in Heisei 10 (1998). Thanks to your support we are now in our 19th year.'

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood coffee shop where staff likely speak mostly Japanese; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through ordering coffee, omurice, or a morning set.

places.name: カフェ・葉風泰夢 (Cafe Half Time (Hafutaimu))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on curry rice, hamburger and fried-chicken sets, baumkuchen and cakes, and other Western-style cafe fare, all of which typically involve wheat (roux, breading, batter, flour-based sweets). There is no indication of celiac-safe preparation or allergen handling. Travelers needing strict gluten-free should not rely on this venue.
places.vegan_detail: No explicitly vegan menu is documented. The cafe serves meat dishes (hamburger, fried chicken), curry, and dairy/egg-based sweets (baumkuchen made with local eggs). Drinks include house-roasted coffee, teas, and herbal infusions, which may suit a vegan in a pinch, but no plant-based meals are advertised. A vegan would need to ask, and options are likely very limited.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (food last order 16:00 / drinks last order ~16:30). Closed Mondays (next day if Monday is a holiday). Hours extend to 19:00 on museum evening-opening days and to 18:00 during the Shosoin Exhibition period.
places.neighborhood: Nara Park area, inside the Nara National Museum (Noboriojich
places.notes: Cafe Half Time sits one level below the galleries of the Nara National Museum, a quiet stop where you can pause between looking at Buddhist art and treasures of the old capital. You don't need a museum ticket to come in, so it works as a calm coffee break in the middle of Nara Pa

chef_bio.background: Cafe Half Time sits one level below the galleries of the Nara National Museum, a quiet stop where you can pause between looking at Buddhist art and treasures of the old capital. You don't need a museum ticket to come in, so it works as a calm coffee break in the middle of Nara Park. It is operated by the Tokokuro group, a local hospitality company that also runs a Chinese restaurant and a bakery in the city.
chef_bio.specialty: House specialties include a special curry rice served with soup, a French-style hamburger and fried-chicken set, and a baumkuchen made with pesticide-free tea leaves from Yamabe and specialty eggs from Yamato-Koriyama. Coffee is roasted in-house under the group's 'mamejicafe' label.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's signature baumkuchen draws on the museum's setting: its name and the calligraphy for it were given by the chief abbot (choro) of Todai-ji Temple, Kokei Kitagawara.
Coffee is roasted in-house and sold under the group's own 'mamejicafe' label.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around wheat-containing dishes (curry roux, breaded/fried items, flour-based baumkuchen and cakes); no gluten-free preparation or allergen control is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen handles meat (hamburger, fried chicken) and egg/dairy sweets; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed museum cafe where you can drop in without buying a museum ticket; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a coffee and curry comfortably.

places.name: 工場跡事務室 (Kojoato Jimushitsu)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese cafe built around bread, dango, and tea. Signature items — the croquette bread (コロッケパン), fermented-butter toast, sandwiches, and white dango (mochi rice flour) — are wheat- or gluten-containing, and there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac protocol. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A drink-and-light-food cafe rather than a vegan destination. Plant-based options are incidental: Yamato tea (大和茶), persimmon-leaf tea (柿の葉茶), and a fig compote are vegan-friendly, but the hojicha latte and butter toast contain dairy and breakfast/lunch sets are not designed to be vegan. No documented vegan menu; ask staff about specific items.
places.hours_raw: Fri 11:00-18:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 09:00-18:00 (Tabelog lists Fri 11:00-17:30 and Sat/Sun/hol 08:30-17:30). Breakfast served Sat/Sun/holidays. Closed Mon-Thu.
places.neighborhood: きたまち (Shibatsuji-cho, northwest of Todai-ji Kaidan-do; ~15 m
places.cuisine: Cafe / kissaten in a converted 1920s lactic-
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The building was raised in 1925 by Kazuo Kita's great-grandfather as the Chojukai Bacterial Research Institute, a factory that researched lactic-acid bacteria and produced a probiotic drink called 'Futurmin,' along with studies of edible fungi and bread yeast. It was designed by

chef_bio.background: The building was raised in 1925 by Kazuo Kita's great-grandfather as the Chojukai Bacterial Research Institute, a factory that researched lactic-acid bacteria and produced a probiotic drink called 'Futurmin,' along with studies of edible fungi and bread yeast. It was designed by master carpenter Yoshitaro Oki, who also worked on Nara's Anglican church, and features hand-cut glass lamps by craftsman Koji Fujimoto. After the factory closed in the late 1970s it sat unused for more than three decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kita's guiding idea was to preserve the building by using it — 'preserve it while it lives,' so visitors can feel an atmosphere that cannot be recreated in anything new.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato tea and a weekend breakfast set, the much-loved croquette bread (a butcher's croquette with sweet sauce in fluffy bread, branded with a deer mark), fermented-butter toast, hojicha latte, and white dango.

anecdotes.text: To reopen the long-shuttered factory as a cafe in 2009, Kita spent about a year cleaning it out, washing more than 10,000 glass bottles and sorting the old equipment by hand.
The croquette bread is stamped with a deer brand, a nod to Nara's famous deer, making it a small local keepsake as much as a snack.
In 2020 the former factory and its product storehouse were listed as a Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread (croquette bread, toast, sandwiches) and rice-flour dango are core menu items in a small shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (butter, latte milk) is used throughout; vegan separation is not documented.

positives.text: Several plant-based drinks are offered, including Yamato tea and persimmon-leaf tea, plus a fig compote.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, individually-run heritage cafe where a few words of Japanese and a calm, respectful manner go a long way; English menus are not guaranteed, so pointing and basic phrases help.

places.name: 鹿鳴園 (Rokumeien)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes — curry udon and nyūmen (thin somen-style noodles) — are wheat-based, and broths are dashi-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The udon and nyūmen broths rely on fish dashi, so the noodle dishes are not vegan. Some shaved ice (kakigori) options — such as those with kinako (roasted soybean) powder, honey/mizore syrups, and red bean paste — may be plant-based, but milk-based syrups are also used, so a vegan would need to ask which syrups and toppings are dairy-free. House-made warabi mochi is typically plant-based but should be confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-17:00 (general listing). Shaved ice (kakigori) served seasonally June–September, 10:00-16:30.
places.neighborhood: Nara Park / Todai-ji grounds (Zōshi-chō), near Nigatsu-dō, c
places.cuisine: Shōwa-retro local teahouse and food stall
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rokumeien is a long-running, old-fashioned teahouse and food stall tucked into the wooded grounds of Todai-ji, on a quiet hill beyond the Great Buddha Hall near Nigatsu-dō. Surrounded by greenery in a free-to-enter part of the temple precinct, it has the unhurried, Shōwa-era char

chef_bio.background: Rokumeien is a long-running, old-fashioned teahouse and food stall tucked into the wooded grounds of Todai-ji, on a quiet hill beyond the Great Buddha Hall near Nigatsu-dō. Surrounded by greenery in a free-to-enter part of the temple precinct, it has the unhurried, Shōwa-era character of a roadside tea shop — a place where visitors rest in the shade away from the crowds.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on simple, handmade, locally sourced fare — pure ice with house-made syrups and Nara ingredients, served in a calm setting away from the tourist crush.
chef_bio.specialty: Curry udon and pork-soup udon with a rich dashi broth, seasonal curry nyūmen, house-made warabi mochi served with home-cooked red beans, and shaved ice (kakigori). The signature kakigori is a rice-themed bowl combining amazake syrup, rice-flour dumplings, and concentrated red bean paste; the kinako (roasted soybean) milk ice with honey mizore is also a favorite, praised for fluffy, finely shaved ice and a clean, non-cloying finish.

anecdotes.text: Described by a visitor as a hidden gem of shaved ice deep within Todai-ji — surrounded by greenery, far from the lines that form at Nara's more famous kakigori shops, ideal for those who dislike waiting.

The kinako milk ice is served with fluffy, finely shaved ice and honey-infused mizore beneath a milk syrup, with the kinako powder brought separately so it doesn't spill — a small touch of care from the shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (udon and nyūmen/somen) are central to the menu and are cooked in shared kitchen space; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths are dashi/soy-based; standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat should be assumed. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths use fish dashi and the menu mixes dairy (milk syrup) and animal-free items; vegans should ask which kakigori syrups and toppings are dairy- and animal-free.

positives.text: Shaved ice is made with pure ice and house-made syrups using Nara ingredients, with some plant-leaning options like kinako, honey/mizore syrups, red bean paste, and house-made warabi mochi that a careful diner can ask about.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available, but this is a small old-fashioned local teahouse where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa will go a long way.

places.name: そば処 吟松 奈良町店 (Soba-dokoro Ginsho Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The soba is nihachi (二八), an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat-flour blend, so the noodles themselves contain wheat. Udon (pure wheat) is also offered, tempura is wheat-batter fried to order, and the broth uses soy-based tsuyu. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Unsuitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba dipping sauce and broth are made with bonito (katsuo) dashi, and the most popular dishes are tempura zaru soba and tendon. No documented vegetable-only broth or vegan options. A traveler could ask for plain zaru soba, but the standard tsuyu/dashi contains fish, so it cannot be assumed vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–14:00 (closes when sold out) per the shop's own listing; some directories list 11:00–14:00. Closed Mondays and Fridays (irregular holidays) per the shop/Naramachi info site; some directories list Mondays only.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishi-Shinya-cho), near the Naramachi Shiryokan,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small machiya soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi quarter near the Naramachi Shiryokan, Ginsho has been making soba in the neighborhood for around 32 years. Behind its nostalgic wooden lattice door and red noren, the room glows with warm lighting and Japanese knick-kna

chef_bio.background: A small machiya soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi quarter near the Naramachi Shiryokan, Ginsho has been making soba in the neighborhood for around 32 years. Behind its nostalgic wooden lattice door and red noren, the room glows with warm lighting and Japanese knick-knacks, the kind of unhurried lunch spot locals have leaned on for decades. It is a branch of the family's main Ginsho shop over in the Takabatake area near Shin-Yakushi-ji.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop keeps things simple and fresh: noodles and tempura made to order, a bonito-dashi broth left largely unadorned to let the soba speak, and a willingness to keep going only as long as the day's hand-cut soba lasts.
chef_bio.specialty: Thin, fragrant nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) made from stone-ground Shinshu buckwheat flour, prized for its delicate texture and smooth throat-feel. The runaway favorite is tempura zaru soba — cold soba with crisp tempura (shrimp, pumpkin, eggplant, green beans, nori) fried to order — alongside tendon and a mini tempura rice bowl. The zaru is finished with a scattering of chopped nori, an unusual touch.

anecdotes.text: The owner writes that, supported by patrons, they have run a soba shop in Naramachi for about 32 years.
The cold zaru soba is traditionally topped with chopped seaweed, an uncommon preparation, and the dipping broth leans on a clean bonito dashi with minimal embellishment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba is nihachi (二八), an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat blend, so the noodles inherently contain wheat; udon (pure wheat) is also cooked here, and tempura is wheat-batter fried in shared oil.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a bonito-dashi tsuyu/broth; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is soy-sauce based, which normally contains wheat. No wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth and dipping sauce use bonito (fish) dashi, and signature dishes are tempura and tendon, so dishes are prepared in a fish- and animal-using kitchen with no documented vegan separation.
