chef_bio.philosophy: The host's idea is to choose your tea and tea vessel according to your body and mood and brew it carefully for yourself — 体調や気分に合わせてお茶を選び、茶器を選び、自分の為に丁寧に淹れる. The space doubles as an art cafe, inviting reading, drawing, and writing while you sip.
chef_bio.specialty: A tea service (about 1,000 yen) where you choose a Nara tea to match your mood, brewed in an iron kettle (tetsubin) for three to five infusions and paired with a seasonal fresh wagashi (jō-namagashi). There are also dessert plates and glass drinks such as hojicha latte, roasted chai, and ginger ale.

anecdotes.text: Beyond tea, the cafe offers hands-on experiences using Nara craft materials, including suminagashi (marbled ink painting) with local brushes, ink and paper, plus casual and formal tea-ceremony (otemae) sessions.

positives.text: The menu is built around brewed Nara teas, which are naturally gluten-free and vegan; pairing sweets can be discussed item by item with the host.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional shoes-off tearoom run by a tea practitioner; a foreign student is welcome but should expect a calm, Japanese-language setting where you choose and savor tea slowly.

places.name: 音楽と珈琲 ジョニー (Ongaku to Coffee Johnny)
places.gf_detail: This is a coffee shop whose food menu centers on cheesecake and toast — both wheat-based — so it is not a gluten-free destination and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or celiac accommodation. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the kitchen is not set up for cross-contamination control.
places.vegan_detail: A small owner-run kissaten focused on coffee. Black coffee can be ordered without dairy, but the food offerings (cheesecake, toast) are not vegan and there is no documented vegan menu. A vegan would need to limit themselves to plain coffee and confirm directly.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（餅飯殿センター街付近）／近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏
places.cuisine: レコードの流れる純喫茶・珈琲とチーズケーキ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A hideaway coffee shop tucked on the second floor above the Mochiidono shopping street in the old Naramachi quarter, reached by a narrow passage and a steep flight of stairs that open onto a quiet, retro-styled room set apart from the bustle of the city.

chef_bio.background: A hideaway coffee shop tucked on the second floor above the Mochiidono shopping street in the old Naramachi quarter, reached by a narrow passage and a steep flight of stairs that open onto a quiet, retro-styled room set apart from the bustle of the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coffee and music, side by side: the owner runs the place single-handedly, brewing each cup with care while jazz plays from vinyl records, creating an unhurried space made for slowing down.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully brewed coffee paired with homemade cheesecake; toast is also on the short, coffee-centered menu.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden, secret-base-like coffee house where jazz drifts from records and the master quietly tends the counter.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, owner-run vinyl-records kissaten reached by narrow alley stairs — comfortable for a foreign student who can order simply in a little Japanese and respects the calm, no-fuss atmosphere.

places.name: そば処 喜多原 (Soba-dokoro Kitahara)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house soba is "gai-ichi" (外一) / 九割そば: 90% buckwheat flour with 10% wheat flour used as a binder, so even the noodles contain wheat. The dipping broth (tsuyu) and tempura batter are also wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Unsuitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. As a traditional soba shop the tsuyu (dipping/serving broth) is made with fish-based dashi, so standard soba dishes are not vegan. Some components could in principle be plant-based — e.g. Yoshino kudzu sashimi tofu, Yamato yam tororo, and vegetable tempura (Nara vegetables) — but the broth and shared kitchen mean a fully vegan meal is not confirmed. A vegan diner would need to ask in advance; no documented vegan handling.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (open until soba sells out); evening service available by request. Closed Tuesdays and the second Wednesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: 水門町（東大寺・奈良公園そば）
places.cuisine: 石臼挽き北海道産そば粉の手打ち九割そば
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kitahara occupies a traditional single-family house in the quiet Mizumon-cho lane just beside the grounds of Todai-ji and Nara Park. The shop took its current name and reopened on April 1, 2011, having previously operated under the name よし川 (Yoshikawa). A large Shigaraki tanuki s

chef_bio.background: Kitahara occupies a traditional single-family house in the quiet Mizumon-cho lane just beside the grounds of Todai-ji and Nara Park. The shop took its current name and reopened on April 1, 2011, having previously operated under the name よし川 (Yoshikawa). A large Shigaraki tanuki statue at the entrance is its landmark, and windows look out onto flowers, birds and the greenery of Nara Park while you eat.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on freshly stone-milled buckwheat and Nara-grown vegetables, prizing the aroma and texture that comes only from grinding and cutting the soba each day.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut 九割 (gai-ichi/外一) soba — 90% buckwheat to 10% wheat binder — made from Hokkaido buckwheat that is stone-milled fresh in-house every morning, served the traditional 'three fresh' way (freshly milled, freshly cut, freshly boiled). Signature plates include ebi-oroshi soba (shrimp tempura with grated daikon), tempura zaru soba with seasonal Nara vegetables, and Yamato-yam tororo soba; the thickened soba-yu (buckwheat broth) poured at the end is a favorite, alongside Yoshino-kudzu sashimi tofu.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat seeds are ground every morning on a stone mill in the shop, and the soba is cut by hand and boiled to order, so each bowl reflects the day's fresh milling.

Housed in a converted single-family home near Todai-ji and marked by a big Shigaraki tanuki, with views of Nara Park's flowers and birds from the windows; the signature ebi-oroshi soba is praised for its fresh, smooth-yet-springy noodles with grated daikon and crisp shrimp and vegetable tempura.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba itself is 90% buckwheat / 10% wheat (gai-ichi), so the noodles contain wheat; tempura batter and a shared kitchen mean wheat is present throughout. Not suitable for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional soba tsuyu is soy-sauce based, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free broth is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soba broth (tsuyu/soba-yu) at a classic soba shop is made with fish-based dashi, and tempura share fryer/kitchen with seafood items, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

positives.text: Buckwheat is stone-milled in-house each morning and the soba is hand-cut and boiled to order, so ingredients are fresh and the menu is simple and transparent.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional walk-in soba house in a quiet residential lane near Todai-ji; a few words of Japanese and patience with a cash-only, no-reservations spot will go a long way for a foreign student.

places.name: 日本酒とおばんざい 新奈 (Nihonshu to Obanzai Niina)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any meaningful sense for a celiac. This is a sake bar built around omakase obanzai (Japanese/Western/Chinese-influenced home cooking) where dishes routinely use soy sauce, miso and pasta, and sake itself is rice-based but the kitchen makes no gluten-free claims. No dedicated gluten-free menu or handling is documented. A celiac traveler should treat this as not safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu is omakase obanzai that frequently includes fish, meat and seasonal Tanba game (jibie), and dishes typically rely on fish/bonito dashi. Some individual vegetable obanzai exist (e.g. beet potage, dried daikon, vegetable rolls), but there is no documented vegan menu and the proprietor decides the courses, so a vegan would need to discuss requirements in advance via Instagram DM; outcome is not guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (Tabelog); one local guide lists 17:00-22:00. Evening-only operation. Irregular closures announced via Instagram.
places.neighborhood: 東向北商店街 (Higashimuki-kita shopping street), immediately by Ki
places.cuisine: Japanese sake bar serving home-style obanzai
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Niina opened on August 4, 2023, in a restored traditional townhouse along the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is a one-woman counter place: an L-shaped counter for only eight guests, lit by hanging lamps over warm wood, with a quiet, no

chef_bio.background: Niina opened on August 4, 2023, in a restored traditional townhouse along the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is a one-woman counter place: an L-shaped counter for only eight guests, lit by hanging lamps over warm wood, with a quiet, nostalgic Showa-era feel that glows like a hideaway after the surrounding shops close early for the night.
chef_bio.philosophy: There is no printed sake list; the bottles lining the shelves are all available, and the proprietress talks with each guest about preferred flavors and serving temperatures before suggesting a few to choose from. The aim is gentle, attentive hospitality that pairs everyday home cooking with sake that suits the moment.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai served as a small omakase course alongside carefully chosen sake, with a particular love of warm sake (kanzake). Dishes are humble home cooking elevated with a little extra technique and span Japanese, Western and Chinese influences; seasonal game (jibie) from the Tanba area of Kyoto appears in the colder months. Sampled plates have included beet potage, octopus-and-pasta salad, dried daikon with perilla, cabbage rolls with yacon, and yellowtail-and-daikon rolls.

anecdotes.text: The proprietress holds a SAKE DIPLOMA and is a certified sake sommelier (kikisake-shi) with a special fondness for warm sake, and is known for soft, gentle hospitality that guides guests through her selection by first learning their taste.
Because it is evening-only and the rest of the Nara shopping street closes early, its lit doorway around 8pm reads like a hidden retreat tucked into the quiet arcade.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is home-style obanzai blending Japanese, Western and Chinese cooking, which typically involves soy sauce and miso (wheat-containing) and dishes such as pasta salad; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Omakase courses regularly include fish, meat and seasonal game and generally rely on Japanese dashi, so vegan separation is not assured; requirements would need to be arranged in advance.

positives.text: With only eight counter seats and a single proprietress who chooses the courses and discusses each guest's preferences directly, there is room to talk through dietary needs in advance via Instagram, though no formal allergy program is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat counter run by one proprietress with no printed menu and a budget around 6,000-8,000 yen, so a foreign student should expect Japanese-language conversation, cash payment, and an advance reservation (Instagram DM) to feel comfortable.

places.name: カフェ クロコ (Cafe CROCO)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings documented. This is a traditional Japanese coffeehouse whose food menu centers on wheat-based items: an egg sandwich on bread, toast morning sets, Taiwanese castella cake, and homemade cakes/pudding. Nothing on the menu or in any source indicates gluten-free preparation or awareness, and there is no dedicated gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The signature food is an egg sandwich, and morning sets include toast with a boiled egg; cakes and pudding are dairy/egg based. Drinks are coffee-focused (hand-drip blends) and could be taken black, but milk-based and dessert items dominate and no plant-milk or vegan menu is mentioned. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 火～日 08:00-18:00（モーニングは11時まで）／月曜定休
places.neighborhood: きたまち・花芝商店街（近鉄奈良駅 北側）
places.cuisine: 純喫茶・自家焙煎コーヒー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe CROCO carries a half-century of Nara coffee history. The original master opened the first shop near JR Kyobate in 1970, then moved it to the Yamato-Saidaiji area in 1973, where it became beloved as 'Kohii-kan Kuroko.' When that building aged, the shop relocated in 2017 to th

chef_bio.chef_name: Higo Yoko (current owner); Higo Tatsuya (second-generation master)
chef_bio.background: Cafe CROCO carries a half-century of Nara coffee history. The original master opened the first shop near JR Kyobate in 1970, then moved it to the Yamato-Saidaiji area in 1973, where it became beloved as 'Kohii-kan Kuroko.' When that building aged, the shop relocated in 2017 to the Hanashiba shopping street on the north side of Kintetsu Nara Station, where it continues today.
chef_bio.philosophy: Self-roasting was pioneered here around 1990 when the founder taught himself the craft - rare for the time - and that dedication to roasting and careful hand-drip brewing has been handed down through the generations and even spun off a separate roaster, TABI Coffee Roaster.
chef_bio.specialty: The Croco Blend - four bean varieties roasted dark, freshly ground and hand-dripped one cup at a time for deep bitterness balanced by bright acidity. The fluffy tamago (egg) sandwich with cucumber and a light hand on the mayonnaise is a signature, alongside Taiwanese castella sweetened with brown sugar and honey, plus homemade cakes and pudding.

anecdotes.text: Antique stained-glass doors, vintage coffee mills, bean-filled jars behind the counter, and oversized custom ceramic cups made by a Kiyomizu potter give the room a timeless, retro character that draws students, office workers, and devoted regulars alike.

A legendary novelty item once existed: a 9,650-yen coffee served in an enormous cup holding about 30 servings, created to honor Nara's traditional Ocha-mori tea ceremony - reportedly ordered by only one group.
The shop's commitment to roasting was so formative that an employee went on to independently open TABI Coffee Roaster in 2022, which now roasts CROCO's beans.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, family-run neighborhood coffeehouse where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way; English is unlikely, but pointing at the menu and ordering coffee with toast is easy and welcoming for a foreign student.

places.name: 大和野菜とせいろ蒸し すみか (Yamato Yasai to Seiro Mushi Sumika)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a Japanese izakaya where dishes are seasoned with soy sauce (which contains wheat) and typically use fish dashi; the menu also features Miwa sōmen, which is wheat noodles. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergy protocol, so celiac and gluten-sensitive diners cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-focused, but vegetable-forward. The kitchen centers on Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers a vegetable seiro (bamboo-steamer) basket and vegetable appetizers such as bagna cauda with root vegetables and sautéed mushrooms. However, the restaurant primarily serves Yamato chicken and pork, and standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, likely fish dashi) are used, so most dishes are not vegan by default. A strict vegan would need to ask in advance about which vegetable dishes can be prepared without animal-derived stock or fish products; no dedicated vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-23:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 16:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Open year-round (年中無休).
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（小西町・三条通エリア）
places.notes: A modern Japanese izakaya a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the Konishi-chō area near Sanjō-dōri. The dining room has a calm wood-and-white interior with counter and table seating and half-private nooks.

chef_bio.background: A modern Japanese izakaya a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the Konishi-chō area near Sanjō-dōri. The dining room has a calm wood-and-white interior with counter and table seating and half-private nooks.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated focus is showcasing local Nara ingredients - Yamato vegetables, Yamato chicken, and regional sake - and cooking them gently by steaming to draw out each ingredient's flavor in a healthy way.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is seiro-mushi: ingredients steamed in stacked bamboo baskets so they keep their nutrients and natural flavor. Baskets come in vegetable, pork, and a three-variety combination, built around Yamato (Nara) vegetables and Yamato chicken. Other dishes include jako-Manganji (small fish with green peppers), shrimp-and-lotus-root ahijo, bagna cauda with root vegetables, and Miwa sōmen, paired with local Nara sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and dishes seasoned with soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese izakaya seasoning relies on soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen prepares Yamato chicken and pork alongside vegetable dishes; vegetable items likely use fish dashi or shared cooking, so vegan diners must ask in advance.

positives.text: Strong vegetable-forward focus with a dedicated vegetable seiro basket and several Yamato-vegetable appetizers, giving plant-based diners real (if not certified-vegan) choices.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed modern izakaya where a foreign student should be comfortable with a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude; an English menu is not guaranteed, so pointing at dishes and basic phrases help.

places.name: 大和薬食処 ならやま茶館 (Yamato Yakushokudokoro Narayama Sakan)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe by default. The cuisine centers on chagayu (Yamato tea porridge) and Japanese home-style preparations that typically involve wheat-containing soy sauce and miso, and the kitchen uses natural seafood-based broth. The restaurant does take a constitution/preference check sheet and says it can tailor dishes to a guest's preferences and symptoms, so a gluten-sensitive diner could ask in advance — but no dedicated gluten-free handling, gluten-free soy sauce, or cross-contamination control is documented. Treat as "ask in advance," not safe for strict celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward but not vegan. The menu can accommodate guests who prefer no meat, however the kitchen explicitly still uses fish, eggs, and dairy, and the broth/dashi is made from natural ingredients from the sea (i.e., fish-based). So a no-meat (vegetarian-leaning) plate is possible on request, but a strict vegan meal is not documented. Vegans would need to ask whether fully plant-based dishes and a kombu-only broth can be prepared in advance; this is not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:30-13:45 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. food 19:00, drinks 19:15); Tue-Sun 11:30-13:45 (lunch), 17:30-19:00 (dinner). Dishes are prepared to order from reservations.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（ならまち・えびす小路）
places.notes: Narayama Sakan is a small medicinal-cuisine (yakuzen) house tucked into a traditional machiya along Ebisu Alley (Ebisu Koji) in Naramachi, a few minutes' walk from central Nara. The kitchen relocated here to the historic merchant quarter after earlier operating in the Takano area

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara City (after an earlier location in Takano)
chef_bio.background: Narayama Sakan is a small medicinal-cuisine (yakuzen) house tucked into a traditional machiya along Ebisu Alley (Ebisu Koji) in Naramachi, a few minutes' walk from central Nara. The kitchen relocated here to the historic merchant quarter after earlier operating in the Takano area, settling into the kind of old wooden townhouse setting that suits its nostalgic, home-cooking spirit.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking is guided by the idea that everyday meals quietly adjust the body and mind. The kitchen leans on locally sourced seasonal organic vegetables and natural broths rather than chemical seasonings, aiming for gentle, easy-to-eat flavors that move away from the bitter image people often associate with medicinal food. Guests fill out a constitution/preference sheet so dishes can be tailored to their tastes and how they're feeling.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato-style medicinal home cooking built around seasonal organic Yamato vegetables and the local tradition of chagayu (tea porridge). Signature offerings include a seasonal medicinal-food course and an omakase plate that rotates with the seasons -- typically a protein such as mackerel or balsamic-style chicken, vegetable tempura, several small side dishes using different techniques, rice that can be served as grains or as tea porridge, and a dessert -- alongside herbal teas chosen for their documented wellness benefits.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant accommodates solo diners and offers a children's plate by reservation, and can adjust toward a no-meat meal on request (though fish, eggs, and dairy are still used).
It moved from the Takano area into a machiya along Ebisu Alley in the Naramachi district, reflecting a turn toward the historic merchant quarter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cuisine centers on tea porridge and Japanese home-style dishes that typically use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Yamato home cooking and tea porridge sides are normally seasoned with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce; gluten-free soy sauce is not mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth/dashi is made from natural ingredients from the sea (fish-based), and the kitchen uses fish, eggs, and dairy even on no-meat plates, so dishes are not vegan by default.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests complete a constitution/preference check sheet and the kitchen says it can prepare dishes tailored to a diner's preferences and symptoms, so dietary needs can be discussed when reserving.

positives.text: Heavily vegetable-forward, using locally sourced seasonal organic Yamato vegetables prepared without chemical seasonings, with the option to adjust toward a meat-free plate on request.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, reservation-based machiya where dishes are made to order, so book ahead and be ready to communicate your needs in Japanese (or with a translation app); the owner uses a preference/health check sheet that helps bridge the gap.

places.name: 土佐料理とおばんざい 柚のす (Tosa Ryori to Obanzai Yunosu)
places.gf_detail: This is a Tosa-style izakaya built around fish, dashi, and soy-based seasoning, with deep-fried items (croquettes, dumplings) and ponzu/soy on signature dishes like bonito tataki. No gluten-free menu, no celiac accommodation documented, and soy sauce here contains wheat. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A Kochi regional seafood/meat izakaya: straw-grilled bonito (katsuo) tataki, Shimanto eel, Tosa akaushi beef hamburger, and dashi-rolled egg are the core offerings. Dishes rely on fish dashi and bonito, so even the obanzai sides are generally not vegan. No vegan menu documented.
places.hours_raw: 15:00-23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30). Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・JR奈良駅 徒歩圏（やすらぎの道沿い）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yunosu is a small Tosa-cuisine and obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a mixed-use building along Yasuragi-no-michi, a short walk from central Nara's stations. It is run by a warm, Kochi-connected proprietress who brings the flavors and hospitality of her home prefecture

chef_bio.background: Yunosu is a small Tosa-cuisine and obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a mixed-use building along Yasuragi-no-michi, a short walk from central Nara's stations. It is run by a warm, Kochi-connected proprietress who brings the flavors and hospitality of her home prefecture to Nara, sourcing ingredients shipped directly from Kochi.
chef_bio.philosophy: To share authentic Tosa home cooking and hospitality in a relaxed, unhurried setting, letting guests linger over Kochi ingredients and sake.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is straw-grilled bonito tataki (warayaki katsuo no tataki) served Tosa-style; the recommended shio-tataki pairs Tosa salt with yuzu juice to lift the fish. Other Kochi specialties include Shimanto eel served three ways (kabayaki, shirayaki, and salt-grilled), a Tosa akaushi beef hand-kneaded hamburger, one-bite Anbei-style gyoza, house-made bonito-flake croquettes with fluffy potato, and a dashi-rolled egg fragrant with aonori from the Shimanto River.

anecdotes.text: The drinks list leans hard into Kochi: regional sake such as Suigei and Miyoshigumi's Bishafu, alongside Reihoku shochu made from Kochi rice, plus a rotating selection of regional sake from around Japan.
The counter doubles as a display of the day's obanzai home-style dishes, with tatami seating in back, making it comfortable for everyone from solo diners to small groups.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes are seasoned with ponzu and soy sauce (e.g., bonito tataki), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fish- and dashi-centered (bonito tataki, bonito-flake croquettes, dashi egg), so animal-derived stock is used broadly across dishes including sides.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, owner-run local izakaya where a little Japanese helps; the proprietress is friendly and chatty, so a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese (or a translation app) will be well looked after.

places.name: グリル・チェンバー (Grill Chamber)
places.gf_detail: This is a Showa-era yōshoku (Japanese-Western) diner whose menu is built on wheat-heavy, gluten-laden dishes: チェンバーライス is finished with demi-glace sauce (wheat-roux based), and the lineup centers on breaded chicken cutlets (チキンカツ), hamburg steak, and dishes dressed in Worcestershire/demi-glace/ketchup sauces. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no allergen handling described. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-focused Western diner. The signature dish is seasoned/garlic rice topped with egg and sausage under a meat-based demi-glace; other mains are chicken cutlet and hamburg steak. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and core sauces are meat/dashi-based. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:30-23:00（ランチ・ディナー）。日曜・祝日定休。（注: 一部情報源ではランチ開始を11:00と記載）
places.neighborhood: 高天町（近鉄奈良駅直結・近鉄高天ビル地下）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running family-style Western diner tucked into the underground arcade of the Takaten building, directly linked to Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop traces back to the postwar Showa era, originally opened as a restaurant tied to the Nara Chamber of Commerce; when that buildin

chef_bio.background: A long-running family-style Western diner tucked into the underground arcade of the Takaten building, directly linked to Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop traces back to the postwar Showa era, originally opened as a restaurant tied to the Nara Chamber of Commerce; when that building was rebuilt roughly four decades ago, the diner relocated to its present basement spot, carrying its retro Showa atmosphere with it.
chef_bio.specialty: The namesake チェンバーライス (Chamber Rice): seasoned garlic pilaf-style rice crowned with soft egg and sausage and blanketed in a rich house demi-glace that locals describe as a peppery, garlicky blend reminiscent of Worcestershire, demi-glace and ketchup found nowhere else. Diners can bump it up to a volume version by adding fried chicken, chicken cutlet, hamburg steak or extra egg. The kitchen also turns out classic yōshoku plates like chicken cutlet and hamburg steak.

anecdotes.text: The room keeps an unmistakable Showa-era mood, more like a snug old bar-café than a modern restaurant, with counter and table seating for about 25.
Regulars describe the master as a warm, kind-hearted host whose courtesy toward customers is felt at the table.

Said to date to the postwar Showa period and to have started as the Nara Chamber of Commerce restaurant before moving to its current underground location when that building was rebuilt about 40 years ago.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-centric: demi-glace sauce (wheat-roux based) tops the signature rice dish, and mains include breaded chicken cutlet and hamburg steak — high gluten exposure with no documented gluten-free handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are built on meat, egg, sausage and meat-based demi-glace; no vegan or vegetarian preparations are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, cash-only old-school diner where the menu and warm master are entirely in Japanese, so basic Japanese (or pointing at the famous Chamber Rice) makes the visit smoothest.

places.name: エニー ビー&ビー+コーヒー（ANY B&B+COFFEE） (ANY B&B + COFFEE)
places.gf_detail: A specialty coffee cafe (not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen) that offers rotating gluten-free baked goods and sweets, often sourced from a local organic partner. Hot sandwiches and toast on the menu are wheat-based, so a shared/non-dedicated environment should be assumed. Confirm specific gluten-free items available on the day, as offerings rotate.
places.vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly options are available: plant-based (vegan) baked goods and sweets that rotate daily, plus house-made granola, and alternative (non-dairy) milks for coffee drinks. Not a fully vegan kitchen — egg/cheese sandwiches and dairy are also served — so ask which items are vegan on the day.
places.hours_raw: 月・火・金・土・日 08:00-17:00／水・木 定休（食べログ表記）。公式サイトでは平日7:00-17:00、週末8:00-17:00、木曜定休と案内。営業時間・定休日は媒体により表記が異なるため要確認。
places.neighborhood: ならまち／西城戸町（やすらぎの道沿い）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー＆カフェ
places.notes: ANY B&B + COFFEE is run by a husband-and-wife team, Yutaro and Chiharu, who developed their craft as a barista and roaster in Melbourne, Australia before returning to Nara. They built a small in-house roastery and cafe along Yasuragi-no-michi in central Nara, a short walk from bo

chef_bio.origin: Nara, Japan (owners trained in Melbourne, Australia)
chef_bio.background: ANY B&B + COFFEE is run by a husband-and-wife team, Yutaro and Chiharu, who developed their craft as a barista and roaster in Melbourne, Australia before returning to Nara. They built a small in-house roastery and cafe along Yasuragi-no-michi in central Nara, a short walk from both Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations, with a single B&B room upstairs where overnight guests wake to the smell of fresh coffee and a complimentary breakfast.
chef_bio.philosophy: The couple frame their work around storytelling and human connection — choosing beans with meaningful backstories and economic significance to producing countries, and leaning into sustainability with compostable coffee bags, glass-bottle reuse, and zero-waste practices.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted specialty coffee — filter and espresso drinks — built largely around African beans from origins such as Kenya and Ethiopia, served alongside all-day breakfast and brunch, house-made granola, hot sandwiches, and a daily-rotating selection of gluten-free and vegan baked goods.

anecdotes.text: The owners met and trained in Melbourne as barista and roaster, then brought that specialty-coffee culture back to Nara, building a cafe that doubles as a one-group-per-day bed and breakfast.

They focus on African-origin beans (Kenya, Ethiopia) chosen for their stories and value to the growing regions, and serve drinks in handmade ceramics, including handleless cups by a Melbourne-based Scottish ceramic artist.
Gluten-free and vegan baked goods rotate daily and are sourced in part from a local organic partner shop; non-dairy milk options are available for coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen; wheat-based hot sandwiches and toast are prepared in the same small cafe, so cross-contact is possible for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and egg/cheese items (e.g., egg-and-cheese sandwiches, cow's milk) are handled in the same space, so vegan items are made in a non-vegan environment.

positives.text: Offers a daily-rotating selection of gluten-free and vegan baked goods and sweets alongside house-made granola.
Provides non-dairy (alternative) milk options for coffee drinks.
Melbourne-trained, English-comfortable owners make it easy to ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: The owners trained in Melbourne and run an English-language hospitality podcast, so English-speaking visitors will feel comfortable ordering and asking about menu items.

places.name: 白（つくも） (Tsukumo)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. This is a traditional kaiseki kitchen where soy sauce (wheat-based), dashi, miso and seasonal courses are central, so it is not celiac-safe by default. The chef's experience with shojin and bespoke omakase means dietary requests can sometimes be honored, but a celiac traveler must arrange this explicitly at booking and cannot assume cross-contamination control.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant; the standard omakase features Yamato beef, Yamato chicken and fish/dashi, so default courses are not vegan or vegetarian. However, chef Masato Nishihara was the opening head chef at Kajitsu in New York (a shōjin/Buddhist-vegetarian restaurant that earned two Michelin stars) and weaves shōjin elements into his cooking, so a fully plant-based or vegetarian course can potentially be requested in advance. Vegan diners should arrange this by phone when reserving rather than expecting it on the day.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00 / Dinner 17:30 (reservation-only fixed-start omakase). Tue–Sun dinner 17:30, last entry 19:00; lunch Wed–Sun 12:00 (no lunch Tue, and no lunch on the 1st of the month).
places.neighborhood: 紀寺町／京終（近鉄奈良駅・ならまち南）
places.notes: Chef Masato Nishihara began cooking straight out of high school at the celebrated Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto, where he trained for about ten years under Teiichi Yuki, learning not only the kitchen but also flower arranging, the tea ceremony and calligraphy. He went on to cook at

chef_bio.background: Chef Masato Nishihara began cooking straight out of high school at the celebrated Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto, where he trained for about ten years under Teiichi Yuki, learning not only the kitchen but also flower arranging, the tea ceremony and calligraphy. He went on to cook at Toma, a soba-kaiseki house in Karuizawa, and in 2009 was recruited to open Kajitsu in New York, a shōjin (Zen Buddhist vegetarian) restaurant that earned two Michelin stars within a year. He also gained experience in London before returning to Japan and settling in Nara, where he opened Tsukumo.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name plays on a Japanese idea: take one (一) away from one hundred (百) and the character 白 (white) remains — read 'tsukumo,' it evokes 99 and the beauty of the not-quite-complete (未完の美). Nishihara is drawn to the beauty of the incomplete and the playful sense of possibility it leaves open, an aesthetic he carries into a city whose ancient temples and treasures he treats as living heritage.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki omakase built around Nara's own ingredients — Yamato vegetables (including Uda kintoki burdock), Yamato beef and Yamato jidori chicken — with shōjin sensibilities and occasional bolder accents such as truffle. The menu changes on a monthly theme and is served as a single fixed course at lunch and dinner.

anecdotes.text: Nishihara was headhunted in 2009 to become the opening chef of Kajitsu in New York, a Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) restaurant that won two Michelin stars within a year of opening.
He trained for roughly a decade at Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto under Teiichi Yuki, studying flower arranging, tea ceremony and calligraphy alongside cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional kaiseki cooking here relies on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso, dashi); no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free protocol is documented, so wheat exposure should be assumed unless arranged.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard omakase includes Yamato beef, Yamato chicken and fish/dashi, so dishes are not vegan or vegetarian by default and would need to be specially prepared on request.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reservation-only fixed omakase with a small kitchen; the chef's shōjin background suggests plant-based or dietary courses can sometimes be arranged, but only if requested in advance when booking.

positives.text: Chef trained as the opening head chef of a two-Michelin-star shōjin (vegetarian) restaurant, so the kitchen genuinely understands plant-based Japanese cooking when given notice.

cultural_comfort.note: The chef has cooked in New York and London and the restaurant has an English website and takes international reservations, so a foreign student would be comfortable — though this is a high-end formal omakase experience (around ¥25,000+), not a casual drop-in.

places.name: 洋食TSUBAKI (Yoshoku TSUBAKI)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese-style Western (yoshoku) restaurant where the core menu is built around wheat: mixed fried platters and chicken nanban use a flour/breadcrumb coating, the stewed hamburger steak and curries use wheat-based roux, and pasta (peperoncino) is wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and fish — wagyu roast beef, chicken nanban, mixed fried platters, stewed hamburger steak, shrimp/fried-chicken curries, and a fish bouillabaisse. Dishes also use egg (soft-boiled egg garnish). No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (daily, when open); Dinner 17:30-20:00 (Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat only). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays. Hours/days can vary — check the shop's social media calendar.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（毘沙門町）／京終駅・近鉄奈良駅周辺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on May 1, 2026, this Western-style restaurant is the independent successor to 'cafe 春,' which ran for about eight and a half years in Nara's historic Naramachi district near the World Heritage Gangoji Temple. The space occupies a renovated traditional wooden townhouse, wit

chef_bio.background: Opened on May 1, 2026, this Western-style restaurant is the independent successor to 'cafe 春,' which ran for about eight and a half years in Nara's historic Naramachi district near the World Heritage Gangoji Temple. The space occupies a renovated traditional wooden townhouse, with red lattice work and walls evoking the area's old quarter, antique furniture, and high ceilings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner wanted guests to be able to linger over a relaxed meal with a drink, which is why dinner service and a curated sake and wine list were added — emphasizing ingredients from the owner's hometown of Hiroshima together with Nara-grown produce, so that both small and large groups can dine casually.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature dishes carry over and expand on the cafe's beloved kettle-baked curry (available with shrimp or fried chicken), alongside hearty yoshoku staples like chicken nanban, mixed fried platters, and stewed hamburger steak. A standout newer dish is a roast beef peperoncino made with pesticide-free Hiroshima lemons and rare-cut wagyu, finished with a soft-boiled egg. Evening service adds appetizers and a fish bouillabaisse.

anecdotes.text: The drinks list pairs Hiroshima sake (the pure-rice 'Kamerei') with Nara's 'Mimuro Sugi,' reflecting the owner's wish to bring a taste of their Hiroshima roots together with local Nara flavors.
The kettle-baked curry is a holdover from the previous 'cafe 春,' carried forward into the new restaurant so longtime regulars could keep enjoying it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around fried, breaded, and roux-based dishes (mixed fry, chicken nanban, hamburger, curry) plus wheat pasta, so heavy shared-wheat exposure is expected. No GF protocol documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on meat, fish, and egg dishes; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run townhouse restaurant in Naramachi where ordering and signage are in Japanese; a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will make ordering smooth, and cash is required.

places.name: 大宮飯店 (Omiya Hanten)
places.gf_detail: This is a Chinese diner built around ramen, gyoza, and stir-fries — wheat noodles, wheat-skin dumplings, and soy/oyster sauces are everywhere, and there is no documented gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood Chinese menu (ramen, mapo tofu with pork, tenshinhan with egg, shark fin soup, stamina ramen). No documented vegan dishes; broths and sauces rely on meat/seafood. Not a vegan destination.
places.neighborhood: 杉ヶ町・JR奈良駅 (Sugigacho, near JR Nara Station)
places.cuisine: 町中華 / 中華料理・ラーメン・餃子
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 大宮飯店 is a family-style Chinese diner that built its reputation over more than 40 years in the Shin-Omiya district of Nara, where it was a familiar neighborhood fixture (including a long-running local delivery service). It moved to its current spot near JR Nara Station — taking ov

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Shin-Omiya, then relocated to Sugigacho near JR Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: 大宮飯店 is a family-style Chinese diner that built its reputation over more than 40 years in the Shin-Omiya district of Nara, where it was a familiar neighborhood fixture (including a long-running local delivery service). It moved to its current spot near JR Nara Station — taking over the space of the former ramen shop 麺人ばろむ庵 — opening there in October 2022. The new room is described as bright, clean, and a touch more refined than the old shop, with counter seating and a raised tatami (小上がり) area.
chef_bio.specialty: A sprawling menu of over 30 town-Chinese (町中華) dishes — gyoza, fried rice (chahan), and ramen at one end, and heartier or premium plates like shark fin soup at the other. Often-mentioned dishes include tenshinhan, mapo tofu rice bowl, chuka-don, stamina ramen, shrimp chili, and sesame balls for dessert.

anecdotes.text: After more than four decades in Shin-Omiya, the shop relocated into the former premises of the popular ramen shop 麺人ばろむ庵 near JR Nara Station, opening in its new home in October 2022.
Weekday lunch sets are famously cheap — reported around 600-700 yen — drawing in local regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat-based items (ramen noodles, gyoza wrappers) and soy/oyster sauces, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant, dishes use standard soy and brown sauces that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on pork, egg, and seafood dishes (mapo tofu with pork, tenshinhan with egg, shark fin soup), so shared woks and broths make vegan-safe preparation unlikely; no vegan options documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a longtime neighborhood Chinese diner where menus and service are in Japanese, so a little spoken Japanese or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 大和茶カフェ 茶樂茶 (Yamato-cha Cafe Sarasa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are published anywhere. This is a tea café whose food is sweets — the signature purple-sweet-potato Mont Blanc is built on a sponge base and chocolate crumble (wheat), so the menu is not celiac-safe. The tea itself (Yamato kabusecha) is naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF handling is documented. A celiac would need to confirm directly and likely stick to tea only.
places.vegan_detail: The core offering is brewed Yamato tea, which is plant-based, and the tea is finished with an ochitashi (the steeped tea leaves served as a small side). However, the documented sweets (Mont Blanc with whipped cream, warabimochi) contain dairy, so they are not vegan. No vegan menu or substitutions are advertised. A vegan can enjoy the tea service but should ask about any food item.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; closed Monday and Friday
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Kōno-dō-chō), near Kōfuku-ji
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on 2 November 2020 in a quiet corner of Naramachi by an owner named Ishikawa, an avid international traveler who wanted to bring the unhurried richness of overseas tea culture home to Japan.

chef_bio.background: Opened on 2 November 2020 in a quiet corner of Naramachi by an owner named Ishikawa, an avid international traveler who wanted to bring the unhurried richness of overseas tea culture home to Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: To convey, in Japan, the abundance and slowness of tea time the owner experienced abroad, engaging all five senses through aroma, the mist of the kettle, and a space that blends Japanese and Western aesthetics.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully brewed Yamato-cha, especially kabusecha (shaded tea) sourced directly from tea farmers in Nara's Tahara district, served as three successive infusions at different temperatures (the first drawing out umami, the second balancing umami and bitterness, the third a clean finish), and finished with ochitashi, the steeped leaves served as a small side. The most beloved sweet is the purple-sweet-potato Mont Blanc, layering a purple-sweet-potato sponge with hojicha warabimochi, whipped cream, sweet-potato paste, chocolate crumble and edible flowers.

anecdotes.text: The wide counter and seating look out over a small garden, and tea is poured from a flat Tokoname-ware teapot whose micropores are said to mellow the brew and encourage the leaves to unfurl.
The owner enjoys international travel and founded the cafe to share the richness of overseas tea-drinking time with Japan.

positives.text: The house specialty is brewed Yamato tea, which is naturally plant-based and gluten-free, so a tea-only order is the safest choice for restricted diets.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny six-seat counter café focused on a quiet, multi-infusion tea experience; some Japanese helps and the calm, intimate setting rewards visitors comfortable observing local etiquette, though staff are welcoming to curious guests.

places.name: ゐざさ茶屋（ゐざさ中谷本舗 東大寺門前夢風ひろば店） (Izasa Chaya (Izasa Nakatani Honpo, Todaiji M)
places.gf_detail: The house specialty, kakinoha-zushi (vinegared rice and fish wrapped in a persimmon leaf), is naturally wheat-free, and the persimmon-leaf sushi and bamboo-leaf "Izasa-zushi" are rice-and-fish based. However, the teahouse menu also features Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and tempura (wheat batter), and sushi rice/fish are typically seasoned with regular wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated preparation, so a celiac traveler must ask carefully and cannot assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-sushi specialist. The signature kakinoha-zushi is made with mackerel, salmon and other fish, and set meals center on tempura, sōmen and steamed sushi. There is no documented vegan menu; dashi and fish are pervasive, so a vegan would be very limited and should ask in advance.
places.neighborhood: 東大寺門前・夢風ひろば (Tōdai-ji gate, Nara Park)
places.cuisine: 柿の葉ずし・奈良郷土料理
places.notes: Izasa is the dine-in tea house of Nakatani Honpo, a Nara persimmon-leaf-sushi house that began in 1921 (Taishō 10) as a rice merchant, Nakatani Shōten, in the mountain village of Kamikitayama in Okuyoshino. Selling rice in a village that thrived on forestry, the family also sold

chef_bio.background: Izasa is the dine-in tea house of Nakatani Honpo, a Nara persimmon-leaf-sushi house that began in 1921 (Taishō 10) as a rice merchant, Nakatani Shōten, in the mountain village of Kamikitayama in Okuyoshino. Selling rice in a village that thrived on forestry, the family also sold the local kakinoha-zushi; about fifty years ago the previous owners devised their bamboo-leaf-wrapped 'Izasa-zushi,' and the company reinvented itself as a maker of Nara's regional 'specialty sushi,' reaching its 100th anniversary in 2021. This branch sits at the gate of Tōdai-ji inside the Yumekaze Hiroba complex: a sushi shop on the first floor and the Izasa Chaya tea house on the second, where you can eat looking out over the Nandaimon gate and the great roof of the Daibutsuden.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is to let guests savor seasonal Nara flavors in comfort ('季節折々のおいしさを、くつろぎながら'), carrying the food culture of Okuyoshino from the ancient capital of Nara to the wider world in a spirit of 'honoring the old to learn the new' (温故知新).
chef_bio.specialty: Kakinoha-zushi (vinegared rice topped with mackerel, salmon and other fish, each piece wrapped in a fragrant persimmon leaf) and the house bamboo-leaf-wrapped Izasa-zushi. The tea house also serves set meals with Miwa sōmen, tempura and steamed sushi.

anecdotes.text: The trade name 'Izasa' comes from a Kamikitayama legend of Izasa-ō, a giant wild boar with bamboo grass (sasa) growing from its back; it was bestowed by Kōshō Shimizu, a former chief abbot of Tōdai-ji, with the wish that the company act 'for the region' and 'for people' (the character ゐ deriving from 為, meaning 'for the sake of').
Nakatani Honpo, maker of the 'Izasa kakinoha-zushi' often seen at Shinkansen station kiosks, was founded in 1921 as a long-established rice shop in Okuyoshino.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No gluten-free soy sauce is documented; standard sushi/fish seasoning uses wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu also includes wheat sōmen and tempura.

positives.text: The core kakinoha-zushi is naturally a rice-and-fish dish wrapped in a persimmon leaf, so it contains no wheat in the wrapping or rice itself.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed tourist-area tea house at the Tōdai-ji gate where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get you a meal, though detailed allergy questions may need translation help.

places.name: TEGAIMON CAFE（テガイモン カフェ） (TEGAIMON CAFE)
places.gf_detail: This is a juice and fresh-fruit café rather than a wheat-heavy kitchen, so naturally gluten-free choices exist: the fresh fruit juices, fruit punch (fruit/soda based), shaved ice, and roasted sweet potato contain no obvious wheat. However, there is no celiac/allergen program, no documented cross-contamination controls, and no gluten-free certification. Suitable for gluten-avoiders by menu choice; not verified safe for medically strict celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The fresh cold-pressed/hand-squeezed fruit juices (e.g. orange from 3 whole oranges, kiwi) and the winter roasted sweet potato are plant-based and suitable for vegans. However, several signature items are not vegan: the fruit punch includes ice cream and the popular shaved ice includes strawberry-milk/dairy toppings, and coffee may be served with milk. No dedicated vegan menu; choose the straight juices or yaki-imo to stay plant-based. Confirm toppings when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:00. Closed Wednesdays, and 2nd & 3rd Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（東大寺 転害門前）/ 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 町家カフェ / フレッシュジュース
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] TEGAIMON CAFE sits in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, directly across from Todai-ji's Tegaimon Gate, a National Treasure that has stood since the Nara period more than 1,300 years ago. The café occupies a former merchant's storefront that is over 100 years old, renovated into a

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi, in front of Tegaimon Gate)
chef_bio.background: TEGAIMON CAFE sits in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, directly across from Todai-ji's Tegaimon Gate, a National Treasure that has stood since the Nara period more than 1,300 years ago. The café occupies a former merchant's storefront that is over 100 years old, renovated into a retro-modern space with high ceilings and an airy, open feel. It opened in August 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around 'drinkable fruit' — letting visitors recharge with the vitamins of fresh-cut fruit while taking in centuries-old architecture across the street.
chef_bio.specialty: Cold-pressed and hand-squeezed fresh fruit juices — the signature orange juice is made from three whole oranges squeezed by hand — alongside a fruit punch piled with five or more fruits, seasonal shaved ice in summer, and slow-roasted sweet potato baked for about two hours in a handmade clay pot in late autumn and winter.

anecdotes.text: The orange juice is squeezed by hand from three or more whole oranges using a hand juicer, served fresh on the spot.
In the colder months the café serves roasted sweet potatoes in two varieties (Naruto Gold and Beni Haruka), each baked slowly for roughly two hours in a handmade clay pot.
Owner Chikako Nakamori is known for a welcoming smile and fills the space with seasonal decorations and artwork.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items mix dairy with fruit (fruit punch includes ice cream; shaved ice includes strawberry-milk toppings), so vegan-suitable juices are prepared in a space where dairy is also handled; no separation protocol is documented.

positives.text: Menu centers on fresh-cut and hand-squeezed fruit juices and roasted sweet potato, which are naturally plant-based and free of wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, owner-run juice café where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the menu is simple (point-and-order juices, shaved ice, sweet potato), so a foreign student can manage comfortably even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 御菓子司 春日庵 (Kasuga-an)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature satsuma-yaki bun is made of a wheat-flour-and-egg dough wrapped around red bean paste, and the tea room serves wheat-based wagashi alongside mochi and warabi-mochi items. There is no indication of gluten-free options or celiac accommodation, and cross-contact with wheat flour is unavoidable in a wagashi kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Some items appear plant-based in principle (anko/red bean paste, kuzukiri, kuzumochi, warabi-mochi, matcha kakigori), but the signature satsuma-yaki contains egg in its dough, and no allergen or vegan information is published. A strict vegan would need to ask item-by-item; nothing is certified or advertised as vegan.
places.hours_raw: Confectionery shop (1F): 9:00–18:00. Tea room (2F): 11:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:00). Tea room closed Thursdays and the 2nd & 4th Wednesdays (closed all Wednesdays and Thursdays December–March). Shop sales: irregular holidays.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (中新屋町), near Gangō-ji temple and Kōshin-san; appro
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kasuga-an is a long-established wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house in the historic Naramachi district, tucked near Gangō-ji temple and the Kōshin-san shrine. Founded in the Meiji era (sources cite Meiji 30 / 1897), it has spent well over a century making and selling its signa

chef_bio.background: Kasuga-an is a long-established wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house in the historic Naramachi district, tucked near Gangō-ji temple and the Kōshin-san shrine. Founded in the Meiji era (sources cite Meiji 30 / 1897), it has spent well over a century making and selling its signature manjū, with a quiet second-floor tea room added so visitors can rest and taste sweets fresh from the kitchen.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is satsuma-yaki — a sweet-potato-shaped manjū. Smooth koshi-an (strained red bean paste) made from Hokkaido azuki and finished light and not overly sweet is wrapped in a dough kneaded from wheat flour and egg, then skewered and grilled to give it its characteristic browned, sweet-potato-like skin. The tea room also serves mochi, kuzukiri, kuzumochi, warabi-mochi, and seasonal kakigori such as Yamato matcha shaved ice with white dango and red beans, or a kuromitsu-kinako version with warabi-mochi, paired with matcha, coffee, or black tea.

anecdotes.text: The second-floor tea room overlooks the UNESCO World Heritage Gangō-ji temple, letting guests eat freshly grilled satsuma-yaki and seasonal sweets with a view over the old townscape of Naramachi.
The satsuma-yaki bun is shaped like a sweet potato, its skin grilled on a skewer to mimic the look of a roasted satsuma-imo, with a light Hokkaido-azuki koshi-an inside.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen works with wheat flour for the satsuma-yaki dough and other wagashi, so cross-contact with wheat is inherent; no gluten-free handling is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature satsuma-yaki dough contains egg, so it is not vegan; other items like kuzumochi and matcha kakigori may be plant-based but are not labeled, and no separate-utensil or vegan handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming neighborhood sweet shop where a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the picture menu will carry you through; little to no English is likely, but ordering tea and a sweet is simple and low-pressure.

places.name: 珈琲一族 (Coffee Ichizoku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is wheat-heavy — breaded/fried shrimp and cutlets, hamburg steak with demi-glace, curry roux, cakes and toast — and there is no documented gluten-free option or allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: This is a traditional Western-style kissaten built around meat and fried foods (hamburg steak, shrimp fry, cream croquettes, pork cutlet curry). Dishes rely on demi-glace, dairy, eggs and fish-based broths, and no vegan menu is documented.
places.neighborhood: もちいどの商店街（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約7分）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎の喫茶店／ハンバーグ・洋食ランチ・ケーキ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 珈琲一族 (Coffee Ichizoku) is a long-running retro kissaten tucked into the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The storefront is covered in hand-lettered menu boards, and the interior carries an old-Showa, almost cave-like calm across its two floors.

chef_bio.background: 珈琲一族 (Coffee Ichizoku) is a long-running retro kissaten tucked into the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The storefront is covered in hand-lettered menu boards, and the interior carries an old-Showa, almost cave-like calm across its two floors. It is the kind of all-day neighborhood spot that flows from morning service through lunch to evening drinks.
chef_bio.specialty: Generous, affordable Western-style set meals — the signature being the hamburger-and-shrimp-fry teishoku, a coarsely-ground beef hamburg steak under plenty of demi-glace alongside a crisp shrimp fry with tartar sauce, served with rice, miso soup, macaroni salad and pickles. Cream croquettes, katsu curry, and cakes like Mont Blanc round out the lineup, with most sets around or under 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is beloved as a nostalgic, retro coffee house where hearty homemade Western lunch sets stay remarkably cheap, mostly under 1,000 yen.
Locals describe a bright exterior with a historic, cave-like interior atmosphere, and note it offers a wide variety of substantial Western-style lunches.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen routinely fries breaded items (shrimp fry, cutlets) and serves wheat-based hamburg steak, curry roux and cakes, so heavy gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat- and dairy-centric Western menu with no vegan items documented; shared cooking surfaces with meat and fried foods should be assumed.

cultural_comfort.note: A nostalgic, locals-only retro coffee shop with no English menu or English-speaking staff, so a foreign student will be most comfortable pointing at the photo menu boards or going with a Japanese-speaking friend.

places.name: 焼き芋専門店 維新蔵 ならまち店 (Yakiimo Senmonten Ishingura Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: The signature item is a slow-aged, twice-roasted honey sweet potato (蜜芋焼き芋) — plain roasted sweet potato is naturally gluten-free, making it a safe, simple option for those avoiding gluten. However, this is a cafe with a broader sweets menu (e.g. candied sweet potato / 大学芋, cakes) where glazes, batters, or sauces may contain wheat, and there is no documented dedicated gluten-free protocol or allergen handling. Confirm individual items at the counter before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The core product — a roasted honey sweet potato aged 100 days and described as additive-free with sugars developed naturally by the potato itself through a twice-roasting method — is plant-based with no dairy, egg, or animal ingredients, making it genuinely suitable for vegans. Other cafe sweets and drinks may contain dairy or egg (e.g. cheesecake, baked confections), so vegans should stick to the plain roasted sweet potato or confirm each item. No formal vegan labeling.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（下御門町／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
places.cuisine: 焼き芋専門のカフェ／スイーツ・甘味
places.notes: Ishingura is a roasted sweet potato (yakiimo) specialist whose Naramachi shop sits in the historic townhouse district near Gangoji Temple, in the old 'outer capital' area of the former Heijo-kyo. The Naramachi location was renovated and reopened on August 10, 2023, adding eat-in

chef_bio.background: Ishingura is a roasted sweet potato (yakiimo) specialist whose Naramachi shop sits in the historic townhouse district near Gangoji Temple, in the old 'outer capital' area of the former Heijo-kyo. The Naramachi location was renovated and reopened on August 10, 2023, adding eat-in seating and seasonal menu items so visitors can enjoy the sweet potatoes on the spot in the atmospheric old-town setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: The focus is on letting the sweet potato create its own natural, additive-free sweetness through long aging and careful roasting, drawing out a honey-like depth of flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Their signature is the 濃蜜焼き芋『維新蔵の蜜芋』, a rich honey-like roasted sweet potato. The shop ages the sweet potatoes for 100 days and uses a proprietary twice-roasting method so the potato itself develops a variety of natural sugars, producing a creamy texture and a deep, honey-like sweetness rather than plain sweetness, with no additives. They also serve candied sweet potato (大学芋) and seasonal sweet-potato sweets.

anecdotes.text: The Naramachi shop was renovated and reopened on August 10, 2023, introducing eat-in dining and seasonal offerings.
The sweet potatoes are aged for 100 days and twice-roasted so the potato develops its own natural sugars, giving a honey-like, additive-free sweetness.
The terrace seating is dog-friendly.

positives.text: The signature roasted honey sweet potato is described as additive-free, with its sweetness developed naturally by the potato through 100-day aging and a twice-roasting method — a simple, single-ingredient item that is plant-based and naturally gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, walk-in sweet-potato cafe in historic Naramachi where you can simply point to the roasted sweet potato and pay by card or QR, so even with no Japanese it is an easy and welcoming stop for a foreign student.

places.name: 宝島 (Takarajima)
places.gf_detail: A retro Western-style kissaten built around omurice (egg-wrapped ketchup rice), pork cutlet, and curry — all wheat- and flour-heavy dishes. Ketchup sauces and breaded cutlets, plus the typical kissaten kitchen, mean there is no gluten-free menu and no celiac-safe preparation. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg (omurice), pork cutlet, and chicken/meat-based Western dishes; coffee and dairy-based drinks and coffee jelly round it out. No vegan or vegetarian items are documented, and the signature dishes rely on egg, butter, and meat. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・下御門商店街（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約10分）
places.cuisine: レトロ喫茶店／オムライス・洋食
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Takarajima is a longtime retro kissaten tucked along the Shimogomon shopping street in Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It has been quietly serving the neighborhood for over twenty years, run by an older proprietor who keeps oldies and jazz playing through a ca

chef_bio.background: Takarajima is a longtime retro kissaten tucked along the Shimogomon shopping street in Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It has been quietly serving the neighborhood for over twenty years, run by an older proprietor who keeps oldies and jazz playing through a calm, time-stopped interior decorated in classic Showa-era café style.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, nostalgic refuge: the owner curates the music and the slow pace as much as the food, the kind of neighborhood spot regulars return to for comfort rather than novelty.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-fashioned omurice — buttery ketchup chicken rice wrapped in a soft omelet — is the signature, most often ordered as a set with a small pork cutlet or curry. Lunch sets come with salad and a post-meal coffee or tea, and the house coffee and coffee jelly are local favorites.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a nostalgic jazz-café ambiance with oldies flowing through a 'fallen-out-of-time' room, and praise the butter-scented, ketchup-laced old-style omurice as the dish to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (omurice, breaded pork cutlet, curry) are flour- and wheat-based and prepared in a shared kissaten kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items rely on egg, butter, and meat, with no vegan options noted; vegan diners cannot be reliably accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, longtime local kissaten where the menu and atmosphere are entirely Japanese — knowing how to order an omurice or cutlet set in basic Japanese will make the visit smooth, and cash is essential.

places.name: 京家 (Kyoya)
places.gf_detail: Udon is the house specialty and is made from wheat, and tempura batter and soy-based seasonings add further gluten. There is no gluten-free menu or documented accommodation, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-and-meat-forward Japanese eatery: udon broth, sashimi, tempura and chicken dishes all rely on fish dashi or animal ingredients. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and even the udon sets are not plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:30-14:00, 17:00-22:00; Sunday closed.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Minami-ichi-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kyoya began as an udon specialist on Nishi-Kitsutsuji before relocating into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's atmospheric Naramachi quarter, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station near Sarusawa Pond. After the move it broadened from a pure udon shop into a full Japanese

chef_bio.background: Kyoya began as an udon specialist on Nishi-Kitsutsuji before relocating into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's atmospheric Naramachi quarter, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station near Sarusawa Pond. After the move it broadened from a pure udon shop into a full Japanese kitchen while keeping the smooth, springy noodles that built its name. By day it is a calm, casual lunch spot; in the evening it shifts into an izakaya mood.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-served udon (bukkake and kama-age styles) with smooth, throat-pleasing noodles, plus the changing Kyoya Gozen lunch set that pairs udon with seasonal fish tempura, small side dishes, rice and pickles. Evening favorites include fresh sashimi platters, grilled chicken, and eggplant dengaku.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the converted townhouse as a hidden-gem retreat, with shoes-off sunken-hearth seating downstairs and quiet tatami rooms upstairs giving an old-fashioned, peaceful feel.
The kitchen carried over the silky, pleasing udon texture from its original udon-shop days even after expanding the menu in Naramachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat udon is the central dish and tempura is fried on site, so wheat is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as bukkake udon, eggplant dengaku and grilled chicken use Japanese soy-based seasonings, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Udon broth and most sets rely on fish dashi, and sashimi and chicken are prepared in the same kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, homey local spot where you remove your shoes for tatami/hearth seating; staff are warm but communication is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help.

places.name: 染谷商店 (Someya Shoten)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature dashi-maki tamago and set meals rely on Japanese dashi and seasonings that typically include wheat-based soy sauce, and rice is served alongside. No gluten-free protocols are documented, so a celiac diner would need to ask directly and exercise caution.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-suitable. The core offering is egg-based (dashi-maki tamago, egg sandwiches, omurice) and the dashi is made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu, so it contains fish. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:15), open daily including holidays; closes when sold out. Hours have varied across sources (one local report lists 10:30-18:00, closed Wednesdays). Closures are irregular — check before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Higashishiodo-cho), near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Someya Shoten began about 70 years ago as a specialist workshop making thick-rolled egg for the sushi trade. Founder and master craftsman Masahiro Someya, now in his 80s, supplied gyoku (sushi-topping egg) and sweet datemaki egg rolls wholesale to fish markets and sushi restauran

chef_bio.background: Someya Shoten began about 70 years ago as a specialist workshop making thick-rolled egg for the sushi trade. Founder and master craftsman Masahiro Someya, now in his 80s, supplied gyoku (sushi-topping egg) and sweet datemaki egg rolls wholesale to fish markets and sushi restaurants. As the family workshop's members aged past 70 and questions of succession and physical strain set in, the family decided to preserve the craft in a new form, converting the ground floor of their three-story former workshop-residence into a small cafe in the summer of the previous year.
chef_bio.philosophy: The founder's eldest daughter Kiyoko, who now runs the cafe, describes the aim that customers remember their dashi-maki tamago so warmly they would want to taste it one more time. Ingredients are sourced locally: eggs, Koshihikari rice, Yamato (Nara) vegetables, and domestic bonito and kombu for the broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Dashi-maki tamago (dashi-rolled omelet) teishoku set meals; also a thick egg sandwich and classic omurice. The signature omelet is built around four to five eggs and a generous amount of dashi for a soft, fluffy texture, cooked fresh to order.

anecdotes.text: The founder said he wanted to make the decision to wind down the original factory while he was still physically able to handle disposing of the large machinery.
The cafe occupies the converted first floor of the family's former three-story workshop-residence in Naramachi, and the dashi-maki tamago is prepared fresh for each order.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals and the dashi-based omelet use traditional Japanese seasonings; soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-containing, and no gluten-free soy sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers entirely on egg dishes and uses fish (bonito) and kombu dashi, so animal products are pervasive throughout the menu.

positives.text: Ingredients are simple and locally sourced: local eggs, Koshihikari rice, Nara vegetables, and domestic bonito and kombu broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny family-run spot in an old house with a one-order-per-person and no-reservations policy; a little Japanese and patience help, as this is a traditional local eatery rather than a tourist-oriented restaurant.

places.name: 柳茶屋 (Yanagi Chaya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch (warabiko), which is wheat-free at its base, but the shop is a traditional sweets house with no published allergen handling and no dedicated preparation for celiacs. Matcha and Japanese sweets are generally low-wheat, but the savory side (奈良茶飯 / nara chameshi sets, often accompanied by pickles and side dishes) and any soy-sauce-based seasonings are not verified gluten-free. A celiac diner must ask directly; cross-contact cannot be assumed safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Warabi mochi (bracken starch, sugar, kinako soy flour) can be plant-based, but the kinako/anko and matcha sweets may involve dairy in some variations (e.g., the milk warabi mochi version), and savory dishes such as 奈良茶飯 are not confirmed dashi-free. Vegan-leaning diners should ask which sweets are free of dairy; this is not a dedicated vegetarian/vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); some listings note Wed/Thu opening at 11:00. Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池畔・興福寺そば、近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yanagi Chaya was founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) beside Sarusawa Pond, in the shadow of Kofukuji's five-story pagoda in central Nara. It was opened specifically to keep alive Nara's old tea-rice tradition: 'nara chameshi' (奈良茶飯), rice cooked with tea and roasted, crushed soybeans, a d

chef_bio.background: Yanagi Chaya was founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) beside Sarusawa Pond, in the shadow of Kofukuji's five-story pagoda in central Nara. It was opened specifically to keep alive Nara's old tea-rice tradition: 'nara chameshi' (奈良茶飯), rice cooked with tea and roasted, crushed soybeans, a dish eaten in Nara since ancient times that had been fading from memory. The shop occupies a wooden building that has stood since the Meiji era, and visitors are seated in tatami rooms, at tables, or in private rooms looking out toward the pond.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserving Nara's tea-rice culture so that it would not be forgotten, served in a historic wooden tea house overlooking Sarusawa Pond.
chef_bio.specialty: Warabi mochi (bracken-starch jelly served with matcha) is the flagship, available year-round; there is also a milk warabi mochi variation with red bean and chestnut, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice with strawberry or Uji kinako). The founding signature is the nara chameshi set.

anecdotes.text: The founder opened the tea house in 1902 to ensure Nara's tea rice, made from tea and roasted crushed soybeans, would not disappear from the city's table.
The setting beside Sarusawa Pond is considered scenic enough to be worth the visit on its own, with views toward Kofukuji's pagoda.

positives.text: The signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch (warabiko), a naturally wheat-free base, served with matcha.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-familiar traditional tea house by Sarusawa Pond where a friendly greeting and pointing at the warabi mochi or matcha set will get you served comfortably even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 天迦久珈琲 (Amenokaku Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature food is in-house-baked 食パン (wheat shokupan) toast, and there are no documented gluten-free items or celiac accommodations. Plain black coffee is naturally gluten-free, but the kitchen is bread-focused, so this is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Black filter coffee and the water-diluted BLACK espresso are incidentally plant-based, but the WHITE drink uses steamed milk and the signature toast uses wheat bread with butter/honey toppings. A vegan can drink black coffee but has no documented vegan food option; confirm with staff.
places.hours_raw: Official site: 7:00–17:00 (Naramachi main shop). Opening interview / local guides: 7:00–15:00 with irregular closures. Tabelog lists 12:00–17:00 closed Friday. Hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー・喫茶
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 天迦久珈琲 opened on March 1, 2021 in Nara's historic Naramachi district. The founder, Yui Kitaguchi, was 27 at opening and is a licensed English-speaking tour guide who had lived and taught English in English-speaking countries before returning to open the café.

chef_bio.background: 天迦久珈琲 opened on March 1, 2021 in Nara's historic Naramachi district. The founder, Yui Kitaguchi, was 27 at opening and is a licensed English-speaking tour guide who had lived and taught English in English-speaking countries before returning to open the café.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 天迦久 (Amenokaku) comes from a deity in the Kojiki who overcomes hardship; the owner chose it hoping to surpass whatever difficulties lie ahead. She washes green beans before roasting for cleanliness and uses spring water (Gororo Water from Tenkawa village) for brewing, prizing the iron-cast Old Probat roaster for the deep sweetness and flavor it brings out.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee served three ways: WHITE (espresso with steamed milk), BLACK (espresso let down with natural spring water), and FILTER (hand drip), each around 550 yen; plus thick toast made from house-baked shokupan, topped with Nara-prefecture honey or peanut butter. Beans are roasted on a 1996 Old Probat machine.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a licensed English-interpreter guide who planned weekend English-conversation café sessions to help the local community welcome international visitors.
The shop logo was designed by Ouka, a calligraphy artist known for yokai-themed work and an official Nara tourism ambassador.
Beans are roasted on a 1996 Old Probat machine, prized because the iron casting brings out stronger sweetness and flavor than modern equipment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The café bakes its own wheat shokupan in-house and serves it as toast, so flour and gluten are present on-site; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Milk is used for the WHITE espresso drink and butter/honey for the toast; no documented separation for vegan orders.

positives.text: The owner is a licensed English-speaking guide, so dietary questions can be asked directly in English.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner is a licensed English-speaking guide who has lived and taught abroad and has run weekend English-conversation café sessions, so a foreign student should feel very comfortable here.

places.name: 奈良 オモテナシ食堂 (Nara Omotenashi Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. This is a Japanese teishoku-style kitchen; menus include sixteen-grain rice, soup and various vegetable preparations that in Japan typically rely on soy sauce and dashi (both common gluten sources via wheat-based soy sauce and fish-stock seasonings). There is no documented dedicated gluten-free menu, no stated cross-contamination controls, and a shared home-style kitchen, so a celiac traveler should not assume safety and would need to ask directly about every dish, including the soy sauce and stock used.
places.vegan_detail: The signature からだ想いベジプレート (body-kind vegetable plate, ~1,650 yen) is a meat-free, monthly-changing plate of about eight vegetable dishes with roughly 350g of vegetables (e.g. lentil salad, samosa-style potato gyoza, simmered kombu, daikon-and-hijiki, marinades, a detox soup, sixteen-grain rice). It is explicitly meat-free, but there is no published confirmation that it is fully vegan: Japanese vegetable simmered dishes and soups very commonly use fish dashi (bonito/katsuo) and the gyoza/田楽 components may contain non-vegan seasonings. The rest of the menu features meat- and fish-based plates. A vegan diner should confirm dashi, fish products and egg/dairy in each component before ordering.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約5分
