places.cuisine: 創作和食・健康ごはん
places.notes: A small individually-run cafe-restaurant a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, reached by heading down the Konishi-dori arcade and crossing Sanjo-dori. It built its identity around home-style cooking you can eat with confidence every day.

chef_bio.background: A small individually-run cafe-restaurant a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, reached by heading down the Konishi-dori arcade and crossing Sanjo-dori. It built its identity around home-style cooking you can eat with confidence every day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the idea of 'homemade, body-kind food you can eat with peace of mind every day,' emphasizing generous vegetables and nutritional balance.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the からだ想いベジプレート (body-kind vegetable plate), a monthly-rotating, meat-free plate of about eight vegetable dishes with roughly 350g of vegetables served with sixteen-grain rice. The kitchen also serves meat- and fish-centered plates, healthy curries, and takeout bento.

anecdotes.text: The interior is finished with a generous amount of hinoki cypress grown in Nara Prefecture, giving the counter, table and terrace seating a bright, warm feel.

The vegetable plate changes monthly and has featured items such as lentil salad, samosa-style potato gyoza, simmered kombu, shredded daikon with hijiki, eggplant-and-tomato marinade, and a spring-cabbage and yuzu-pepper detox soup.

positives.text: Offers a clearly meat-free vegetable plate with around 350g of vegetables across roughly eight dishes, making it an easy choice for diners avoiding meat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, calm individually-run teishoku spot where a little spoken Japanese will help you order and ask about ingredients, though pointing at the vegetable plate works in a pinch.

places.name: OYATSUYA 323（おやつや みつみ） (OYATSUYA 323 (Oyatsuya Mitsumi))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire lineup is wheat-flour baked goods — canelés, financiers, madeleines, Breton galettes, scones, and butter sandwich cookies — so it is unsuitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners. No gluten-free items are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The baked goods are built around cultured butter, eggs, and dairy (financiers with browned butter, butter-rich Breton galettes, butter sandwich cookies); no vegan items are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00, 不定休 (irregular closures)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Mochiidono-cho, back alley off Mochiidono shoppin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] OYATSUYA 323 (おやつや みつみ) opened on November 1, 2022, in a quiet back alley off the Mochiidono shopping street in Nara's old-town Naramachi district. It is a tiny one-shop sweets atelier run by a pastry chef who previously trained and worked at prestigious hotels, including the Imp

chef_bio.background: OYATSUYA 323 (おやつや みつみ) opened on November 1, 2022, in a quiet back alley off the Mochiidono shopping street in Nara's old-town Naramachi district. It is a tiny one-shop sweets atelier run by a pastry chef who previously trained and worked at prestigious hotels, including the Imperial Hotel Osaka and a JW Marriott property, before setting out to open a small neighborhood bakery of their own.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small, approachable neighborhood okashi shop that pairs serious hotel-trained pastry technique with a sense of humor, seen in pun-named items like the madeleine.
chef_bio.specialty: French-style baked confections: rum-and-vanilla canelés with a crisp shell and chewy center, almond browned-butter financiers made with cultured butter, thick rum-scented Breton galettes, bite-size 'scrap' scones (plain, chocolate, Earl Grey), and playful sea-bream-shaped butter sandwich cookies filled with red bean and butter.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a pastry chef with a hotel background (Imperial Hotel Osaka, JW Marriott) who chose a narrow Naramachi side street to open their own small okashi shop.
Signature items include a large rum-and-vanilla canelé with a crisp exterior and chewy interior, and a sea-bream-shaped butter sandwich cookie filled with red bean and butter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything sold is wheat-flour baked goods made in the same small kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable throughout the shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Items are made with butter, eggs and dairy; no separation from animal products is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual counter sweets shop where you can simply point at the baked goods you want, making it very easy for a foreign student even with no Japanese.

places.name: アフタヌーンティー ロシェ (Afternoon Tea Rocher)
places.gf_detail: This is an afternoon tea specialist built around wheat-based pastries, scones, quiche/pies, cakes and petit fours, so it is not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten-free diets. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. A gluten-free traveler would have essentially nothing safe to eat here.
places.vegan_detail: The set menu centers on dairy, eggs and cured meat: butter-based scones and pastries, Mont Blanc, meringue, nougat glacé, prosciutto cake salé and anchovy/fish pie, plus quiche. Nothing in the documented menu is vegan and there is no stated vegan option, though some plant-forward items (carrot salad, Nara-vegetable elements) exist. A vegan should ask in advance; expect very limited or no suitable options.
places.hours_raw: 月・火・水・金・土・日 12:00-17:00（料理L.O. 15:00）／木曜定休。平日12:00-15:30、土日祝は16:00頃までとする情報もあり。90分制。
places.neighborhood: ならまち（南市町）／近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 築100年超の町家を改装したアフタヌーンティー専門店。パティシエが手がける奈良県産食材の
places.notes: Owner-pastry chef Iwasaki trained at well-regarded patisseries in Tokyo, Fukuoka, Paris and Normandy before building a string of beloved Nara dessert spots, including the patisserie Bon Chic, Patisserie Karaku, the Taiwanese sweets shop Sarusawa Touka, and Cheesecake Rocks. He is

chef_bio.background: Owner-pastry chef Iwasaki trained at well-regarded patisseries in Tokyo, Fukuoka, Paris and Normandy before building a string of beloved Nara dessert spots, including the patisserie Bon Chic, Patisserie Karaku, the Taiwanese sweets shop Sarusawa Touka, and Cheesecake Rocks. He is also a certified tea advisor and personally curates the tea list.
chef_bio.philosophy: A fusion of Japanese and Western aesthetics inside a century-old machiya, offering an accessible luxury: a full, professionally made afternoon tea from around ¥3,400, in a setting that warmly welcomes solo guests rather than requiring groups.
chef_bio.specialty: Three-tier afternoon tea built on Nara-prefecture ingredients: savories like prosciutto cake salé, anchovy fish pie and Nara-vegetable quiche; scones made with local hōjicha and Yamato tea with chocolate chips and local honey; and petit fours such as pumpkin-caramel tart, Mont Blanc, strawberry-rose-chocolate tart, champagne biscuit and matcha meringue.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies a machiya over 100 years old in the Naramachi district; the building previously housed a cafe called 'Naramachi-mura,' and Iwasaki took it over through a connection with the former owner, preserving original wooden beams and pillars while adding William Morris-style wallpaper, European antiques and a preserved tatami tea room. The name 'Rocher' is French for 'rock,' a nod to his surname.

The tea program features Nara producers and a touch of history: award-winning 'Tsuki no Kaori' black tea from Tea Farm Inokura, premium Yamato hōjicha from Kenichi Shizen Noen, and a Naramachi masala chai blended with a long-established Kikuoka kanpo (herbal) apothecary, alongside German Ronnefeldt teas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built on wheat (scones, pastries, cakes, quiche, pies, biscuits), so cross-contamination is inherent and the venue is not suitable for celiacs. No gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared with shared dairy, eggs and meat (prosciutto, anchovy); no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

positives.text: Menu items and ingredients (Nara-vegetable quiche, carrot salad, prosciutto cake salé, anchovy fish pie, local-tea scones, named petit fours) are described in detail by published articles, helping diners identify what they are eating, though no formal allergen program is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student would find a warm welcome to solo diners but should expect a primarily Japanese-language setting and reservations made by phone or Instagram DM, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps.

places.name: そば処 季のせ (Soba-dokoro Kinose)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is made with soy sauce, which contains wheat, and even the 100% buckwheat (jūwari) soba is prepared in a kitchen handling wheat-based two-eight (nihachi) soba and tempura, so cross-contamination is highly likely. No celiac accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Soba broth is based on fish dashi (bonito/kombu), and most dishes involve fish, egg (e.g. soft-boiled egg soba), or tempura. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun & holidays 11:00-14:30; closed Tuesdays. (An older local-guide listing also showed a 17:00-20:00 dinner service with Tuesday and Friday evening closed, but current Tabelog/Gnavi listings show lunch only.)
places.neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池・近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約8分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, intimate hand-made soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi district, directly in front of the Naramachi Center and a short stroll from Sarusawa Pond. It blends a traditional townhouse setting of table and tatami seating with a quietly modern touch, with jazz playing

chef_bio.background: A small, intimate hand-made soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi district, directly in front of the Naramachi Center and a short stroll from Sarusawa Pond. It blends a traditional townhouse setting of table and tatami seating with a quietly modern touch, with jazz playing softly in the dining room.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on craftsmanship and quality ingredients, pairing carefully made soba with sake and shochu for an unhurried soba-shop drink before the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba made from 100% domestic buckwheat flour and local spring water. The house standard is a refined nihachi (two-eight) soba described as pale, thin, and crisply refreshing, served with a Kanto-style broth; a pure 100% buckwheat (jūwari) version is available for a small supplement. Signature plates include soba topped with soft-boiled egg and tempura bits, grated-yam tororo soba, and soba set meals, and diners are invited to taste the noodles with a pinch of salt.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits right in front of the Naramachi Center and plays jazz in the background, mixing table and tatami seating for a calm, refined atmosphere.
Noodles are made from 100% domestic buckwheat flour and natural spring water, with a pure 100% buckwheat option offered alongside the standard two-eight blend.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares both 100% buckwheat soba and wheat-containing two-eight (nihachi) soba plus tempura, so even the jūwari soba is highly likely to share equipment and water with wheat products.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a Kanto-style broth/dipping sauce based on soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-dashi based and the menu includes egg and tempura dishes; no vegan-dedicated preparation is documented.

positives.text: Diners may eat the soba with a pinch of salt instead of sauce, which avoids the wheat-containing broth (though the noodles themselves are not certified gluten-free).

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood soba shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff likely speak limited English.

places.name: クルル (KURURU)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen states it uses no wheat in its dishes and describes itself as "添加物・グルテン不使用" (additive- and gluten-free). The staple is organic sprouted brown rice, which can also be eaten as house-made gluten-free brown rice noodles (玄米麺), served cold in spring/summer. This is a genuinely wheat-avoiding, grain-conscious kitchen, so it is strong for gluten-free diners. However, it is not explicitly described as a certified celiac-safe facility and no dedicated-equipment / cross-contamination protocol is documented, so a strict celiac should still confirm by phone (Japanese) before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe's menus are made without fish, meat, dairy, or wheat, with carefully selected grains and vegetables and minimal additives/pesticides — explicitly described as suitable for people practicing veganism. The signature tofu hamburger plate (豆腐ハンバーグプレート) comes with pre-meal shiso tea, the tofu patty with carrot puree, small side dishes, salad, sprouted brown rice, and miso soup, all plant-based. This is a genuinely vegan kitchen, not an omnivore place with a few options.
places.hours_raw: 食べログ表記: 09:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00)。公式サイト受付時間: 10:00-18:00。営業時間は時期・予約状況により変動。
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅・東向商店街エリア）
places.cuisine: オーガニック・ヴィーガン／フードセラピーカフェ
places.notes: KURURU is a natural-food cafe tucked on the second floor of the Inoue Building along the Higashimuki shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station, open since the mid-2000s. The owner developed the menu through independent research into therapeutic, plant-based eating, and the cafe

chef_bio.background: KURURU is a natural-food cafe tucked on the second floor of the Inoue Building along the Higashimuki shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station, open since the mid-2000s. The owner developed the menu through independent research into therapeutic, plant-based eating, and the cafe bills itself as a food-therapy specialty house — the idea of 'eating fasting' (食べるファスティング), where simple, clean dishes nourish and gently cleanse at the same time.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking is kept simple to draw out the natural umami of the ingredients, prioritizing Nara-grown produce and carefully chosen seasonings while avoiding fish, meat, dairy, wheat, and unnecessary additives or pesticides. The owner frames mindful, lighter eating as a small contribution toward the SDGs.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the tofu hamburger plate (豆腐ハンバーグプレート): a tofu patty with carrot puree served with pre-meal shiso tea, three small side dishes, salad, sprouted brown rice, and miso soup. The kitchen also makes its own gluten-free organic brown-rice noodles (玄米麺), served cold in the warmer months, plus handmade sweets like banana cake.

anecdotes.text: The cafe shares its space with a wellness salon floor offering aromatherapy, herbs, footbaths, and yoga, reflecting its whole-body 'total therapy' approach to food and health.
Its house-made gluten-free brown-rice noodles are popular enough to be sold for delivery beyond the cafe itself.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner has independently researched and developed vegan, vegetarian, and allergen-conscious dishes, indicating awareness of dietary needs (no documented formal protocol).

positives.text: All menus are prepared without fish, meat, dairy, or wheat, using carefully selected grains and vegetables with minimal additives and pesticides.
The kitchen describes its food as additive- and gluten-free (添加物・グルテン不使用), built around organic sprouted brown rice and house-made brown-rice noodles.
The signature tofu hamburger plate is explicitly noted as suitable for people practicing veganism.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, owner-run natural-food cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the popular tofu hamburger plate will get you far, though menu explanations are mostly in Japanese so a translation app helps.

places.name: 江戸川 ならまち店 (Edogawa Naramachi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature kabayaki tare (sauce) used on every eel dish is a soy-sauce/mirin-based glaze, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; rice bowls are served with this sauce. Side dishes and set components also use soy-based seasonings and dashi. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a specialist eel restaurant. The core of the menu is unagi (eel) served as unaju/unadon and hitsumabushi, all built on a fish-based kabayaki tare and rice. Set meals and side dishes rely on fish dashi and animal proteins. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and nothing here is suitable for a strict plant-based diet.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-21:00. Closed the 3rd Thursday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Shimomikado-cho), within walking distance of Kint
places.notes: The restaurant occupies a roughly 150-year-old former merchant house in the heart of Naramachi. Built in the early Meiji era and later home to the Miyajima family, it operated for generations as a kimono and textile shop—its latticed itoya-goshi facade still signals that fiber-tr

chef_bio.background: The restaurant occupies a roughly 150-year-old former merchant house in the heart of Naramachi. Built in the early Meiji era and later home to the Miyajima family, it operated for generations as a kimono and textile shop—its latticed itoya-goshi facade still signals that fiber-trade past. After the shop closed in the 1980s, the building was carefully converted into a dining house, keeping original details such as a wooden box-staircase (hakodansu) that is still in use, along with sunken-hearth seating and restored garden views.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows the old eel-cook's creed, 'three years to skewer, eight years to fillet, a lifetime to grill,' and continually replenishes a founding tare said to carry decades of accumulated flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Tokyo (Kanto)-style eel: the fish is split along the back, steamed, then grilled so it turns soft, fluffy and lighter in fat. Signature dishes include unaju/unadon over rice with a long-aged kabayaki sauce, and hitsumabushi served so you can enjoy it three ways. Local Yamato ingredients also appear in set meals.

anecdotes.text: The converted warehouse next door, called Kura-no-ma, looks out over a restored garden and is used as a quiet space for private gatherings.
The Edo-yaki method—back-splitting, an initial grill, steaming to render out fat, then a final grill—is what gives the eel its tender, fluffy texture.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The eel is finished with a soy-sauce/mirin-based kabayaki tare brushed onto every dish; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the glaze is a gluten source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on eel and fish-based sauce and dashi throughout; no plant-based preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished but welcoming eel restaurant where a foreign student can order comfortably by pointing at picture menus and saying a few words of Japanese; English support is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps.

places.name: Cafe 春 (Cafe Haru)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu (baked curry with curry roux, pasta, gratin/doria, cream croquettes, breaded cutlets, dessert plates) is wheat-heavy, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature dishes center on shrimp, beef/pork hamburger, roast beef, chicken nanban, and cheese; the tomato cream pasta sauce reportedly uses sakura-shrimp paste, bonito, and kelp. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:30 (L.O.); some sources list 11:00-15:00 or until ingredients sell out
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (奈良町), Nara City — within walking distance of Kint
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe Haru is the third venture of the well-loved Western-style restaurant Yoshoku Haru, set inside a renovated traditional Naramachi townhouse with red lattice work and vintage furnishings that echo the area's old pleasure-district character. It opened in October 2017, a short tw

chef_bio.background: Cafe Haru is the third venture of the well-loved Western-style restaurant Yoshoku Haru, set inside a renovated traditional Naramachi townhouse with red lattice work and vintage furnishings that echo the area's old pleasure-district character. It opened in October 2017, a short two-to-three-minute walk from its perpetually busy original location, offering a calmer place to sit during a stroll through the historic district.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing a hometown specialty to Nara: the owner, who is from Kitakyushu where baked curry is a local signature, recreates that dish in a comfortable townhouse setting with generous, good-value portions.
chef_bio.specialty: Baked curry (yaki curry) — especially the shrimp baked curry, served sizzling in a hot iron pot with crispy rice, beef curry, melted mozzarella, a soft egg, and grilled prawns; also tomato cream pasta with tiger prawns, stewed Yamato-beef-and-pork hamburger, chicken nanban, and dessert plates.

anecdotes.text: The owner hails from Kitakyushu, where baked curry is one of the region's signature dishes, and brought that specialty to his Naramachi cafe.
Positioned as a less-crowded alternative just a couple of minutes from the always-busy original Yoshoku Haru, while sharing the same culinary identity.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tomato cream pasta sauce is reported to combine sakura-shrimp paste, bonito, and kelp, and signature dishes feature shrimp, beef/pork, and cheese, so animal products are central to the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local townhouse cafe where a foreign student can comfortably point at the signature baked curry on a picture menu, though staff communication is mainly in Japanese.

places.name: 茶粥茶論 月日星 (Chagayu Salon Tsukihiboshi)
places.gf_detail: The core meal is rice-based: freshly cooked chagayu (rice simmered in tea), white rice, and a buffet of 20+ pickled Yamato vegetables, which is naturally a more rice-forward, low-wheat spread than most Japanese restaurants. However there is no gluten-free program, no labeling, and no dedicated handling. Many Japanese pickles are seasoned with soy sauce (which usually contains wheat), and the miso soup most likely uses standard miso/dashi. A celiac visitor cannot assume any specific dish is safe and would need to ask, in Japanese, which pickles avoid soy sauce/wheat. Treat as not verified celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The format is vegetable-forward: rice, chagayu and 20+ pickled seasonal Yamato vegetables, so a largely plant-based plate is achievable. But the restaurant makes no vegan claim. Some pickles may be cured with bonito (katsuo) or other fish-derived seasonings, and the miso soup most likely uses fish dashi, so it is not a verified vegan venue. A vegan can probably build a satisfying plate from the pickle and rice buffet but should ask which pickles are free of fish/bonito and skip the miso soup.
places.hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（定休日 不定休）— Tabelog lists kitchen/serving to 15:30
places.neighborhood: Near Nara Park, by the Nara National Museum / Himuro Shrine
places.notes: A small, independent cafe-restaurant set right by Nara Park, between the Nara National Museum and Himuro Shrine on Noborioji-dori, a roughly 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Todai-ji. It celebrates one of Nara's oldest everyday foods, chagayu (rice slowly simmered

chef_bio.background: A small, independent cafe-restaurant set right by Nara Park, between the Nara National Museum and Himuro Shrine on Noborioji-dori, a roughly 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Todai-ji. It celebrates one of Nara's oldest everyday foods, chagayu (rice slowly simmered in tea), pairing it with the region's deep pickling tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: The place leans into Nara's identity as a city of pickles and tea porridge, letting guests graze slowly through many small vegetable pickles rather than ordering a single fixed plate.
chef_bio.specialty: An all-you-can-eat spread built around freshly cooked chagayu and Nara-grown rice, served with a buffet of more than 20 carefully chosen pickles made from seasonal Yamato (Nara) vegetables, plus miso soup, roasted tea and house-made monaka, all refillable.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is a bright, open space hung with photographs of Nara's temples, shrines and Buddha statues, and there is a garden terrace where you can eat in the green shade of trees said to be several hundred years old; it is also pet-friendly.

Local Nara guide Narakko describes it as a spot to enjoy Nara's local chagayu with a pickle buffet of 20-plus seasonal prefectural-vegetable pickles, freshly cooked chagayu and local rice, and refillable house-made sweets.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The meal centers on many small vegetable pickles; Japanese pickles are commonly seasoned with soy sauce, which usually contains wheat, and the menu provides no wheat/gluten labeling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some Nara-style pickles can be cured with bonito or other fish-derived seasonings, and the accompanying miso soup most likely uses fish dashi, so a strict vegan should confirm which pickles are fish-free and skip the soup.

positives.text: The format is buffet-style with rice, tea porridge and 20-plus vegetable pickles you serve yourself, so it is easy to build a vegetable-and-rice plate and to see what you are taking.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed buffet-style spot where you can serve yourself rice, chagayu and pickles, so a foreign student gets by fine with a friendly konnichiwa and gestures, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese and it is cash only.

places.name: 奈良町 あしびの郷 (Naramachi Ashibi-no-Sato)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature udon is made with premium wheat flour, the set meals include grilled fish marinated in miso, and dishes rely on fish dashi and Nara-pickle seasonings that typically include wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. Set meals center on Nara/Yamato pickled vegetables but are served with grilled fish and miso soup, and broths use fish dashi. While the pickle assortment itself is plant-based, the meals as composed contain fish and dashi; there is no documented vegan set, so a vegan would need to ask and likely receive only the vegetable/pickle portions.
places.hours_raw: Lunch/café reception 11:30-16:30 (lunch service typically ends around 15:00 or when sold out); dining venue open to 22:00-23:00, reservations required after 17:00. 不定休 (irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
places.cuisine: Japanese set-meal restaurant and café run by
places.notes: Ashibi-no-Sato is the dining and retail home of Ashibiya Honpo, a Nara-pickle (narazuke) maker whose family roots in the craft reach back to the Edo period. The business resumed and was formally re-established after the Second World War, became a company in 1970, and in March 199

chef_bio.background: Ashibi-no-Sato is the dining and retail home of Ashibiya Honpo, a Nara-pickle (narazuke) maker whose family roots in the craft reach back to the Edo period. The business resumed and was formally re-established after the Second World War, became a company in 1970, and in March 1996 opened this venue on the site of its old number-one pickling storehouse in the heart of Naramachi, the historic merchant district that survived wartime bombing. A small chapel was later added in 2012 for concerts, parties, and weddings.
chef_bio.philosophy: The place describes itself as 'a space of constant incompletion where one can feel green, light, water, and wind,' set within the preserved townscape of old Nara. Its name comes from the ashibi (Japanese andromeda) flower native to the region.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature おつけもの御膳 (pickle set) showcases the house narazuke and Yamato pickled vegetables alongside Nara-grown rice, miso soup, and miso-marinated grilled fish; another house specialty is hand-pulled udon made with premium flour and rare Yoshino kuzu, plus Yamato-tea sweets. The shop is also known for unusual pickles found nowhere else, including a summer-only fresh-pickled gourd and a patented odorless garlic narazuke called 'Ninniku Shichisen.'

anecdotes.text: The dining house stands on the former site of Ashibiya Honpo's number-one pickling storehouse, repurposed in 1996 into a machiya-style space for meals, retail, and events.
Among its exclusive products is a patented garlic narazuke, 'Ninniku Shichisen,' said to retain garlic's nutrients while being completely free of the usual pungent smell.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen serves wheat-flour udon and miso-marinated fish in the same set meals, so cross-contact with wheat is likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals rely on miso and pickle seasonings; narazuke and Japanese miso/soy seasonings typically contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pickle and vegetable dishes are plated alongside grilled fish and fish-dashi soups in the standard sets, so vegan diners cannot assume separation.

positives.text: The menu is built around pickled Yamato vegetables and Nara-grown rice, so vegetable-forward, lighter options are available even if not certified vegan or gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Nara pickle house with a calm machiya-style dining room; menus and service are mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student order comfortably.

places.name: ヒサゴ屋食堂 (Hisagoya Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: A traditional Japanese diner (taishu shokudo) whose menu is built around wheat- and dashi-heavy dishes: udon, sara-udon, katsudon, oyakodon, and tonkatsu. Soups and broths use fish dashi and Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), and fried items share fryers. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore comfort-food diner centered on meat, egg, and pork dishes (gyusui/torisui meat-and-broth, katsudon, oyakodon, niku-udon). Broths rely on fish/meat dashi. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes, and no information that broths can be made without animal products. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 火・水・木・土・日：11:00-15:00（食材が無くなり次第終了）／月・金：定休日
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hisagoya Shokudo is a long-running neighborhood diner founded in 1950 (Showa 25), tucked into Nara's historic Naramachi district on the slope of the old approach toward Gangoji and Kofukuji temples. Once tied to a soy-sauce shop, it has carried on for generations as a classic tai

chef_bio.background: Hisagoya Shokudo is a long-running neighborhood diner founded in 1950 (Showa 25), tucked into Nara's historic Naramachi district on the slope of the old approach toward Gangoji and Kofukuji temples. Once tied to a soy-sauce shop, it has carried on for generations as a classic taishu shokudo — a humble, everyone-welcome eatery — and was refreshed with a clean, tidy interior in recent years while keeping its old-town warmth.
chef_bio.philosophy: From its earliest days the kitchen has prized good water, drawing on Nara's tasty local groundwater for both cooking and drinking — a quiet point of pride for the family table it sets.
chef_bio.specialty: The house is known for its regional comfort dishes: gyusui and torisui (savory beef and chicken broth bowls), niku-udon, katsudon, oyakodon, sara-udon, and Kansai specialties like kitsune-don and konoha-don. Bowls come hearty and homey, with the cutlet and egg dishes praised by visitors.

anecdotes.text: A diner who visited called it the very essence of a Japanese food hall, noting Kansai dishes like kitsune-don and konoha-don that surprise visitors from Tokyo, set in a building with a genuine old-city feel near Kofukuji.
Local listings highlight charming touches of an old-school diner, including the menu breadth and even Oronamin C among the drinks, alongside an English menu that draws foreign guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat noodles (udon, sara-udon) and breaded/fried tonkatsu and katsudon, so shared cooking surfaces and fryers make cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth and donburi dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, and rely on fish dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built around beef, chicken, pork, and egg with fish/meat broths; no plant-only options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the casual diner sees many foreign visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably without much Japanese.

places.name: ブタとエスプレッソと (Buta to Espresso to)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac diners. The menu centers on wheat-breaded tonkatsu (deep-fried pork cutlet), katsu curry and katsudon, and the curry roux is built on a long-simmered base; flour-thickened roux and breaded fried items make heavy gluten exposure and shared cooking surfaces near-certain. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is a pork specialist. The signature curry is built around roughly 250–280g of sous-vide braised pork belly, and the curry sauce itself is simmered with chicken stock (鶏ガラ) alongside fruits and vegetables, so even the sauce is not vegetarian. Sides include miso soup (typically fish dashi). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small counter eatery tucked into the Palace Building on the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop has moved location more than once, and the owner has spoken of wanting to settle and stay put here for the long haul. It is famously

chef_bio.background: A small counter eatery tucked into the Palace Building on the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop has moved location more than once, and the owner has spoken of wanting to settle and stay put here for the long haul. It is famously hard to find: the way in runs through the neighboring ramen shop, so you walk through and head to the counter at the back.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, generous, pork-and-spice-obsessed kitchen — big portions, careful low-temperature cooking of the pork, and a curry built more on depth than on heat.
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship is the Super Pork Curry (超ブタカレー): a single massive slab of sous-vide braised pork belly — around 250 to 280g of Kazakaze sangen pork — laid over a generous bowl of rice, so tender it falls apart with a spoon. The curry sauce is its own quiet showpiece, slow-simmered with coffee and cacao, chicken stock, and a large amount of fruit and vegetables for a deep, fruity, only-mildly-spicy flavor. The shop also serves tonkatsu, katsu curry, katsudon (including a sauce katsudon made with hard-to-find Hermes sauce) and hamburg steak, with set meals offering free rice and curry refills.

anecdotes.text: The Super Pork Curry built a following beyond Nara: it reportedly sold around 1,500 portions over two days when the shop took it to the Fuji Rock Festival in 2022.
Getting in is part of the legend — the entrance is so hidden that you have to walk through the ramen shop next door to reach the counter seating at the back.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen deep-fries wheat-breaded pork cutlets (tonkatsu, katsu curry, katsudon) in a small space, making cross-contact with gluten very likely; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry sauce itself is simmered with chicken stock (鶏ガラ) and the menu is entirely pork-centered, so there is no vegan-safe dish to begin with.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter shop where a few words of Japanese and patience help, especially since the entrance is famously hard to find — you reach it by walking through the neighboring ramen shop to the seating at the back.

places.name: 吉川亭 (Yoshikawa-tei)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic yōshoku (Japanese-Western) diner whose core dishes are inherently wheat-heavy and not celiac-safe. The signature items — hamburg steak and cutlets — are bound and breaded with wheat, and the demiglace and white (gratin) sauces are thickened with wheat flour (roux). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no allergen information, and no indication of a dedicated fryer or any cross-contamination control. Not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-centered Western diner: the menu is built around hamburg steak, beef cutlet (bifukatsu), chicken cheese cutlet, beef stew, and cheese gratin. Sauces are meat-based demiglace and dairy white sauce. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option, and the small one-person kitchen offers no indication of plant-based accommodation.
places.hours_raw: 火～日 11:30-14:00（ランチ中心）。月曜定休。出典により夜営業（〜20:00）の記載もあり、夜の営業有無は要確認。
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（花芝町・花芝商店街）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yoshikawa-tei is a long-standing family-run Western diner founded in 1981 in Nara's Hanashiba neighborhood, a short walk north of the city center through the covered shopping arcades. It is run single-handedly by its now-elderly owner-chef, who trained in his younger years at Kik

chef_bio.background: Yoshikawa-tei is a long-standing family-run Western diner founded in 1981 in Nara's Hanashiba neighborhood, a short walk north of the city center through the covered shopping arcades. It is run single-handedly by its now-elderly owner-chef, who trained in his younger years at Kikusuirou (菊水楼), one of Nara's most venerable traditional restaurants, before opening his own intimate yōshoku kitchen.
chef_bio.specialty: Old-school yōshoku: a thick-cut chicken thigh dressed in a thin batter with melting cheese and finished in demiglace (chicken cheese cutlet), alongside hamburg steak, beef cutlet (bifukatsu) under generous demiglace, beef stew, and a rich, piping-hot cheese gratin.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained at the historic Nara restaurant Kikusuirou before opening Yoshikawa-tei in 1981, and has kept the small Showa-era dining room going for more than four decades.
Regulars affectionately describe the owner as gruff and blunt in appearance, but find the warmth of this tiny, nostalgic room endearing rather than off-putting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded cutlets and roux-thickened demiglace/white sauces, so wheat is pervasive across the menu and shared cooking surfaces; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat- and dairy-centered kitchen (hamburg, cutlets, beef stew, cheese gratin) run by a single chef; no plant-based preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only neighborhood diner run solo by an elderly chef with a gruff but warm manner — go relaxed, expect little to no English, and a few words of Japanese will go a long way.

places.name: ごはん 芽や (Gohan Meya)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on Japanese set meals using soy sauce and dashi, with dishes like croquettes and hamburgers that contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac-safe handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward seasonal cooking (one course features around 10 seasonal vegetable dishes), but the kitchen uses fish dashi/stock and the menu includes meat and fish mains, so it is not vegan by default. The owner asks that you communicate dietary needs in advance by email; whether a fully vegan meal is possible would need to be confirmed directly.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 14:30); lunch-focused. Tabelog lists 11:00 open, L.O. 13:00, close 13:30. Closed Mondays and irregular days; hours announced on the official blog/Instagram.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（東笹鉾町・東大寺近く）
places.cuisine: 定食・家庭料理の食堂
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ごはん 芽や is a very small, owner-run lunch eatery tucked into the quiet Higashisasahoko-cho area near Todai-ji in central Nara, behind an unassuming storefront with a large glass window.

chef_bio.background: ごはん 芽や is a very small, owner-run lunch eatery tucked into the quiet Higashisasahoko-cho area near Todai-ji in central Nara, behind an unassuming storefront with a large glass window.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on careful, handmade, heartfelt home cooking centered on seasonal Yamato/Nara vegetables, served in a deliberately intimate setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade seasonal set lunches (teishoku) built around vegetables of the season, served as a two-course choice with two main dishes; recent examples include cod-roe and potato croquettes, chicken-burdock hamburger, and chicken roll in tomato cream sauce.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps a deliberately personal, small-scale operation: reservations are requested by email the day before, parties are limited to three people, children under ten are not admitted, and customers are asked to mention any dietary concerns ahead of time.
Daily set lunches rotate with the seasons, with two mains offered at a fixed lunch price and small extra portions of rice available on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Japanese set-meal kitchen using soy sauce, dashi, and wheat-containing dishes (croquettes, hamburgers); no gluten-free protocol documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce are used; soy sauce typically contains wheat and no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi/stock is used in cooking and meat/fish mains are on the menu, so vegan and vegetarian dishes share a non-vegan kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner asks customers to communicate dietary concerns and allergies in advance when reserving by email.

positives.text: Cooking is centered on seasonal vegetables, with a course featuring around ten seasonal vegetable dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, owner-run lunch spot with strict policies (email reservation in advance, groups of three or fewer, no children under 10, cash only), so some conversational Japanese and advance planning will make your visit much smoother.

places.name: CHEESE CAFE soan（ソアン） (CHEESE CAFE soan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. The kitchen is cheese-and-baking focused: the lunch features quiche (wheat pastry), and the signature items are baked cheesecakes, so wheat is central to the menu. There is no evidence of gluten-free preparation, and the place is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: This is a cheese-specialty café; dairy is the core of nearly every dish (cheese boards, cheesecakes, cheese-laden lunch). No vegan menu is documented. The lunch does include organic/pickled Nara vegetables as components, but the menu is built around dairy and is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–20:00. Wednesday is takeout only. Closed Thursdays and alternate Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅）
places.cuisine: チーズ料理カフェ／国産ナチュラルチーズ・手作りランチ・チーズケーキ
places.notes: The owner, Miho, is a certified Cheese Professional who moved from Tokyo to open a café devoted to Japanese natural cheese. The shop first operated inside the Mochiidono 'Dream CUBE' challenge-shop space, then relocated and reopened a short walk away on もちいどのセンター街 in September 20

chef_bio.origin: Relocated from Tokyo to Nara, a city she grew to love during her university years.
chef_bio.background: The owner, Miho, is a certified Cheese Professional who moved from Tokyo to open a café devoted to Japanese natural cheese. The shop first operated inside the Mochiidono 'Dream CUBE' challenge-shop space, then relocated and reopened a short walk away on もちいどのセンター街 in September 2024.
chef_bio.philosophy: Miho is fascinated by Japan's natural cheese world and the individual personalities of the makers she sources from. She is drawn to Nara as the historic home of 'so' (蘇), an ancient form of cheese, and designs her layered cheesecakes around the idea of two distinct characters combining into one harmony.
chef_bio.specialty: Curated boards of domestic natural cheese from small Japanese makers (selections from prefectures such as Saga, Shiga, Saitama and beyond), handmade lunches built around quiche, soup and organic Nara vegetables, and Miho's signature two-layered cheesecakes in rotating seasonal flavors like pear-caramel and purple-sweet-potato-parmigiano.

anecdotes.text: Miho was drawn to Nara partly because of its connection to 蘇 (so), an ancient Japanese cheese, and she frames her shop as celebrating individual small-batch Japanese cheesemakers whose lifestyles she deeply admires.
The café relocated about 50 meters from its original Dream CUBE space to a renovated storefront on もちいどのセンター街, and has expanded its lineup to include tea, jam and cup gelato.

positives.text: Lunch plates include organic Nara vegetables prepared as pickles/sides, so vegetable components are part of the menu (though dishes are still dairy-centric).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, friendly owner-run cheese café where a warm greeting in Japanese goes a long way; pointing at the day's cheese board or cheesecake works fine even with limited Japanese.

places.name: イタリア食堂 パインストア (Italia Shokudo Pine Store)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no GF program is documented. This is an Italian-rooted kitchen using Japanese seasonings (miso, pickles, likely soy/fermented condiments) plus smoked and fermented preparations, so cross-contact and hidden wheat are realistic. Celiacs should not assume any dish is safe; you would need to ask directly, and confirmation cannot be relied upon here.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is meat- and fish-forward — signature plates include smoked salted mackerel, local pork ham, and cheese-based items. There are vegetable dishes featuring Yamato vegetables, but at least one mushroom dish uses a fermented mushroom dashi and others likely rely on animal-derived seasonings, so vegetable plates are not reliably vegan. No vegan offering is documented; ask in advance about a plant-based plate.
places.neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約3〜5分）
places.cuisine: Creative Italian rooted in Nara, with fermen
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef trained in Italian cooking and spent time working in both Italian and Vietnamese kitchens before opening this small counter restaurant in Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade. That mixed lineage shows up in the cooking, which takes an Italian foundation and reinterprets it

chef_bio.background: The chef trained in Italian cooking and spent time working in both Italian and Vietnamese kitchens before opening this small counter restaurant in Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade. That mixed lineage shows up in the cooking, which takes an Italian foundation and reinterprets it through Nara's pantry.
chef_bio.philosophy: To build on an Italian base while making the most of Nara's own produce and Japanese seasonings, aiming for a flavor that feels somehow nostalgic. As the kitchen puts it, Italian technique plus Nara ingredients and Japanese condiments creates 'a taste that feels familiar.'
chef_bio.specialty: Fermentation- and smoke-driven small plates built on local ingredients. House favorites include an 'addictive' smoked salted mackerel (癖になる焼き塩鯖), ham made from locally raised Sato/Go pork (郷ポークのももハム), a mushroom stir-fry finished with a fermented mushroom dashi (十津川きのこ炒め), and a playful house take on smoked daikon pickle called 偽いぶりがっこ (a 'fake' iburigakko). Lunch has featured a Hainanese-style chicken rice.

anecdotes.text: The chef calls the cooking 'fermentation and smoking basin cuisine' (発酵と燻製の盆地料理), a nod to Nara's landlocked basin and to the smoked, fermented small plates that define the menu.
The room is deliberately tiny and personal — six counter seats and a single four-top across two floors, with carefully chosen dishware and quirky decorative touches.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork ham, smoked mackerel, and cheese, and even a vegetable dish (mushroom stir-fry) uses a fermented mushroom dashi, so vegetable plates are not reliably plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, chef-run counter spot where ordering and dietary questions will go most smoothly in basic Japanese, so come prepared or with a translation app.

places.name: チャイナダイニング飛天 (China Dining Hiten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. This is a Chinese restaurant where dim sum wrappers, dumplings, and noodle dishes (including the vegetable tanmen) are wheat-based, and most savory dishes use soy sauce, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or cross-contamination control, so a celiac would need to ask carefully in advance and should treat the kitchen as not celiac-safe. The vegan dishes are not automatically gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Genuinely vegan-friendly for a mainstream Chinese restaurant: there is a documented dedicated vegan section and a 7-course vegan tasting course (around 3,300 yen). Individual plant-based dishes include a vegetable salad with house carrot dressing, a vegetable tanmen in a vegetable-consommé (not fish-dashi) broth, a greens/mushroom/vermicelli soup, soy-meat with cashews, soy-meat black-vinegar "sweet and sour pork," and a greens-and-yuba stir-fry. The restaurant frames these around the Chinese 素菜 (Buddhist vegetable cookery) tradition and an 医食同源 ("food and medicine from the same source") concept. Because the bulk of the menu is meat- and seafood-based and standard Chinese seasonings can contain animal-derived stock or oyster/fish sauce, a strict vegan should confirm each non-course dish and request no animal stock; the labeled vegan course is the safest choice.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（東向商店街）
places.cuisine: 中華料理・点心・飲茶
places.notes: A long-established Chinese restaurant tucked along the Higashimuki covered shopping arcade a few minutes' walk from the heart of central Nara. It sits a notch above the typical neighborhood Chinese place: the dining room is styled like a Chinese restaurant in an Asian resort, wit

chef_bio.origin: Nara, Japan (Higashimuki arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: A long-established Chinese restaurant tucked along the Higashimuki covered shopping arcade a few minutes' walk from the heart of central Nara. It sits a notch above the typical neighborhood Chinese place: the dining room is styled like a Chinese restaurant in an Asian resort, with interior furnishings imported from Bali, and it runs sister restaurants elsewhere in the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on the Chinese idea of 医食同源 — that food and medicine share the same source — and on the temple-rooted 素菜 (vegetable cookery) tradition, with the aim that eating things that are both delicious and good for the body supports good health.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made dim sum and dumplings, oversized deep-fried sesame balls with a chewy mochi center, a black-vinegar 'sweet and sour pork' millefeuille made with local Yamato pork wrapped around mountain yam, and house-made almond tofu for dessert. A standout for travelers is the dedicated vegan course built on soy-meat, yuba, seasonal greens and mushrooms.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant explicitly draws on China's 素菜 Buddhist-vegetarian cooking heritage and an 医食同源 concept, presenting a full vegan course alongside its meat menu.
The dining room is themed as a Chinese restaurant in an Asian resort, decorated with interior pieces imported directly from Bali.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a dim sum and noodle-focused Chinese kitchen where wheat wrappers, dumplings and wheat noodles are central; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Many savory dishes are seasoned in the Chinese style with soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so soy-sauce-based dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan dishes (soy-meat, yuba, greens) are prepared in a kitchen that primarily cooks meat and seafood; no separate-equipment or cross-contact policy is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegewel lists the restaurant as having both vegan and vegetarian menus and English-speaking staff plus English menus, which helps when discussing dietary needs, but no specific allergy-handling procedure is described.

positives.text: A clearly labeled dedicated vegan section and a 7-course vegan tasting menu give plant-based diners reliable, pre-vetted choices rather than ad-hoc substitutions.
The vegetable tanmen and the greens/mushroom/vermicelli soup are built on a vegetable consommé rather than fish dashi, making them suitable for vegans.

cultural_comfort.note: English-speaking staff and English menus are available, and there is a clearly labeled vegan section, so a foreign student can order plant-based dishes here with relative ease.

noodles.notes: Medium, fairly straight Chinese-style wheat noodles that grip the thick sesame-chili broth; exact gauge and hydration not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: 自家製 胡麻たっぷりタンタンメン (Homemade Sesame-Rich Sichuan Tantanmen) — a Sichuan-style ma-la tantanmen built on a generous sesame-paste base over a savory broth, finished with chili oil and sansho; heat level can be adjusted.

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a sesame-and-soy seasoned Sichuan broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented, so this is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The kitchen advertises vegetarian/vegan menu options, but no specific plant-based (no-meat, no-fish) version of the tantanmen is documented — the standard bowl carries minced pork and likely a meat-based broth, so confirm a true vegan noodle dish in advance.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen-shop ramen but the Sichuan original from which Japanese tantanmen descends: a thick, nutty sesame-paste broth lit up by chili oil and tongue-buzzing sansho, served at a long-running Nara Chinese kitchen. You taste the Chengdu lineage in the soup — sesame richness and ma-la numbness rather than the soy-and-dashi soul of a Japanese noodle bar.

places.highlight: This is not a ramen-shop ramen but the Sichuan original from which Japanese tantanmen descends: a thick, nutty sesame-paste broth lit up by chili oil and tongue-buzzing sansho, served at a long-running Nara Chinese kitchen. You taste the Chengdu lineage in the soup — sesame richness and ma-la numbness rather than the soy-and-dashi soul of a Japanese noodle bar.

places.name: 寧奈 (Sourdough Cafe NEINA)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire concept is naturally-leavened sourdough and focaccia made from wheat and rye flour, so bread is the core of nearly everything served. There is no indication of gluten-free bread or menu items, and the shared bakery environment makes this unsuitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Mostly not vegan. The signature sandwiches feature meat (grilled chicken, ham), dairy (Havarti, ricotta cheese), and the breads/brownies are unlikely to be vegan. A vegan diner might manage a plain bread item, the craft cola, or the lemonade, but there is no documented vegan menu. Ask in advance (the owner speaks fluent English).
places.hours_raw: 10:00-16:00 (cafe); bread/goods sold until 16:00 or until sold out. Some listings note 8:00/8:30 openings for retail bread. Closed Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai arcade (もちいどの夢CUBE), near Kintetsu Nar
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] NEINA is a small, owner-run cafe tucked into the innermost unit of the Yume CUBE artisan incubator in Nara's historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It opened in autumn 2025. The proprietor is a fluent English speaker with a foreign backgroun

chef_bio.background: NEINA is a small, owner-run cafe tucked into the innermost unit of the Yume CUBE artisan incubator in Nara's historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It opened in autumn 2025. The proprietor is a fluent English speaker with a foreign background, and the shop quickly became a favorite of international visitors as well as locals. The interior is finished in warm Yoshino cypress, giving the six-seat counter a calm, unhurried feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: Great bread starts with time, patience, and quality ingredients. NEINA uses a centuries-old natural fermentation technique and invites guests to slow down, savor the moment, and enjoy the simple pleasures of artisan bread.
chef_bio.specialty: Naturally-leavened sourdough breads and focaccia, baked in-house in a large oven from wheat and rye flour fermented with wild yeast and lactic-acid cultures. About three kinds of bread are offered each day, turned into sandwiches such as a focaccia sandwich with ham, Havarti and roasted vegetables with pesto, a grilled chicken sandwich, and a fruit sandwich with lemon syrup, strawberries, apple and ricotta. These are paired with locally-made craft colas and craft beers.

anecdotes.text: The cafe leans into letting the ingredients speak for themselves, with hard-style breads served simply with olive oil and sea salt alongside the sandwiches.
Set in the back of the Yume CUBE artisan units in the Mochiidono arcade, the shop's English-speaking owner has made it a draw for overseas visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working sourdough bakery; wheat and rye flour are central to the entire operation, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and sandwiches share the same kitchen and counter as dairy and meat fillings; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner speaks fluent English, so allergy or ingredient questions can be discussed directly, though no formal allergen handling is documented.

positives.text: Bread is made in-house from wild-yeast and lactic-acid natural fermentation with carefully selected ingredients, and the English-speaking owner can answer ingredient questions.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner is fluent in English and the cafe is popular with international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English.

places.name: 御菓子処 優月 (Okashidokoro Yuzuki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional wagashi shop whose signature items — dorayaki-style "Saho no Kawase," chestnut pastries, manju, and baked confections — are made with wheat flour, and there are no gluten-free offerings or accommodations. Even the rice-based items (mochi, yokan, ohagi) are produced in a shared workshop with wheat-flour goods. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Some items are plant-based by tradition (mochi, yokan, ohagi, and azuki/mugwort sweets typically use only rice, beans, and sugar), but baked goods like dorayaki, castella-style items, and manju commonly contain egg and dairy. There are no labeled vegan products and the shop is not a vegan destination, so a vegan would need to ask item by item.
places.hours_raw: 9:00～18:30 (official site); Tabelog lists 09:00–18:00 daily. Closed Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono Center Street (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yuzuki is a small, owner-run wagashi shop tucked into the middle of Nara's Mochiidono arcade. The proprietor trained for thirteen years in Takatsuki before opening this shop, and continues to make everything by hand in a workshop at the back of the store so the sweets are always

chef_bio.origin: Nara, on the Mochiidono covered shopping arcade that leads toward the historic Naramachi district
chef_bio.background: Yuzuki is a small, owner-run wagashi shop tucked into the middle of Nara's Mochiidono arcade. The proprietor trained for thirteen years in Takatsuki before opening this shop, and continues to make everything by hand in a workshop at the back of the store so the sweets are always fresh.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated approach is to carefully select ingredients, let each material's natural quality shine, and make its sweets by long-standing traditional methods.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature confection is 'Saho no Kawase,' a dorayaki-style sweet of yokan wrapped in a fragrant baked crust, named after a poem in the Man'yoshu. Other favorites include wakakusa-mochi (mugwort mochi heaped with bean paste) and kuri-i chestnut pastries, alongside seasonal sweets such as sakura-mochi in spring, kashiwa-mochi and warabi-mochi in summer, and freshly pounded mochi at New Year.

anecdotes.text: The owner spent thirteen years training in Takatsuki before setting up this shop on the Mochiidono arcade.
The flagship sweet 'Saho no Kawase' takes its name from a poem in the Man'yoshu, Japan's oldest poetry anthology.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour confections (dorayaki-style sweets, manju, baked goods) are made in the same back-of-shop workshop as rice- and bean-based sweets, so cross-contamination with wheat is likely.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny family-run sweets counter where staff likely speak little English, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the beautifully displayed confections will go a long way.

places.name: アカトンボコーヒー (aka tombo coffee)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The signature items are wheat-pastry Aussie meat pies, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated handling, or allergy protocol is mentioned on the official site, the Nara tourism page, or Tabelog. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe centers on espresso drinks (typically with dairy milk, e.g. flat whites) and Angus beef meat pies. Plant-milk substitution for coffee may be possible to ask about, but no vegan food options are confirmed. A vegan traveler would need to ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sat, Sun & holidays 07:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); Tue 07:00-12:30 (L.O. 12:00); closed Friday (except holidays). On holidays, Tuesday and Friday run 07:00-19:00.
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町 (Mochiidono arcade), near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] aka tombo coffee is a tiny handmade cafe tucked up on the second floor of a building in the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It was created by Chris, a Melbourne native who fell in love with Nara, together with his wife Keiko. Inspired by the r

chef_bio.origin: Melbourne, Australia (run with his Nara-born wife Keiko)
chef_bio.background: aka tombo coffee is a tiny handmade cafe tucked up on the second floor of a building in the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It was created by Chris, a Melbourne native who fell in love with Nara, together with his wife Keiko. Inspired by the restoration of a traditional Japanese storehouse, Chris hand-built much of the interior himself, including a hand-plastered (shikkui) counter, custom shoji screens, and the tables. The couple wanted a place where visitors from around the world could meet and talk over good coffee.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe's guiding idea is 'east meets west' - bridging Japanese and Australian cultures. The name aka tombo means 'red dragonfly': in Japanese tradition the dragonfly is believed to carry ancestors and loved ones home during the Obon season, and the couple chose it to symbolize connecting two worlds. Keiko, who holds top English qualifications, also runs relaxed English-conversation sessions, making the cafe a meeting point for locals and travelers alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Melbourne-style espresso drinks (flat whites and other espresso-based coffees) paired with Australian-style Angus beef meat pies. The cafe also has fun with latte art such as '3D cat cappuccinos' at special events, and serves a morning/breakfast menu.

anecdotes.text: The name 'aka tombo' (red dragonfly) was chosen because, in Japanese tradition, dragonflies are thought to carry ancestors and loved ones home during Obon - mirroring the couple's wish to connect East and West.
Owner Chris, a Melbourne native, handcrafted much of the cafe himself - a hand-plastered counter, shoji screens, and tables - after being inspired by traditional Japanese storehouse restoration.
Keiko, Chris's wife, holds TOEIC 990 and Eiken Grade 1, and leads the morning English-conversation sessions that give the cafe its bilingual, welcoming character.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student can feel completely at ease here: the Australian owner and his English-fluent Japanese wife run a daily English-conversation cafe with an English menu, free Wi-Fi, and power outlets.

places.name: トラットリア・ピアノ (Trattoria Piano)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen is built around wheat: wood-fired Neapolitan pizza and fresh pasta are the core menu, so flour and gluten are everywhere and cross-contamination is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no GF accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan place, but vegetable-forward. The restaurant is genuinely proud of Yamato traditional vegetables and offers a signature vegetable-heavy pizza ('Pizza Yamato'), so a plant-leaning diner may find something — but cheese, meat, and likely animal-based broths/dashi run through much of the menu. There is no documented vegan menu and no allergy/dietary labeling, so a vegan must ask directly about cheese, butter, eggs, and stock on each dish. Strict vegans should not assume any pizza or pasta is safe without confirming.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat/Sun/holidays lunch runs to 15:30 (L.O. 15:00); Closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: 橋本町 (Hashimoto-cho), off Sanjo-dori near the entrance to Nar
places.cuisine: Italian / Neapolitan wood-fired pizza and fr
places.notes: An established casual Italian trattoria at the edge of Nara Park, easy to spot by the stacked firewood, the piano-key motif, and the Italian flag out front. It sits in the Hashimoto-cho shopping-street area a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, making it a natural rest stop be

chef_bio.background: An established casual Italian trattoria at the edge of Nara Park, easy to spot by the stacked firewood, the piano-key motif, and the Italian flag out front. It sits in the Hashimoto-cho shopping-street area a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, making it a natural rest stop between Todai-ji, Kasuga Taisha, Kofuku-ji, and the Nara National Museum.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carries Italy's idea of cooking that honors regional ingredients and applies it to Nara: the kitchen sources lively produce directly from committed growers, including Yamato traditional vegetables, and pairs them with Nara brand meats like Yamato beef, Yamato pork, and Yamato jidori chicken.
chef_bio.specialty: Neapolitan-style pizza fired in a wood oven that reaches around 500C, giving a puffed, slightly charred cornicione edge with a thin, light center. The standouts are the locally minded pies like the original vegetable-piled 'Pizza Yamato', alongside Neapolitan classics such as Margherita and Quattro Formaggi, plus a rotating line of fresh pasta. Drinks lean into Italian, French, and Japanese wines and Italian beer.

anecdotes.text: The shop is recognizable from the street by its stacked firewood, piano-key decor, and Italian flag, signaling the wood-fired oven inside.
Beyond classic Margheritas and Quattro Formaggi, the kitchen builds an original 'Pizza Yamato' loaded with Nara vegetables, leaning fully into local produce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wood-fired pizza and fresh pasta, so wheat flour is central to the kitchen and airborne/surface flour cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pizzas and pastas typically involve cheese, butter, and eggs, and meat dishes use Nara brand meats; shared oven and prep surfaces mean cross-contact with animal products is likely. No vegan menu is documented, so confirm each dish with staff.

positives.text: Strongly vegetable-forward for an Italian restaurant, with Yamato traditional vegetables featured prominently and a dedicated vegetable-heavy 'Pizza Yamato', so plant-leaning diners have real options to discuss.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly trattoria at the edge of Nara Park where pointing at the pizza or pasta menu works fine, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese so a few basic phrases or a translation app help.

places.name: カフェ エトランジェ・ナラッド (Cafe Etranger Narad)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on Neapolitan pizza, pasta and bread-based dishes, so wheat is prevalent throughout the kitchen. There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac travelers should not assume any dish is safe and would need to ask staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen builds dishes around Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers plant-leaning items, notably a signature tofu hamburger served in a wooden box with ancient-grain rice and abundant local vegetables, which one listing describes as accommodating vegan, vegetarian and allergy needs and being additive-free. A vegetarian menu is noted as available. However the broader menu includes meat, fish, cheese and prosciutto, and many dishes likely use dashi or dairy, so vegan diners should confirm specific items and preparation with staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sun: 11:00-22:00; Fri, Sat, day before holidays: 11:00-23:00. Lunch 11:00-15:00, dinner from 17:00 (last orders around 21:30-22:00). Open year-round (no regular closing day).
places.neighborhood: Kamisanjo-cho, Sanjo-dori, near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: Cafe Etranger Narad opened inside NARANICLE, the renovated Nara City Tourist Center on Sanjo-dori, which reopened in June 2017. It was conceived as a meeting place where travelers passing through Nara and local residents can mix, doubling as an information hub and occasionally ho

chef_bio.background: Cafe Etranger Narad opened inside NARANICLE, the renovated Nara City Tourist Center on Sanjo-dori, which reopened in June 2017. It was conceived as a meeting place where travelers passing through Nara and local residents can mix, doubling as an information hub and occasionally hosting vegetable markets (marche).
chef_bio.philosophy: To present the appeal of Nara on a single plate by celebrating local Yamato vegetables and regional producers, serving as a crossroads between visitors and the community.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature Etranger Box is a wooden-box meal showcasing Yamato (Nara) vegetables and Japanese ingredients, anchored by a tofu hamburger with ancient-grain rice from Nara-grown rice, alongside items like red-miso gratin, caponata and salads. The kitchen is also known for Neapolitan pizza, colorful local-vegetable salads, matcha desserts, and pairings with wine and local Nara sake from the Toyosawa brewery.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits within NARANICLE, the Nara City Tourist Center reborn in June 2017, and frames itself as a meeting point for travelers and locals, sometimes hosting vegetable markets on site.
Its signature wooden-box meal highlights Yamato vegetables and Nara-grown ancient-grain rice with a tofu hamburger, reflecting a farm-to-table focus on regional ingredients.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around Neapolitan pizza, pasta and bread, so wheat is widespread in the kitchen and cross-contamination is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One listing for the signature tofu hamburger box states it can accommodate vegan, vegetarian and allergy requests and is additive-free, suggesting some willingness to adapt, but this is not detailed for the full menu and should be confirmed directly.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes local Yamato vegetables and offers a tofu-based hamburger box, giving vegetarian and plant-leaning diners a real anchor dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside Nara's main tourist center on Sanjo-dori, this is a welcoming, casual spot used to international visitors, with picture-friendly dishes and card payment, so a foreign student can drop in comfortably with little Japanese.

places.name: french o・mo・ya 奈良町 (French o・mo・ya Naramachi (Furenchi Omoya Nar)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The cuisine is wheat-heavy: courses include bread, French-style sauces, and breaded/floured preparations, and there is no celiac-safe handling described. The restaurant says it can remove specific allergens if requested well in advance when staff coordinate with the chef, but there is no dedicated gluten-free kitchen or cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiacs; gluten-sensitive diners should call ahead.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan destination. Set courses are built around meat and fish (pork shoulder, wagyu, horse tartare, eel, sea bream, fresh fish) and French sauces that typically use butter, cream, and meat/fish stock. No vegan or vegetarian course is published. The restaurant will accommodate allergen removal if arranged in advance via the chef, so a meat-free request might be possible by prior phone arrangement, but nothing vegan-specific is documented. Vegans should not rely on this without advance confirmation.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30–21:30 (L.O. 19:30). Closed Mon & Tue. Additional closures around Golden Week, Obon, Christmas, and New Year.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Kounodo-cho), near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.cuisine: Creative French in an Edo-period machiya, us
places.notes: Set inside a roughly 150-year-old wooden townhouse from the late Edo period, this restaurant brings French cooking into a quintessentially Nara setting. The sukiya-style machiya keeps its old soul intact: a box staircase, a woven cedar ceiling, snow-viewing sliding windows, and p

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi, the historic merchant quarter of central Nara
chef_bio.background: Set inside a roughly 150-year-old wooden townhouse from the late Edo period, this restaurant brings French cooking into a quintessentially Nara setting. The sukiya-style machiya keeps its old soul intact: a box staircase, a woven cedar ceiling, snow-viewing sliding windows, and panes of rare hand-drawn glass frame the meal. It is part of a small family of o・mo・ya and omo cafe dining spaces in the old town.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberate fusion of Japanese and Western sensibility — French technique expressed through local produce, native ceramics, and the rhythms of a traditional Nara house.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative French course menus built on seasonal Nara-area ingredients, plated on rustic Shigaraki pottery and eaten with chopsticks. Lunch offers tiered full courses (a lighter Petit course, a popular Regular course, and a celebratory Authentic course with the day's fresh fish), while dinner is a chef's tasting that has featured horse tartare, eel, scallop-and-shrimp soup, sea bream, and a wagyu roast.

anecdotes.text: Diners eat refined French courses with chopsticks off rustic Shigaraki-ware plates, inside a sukiya-style townhouse preserved with box stairs, a woven cedar ceiling, snow-viewing windows, and rare hand-drawn glass.
Lunch is offered as three tiers — a Petit course for first-timers, the popular Regular course, and an Authentic course featuring the day's fresh fish for anniversaries and celebrations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Courses include bread and French sauces, and there is no gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling described, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus center on meat and fish with French sauces typically using butter, cream and stock; no vegan or vegetarian course is published, so shared preparation with animal products is expected.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant indicates it can remove specific allergens on request when arranged in advance, with staff coordinating with the chef.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, refined sit-down course restaurant where reservations are expected and a little Japanese helps; a foreign student can manage with a polite greeting and a pre-booked course, but it is a special-occasion outing rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 発酵薬膳料理 らふぁえろ (Hakko Yakuzen Ryori Raffaello)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The cooking is built on fermented vegetables and a house "fermentation water," and the kitchen prides itself on using almost no conventional seasonings, drawing umami from the ingredients themselves — which is promising, but there is no published statement about wheat, soy sauce, or celiac-level cross-contamination control. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly about every component (especially any seasoning, miso, or sauce) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-forward, but NOT vegan by default. The signature 一汁三菜発酵膳 set (around 2,000 yen) is described as featuring grilled fish (焼き魚) as a main alongside many small vegetable dishes, and the menu makes no vegan/vegetarian distinction. The fermentation-water concept could lend itself to a plant-only meal, but you must phone ahead and ask whether a fully plant-based version (no fish, no fish dashi) can be prepared.
places.hours_raw: Cafe 10:00–16:00 / Lunch 11:00（11:30）–16:00; closed Wednesday & Thursday
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, between Naramachi and Kasuga
places.notes: Raffaello (らふぁえろ) occupies a roughly century-old Taisho-era machiya on a quiet street in Nara's Takabatake neighborhood, the old quarter between the Naramachi townscape and Kasuga Shrine. Stepping in feels like visiting a countryside grandmother's house: tatami rooms, a veranda o

chef_bio.background: Raffaello (らふぁえろ) occupies a roughly century-old Taisho-era machiya on a quiet street in Nara's Takabatake neighborhood, the old quarter between the Naramachi townscape and Kasuga Shrine. Stepping in feels like visiting a countryside grandmother's house: tatami rooms, a veranda overlooking a garden, and an unhurried pace meant for savoring each small dish. It is run as part of a local social enterprise that pairs the kitchen and cafe with a women's clothing atelier and upstairs woodworking, operating as a work-support workshop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every dish is made with house 'fermentation water' produced from a culture they nickname the magic egg, ハッコーはる, fermented in a salt-free rice-bran bed. The kitchen holds that letting all the ingredients undergo enzyme fermentation draws out natural umami, so they use almost no added seasoning and serve very lightly seasoned food that lets each ingredient's own flavor come through — cooking they describe as gentle on body and mind, using pesticide-free vegetables sourced directly from contract farms.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 一汁三菜発酵膳 (a one-soup, three-dish fermented set, around 2,000 yen): a large tray of colorful, locally grown vegetables — mountain-vegetable appetizers, a grilled main, and many small fermented side dishes.

anecdotes.text: The whole menu revolves around a 'fermentation water' made from a house culture nicknamed the magic egg (ハッコーはる), fermented in a salt-free nuka bed and used in every dish so that almost no conventional seasoning is needed.
Set in a roughly 100-year-old machiya in Takabatake with tatami seating and a garden veranda; the signature 一汁三菜発酵膳 lays out abundant local vegetables and a grilled main across a large tray.
