vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature fermented set meal is described as including grilled fish as a main alongside the vegetable dishes, with no vegan/vegetarian distinction noted, so plant-based diners cannot assume the standard meal is fish-free.

positives.text: Cooking relies on fermented, pesticide-free vegetables and a house fermentation water, with the kitchen stating it uses almost no conventional seasoning and serves very lightly seasoned, vegetable-forward food.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, mostly-Japanese-speaking old-house cafe where a few polite Japanese phrases (and pointing at the set meal) will go a long way; phoning ahead in Japanese to ask about a fish-free version is wise.

places.name: 日本料理 滴翠 (Nihon Ryori Tekisui)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a Japanese kaiseki kitchen that relies heavily on dashi and soy sauce (typically wheat-containing), with tempura and other wheat-based items on the menu, so it is not celiac-safe by default. The kitchen asks guests to declare allergies and dietary needs at the time of reservation, so a celiac would need to communicate requirements well in advance and confirm directly; cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. While the cooking showcases fresh Yamato vegetables, wild yam and Japanese herbs, the cuisine is built on fish dashi and also features sashimi and Yamato/domestic beef, so standard courses are not vegan. The hotel requests allergy and dietary preferences at reservation, so a plant-based meal might be arranged on advance request but is not a standing offering and must be confirmed directly.
places.hours_raw: Lunch / cafe 11:30-15:00 (food L.O. 14:00, drinks L.O. 15:00). Dinner and breakfast service is reserved for hotel guests. Non-guests welcome for lunch.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, near Nara Park (Sagiike pond), inside the Fu
places.notes: Tekisui sits on the southern edge of Nara Park beside the tranquil Sagiike pond, occupying the dining space of the luxury resort hotel Fufu Nara, which opened in 2020. Guests dine in calm private rooms looking out over a Japanese garden. While dinner and breakfast are reserved fo

chef_bio.background: Tekisui sits on the southern edge of Nara Park beside the tranquil Sagiike pond, occupying the dining space of the luxury resort hotel Fufu Nara, which opened in 2020. Guests dine in calm private rooms looking out over a Japanese garden. While dinner and breakfast are reserved for hotel guests, the lunch and cafe service welcomes visitors from outside the hotel.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated theme is 'karada ni yasashii Nihon ryori' (Japanese cuisine that is gentle on the body), pairing classical Japanese culinary technique with Nara's local character and its heritage as the birthplace of pharmaceutical knowledge, so that a meal is both flavorful and nourishing.
chef_bio.specialty: Body-friendly Japanese cuisine drawing on Nara's deep history as a cradle of medicinal knowledge: fresh Yamato vegetables, grated wild mountain yam (jinenjo tororo), aromatic Japanese and Chinese herbs, and Yamato beef. Documented dishes include the seasonal Tekisui set, a domestic beef loin cutlet with local tomatoes, and a gentle herb-spiced curry, alongside a handmade castella and house-made pudding.

anecdotes.text: Every private dining room overlooks the hotel's Japanese garden and the adjacent Sagiike pond, framing the meal with a quiet view of greenery just south of Nara Park.
The cuisine leans on Nara's identity as a historic center of medicinal knowledge, weaving Japanese-Chinese herbs and wild yam into the dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel asks guests to inform them of any allergies or food preferences at the time of reservation.

cultural_comfort.note: As the restaurant of a luxury resort hotel, the setting is refined and reservations are recommended, but staff are used to non-Japanese visitors and lunch is approachable; a few polite Japanese phrases and advance notice of any dietary needs will go a long way.

places.name: 喫茶 祥瑞 (Kissa Shonzui)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on rice dishes, fried items (shrimp fries, hamburger), Neapolitan pasta, and kinako-based desserts, and the chicken rice uses soy-based sauces; Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. No gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The signature dishes are chicken-based (Hainanese chicken rice, an "adult's children's lunch" with hamburger and shrimp), and even desserts like the kinako pudding are dairy/egg-style sweets. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 17:00-22:00 (LO); closed Wednesdays (and irregular closures)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishiterabayashicho), about an 8-minute walk from
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small café tucked into a traditional Nara machiya (townhouse) on the Nishiterabayashi shopping street in Naramachi, behind a natural-linen noren curtain. The same building runs a daytime café and an evening sake bar.

chef_bio.background: A small café tucked into a traditional Nara machiya (townhouse) on the Nishiterabayashi shopping street in Naramachi, behind a natural-linen noren curtain. The same building runs a daytime café and an evening sake bar.
chef_bio.specialty: By day the kitchen is known for its 海南鶏飯 (Hainanese chicken rice) — moist poached chicken breast with a scallion-and-ginger sauce, served with broth and adjustable with black soy and sweet chili — alongside year-round shaved ice (かき氷) and a 'black-molasses kinako pudding' made with roasted soybean flour and walnuts. A playful 'adult's children's lunch' piles together omelette-topped chicken rice, a hamburger, shrimp fries, Neapolitan pasta, salad and soup. By night the owner pours a curated selection of local Nara sake (奈良酒).

anecdotes.text: The chicken rice is a seasonal lunch item and service is suspended in winter, while shaved ice and pudding stay on the menu year-round.
The owner has asked food bloggers to remove the phone number and emphasizes a no-phone-call preference, reflecting how personal and small the operation is.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken rice is served with soy-based sauces (black soy / sweet chili); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, but no allergen detail is published.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run machiya café where Japanese will go furthest; the owner prefers no phone calls, so use the in-person counter or Instagram, and a few polite Japanese phrases will help.

places.name: ならまち分校スイーツ部 (Naramachi Bunko Sweets-bu)
places.gf_detail: This is a Western-style patisserie sweets cafe. Crepes, galettes, and the wafer/cookie toppings on the kakigori contain wheat, and shaved-ice syrups have not been documented as gluten-free. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation noted. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The signature kakigori and soft-serve are built on dairy (cherry-milk syrup, matcha espuma/cream, soft serve) plus pistachio and other non-vegan components. No vegan options or plant-based substitutes are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（南城戸町・京終エリア）
places.cuisine: かき氷専門・古民家スイーツカフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked down a quiet lane in old-town Naramachi, this dessert cafe is officially produced by patisserie trico, the well-loved Western-pastry shop based in nearby Kawai Town. The space is designed to recreate a rural Japanese branch-school classroom of the Showa era (1930s-60s): gu

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara City; the dessert craft comes from patisserie trico of Kawai Town.
chef_bio.background: Tucked down a quiet lane in old-town Naramachi, this dessert cafe is officially produced by patisserie trico, the well-loved Western-pastry shop based in nearby Kawai Town. The space is designed to recreate a rural Japanese branch-school classroom of the Showa era (1930s-60s): guests sit at wooden student desks facing a real blackboard you can write on, surrounded by children's calligraphy, exposed beams, and a vintage rotary telephone.
chef_bio.philosophy: Blending Japanese and Western flavors in playful, Nara-themed desserts within a nostalgic classroom setting that invites lingering.
chef_bio.specialty: Towering, multi-layered kakigori (shaved ice) made with trico patisserie technique. The flagship is the 'Wakakusayama' ice, inspired by Nara's iconic three-tiered grassy mountain, layering pistachio and Uji matcha with hidden cherry-milk syrup and raspberry sauce, topped with a fawn-shaped wafer; newer creations such as the black-sesame-pistachio ice continue a numbered series. Crepes, galettes, and soft-serve ice cream round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The kakigori are numbered, and from the 50th creation onward numbers are assigned in order of release, so a higher number means a more recent invention.
The blackboard at the front of the 'classroom' is free for guests to write on and is updated daily.
The signature Wakakusayama ice is themed on Nara's famous Mt. Wakakusa and crowned with a cute fawn-shaped decoration.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Desserts are built on dairy (milk syrups, matcha cream/espuma, soft serve) and the kitchen also makes crepes and galettes, so plant-based requests cannot be reliably accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A charming nostalgic spot tucked in old-town Naramachi, but it is a tiny local Japanese cafe with no English menu or signage documented, so a foreign student is best off coming with a guide or a translation app and patience.

places.name: 米粉Cafe 沙羅双樹 (Komeko Cafe Sarasoju)
places.gf_detail: A dedicated gluten-free rice flour cafe. The owner developed a wheat allergy herself and built the entire menu around a 100% domestic rice flour blend (rice from Nara, Kyoto and Kyushu) - all bread, chiffon cakes, cheesecakes, caneles, plus lunch pasta and quiche are made with rice flour rather than wheat. Because the kitchen is built around rice flour for everything, this is one of the safer choices in central Nara for someone avoiding gluten. Still, no explicit celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is documented, so a strict celiac should confirm details directly.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-focused cafe. Signature items use dairy and eggs (chiffon cake with whipped cream, baked and Basque cheesecakes, caneles; the lunch pasta uses a homemade rice-flour white sauce and cheese). No documented vegan menu, so vegans should ask in advance about what, if anything, can be prepared without animal products.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・下御門商店街（下御門町）
places.cuisine: グルテンフリー米粉カフェ
places.notes: The cafe was opened in late November by Ms. Kawai, who developed a wheat allergy and went searching for foods she could eat safely. That search led her to gluten-free rice flour, and she went on to earn certification as a rice flour master (米粉マイスター) before opening her own cafe.

chef_bio.origin: It sits in the Shimomikado shopping arcade just a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in a renovated old townhouse that was once a shoe shop. Kawai kept original architectural touches - including the showcase's frosted decorative glass, reused as transom panels, and a wooden counter - giving the room a quiet Japanese-modern feel.
chef_bio.background: The cafe was opened in late November by Ms. Kawai, who developed a wheat allergy and went searching for foods she could eat safely. That search led her to gluten-free rice flour, and she went on to earn certification as a rice flour master (米粉マイスター) before opening her own cafe.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kawai wants the cafe to be unhurried: in her words, 'スピーディーさは無いが、ゆったりとした空間でほっこりとしてもらえるものを提供したい' - there is no rush here, but a relaxed space where guests can feel at ease. Her broader aim is to share 'health and happiness through food' and one day teach rice-flour baking classes.
chef_bio.specialty: Everything is built on a custom 100% domestic rice flour blend (rice from Nara, Kyoto and Kyushu), all handmade. The flagship is the chiffon cake, in flavors such as plain, wasanbon, Kaga hojicha, Sri Lankan Earl Grey, Uji matcha and Swiss chocolate, sweetened with mineral-rich kibi brown sugar and served with seasonal fruit and cream. Also notable are the baked and Basque cheesecakes, caneles and rice-flour bread, plus a lunch menu of rice-flour pasta and quiche.

anecdotes.text: The building previously appeared as a location in a film set in Nara, and the owner deliberately preserved the old shoe shop's fittings - such as the decorative frosted glass - when converting it into the cafe.
The interior is furnished with specialty coffee, custom-commissioned ceramics and items gathered from the owner's travels through some two dozen countries.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner has a wheat allergy herself and built the cafe specifically around gluten-free rice flour, so staff understand wheat avoidance from personal experience.

positives.text: All bread, cakes and lunch dishes are made from a 100% domestic rice flour blend rather than wheat flour, making this a rare central-Nara cafe where the whole menu is gluten-free by design.
The owner became interested in gluten-free rice flour after developing a wheat allergy and earned rice flour master certification before opening, reflecting genuine expertise in wheat-free baking.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, calm owner-run cafe in a renovated old townhouse where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, though the gluten-free focus makes it especially welcoming for a foreign student avoiding wheat.

places.name: 喫茶 田川 (Kissa Tagawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a single-dish curry-rice kissaten. Japanese curry is almost always thickened with a wheat-flour roux, so the only menu item is not gluten-free. There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation and no documented allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The sole offering is beef curry rice — the curry is made from onions and beef chuck simmered for hours. There is no vegetarian or vegan option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (often sells out and closes early). Closed Sundays and the 1st and 3rd Wednesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（南半田西町）
places.cuisine: 昭和の老舗喫茶・家庭的な大盛りカレーと珈琲
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Tagawa opened in 1966 in Nara's Kitamachi district and has been a beloved neighborhood fixture for over half a century. The shop's founder hailed from Tagawa City in Fukuoka Prefecture — the source of the shop's name — where he had run a bicycle shop before moving to Osaka

chef_bio.origin: Tagawa City, Fukuoka Prefecture (founder's hometown, source of the shop name)
chef_bio.background: Kissa Tagawa opened in 1966 in Nara's Kitamachi district and has been a beloved neighborhood fixture for over half a century. The shop's founder hailed from Tagawa City in Fukuoka Prefecture — the source of the shop's name — where he had run a bicycle shop before moving to Osaka to work as a bartender and in the coffee wholesale trade. The current owner, Okabe Ayako, is the second-generation proprietor, carrying on her father's shop. After the original premises (the ground floor of the aging Handa Apartment building) had badly deteriorated, the shop was given a full renovation and reopened on the same corner in March 2022, this time with an adjoining parking area.
chef_bio.philosophy: An honest, unfussy 'curry only' shop: a hand-lettered note from the owner apologizes that they serve nothing but curry. The curry is made simply from onions and beef chuck simmered slowly for five to six hours, with no hidden seasonings or special spices — the owner describes portioning the rice purely 'by feel.' It is celebrated as the ultimate in gentle, homestyle Japanese curry.
chef_bio.specialty: Enormous, mountainous portions of homestyle beef curry rice, served in small, medium and large sizes. The rice is piled so high that diners are offered extra curry sauce for free when the roux runs short.

anecdotes.text: The curry contains only onions and beef chuck, slow-simmered for five to six hours with no secret ingredients; the shop is famous for rice portions so towering that they sometimes finish the curry sauce before the rice, so extra sauce is offered free of charge.

Founded in 1966 by an owner from Tagawa City in Fukuoka — a former bicycle-shop keeper turned bartender and coffee wholesaler — the shop is now run by his daughter, second-generation owner Okabe Ayako, and reopened after a full renovation in 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only dish served is Japanese-style curry rice, which is conventionally thickened with a wheat-flour roux; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single menu item is beef curry made with beef chuck and onions, so it is not vegan or vegetarian.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, old-school local coffee shop with counter seating and cash only — basic spoken Japanese and a relaxed attitude will help, as there is no English menu and the format is a simple "curry only" affair.

places.name: サンバード (Sunbird)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire concept centers on soy-sauce-based omurice — the signature dishes use Igeta soy sauce (Inoue Honten), which is a wheat-brewed Japanese soy sauce, plus demi-glace, cream and other sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no information about a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is an egg-and-chicken-rice omurice specialist — every signature dish is built on eggs (Ōmiya Farm eggs from Kashihara) over chicken rice, with soy-sauce, demi-glace, cream or mentaiko sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Sat/Sun/holidays to 15:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 on Wed, Sat, Sun only. Closed Thursdays. Order via ticket machine after being seated; roughly one-hour seating limit at busy times.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi (きたまち), near Nara Women's University; Nabeyacho, c
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sunbird is a one-man omurice specialist run by an owner-chef (surname Kamimura) originally from Kōchi, who built up experience running ethnic-cuisine restaurants and cafes in Nara before opening his own omelette-rice shop. He cooks alone, which can mean a wait at busy times. The

chef_bio.background: Sunbird is a one-man omurice specialist run by an owner-chef (surname Kamimura) originally from Kōchi, who built up experience running ethnic-cuisine restaurants and cafes in Nara before opening his own omelette-rice shop. He cooks alone, which can mean a wait at busy times. The shop opened in Naramachi in 2017 and relocated in September 2022 to a quieter second-floor space in the Kitamachi (きたまち) area near Nara Women's University, where it has built a loyal base of local regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on carefully sourced local Nara ingredients: specialty free-range eggs from Ōmiya Farm in Kashihara, Igeta soy sauce from the long-established Inoue Honten, and after the move a switch to fragrant, chewy Nara-grown Hitomebore rice.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the lemon butter soy sauce omurice — fluffy egg over soy-based chicken rice finished with a house teriyaki-style sauce made from lemon, butter and Edo-era Igeta soy sauce. Other favorites include the egg-yolk soy sauce omurice (topped with shredded nori, shichimi and green onion), demi-glace omurice, and cream-peperoncino and mentaiko-cream versions.

anecdotes.text: Relocated from busy Naramachi to a calmer second-floor space in Kitamachi near Nara Women's University in September 2022; the owner says he likes the quieter neighborhood and the local residents who come in regularly.

After the move he switched the rice to the Hitomebore variety for its aroma and chewy texture, and has spoken about wanting to do pop-ups in his home prefecture of Kōchi and add side dishes and egg sandwiches.
The lemon butter soy sauce omurice is described by diners as a flavor you can't get anywhere else.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature omurice dishes are finished with a sauce made from Igeta soy sauce (Inoue Honten), a traditional wheat-brewed Japanese soy sauce, so the core menu contains wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are egg-based omurice over chicken rice; no plant-based options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: You order from a touchscreen ticket machine after sitting down, so little spoken Japanese is needed, but the menu and machine are in Japanese, so a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend helps a foreign student order confidently.

places.name: 日本料理 ひとしずく (Nihon Ryori Hitoshizuku)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki kitchen where dishes rely heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and fish dashi; courses include items that are not celiac-safe. There is no published allergen handling, so this is not a safe choice for someone with celiac disease unless arranged directly in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu centers on seasonal fresh fish and Kuroge Wagyu beef, and Japanese cooking here uses fish-based dashi throughout, so the food is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based course is advertised; any vegan request would need to be discussed with the owner-chef ahead of time and is not guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–13:30 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30– (L.O. 20:00, reservation only). 不定休 (irregular closures). Source: official site nara-hitoshizuku.com.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち界隈 / Takabatake-cho, Naramachi area）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A solo owner-chef runs every part of Hitoshizuku, sourcing ingredients from across Japan and personally handling everything from purchasing to cooking. Guests sit at a single wood-toned counter and watch the dishes being prepared and served the moment they are ready.

chef_bio.origin: Tucked into the quiet Takabatake-cho corner of Nara's Naramachi district, about a 14-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.background: A solo owner-chef runs every part of Hitoshizuku, sourcing ingredients from across Japan and personally handling everything from purchasing to cooking. Guests sit at a single wood-toned counter and watch the dishes being prepared and served the moment they are ready.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests savor premium Japanese cuisine made from carefully selected ingredients in a warm, wood-grained space.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Japanese kaiseki built around the day's fresh fish and Kuroge Wagyu beef, including a wagyu steak set; lunch courses are more approachable while dinner is a reservation-only multi-course experience tailored to budget and party size.

anecdotes.text: The counter seats just seven, with no private rooms, and can be reserved in full as a private buyout for a small group.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate seven-seat counter where the owner cooks everything himself, so come with a reservation and at least basic Japanese, since menus and conversation are likely Japanese-only and there is no published English support.

places.name: おかる (Okaru)
places.gf_detail: This is an okonomiyaki and akashi-yaki shop where the core dishes are wheat-flour batters and the dashi/sauce typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Dishes are built around eggs, pork, squid, octopus, shrimp and fish-based dashi; akashi-yaki is dipped in dashi broth. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00 - 21:00 (last order around 20:30; one source lists ~20:00 close)
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki Shopping Arcade (near Kintetsu-Nara Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Okaru is a long-established okonomiyaki and akashi-yaki shop tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, and it is treated as something of a local soul food by Nara residents. Diners sit at table-side griddles where staff cook in front of

chef_bio.background: Okaru is a long-established okonomiyaki and akashi-yaki shop tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, and it is treated as something of a local soul food by Nara residents. Diners sit at table-side griddles where staff cook in front of them, and the retro Kansai interior is decorated with large fish rubbings (gyotaku), hinting that the proprietor is a keen angler.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature draw is the akashi-yaki, fluffy egg-rich dumplings packed with generous octopus and served to dip in warm dashi; the okonomiyaki and modern-yaki (with a choice of soba or udon and pork, squid or minced meat) are equally beloved, alongside an extravagant deluxe Okaru-yaki loaded with beef, pork, prawns, squid, octopus, scallop, oyster and several eggs.

anecdotes.text: The shop gained extra fame after actor Tsuyoshi Domoto visited during a TV program, drawing in fans afterward.
A recent renovation kept beloved decor like the large fish rubbings on the walls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and modern-yaki are wheat-flour batter dishes cooked on a shared teppan; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and dashi at Kansai griddle shops typically contain wheat; no allergen information is published for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Akashi-yaki is served in fish-based dashi and dishes rely on egg, pork, and seafood; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, popular local griddle spot where staff cook at your table; some basic Japanese or pointing at the menu helps, and cash is essential.

places.name: うどん そば 呑み処 三条坊 (Udon Soba Nomidokoro Sanjobo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is wheat-based udon and soba, and broths/curries rely on dashi and standard sauces; there is no gluten-free menu, no allergen documentation, and no indication of a dedicated preparation area. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu lists a small ビーガン (vegan) section - vegan keema curry udon (about 1,180 yen, +50 yen to switch to soba), vegan soy-milk carbonara udon (about 980 yen), and vegan keema curry rice (about 1,180 yen) - but all three are marked 食材が入荷次第販売開始 ('to go on sale once ingredients are in stock'), i.e. not yet actually being served. There are also mountain-vegetable udon and vegetable tempura, but standard noodle broths use fish dashi and noodles are wheat. Call ahead (0742-23-1154) to confirm whether the vegan items are currently available before relying on them.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) daily; closed Dec 30 - Jan 1.
places.neighborhood: 三条通・JR奈良駅周辺（ビエラ奈良内）
places.cuisine: うどん・そば・居酒屋
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A relaxed udon, soba and small-plate drinking spot tucked into the Viera Nara complex right under JR Nara Station, opened in 2010. It pairs a calm, traditional Japanese interior fitting the old capital with hearty noodle dishes built around Nara's local Yamato beef (Yamato-gyu) a

chef_bio.background: A relaxed udon, soba and small-plate drinking spot tucked into the Viera Nara complex right under JR Nara Station, opened in 2010. It pairs a calm, traditional Japanese interior fitting the old capital with hearty noodle dishes built around Nara's local Yamato beef (Yamato-gyu) and regional ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Nara's local Yamato beef and regional produce in approachable, station-side udon and soba while doubling as a casual place for a drink.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato beef dishes and noodle specialties: a 'Stamina Nara' udon developed as a noodle-shop take on Nara's stamina ramen, Yamato beef curry udon, Yamato beef rice bowl, and a signature large 'Daibutsu' udon, alongside tempura and a soba/udon lineup.

anecdotes.text: Its 'Stamina Nara' udon is presented as a collaboration reworking Nara's well-known stamina ramen into udon form.
The menu features dishes built around Yamato beef, a Nara regional brand beef, including curry udon and a beef rice bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat udon and soba cooked in shared water, with no gluten-free menu or allergen labeling shown, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and sauces use standard dashi and seasonings; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free tsuyu is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dishes are listed but marked as not yet on sale pending ingredients, and they would be prepared in a kitchen that handles fish dashi and meat, so cross-contact is likely; confirm by phone.

positives.text: The menu does include a small vegan section (keema curry udon/rice and soy-milk carbonara udon) plus mountain-vegetable udon and vegetable tempura, showing some plant-based intent.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, walk-in udon and soba spot right by JR Nara Station with picture-friendly noodle dishes and card/QR payment, so a foreign student can manage with basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu.

places.name: コムゴン (Com Ngon)
places.gf_detail: Pho and other dishes are built on rice noodles (naturally gluten-free), but the kitchen uses fish sauce and likely soy-based seasonings, and there is no indication of gluten-free protocols or a dedicated setup. A celiac diner would need to ask about each sauce and cross-contact; not a verified gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore Vietnamese restaurant centered on meat and fish: pho uses chicken or beef broth, fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a core seasoning, and most dishes feature chicken, beef, or pork. No vegan menu is documented. Some lighter herb/rice/spring-roll items may exist, but a strict vegan would need to confirm broth and fish sauce; not a reliable vegan destination.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station (ならま
places.notes: Com Ngon is a Vietnamese restaurant tucked up a narrow staircase on the second floor of a building in the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from the center of Nara, with a soba shop occupying the floor below. It is described as a rare find for Nara, where Vietnames

chef_bio.background: Com Ngon is a Vietnamese restaurant tucked up a narrow staircase on the second floor of a building in the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from the center of Nara, with a soba shop occupying the floor below. It is described as a rare find for Nara, where Vietnamese cuisine is uncommon. Cooks brought from Vietnam prepare the food, giving it an authentic character; the name Com Ngon means 'delicious rice'.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for authentic Vietnamese flavor while keeping dishes accessible to Japanese palates, including milder options for those who dislike spicy food.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature dishes are the pho rice-noodle soups — Pho Ga (chicken) and Pho Bo (beef) in the Hanoi style, with a light, refreshing broth balanced by herbs, spices and fish sauce — alongside Hue-style spicy Bun Bo, Bun Cha (grilled and boiled pork over rice noodles), Banh Canh, and fresh and fried spring rolls.

anecdotes.text: The interior leans into Vietnamese atmosphere with bamboo tables, tin-can chopstick holders and ceramic coasters, and sunken kotatsu-style seats looking out over the shopping arcade; Vietnamese handicrafts and food ingredients are displayed and sold near the register.
On a weekday afternoon the room was nearly full and mostly international diners, reflecting strong inbound tourism to the area.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Vietnamese cooking relies on fish sauce (nuoc mam) as a core seasoning, and dipping sauces and marinades may also include soy-based or wheat-containing condiments; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pho and most dishes use chicken or beef broth and fish sauce, and meat dishes (chicken, beef, pork) dominate the menu; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Pho and many noodle dishes are based on rice noodles rather than wheat, which can suit a gluten-sensitive diner who confirms the sauces and broth in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, accessible Vietnamese spot popular with international visitors, with an illustrated/photo menu and affordable set lunches, so a foreign student should feel comfortable ordering here.

places.name: ラホツ (RAHOTSU)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a Thai restaurant whose signature dishes include Miwa-somen tom yum kung (wheat noodles) and curries; Thai cooking typically relies on soy/fish sauces, and no gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented. A celiac traveler should not assume safety here and would need to ask in detail; cross-contamination cannot be confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. There is a vegetable yam (salad) made with produce from neighboring farms and many non-spicy dishes, but Thai cooking here generally uses fish sauce and the kakigori use dairy (yogurt cream, mimolette cheese, coconut milk). No vegan menu or labeling is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask which items can be prepared without fish sauce, shrimp paste, or dairy.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 14:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Kakigori (shaved ice) is by advance reservation only. Tabelog also lists a simplified 11:00-19:00 range; the 11:30-14:00 / 14:30-20:30 split is from a detailed source.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake / Naramachi area (near Kintetsu Nara Station, app
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Run by an owner-chef with a wide-ranging culinary background spanning French cooking, bakeries, and other food-service work, who opened this small Thai restaurant out of a love for Southeast Asia, wanting to share with local diners the kinds of dishes that are everyday fare over

chef_bio.background: Run by an owner-chef with a wide-ranging culinary background spanning French cooking, bakeries, and other food-service work, who opened this small Thai restaurant out of a love for Southeast Asia, wanting to share with local diners the kinds of dishes that are everyday fare over there.
chef_bio.philosophy: Reinterpreting Thai cooking through Nara's local produce, and treating shaved ice as a fast-rotating creative canvas - most kakigori flavors appear and then vanish from the lineup within about a week.
chef_bio.specialty: Thai dishes built on local Nara ingredients - Miwa-somen tom yum kung, a green curry made with Yamato regional chicken, and a vegetable yam (Thai salad) using produce from neighboring farms - alongside a celebrated shaved-ice (kakigori) program. The standout is 'Kopech', an unconventional salt-based kakigori built on coconut, pepper and cheese (the name combines the three), finished with finely shredded mimolette and black pepper over coconut-milk syrup; mango kakigori made with Indian Kesar mangoes, yogurt cream and coconut-milk syrup is another favorite.

anecdotes.text: The kakigori menu rotates so quickly that most flavors disappear from the lineup roughly a week after they appear.
The 'Kopech' shaved ice is so distinctive - salty cheese crunch wrapped in creamy coconut sweetness with occasional bursts of pepper - that fans have given it their own nickname.
There are plenty of non-spicy dishes, so it is enjoyable for families with children and for older diners as well.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes include Miwa-somen tom yum kung, which uses wheat somen noodles, indicating wheat is handled in the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kakigori use dairy (yogurt cream, mimolette cheese) and Thai dishes typically use fish sauce, so vegan-safe preparation is not documented.

positives.text: A vegetable yam (Thai salad) is made with produce from neighboring farms, and many non-spicy dishes are available, so vegetable-forward and mild options exist.

cultural_comfort.note: A small chef-run Thai spot where ordering is comfortable with some basic Japanese or pointing at the menu, and staff are used to welcoming families and first-timers; the shaved ice typically needs an advance online booking.

places.name: 御菓子司 鶴屋徳満 本店 (Okashitsukasa Tsuruya Tokuman Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional wagashi shop whose core products are wheat-based: castella, mikasa (dorayaki-style filled cakes), monaka wafers, and senbei all contain wheat flour. While the signature kakigori (kuzu/wasanbon syrup over shaved ice with house-made azuki bean paste) and pure-azuki items may be naturally low in wheat, the kitchen handles wheat-based confections extensively, so there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation and high cross-contact risk. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not advertised as vegan. Many items are plant-based by tradition (azuki bean paste, kuzu, agar/anmitsu, the signature aoniyoshi rakugan-style sweet made from kudzu starch and sugar), but the shop also makes egg-and-dairy confections such as castella and sponge-based mikasa, and does not label items for vegans. A vegan traveler could potentially eat plain anko-based sweets, the aoniyoshi, or fruit/agar anmitsu, but each item must be confirmed individually with staff for egg, dairy, and honey.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (Thursday closed). A local food guide lists the eat-in service around 10:00-17:00; the shop's posted hours are 9:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: Shimomikado shopping arcade (Shimomikado-cho), at the northe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established Nara confectioner founded in the Meiji era (around 1898), said to have started as an independent venture after splitting from the Osaka house of Tsuruya Hachiman. The main shop sits in the Shimomikado arcade at the northern edge of Naramachi, and after a renova

chef_bio.background: A long-established Nara confectioner founded in the Meiji era (around 1898), said to have started as an independent venture after splitting from the Osaka house of Tsuruya Hachiman. The main shop sits in the Shimomikado arcade at the northern edge of Naramachi, and after a renovation it continues to draw locals and visitors for both takeaway sweets and an in-shop sweets-parlor experience.
chef_bio.philosophy: A confectioner rooted in Nara's classical imagery, naming and shaping sweets after the ancient capital's poetry and history.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is 'Aoniyoshi,' an elegant two-toned strip confection. Once made like rakugan from rice flour, it was reworked using kudzu starch, giving it its distinctive delicate texture. In summer the shop is loved for its kakigori: shaved ice with wasanbon-sugar syrup, black molasses, and house-made red bean paste from premium large azuki, finished with decorative leaf candies; a salted cherry-blossom 'sakura-gori' is also offered. Year-round the cases hold anmitsu, the imperial-style 'Kenjo Mikasa' filled cakes, monaka, yokan, senbei, and castella.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'Aoniyoshi' is said to have been named when Prince Arisugawa visited Chugu-ji temple, and it is treated as a 'kenjo' confection presented to the imperial household.
A regional Nara magazine reported on the roughly 130-year-old confectioner's main-shop renewal, underscoring its standing as a Meiji-founded local institution.
To mark the 1300th anniversary of the Heijo-kyo capital, the shop created a five-colored confection, 'Goen Kyokusui no Utage,' reflecting its habit of designing sweets around Nara's heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop produces a wide range of wheat-based confections (castella, sponge-filled mikasa cakes, senbei, monaka wafers) in the same kitchen, so any naturally wheat-free item carries cross-contact risk; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg- and dairy-containing sweets such as castella and sponge-based mikasa are made alongside plant-based azuki, kuzu, and agar confections; no vegan-specific separation is documented.

positives.text: The signature kakigori is described as shaved ice with house-made red bean paste and wasanbon-sugar/molasses syrup, and the 'Aoniyoshi' is made from kudzu starch and sugar — these particular items are plant-based and wheat-free by tradition, though they should still be confirmed for cross-contact and ingredients on site.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming old-town confectionery where pointing at the display case and a simple greeting will get you served, though menus and conversation are mainly in Japanese.

places.name: 東大寺二月堂南茶所　龍美堂 (Todaiji Nigatsudo Minami Chajo Ryubido)
places.gf_detail: Signature items are naturally low-wheat in their base form — warabi mochi is made from bracken/warabi starch and dusted with kinako (soybean flour), and ohagi is glutinous rice wrapped in homemade red-bean paste. However, this is a traditional tea house with no documented gluten-free program, no allergen menu, and no celiac cross-contamination protocols. The kinako coating and shared utensils are concerns, and staff are unlikely to be equipped for strict celiac requirements. Treat as ask, not safe.
places.vegan_detail: The two signature sweets appear plant-based: warabi mochi (bracken starch, sugar, kinako) and ohagi (rice with adzuki-bean anko) typically contain no animal products. But the menu also includes parfaits and seasonal shaved ice with cream/dairy elements, and no item is labeled vegan. Ingredients are not documented in detail, so a vegan diner should confirm each item directly. Note kinako is soy.
places.hours_raw: 10:00～17:00（ラストオーダー 平日16:20／土日祝16:45）。※冬季変更あり。不定休。
places.neighborhood: 東大寺二月堂南側（奈良公園内）
places.cuisine: 寺門前の茶屋・甘味処
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 龍美堂 sits directly south of Todaiji's Nigatsudo Hall, famous for the Omizutori fire-and-water rites, and has long served as a resting spot for pilgrims. The shop pours tea boiled in an iron kettle said to be over 200 years old and offers a sweeping view over Nigatsudo and the Nara

chef_bio.origin: Nara — within Nara Park, beside Todaiji Nigatsudo.
chef_bio.background: 龍美堂 sits directly south of Todaiji's Nigatsudo Hall, famous for the Omizutori fire-and-water rites, and has long served as a resting spot for pilgrims. The shop pours tea boiled in an iron kettle said to be over 200 years old and offers a sweeping view over Nigatsudo and the Nara Basin. It also displays rare items connected to Nigatsudo and the Omizutori ceremony.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is the officially permitted maker of Gyoho miso, the secret-recipe miso said to have been used by the ascetic monks who shut themselves away during Nigatsudo's rites to build their spiritual strength — a practice the temple traces back over a thousand years to the priest Jitchu, disciple of Todaiji's founding abbot Roben.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade warabi mochi generously dusted with freshly-ground kinako (soybean flour), and large old-fashioned ohagi made with the shop's own homemade red-bean paste. The shop is also the licensed maker of 行法味噌 (Gyoho miso), sold as a souvenir; in summer it serves shaved ice with house anko.

anecdotes.text: 二月堂と奈良盆地を望む抜群のロケーションで、龍美堂自慢の甘味をご賞味下さい。
この行法は天平の昔、東大寺開山良辧僧正の高弟實忠和尚によって創められましてから今日まで千何百年の間一度も途絶えたことのないあらたかな秘法でございます。

positives.text: Signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch and kinako rather than wheat flour, and ohagi is glutinous rice with red-bean paste — base recipes that typically contain no wheat and no animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional temple-side tea house where pointing at the warabi-mochi and ohagi display works fine, but there is no English menu or allergen support, so a foreign student with dietary needs should arrive with a guide or translated phrases.

places.name: かふぇたまき (Cafe Tamaki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional kissaten whose mainstays are coffee, toast, cake, and homestyle dishes — all wheat-heavy and prepared in a shared kitchen with no gluten-free accommodation documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. Black hand-roasted coffee is the safest plant-based option, but food items (toast, cake, pudding, homestyle cooking) typically contain dairy, eggs, or animal stock. Vegans would need to ask, and options will be very limited.
places.hours_raw: 08:30-21:00; closed Thursdays
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Kintetsu-Nara Station area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] One of the oldest coffee houses in Nara — by the proprietor's own account effectively the oldest still standing, as the two that preceded it have closed. It sits on a narrow lane heading into the historic Naramachi quarter, in a quiet wood-toned space behind a white-and-black ext

chef_bio.background: One of the oldest coffee houses in Nara — by the proprietor's own account effectively the oldest still standing, as the two that preceded it have closed. It sits on a narrow lane heading into the historic Naramachi quarter, in a quiet wood-toned space behind a white-and-black exterior.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner insists the place is a 'cafe' rather than a kissaten, partly because a cafe may serve alcohol — and indeed many regulars drop in to sip spirits cut with their own preferred ratio of water alongside small snacks.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-roasted coffee made by a roasting method handed down from the founder, alongside homestyle cooking and toast; in earlier years the shop was loved for its bittersweet homemade pudding and a notably spicy house curry.

anecdotes.text: The energetic proprietor greets guests together with the shop dog, Kuma, and her warm chatter is regarded as one of the real pleasures of a visit.
After her husband passed away, the menu drifted away from its old signature items toward the homestyle dishes she now cooks herself.
The coffee follows a roasting style preserved from the shop's first-generation owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional coffee house centered on toast, cake, and homestyle dishes in a single shared kitchen; cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan menu is documented; food items generally rely on dairy, eggs, or animal-based stock, so dedicated vegan preparation is not indicated.

positives.text: Plain black hand-roasted coffee is available and is the most reliably plant-based item on offer.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, owner-run old-school coffee house where the chatty proprietor speaks Japanese; basic Japanese or a translation app will make the visit far smoother and more enjoyable.

places.name: 菓匠 千壽庵吉宗 奈良総本店 (Kashō Senjuan Yoshimune Nara Sōhonten)
places.gf_detail: The house specialty is warabimochi, traditionally a wheat-free confection. The standard version blends sweet potato starch with domestic bracken (warabi) starch; the premium "pure" warabimochi (純本生わらび餅) uses 100% domestic bracken starch with only sugar and water. So the core sweet contains no wheat. However, it is dusted with kinako (roasted soybean flour) and the shop publishes no gluten-free or cross-contamination guarantees, and other wagashi sold here may contain wheat. Celiacs should ask directly before eating.
places.vegan_detail: The signature warabimochi is plant-based: bracken/sweet-potato starch, sugar, water, and kinako (soybean flour) topping, with no animal products in its core recipe. This is a confectionery shop, not a vegan-focused destination, and ingredient lists vary by item (some wagashi may use eggs or dairy). No formal vegan menu or cross-contact policy is documented, so vegans should confirm individual items.
places.hours_raw: 物販 10:00～18:00（元日のみ休業）。喫茶「千壽茶寮」は週末・祝日を中心に13:00～17:00頃、わらび餅とお飲みもの限定で営業（かき氷は本店では提供なし／なかまち店で季節提供）。
places.neighborhood: 押上町（県庁東交差点北・東大寺戒壇院近く／奈良公園エリア）
places.notes: Founded in 1936 (Showa 11) by Yamamoto Yoshio, who first learned the confectioner's craft through an uncle's candy business in the early Showa years. After the war the workshop made candies under the name Sanwa Seika, then evolved into traditional wagashi. The second generation,

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1936 (Showa 11) by Yamamoto Yoshio, who first learned the confectioner's craft through an uncle's candy business in the early Showa years. After the war the workshop made candies under the name Sanwa Seika, then evolved into traditional wagashi. The second generation, Yamamoto Kenji, grew the business, and since 1998 the third generation, Yamamoto Tetsuya, has led the shop, now in its ninth decade in Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop aims to create sweets, spaces, and hospitality that make the heart quietly calm — what they call 'おだやかな幸せ' (calm happiness).
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh warabimochi (生わらび餅) — a melt-in-the-mouth bracken-starch sweet dusted with kinako. The standard version blends domestic bracken starch with sweet-potato starch; the top-grade 純本生わらび餅 uses 100% domestic bracken starch with only sugar and water, hand-kneaded by veteran artisans. A historic candy called 古代瓦 (ancient roof tile) is named for the imagery of Todaiji and Nara's old architecture.

anecdotes.text: The shop's warabimochi was recognized with the Prime Minister's Award at the 21st National Confectionery Exposition, the result of extensive research into classical texts to recreate an additive-free, naturally soft sweet.

On weekends and holidays a small tea space, 千壽茶寮, opens in the afternoon serving the fresh warabimochi with a drink; in summer the warabimochi also appears in a fluffy Japanese-style shaved ice at the company's Nakamachi shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a general wagashi confectionery that also sells wheat-containing sweets; no gluten-free production line or cross-contamination policy is published, so shared utensils/surfaces are likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen or staff allergy-handling protocol; commentary on warabimochi allergens recommends contacting the maker directly to confirm ingredients.

positives.text: The premium warabimochi is made from only domestic bracken starch, sugar, and water — no additives — and the core warabimochi recipe contains no wheat, making it a relatively simple, plant-based sweet (note the kinako topping is soy).

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, walk-in-friendly old confectionery shop where you can point at the famous warabimochi and pay easily; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, but no special skills are needed to buy or enjoy a sweet here.

places.name: 純喫茶シンロク×オーシマドーナツ奈良店 (Junkissa Shinroku x Oshima Donut Nara)
places.gf_detail: This is a donut-and-coffee kissaten. The signature items are hand-made fried donuts, which are wheat-based, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
places.vegan_detail: The core offering is fried wheat donuts (typically made with egg, dairy and butter) and coffee. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented, and the parfait/coffee drinks would normally use dairy. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular closing day)
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi / Hannya-ji area, north of Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, calm retro coffee shop in Nara's Kitamachi district, just north of the central station area near Hannya-ji temple. It opened on November 24, 2025, in the space formerly occupied by 'Kissaten Mickey,' a cafe long loved by the local community, and was reborn as a collabora

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi), built on the site of the former neighborhood cafe Kissaten Mickey
chef_bio.background: A small, calm retro coffee shop in Nara's Kitamachi district, just north of the central station area near Hannya-ji temple. It opened on November 24, 2025, in the space formerly occupied by 'Kissaten Mickey,' a cafe long loved by the local community, and was reborn as a collaboration between the Shinroku kissaten concept and Tokyo-based OSHIMA DONUTS. The interior keeps a nostalgic Showa-era calm while pairing it with a modern donut counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: A place where 'nostalgic and new' are meant to live together in harmony, welcoming families, children and neighbors as a casual everyday gathering spot rather than a high-end establishment.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made fluffy fried donuts (nine varieties, with the standard Oshima Donut a favorite) and hot coffee, plus a here-only original creation, the Shinjika ('sacred deer') Donut Parfait. Coffee is offered at a discount when bought as a donut set.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over the premises of the beloved old neighborhood cafe 'Kissaten Mickey,' reopening as 'Junkissa Shinroku x Oshima Donut Nara' on November 24, 2025, blending the old retro charm with hand-made donuts from a Tokyo donut maker.
An original menu item found nowhere else, the 'Shinjika Donut Parfait' (named for Nara's sacred deer), is served alongside the signature 290-yen fluffy fried donuts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop fries wheat-based donuts on site; no gluten-free menu or dedicated equipment is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-friendly neighborhood coffee shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the donut display will get you by, though staff likely speak limited English.

places.name: 鶴屋徳満 奈良三条店 (Tsuruya Tokuman Nara Sanjo-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese-sweets maker. Its core products are wheat-based: the signature 三笠 (mikasa / dorayaki) is made with wheat flour, eggs and honey, and other baked confections similarly use flour. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no allergen labeling for celiacs, and no separate preparation. Not suitable for those requiring strict gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: As a wagashi shop, some items are plant-based by tradition — e.g. red-bean-paste sweets and the kakigori topped with wasanbon syrup, kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) and kinako (soybean flour) over shaved ice and bean paste contain no animal products in their basic form. However, signature baked items like the 三笠/dorayaki and 上生菓子 contain eggs (and the dorayaki contains honey), and there is no vegan labeling or guarantee. A vegan should ask item-by-item; pure adzuki/kanten/kuromitsu/kinako sweets are the safer choices.
places.hours_raw: 官公式サイト（三条店）: 10:00-18:00、定休日 月曜。食べログ表記: 09:00-18:00。/ Official site (Sanjo branch): 10:00-18:00, closed Mondays. Tabelog lists 09:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: 三条通り周辺（近鉄奈良駅～JR奈良駅の間）/ Sanjo-dori area, central Nara
places.notes: Tsuruya Tokuman is one of Nara's long-established wagashi (traditional Japanese confectionery) houses, founded in 1898 (Meiji 31) after branching off from the older Tsuruya Hachiman lineage, with a confectionery tradition the house describes as spanning well over a century. The s

chef_bio.background: Tsuruya Tokuman is one of Nara's long-established wagashi (traditional Japanese confectionery) houses, founded in 1898 (Meiji 31) after branching off from the older Tsuruya Hachiman lineage, with a confectionery tradition the house describes as spanning well over a century. The shop has a presence on the old Mochiidono / Sanjo arcade streets in the heart of Nara, between Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house emphasizes traditional methods and Nara's regional confectionery heritage, taking pride in long-standing craft; products such as the mikasa have reportedly been presented to the imperial household on multiple occasions.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature product is the 三笠 (mikasa) — a generous dorayaki-style pancake about 13cm across, made with large premium adzuki beans and a moist, fluffy batter, with a celebrated 'kenjo mikasa' version. The house also makes 上生菓子 (seasonal fresh sweets) and, in summer, shaved ice (kakigori) — including a wasanbon-syrup 'an-gori' with house-made bean paste and a version dressed with kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) and kinako (roasted soybean flour).

anecdotes.text: The flagship mikasa is named in reference to the three-layered profile of Mt. Wakakusa, and is baked to stay fresh for about a week.

The Main shop's eat-in café was renovated during the pandemic while preserving vintage elements — repurposing old wooden storage boxes (banju) as counter decor and historic sliding doors as table tops, under a vaulted, window-lit ceiling, often watched over by an octogenarian proprietress.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour environment: baked goods such as dorayaki/mikasa are made with wheat flour on-site, so flour is present and cross-contact with any flour-free items cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eggs and honey are used in the signature dorayaki/mikasa and the premises also handle bean-paste-only and kuromitsu/kinako sweets; shared utensils and surfaces mean cross-contact between egg-containing and plant-based items is plausible. No vegan separation is documented.

positives.text: Several items are inherently plant-based by tradition (adzuki bean paste, kanten, kuromitsu black-sugar syrup, kinako soybean flour over shaved ice), giving vegans some workable choices if they confirm ingredients per item.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, casual neighborhood sweets shop where you can point at what you want; staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a translation app or pointing is your best bet, but a confectionery purchase is low-pressure for a first-timer.

places.name: あぜくらや (Azekuraya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The core menu is wheat-based (udon, somen/nyumen, soba blends, mitarashi dango) and broths use bonito-and-kombu dashi; mitarashi sauce and tare are typically wheat-soy-sauce based. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature noodle and porridge sets use fish (bonito) dashi and include items like herring (nishin) and chicken donburi; sweets are plant-based but the kitchen is built around fish-stock broths. No vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (Tabelog lists 11:00-14:30); during Omizutori (Mar 1-14) approx 12:00-18:00. Closes when sold out.
places.neighborhood: Nara Park / Todai-ji area, near Nigatsudo (Kintetsu Nara Sta
places.cuisine: Traditional Nara teahouse and sweets shop
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Azekuraya is a teahouse and sweets shop that traces its founding to the Edo period, tucked within Nara Park on the grounds near Todai-ji and Nigatsudo. Marked by a deer-motif noren curtain, it serves as a rest stop for visitors exploring the ancient capital.

chef_bio.background: Azekuraya is a teahouse and sweets shop that traces its founding to the Edo period, tucked within Nara Park on the grounds near Todai-ji and Nigatsudo. Marked by a deer-motif noren curtain, it serves as a rest stop for visitors exploring the ancient capital.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as offering seasonal handmade cooking served in simple vessels, a place to pause during sightseeing and take in the atmosphere of old Nara.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato tea rice porridge (chagayu) sets, freshly grilled handmade mitarashi dango, homemade warabi mochi, and Rokusai (six-color) udon and noodle sets in a Kansai-style bonito-and-kombu broth.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for freshly grilled handmade mitarashi dango and self-made warabi mochi, with a chagayu (tea porridge) set presented as a gentle, traditional local Nara dish served with side dishes and pickles.
During the Omizutori festival (March 1-14) at Nigatsudo, the shop shifts to special afternoon-into-evening hours (approximately 12:00-18:00) rather than its usual daytime schedule.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is heavily wheat-based (udon, nyumen/somen, soba, dango) prepared in a shared kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi dango sauce and noodle tare are typically based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle and porridge sets use bonito (fish) dashi broth, and the menu includes herring and chicken dishes, so plant-based sweets share a fish-stock kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual traditional teahouse popular with sightseers near Todai-ji; staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will help.

places.name: ぽくぽく (Poku Poku)
places.gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet) specialist. The signature dish is coated in wheat-flour panko breadcrumbs and is not gluten-free. The accompanying vegetable soup and seasoned sides typically rely on standard dashi and seasonings, and the set rice is blended with barley (a gluten grain). There is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac-safe preparation, and no dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A pork specialty restaurant built entirely around Yamato Pork cutlets, ginger-grilled pork, pork steak, and katsu curry. While the meal includes a notably generous amount of vegetables (a heaping plate of cabbage and a vegetable-rich soup), the soup contains imitation crab and the dishes are not prepared as vegan/vegetarian. No plant-based main is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:15-14:30 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:00). Closed Tuesdays, Fridays, the first Monday of the month, plus irregular closures. Reservation phone hours: 10:00-11:00, 15:00-17:00, 20:00-21:30.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅／JR奈良駅）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Tsuyoshi Akeboshi trained at established Japanese restaurants and well-regarded hotels before opening this small shop in the historic Naramachi district in 2011. He runs it together with his wife as an intimate, reservation-only counter-and-tables eatery.

chef_bio.background: Chef Tsuyoshi Akeboshi trained at established Japanese restaurants and well-regarded hotels before opening this small shop in the historic Naramachi district in 2011. He runs it together with his wife as an intimate, reservation-only counter-and-tables eatery.
chef_bio.philosophy: A health-minded, ingredient-first approach: the finest Yamato Pork cooked gently to highlight its natural sweetness, balanced by an abundance of vegetables and digestive teas before and after the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Tonkatsu made from Yamato Pork (a premium Nara-prefecture brand pork prized for tender meat and sweet, oleic-acid-rich fat). The pork is coated in coarse panko for a light, crisp finish and fried gently in soybean oil at a low temperature (around 160C). It is served not with sauce but with Guerande sea salt from France blended with sweet tea, herbs, and the shop's own spices. Sets come with a heaping plate of cabbage, a vegetable-laden soup, a seasoned side, and rice blended with barley, Job's tears, and legumes; the meal opens with mekabu tea and closes with black oolong tea.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so small and the cooking so deliberate that it operates entirely by reservation, with weekend seats often filling up about a month in advance.
Rather than the usual tonkatsu sauce, diners are given Guerande sea salt blended with sweet tea and spices to draw out the pork's flavor.
Even the cabbage and vegetable soup alone are filling, reflecting the deliberately health-conscious composition of the set meals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature dish is wheat-panko-breaded deep-fried pork; the set rice is blended with barley, both gluten sources, so the meal is not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen or soy-sauce ingredient information is documented; standard Japanese seasonings (typically wheat-containing soy sauce) should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a pork specialty restaurant; even the vegetable soup contains imitation crab and no vegan main is offered.

positives.text: The pork is fried in soybean oil at a low temperature, and meals come with a notably generous amount of vegetables and digestive teas.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, completely reservation-only husband-and-wife shop where you should phone ahead in Japanese and arrive on time, so it is most comfortable for a student who can manage some basic Japanese or bring a Japanese-speaking friend.

places.name: あしゅーら (Ashura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. A signature dish is "gluten curry" made with seitan (wheat gluten, グルテン, made from flour to mimic chicken), and the menu includes other wheat items (scones, omurice, spaghetti, papad). Despite the word "gluten-free" appearing in some third-party listing snippets, the kitchen actively uses wheat gluten and is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetarian establishment using no meat or fish. Many dishes are genuinely vegan (the bean/onion-based curries use no animal products, and dishes without egg, milk, or yogurt are available on request). However, it is not fully vegan: the spicy curry sauce can include yogurt (dairy) for sweetness/tang, and omurice/omelette dishes use eggs. Vegans should specifically ask for egg-, milk-, and yogurt-free preparations, which the kitchen accommodates.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-20:30 (Mon, Tue, Sat, Sun); some sources list 12:00-20:00. Closed Wed, Thu, Fri. May close early when dishes sell out.
places.neighborhood: 京終／ならまち南端（川之上突抜町）
places.cuisine: 古民家のオーガニック・ベジタリアン自然食
places.notes: あしゅーら occupies a roughly 100-year-old machiya (traditional townhouse) tucked into the quiet southern edge of Naramachi, near Kyobate. The old wooden interior is dressed with Indian and Asian crafts and a faintly temple-like, oriental atmosphere, giving the room the feel of a hide

chef_bio.background: あしゅーら occupies a roughly 100-year-old machiya (traditional townhouse) tucked into the quiet southern edge of Naramachi, near Kyobate. The old wooden interior is dressed with Indian and Asian crafts and a faintly temple-like, oriental atmosphere, giving the room the feel of a hideaway rather than a busy cafe.
chef_bio.philosophy: A meat- and fish-free kitchen built on organic, pesticide-free vegetables and brown rice. The mood is deliberately unhurried — guests summon the easygoing owner by ringing a bell or tapping a wooden fish block, and are even told they are welcome to doze off after eating.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature plate is a brown-rice (genmai) vegetable curry. The Calcutta-style sauce gets its depth from pesticide-free onions caramelized slowly and beans blended smooth, so it tastes rich without any meat or fish. A popular variation adds 'gluten' (seitan made from wheat flour) that is crisped up to mimic chicken. Other regulars include tofu croquettes, omurice, soup spaghetti, papad, plus a tea menu with chai and fresh carrot juice and house-baked scones.

anecdotes.text: Guests call the relaxed proprietor not by raising a hand but by ringing a small bell or striking a wooden fish block (mokugyo), and the cafe openly invites diners to take a nap after their meal.
The curry's signature 'gluten' is made from wheat flour and fried until crisp so that it tastes and bites like chicken despite being entirely plant-based.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat gluten (seitan) is a core menu ingredient prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable; the venue is not suitable for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Yogurt (dairy) is used in the spicy curry sauce and eggs are used in omurice/omelette dishes, so vegans must request egg-, milk-, and yogurt-free preparations.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as friendly and happy to explain the food, and dishes made without egg, milk, or yogurt are available on request.

positives.text: Fully meat- and fish-free kitchen using organic, pesticide-free vegetables and brown rice, with many naturally vegan curry options.
English-language menu and barrier-free accommodations (braille menu, guide dogs welcome) make ordering and asking about ingredients easier for foreign visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu (and even a braille menu) is available and guide dogs are welcome, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about egg-, dairy-, or wheat-free options.

places.name: cafe Oasis（カフェ オアシス） (cafe Oasis)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes — wood-fired pizza and pasta dominate — so this is not a celiac-safe destination and there is no indication of dedicated equipment or allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Signature dishes center on beef, chicken, sausage, sea urchin (uni) cream, cheese pizzas and dairy, with no stated plant-based menu. A vegan traveler should not rely on this place without contacting the cafe directly.
places.hours_raw: Wednesday-Sunday and holidays 11:30-21:00 (last food order 20:30); closed Monday and Tuesday. Modeled here as 11:30-20:30 service window per the kitchen last-order time.
places.neighborhood: ならまち / Konishi-cho, near Kintetsu Nara Station (Konishi Saku
places.notes: A café tucked into the Konishi Sakura-dori shopping arcade just a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. Despite sitting in the middle of town, the interior leans into a warm, resort-like feel with abundant wood and greenery, built around a wood-fired oven that sits in ope

chef_bio.background: A café tucked into the Konishi Sakura-dori shopping arcade just a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. Despite sitting in the middle of town, the interior leans into a warm, resort-like feel with abundant wood and greenery, built around a wood-fired oven that sits in open view of diners.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen showcases live wood-fired cooking behind glass, aiming for a relaxed, welcoming spot that works from lunch through an evening drink.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired cooking: stone-oven pizzas and a range of pasta, plus heartier plates like a wood-roasted Joshu beef course with grilled vegetables, homemade-sausage pasta in an almond-basil-tomato-uni sauce, and zucchini pizza with spiced chicken and basil sauce. Lunch sets come with salad and dessert options.

anecdotes.text: The plates come from a chef trained at a popular Western (yoshoku/Italian-style) restaurant, which the cafe credits for its hearty, full-flavored cooking.
The wood-fired oven is the centerpiece of the room, with cooking visible through glass for a 'live' dining feel.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, spacious wood-and-greenery café where a foreign student can comfortably order pizza or pasta with a few words of Japanese and a pointed menu, though staff English may be limited.

places.name: 西村邸 (Nishimura-tei)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The café centers on rice-based dishes (hojicha-cooked rice, miso soup, seasonal small plates, Nara pickles), and a homestyle rice meal can be relatively low in obvious wheat, but miso, Nara pickles (often soy/koji-based), and any soy seasoning may contain wheat, and there is no stated celiac/cross-contamination protocol. Galettes and breads on the menu contain gluten. A celiac traveler must ask directly (info@nishimuratei.com / SNS) before relying on it.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetarian and vegan diners can be accommodated for the signature tea-rice set (chameshi-zen): hojicha-cooked rice, miso soup, seasonal vegetable small dishes, and Nara pickles, served without the optional egg ('okan no tamagoyaki' is a paid add-on). Accommodation is documented in travel coverage; because dashi and miso choices are not specified online, a strict vegan should confirm the broth and seasonings (no fish dashi/bonito) when ordering or reserving.
places.hours_raw: Café 11:00–17:00; closed Thursdays. (Lodging check-in 14:00–20:00, check-out by 10:00.) Hours may vary — check SNS.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (south end), Hanazono-chō — roughly 15 min walk fr
places.cuisine: Machiya café in a renovated century-old town
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nishimura-tei occupies a Nara machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) more than 100 years old that opened to the public in autumn 2019. The owner, Sugimoto Yuta, returned to his hometown after working in graphic design and food service, and renovated the family house — keeping ori

chef_bio.background: Nishimura-tei occupies a Nara machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) more than 100 years old that opened to the public in autumn 2019. The owner, Sugimoto Yuta, returned to his hometown after working in graphic design and food service, and renovated the family house — keeping original stairs, beams, and the gardens — into a guesthouse with a small café and rental space at the quiet southern edge of Naramachi.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for food you could eat every day without tiring of it — gentle on the body and quietly nostalgic, evoking the comfort of a grandmother's home. The whole place is built around an 'obāchan-chi' (grandmother's house) feeling of unhurried rest.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the 'everyday tea-rice set' (毎日の茶飯膳): rice cooked with local Nara hojicha roasted tea, miso soup, seasonal vegetable small dishes, and Nara pickles, with an optional homemade rolled egg ('okan no tamagoyaki') as an add-on. The café also serves coffee, tea, and sweets sourced from local Nara shops, plus seasonal items like a winter hibachi (charcoal-brazier) service with toasted handmade bread and mochi.

anecdotes.text: The building was the owner's own family townhouse, restored rather than rebuilt, so guests sit among the original stairs, beams, and two small gardens.
A 'book-trade coffee' idea lets visitors swap a book recommendation for a coffee, reflecting the relaxed, conversational spirit of the house.
Sitting between JR Nara and Kintetsu Nara stations at the south end of Naramachi, it's pitched as a calm rest stop for people walking the old town.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Travel coverage states that vegetarian and vegan guests can be accommodated for the tea-rice set, indicating the kitchen is willing to adjust, but no formal allergy/cross-contact protocol is published.

positives.text: The core meal is a simple, rice-and-vegetable homestyle set with miso soup and Nara pickles, and the egg is an optional add-on, making it easy to keep the plate plant-based on request.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, intimate family-run machiya with just a few seats, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps; the owner is welcoming to travelers, but it has a homey "grandmother's house" feel rather than a tourist-restaurant setup.

places.name: ロッカン ルーム（ROKKAN ROOM） (ROKKAN ROOM)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature nyūmen is made with Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles), and the menu features curries and a custard pudding. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodations are documented; broths almost certainly contain wheat-based seasonings and the place is not set up for cross-contamination control.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Some dishes appear plant-leaning (spiced fried-tofu nyūmen, chai-infused red-bean zenzai), but nyūmen broth is typically made with fish dashi and curries are not confirmed animal-free; the pudding uses Nara eggs. A flexible vegetarian might find an item by asking, but vegans should not assume any dish is safe without confirming dashi and curry ingredients.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-18:00; Sun 11:00-17:00; closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: ならまち（元林院町・近鉄奈良駅周辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ROKKAN ROOM occupies a townhouse more than 140 years old in Motobayashiin-cho, once the heart of Nara's old flower district just south of Sarusawa Pond. The building was formerly an okiya (a residence for geisha), and owner Moe Oyagi actually lived in it before reimagining it as

chef_bio.background: ROKKAN ROOM occupies a townhouse more than 140 years old in Motobayashiin-cho, once the heart of Nara's old flower district just south of Sarusawa Pond. The building was formerly an okiya (a residence for geisha), and owner Moe Oyagi actually lived in it before reimagining it as a café. She kept the layers of its past intact: the original earthen-floored entry, a storehouse (kura), repurposed lacquer tables from the okiya, and a wartime air-raid shelter in the basement now softened with tatami and cushions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking around the themes of fermentation and spice using local Nara ingredients, herbs and medicinal touches, while preserving and celebrating the building's many historical layers through a deliberately eclectic old-meets-new design.
chef_bio.specialty: Nyūmen made with Nara's local Miwa sōmen, served with a spiced fried-tofu pouch that releases curry when broken open; a chai-infused zenzai (red-bean soup) layering cinnamon, ginger and clove over strained sweet beans; and a custard pudding made with Nara-prefecture eggs.

anecdotes.text: The basement, a former air-raid shelter, has low ceilings and bare concrete walls but is laid with tatami and plush cushions, with speakers playing hypersonic sound to help guests relax in a space that feels cut off from the modern world.
A second-floor gallery room displays works by knit artist Chihiro Hasunuma and features a round table that converts to a kotatsu in winter.
The chai-tea zenzai was born from one of the owner's inspirations: the aroma reads as chai, the first sip tastes like zenzai, and the finish returns to chai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature nyūmen uses Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and the menu includes curries, so wheat is central to the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Nyūmen broth is typically fish-based and the custard pudding uses eggs; no vegan menu or animal-free assurances were found.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, art-filled café where a warm greeting in Japanese goes a long way; staff may not speak much English, so a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.

places.name: TABI Coffee Roaster (TABI Coffee Roaster)
places.gf_detail: A specialty coffee stand, not a gluten-free destination. The only food offered is the owner's wife's homemade baked goods (canelé, biscotti), which are wheat-based. No gluten-free items or celiac handling are documented. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but there is no dedicated GF preparation.
places.vegan_detail: This is primarily a coffee stand. Black coffee is plant-based, but milk-based drinks use dairy and no plant-milk option is documented. The accompanying baked goods (canelé, biscotti) typically contain eggs and dairy. No vegan menu or labeling exists; a vegan visitor would need to stick to black coffee and ask about any food.
places.hours_raw: 食べログ: 平日 12:00～18:00 / 祝日 12:00～17:00。定休: 水曜・第1第3日曜ほか不定休。（別ソースのNarakkoは9:30～19:00、日祝～18:00と記載があり営業時間は変動あり）
places.neighborhood: ならまち / 椿井市場（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒースタンド
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] TABI Coffee Roaster opened on July 18, 2017, tucked inside the retro Tsubai Market (椿井市場) arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner spent roughly 11-13 years training as a roaster at the well-known Nara coffee house Kuroko before striking out on his own, and he ru

chef_bio.background: TABI Coffee Roaster opened on July 18, 2017, tucked inside the retro Tsubai Market (椿井市場) arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner spent roughly 11-13 years training as a roaster at the well-known Nara coffee house Kuroko before striking out on his own, and he runs the stand together with his wife, who hand-bakes the pastries.
