chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on careful, small-batch hand-roasting using fully hand-picked premium beans, aiming to make fresh, approachable coffee that appeals to a wide range of ages.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin and blend coffees, including the signature TABI Blend and a deeper-roast version, plus around eight rotating single origins. The shop is small-batch and hands-on: the owner hand-picks world-class green beans to remove defects, then roasts in small lots from medium to dark, checking temperature minute by minute. His wife's homemade canelé and biscotti accompany the coffee.

anecdotes.text: The owner hand-picks each batch of world-class green beans to remove poor beans before roasting in small lots, measuring and adjusting the temperature every minute across medium to deep roasts.

The shop is hidden inside a retro market arcade, standing out for its simple glass-sliding-door aesthetic among the surrounding old vendors; the owner's wife bakes the canelé and biscotti served alongside the coffee.

positives.text: Black drip and single-origin coffees are the core offering and are naturally free of gluten and animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, friendly owner-run coffee stand in a retro market arcade; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, though ordering coffee is simple and approachable for a foreign student.

places.name: わんずはーと・かふぇ ならまち店 (Wanzu Heart Cafe Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature items include fried shrimp and chicken (wheat batter) and a kudzu-udon pasta dish; an official allergen note for the lunch lists shrimp, wheat and eggs. Soy sauce and miso here are standard wheat-containing types, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a homestyle Japanese cafe whose signature plates feature fried chicken, fried shrimp, fish, miso soup and egg-based items. No vegan menu is documented and dishes rely on standard Japanese seasonings and likely fish dashi, so it is not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat & holidays 9:00-17:00 (food L.O. 16:00, drink L.O. 16:30); Sun 9:00-19:00/20:00 (food L.O. 18:00/19:00). Closed Wednesdays. Evening dining available by phone consultation.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良ホテル近く、ならまち南側）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run Japanese cafe tucked into a quiet lane near the Nara Hotel on the southern edge of the old Naramachi district. It pairs casual creative Japanese cooking with house-made sweets in a relaxed, homey setting, and welcomes guests with pet-friendly outdoor se

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run Japanese cafe tucked into a quiet lane near the Nara Hotel on the southern edge of the old Naramachi district. It pairs casual creative Japanese cooking with house-made sweets in a relaxed, homey setting, and welcomes guests with pet-friendly outdoor seating on fine days.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on local Nara ingredients and seasonal home-style cooking, refreshed frequently so regulars find something new.
chef_bio.specialty: The standout is the 'Wanzu Heart no Gohan' plate (around 1,400 yen): a main dish with several small seasonal sides, miso soup and pickles built around Nara-grown vegetables and locally produced Hinohikari rice. A playful 'grown-up's children's lunch' adds fried chicken and fried shrimp with house-made tartar sauce, and a distinctive Yoshino kudzu 'udon pasta' offers a slippery, chewy noodle in a crab-tomato cream sauce. Menus rotate roughly every two weeks with the seasons.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Nara's own produce and Hinohikari rice, and turns Yoshino arrowroot (kuzu) into a chewy noodle served as a crab-tomato cream 'udon pasta,' giving the menu a distinctly local Nara character.

positives.text: An official Nara tourism listing publishes an allergen note for the signature lunch, indicating the kitchen is aware of allergens (it lists shrimp, wheat and eggs).

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally-run Japanese cafe on a quiet backstreet where staff likely speak limited English, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.

places.name: 空気ケーキ。 (Kuuki Cake)
places.gf_detail: This is a cake and baked-goods shop. The signature "air cake," rolls, tarts, sponge cakes, cookies, and sablés are all built on wheat flour, so the menu is fundamentally not gluten-free. No gluten-free items or dedicated GF preparation are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The shop's cakes are made with fresh eggs and milk (the owner specifically highlights using fresh eggs and milk for the delicate texture), plus dairy mousse and butter-based pastries. No vegan/plant-based items are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Shop sales 9:00–18:00; café seating 10:00–17:30 (last order 17:00). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays (open if these fall on a national holiday).
places.neighborhood: Takahata-cho (near Nara Park / Kasuga Taisha, Kintetsu Nara
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Yoshiro Wakita trained as a chief patissier before opening his own shop in Takahata-cho, a quiet residential pocket at the foot of Kasuga Mountain where cake shops were scarce. The location took several years to ready, partly because deer wandering over from nearby Nar

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Yoshiro Wakita trained as a chief patissier before opening his own shop in Takahata-cho, a quiet residential pocket at the foot of Kasuga Mountain where cake shops were scarce. The location took several years to ready, partly because deer wandering over from nearby Nara Park kept eating the greenery during the build.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wakita aims for 'lightness' in everything, and wants the shop to be an 'air-like presence' that quietly stays close to people's hearts, using carefully chosen local Nara ingredients, fresh eggs, and milk.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 'air cake' — a coromboid, ultra-light dome of airy sponge wrapping a soft milk mousse, made in flavors such as plain, matcha, orange, and blueberry cheese. Other favorites include a Kotoka local-strawberry tart built on a cookie-style crust and seasonal fruit cakes.

anecdotes.text: Wakita says the air cake grew out of his love of dorayaki/mikasa (red-bean pancakes), which he reimagined in cake form: 'I took the dorayaki I loved and arranged it my own way into something cake-like,' aiming for a sweet as light as air, as if it could float away.
He developed the cake by building up an image step by step — starting from the name, then the fluffy texture and rounded shape — drawing on the mikasa pancake combined with mousse.
Readying the site took several years because deer from nearby Nara Park kept coming over and eating the foliage during construction.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour sponge, tart, and cookie items are made throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cakes are made with fresh eggs, milk, and dairy mousse, so cross-contact with animal products is inherent to the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood cake café where pointing at the display case works fine, though staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.

places.name: 㐂つね (Kitsune)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly by default. Sukiyaki uses warishita (a soy-sauce-based broth), and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; the menu also includes udon, which is wheat. No gluten-free accommodations or dedicated handling are documented, so a celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: This is a beef sukiyaki specialist; the entire concept centers on Yamato beef, and courses use fish-based dashi and soy-based seasonings. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00; closed Wednesdays (also 12/31, 1/1)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Motorin'in-cho, inside the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Bui
places.notes: Kitsune sits on the ground floor of the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building, a designed complex in the traditional streetscape of Naramachi's Motorin'in-cho. The building's name nods to its three tenants -- a deer (Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten), a monkey (a coffee shop), and a fox (kitsune

chef_bio.background: Kitsune sits on the ground floor of the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building, a designed complex in the traditional streetscape of Naramachi's Motorin'in-cho. The building's name nods to its three tenants -- a deer (Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten), a monkey (a coffee shop), and a fox (kitsune), this restaurant. It is run under the direction of Shusaku Toriba, the chef behind the acclaimed Tokyo restaurant 'sio,' who set out to reimagine sukiyaki here.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team describes the cooking as a 'reconstruction' of sukiyaki -- the playful name expresses the wish to magically transform sukiyaki into something even more delicious. The attention to detail extends beyond the plate to the cutlery (sourced via Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten) and the interior design.
chef_bio.specialty: Sukiyaki built around Yamato beef, prized for its balance of lean meat and marbling. Signature offerings include sukiyaki courses, a sukiyaki donburi and uni-topped sukiyaki donburi, a chirashi-style lunch with Yamato beef over vinegared rice layered with uni and other toppings, and house desserts like a mitarashi pudding.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name and the building it lives in form a deliberate trio -- deer, monkey and fox -- tying the place to the surrounding Nara culture and its neighboring shops.
The team explains they chose the name Kitsune (fox) because they wanted to 'magically transform' sukiyaki into something even more delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sukiyaki is cooked with a soy-sauce-based warishita broth and the menu includes wheat-based udon, so gluten is integral to the core dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki here uses a soy-sauce-based seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around beef and uses fish-based dashi, so there is no vegan-safe preparation documented.

cultural_comfort.note: English-language reservations are supported and the polished setting in a designed Naramachi complex makes it approachable, though it is a refined sit-down restaurant where booking ahead is expected.

places.name: 麺喰 (Udon and Cafe Menkui)
places.gf_detail: A hand-made udon shop, and udon is a wheat-flour noodle, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. Broths and sides also rely on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, etc.). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Menus center on udon in fish/meat-based broths (tori tempura, chicken-and-egg bukkake, offal miso udon, beef stew udon), so most dishes contain fish dashi, meat, or egg. A plain udon like kake or zaru udon might be adaptable, but the broth and dipping sauces typically use fish dashi, so vegans would need to ask directly. No documented vegan options.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Fukuchiin-cho), south of Nara Park; walkable from
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menkui is a small, family-run udon shop set inside a roughly 100-year-old townhouse renovated into a relaxed, home-like dining space where guests remove their shoes before sitting down. The owner trained in udon-making in Sanuki (Kagawa Prefecture), Japan's most famous udon regio

chef_bio.origin: Sanuki (Kagawa Prefecture) udon tradition, brought to Naramachi, Nara.
chef_bio.background: Menkui is a small, family-run udon shop set inside a roughly 100-year-old townhouse renovated into a relaxed, home-like dining space where guests remove their shoes before sitting down. The owner trained in udon-making in Sanuki (Kagawa Prefecture), Japan's most famous udon region, and brought that craft to a quiet lane in the Naramachi area near Fukuchiin Temple, just south of Nara Park.
chef_bio.philosophy: A seasonal approach to the noodle itself: the flour blend is changed between summer and winter so the udon suits the season, with summer noodles made a touch softer for a smoother throat feel.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made udon with a chewy, mochi-like texture and a smooth finish. Signature dishes include chicken-and-egg bukkake udon (tori oyako bukkake) with crispy juicy chicken tempura and a soft-boiled egg, the zaru udon and tori-ten set, offal-miso udon, and a beef-stew udon. A popular ~1,000-yen set pairs a monthly-changing udon with a side, ancient-grains rice (kodaimai), pickles, and dessert.

anecdotes.text: There is a glass-walled work area where you can watch the udon being made, and diners describe hearing a steady pounding sound from the noodle-kneading while they eat.
The interior is stocked with books and magazines so it doubles as a calm cafe space, and it is child-friendly with high chairs available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-made wheat udon is the central product and is prepared on-site, so flour and gluten are pervasive throughout the kitchen; this is not a safe environment for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built around fish/meat-based broths and toppings (tori tempura, chicken-and-egg, offal miso, beef stew), so shared broth and cooking surfaces make vegan cross-contact likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop's listing invites guests to contact them directly with any questions, but no specific allergy-handling procedures are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, family-run shop where you take off your shoes to sit in a converted old house; little to no English is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the photo menu) help, but the atmosphere is welcoming and casual.

places.name: 釜粋 (Kamaiki)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle udon specialty shop — udon, tsukemen and tempura are all wheat-based, so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free options or precautions are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Broths are built on fish/meat dashi (e.g. the signature curry udon uses domestic beef-tendon stock combined with the house dashi), and standard udon dashi and soy-based sauces are not vegan. No documented vegan dishes.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade (Higashimuki-minamicho), ~2 min
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Masahiro Shimoda runs this small udon house rooted in the Sanuki (Kagawa) udon tradition, where he blends his own flour, works the dough carefully, and cuts fresh noodles daily in the workshop at the back of the shop.

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Masahiro Shimoda runs this small udon house rooted in the Sanuki (Kagawa) udon tradition, where he blends his own flour, works the dough carefully, and cuts fresh noodles daily in the workshop at the back of the shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 粋 (iki) stands for the shop's three commitments: quality of flavor, the freshness of made-to-order food, and warm, heartfelt service, with staff offering lively welcomes and farewells.
chef_bio.specialty: Sanuki-style handmade udon served many ways: zaru (cold) udon that shows off the wheat's natural sweetness, freshly fried chicken-and-chikuwa tempura, and a rich curry udon made from slow-cooked domestic beef-tendon stock with caramelized onions and the house broth; a chige hot-pot udon appears in winter.

anecdotes.text: Though grounded in Kagawa's udon culture, the shop aims to serve udon equal to or even surpassing the home prefecture's standard, using a uniquely blended flour and daily fresh-cut noodles.
Noodles are made by the owner in a workshop at the back of the kitchen, with careful attention to temperature and water, and tempura is fried to order for maximum freshness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is wheat udon made in-house and tempura fried in shared oil; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bukkake and dipping styles use soy-based sauces/broths, which conventionally contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths rely on fish/meat dashi (the curry udon uses beef-tendon stock), so no separated vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, locally loved udon counter where a little Japanese helps with ordering; menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app is handy for a foreign student.

places.name: 割烹 まつ㐂 (Kappou Matsuki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki/kappo house whose courses are built on fish/kombu dashi and almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. No gluten-free program is documented and the omakase format gives the diner no control over ingredients. Not suitable for celiacs. A gluten-sensitive diner could in principle ask in advance, but there is no evidence of accommodation.
places.vegan_detail: Although the kitchen showcases Yamato vegetables, the omakase menu is centered on fish, Yamato beef sukiyaki, cod milt, venison, and fish dashi. It is not a vegetarian or vegan destination. No vegan course is documented; a fully plant-based meal is not realistic here.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-14:30 (seating starts at noon, punctual arrival required); Dinner 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 19:00). Reservations taken 10:00-22:00. Closed Sundays and 1st/3rd Mondays (full closure); 2nd/4th Mondays and 1st/3rd Tuesdays are dinner-only (closed for lunch).
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (relocated to Fushinkezushicho, near Nara Hotel; ~
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Osaka-born chef Katsuya Matsumuro trained at established kitchens including Kawanami in Nara and a house in Osaka's Ajiyoshicho before striking out on his own. He chose Nara for its distinctive, unhurried character and opened his solo kappo here.

chef_bio.background: Osaka-born chef Katsuya Matsumuro trained at established kitchens including Kawanami in Nara and a house in Osaka's Ajiyoshicho before striking out on his own. He chose Nara for its distinctive, unhurried character and opened his solo kappo here.
chef_bio.philosophy: He prizes the leisurely flow of time in Nara and wants diners to settle into that rhythm, which is why he runs the room as an omakase-only, reservation-required counter. The house motto is bimi-magokoro — sincerity expressed through delicious food and warm hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Chef's-choice omakase kaiseki built on seasonal Yamato ingredients. Signature plates include a thick tamago omelet slow-baked for about three hours at low temperature (so beloved that guests ask for seconds), Yamato beef sukiyaki, a sesame-tofu soup with Yamato turnips and carrots and seared cod milt, and a hassun plate featuring local black soybeans dusted with gold leaf and Soni venison.

anecdotes.text: The thick tamago omelet is baked slowly at low temperature for roughly three hours, and it is so popular that diners regularly request a second helping.

After eight years building a following in Naramachi's Higashijodocho, the restaurant relocated in December 2025 to a renovated Taisho-era timber merchant's machiya near the Nara Hotel, keeping the warm townhouse atmosphere intact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kappo/kaiseki kitchen, dishes rely on dashi and standard Japanese seasonings, which typically include wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are centered on fish, cod milt, Yamato beef, and venison alongside fish/kombu dashi, so vegan diners cannot avoid animal products in the omakase format.

cultural_comfort.note: A formal, reservation-only counter restaurant where punctual arrival is enforced and the courses run ¥8,800 and up — comfortable if you have basic Japanese and dining etiquette, but daunting and pricey for a casual student visit.

places.name: 路地裏のおにぎり屋さん 一穂二穂 (Rojiura no Onigiri-yasan Ippo Niho)
places.gf_detail: An onigiri shop built on rice (Hinohikari), nori, and salt, so the simplest salted rice balls are naturally wheat-free. However it is not a gluten-free establishment: many fillings are soy-sauce based (egg-yolk soy sauce, etc.), and several offerings use prepared broths simmered from fish that may contain soy/wheat seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated prep, so a celiac traveler would need to ask which specific onigiri are wheat-free and accept cross-contamination risk in a small shared kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The owner prepares broths from multiple kinds of fish, and popular items use meat (ginger meat soboro, yuzu-miso meat crumbles, meat-wrapped onigiri sticks) and egg. A plain salt onigiri or a nori/pickle (Nara-zuke) onigiri may be plant-based, but vegans should confirm each filling individually as fish-broth and animal fillings dominate the menu.
places.hours_raw: 平日 11:00〜20:00 / 土日祝 10:30〜（お米がなくなり次第終了）。定休日：火曜・水曜
places.neighborhood: 下御門商店街（ならまち／近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked down an alley in the Shimomikado arcade of Naramachi, a short walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station, this small onigiri shop opened in June 2023. You find it beside a roasted-sweet-potato shop, marked by a little blue sign. The owner makes each rice ball to order from freshl

chef_bio.background: Tucked down an alley in the Shimomikado arcade of Naramachi, a short walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station, this small onigiri shop opened in June 2023. You find it beside a roasted-sweet-potato shop, marked by a little blue sign. The owner makes each rice ball to order from freshly cooked rice, so they arrive warm and just-pressed.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's name and approach center on the idea that the rice and the fillings should lift each other up rather than compete. The owner pays close attention to how much air to fold in while shaping so each grain stays distinct yet melts gently in the mouth, avoids chemical seasonings, and tunes salt, fillings, and broths through repeated tasting.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order onigiri in around a dozen varieties, from a simple salted rice ball to fillings like ginger meat soboro, yuzu-miso meat crumbles, Nara-zuke pickles, and a meat-wrapped onigiri stick. Several are paired with broths the owner simmers from fish, and vegetables in them are finely chopped so even children eat them happily.

anecdotes.text: The owner is devoted to the rice itself, choosing a Hinohikari from a grower, pairing it with Ariake nori and Goto salt, and even letting prepared ingredients rest overnight so they marry well with the rice.
Inside there is a small grassy spot where you slip off your shoes and eat picnic-style after a stroll through Naramachi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several fillings are soy-sauce based (for example egg-yolk soy sauce), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The owner simmers broths from multiple kinds of fish and many fillings are meat or egg, so animal ingredients are prepared in the same small kitchen.

positives.text: Owner-made food with carefully selected, simple core ingredients (a chosen rice, Ariake nori, Goto salt) and a stated avoidance of chemical seasonings, so the owner can usually explain exactly what is in each onigiri.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run counter where ordering is simple and pointing at the day's onigiri works fine, though menus and the owner's chat are in Japanese, so a few friendly words go a long way.

places.name: おむすび 番旬 (Omusubi Banshun)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. Onigiri fillings and the soy-sauce version of the Nara tea-rice rice ball use Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Other fillings (salt-koji salmon, mackerel takikomi rice, kombu) and takikomi-style rices are seasoned with soy sauce and broth that are very likely to contain wheat, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation or allergen control. A celiac traveler should not assume any item is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. Some items are plant-based in principle: the salted version of the Nara tea-rice onigiri (rice cooked with roasted hojicha tea and soybeans) and pickled-plum-and-shiso onigiri contain no animal ingredients, and the soy-sauce tea-rice version is plant-based aside from the soy sauce. However, many of the ~10-12 daily varieties use fish, egg, or meat (salt-koji grilled salmon, mackerel-and-ginger rice, tamagoyaki, fried chicken), and the complimentary dine-in miso soup likely uses fish (katsuo/bonito) dashi. No vegan labeling or dashi sourcing is documented, so a vegan should ask item by item.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (Thursday closed; some sources note an irregular schedule)
places.neighborhood: ならまち寄り（餅飯殿センター街 / 下御門商店街エリア、近鉄奈良駅周辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Omusubi Banshun opened on December 20, 2023 along Mochiidono Center Street, said to be Nara's oldest shopping arcade, in the Naramachi-leaning area near Kintetsu Nara Station. It is an open-fronted little shop where rice balls are displayed on one side and a small eat-in counter

chef_bio.background: Omusubi Banshun opened on December 20, 2023 along Mochiidono Center Street, said to be Nara's oldest shopping arcade, in the Naramachi-leaning area near Kintetsu Nara Station. It is an open-fronted little shop where rice balls are displayed on one side and a small eat-in counter sits on the other.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around reviving and sharing a Nara regional dish: Nara tea rice was eaten as a quick, nourishing meal along the Edo-period Tokaido, and the owner uses the modern onigiri boom as a way to spotlight that local heritage rather than simply follow a trend.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature item is the Nara tea-rice (奈良茶飯) onigiri, available salted or with soy sauce, made from rice cooked together with roasted soybeans and an original house blend of hojicha (roasted tea). Around 10-12 handmade varieties are offered daily, with the salt-koji grilled salmon and kombu among the top sellers; each onigiri comes with a sheet of nori that diners wrap themselves so it stays crisp, and dine-in guests get a free bowl of miso soup.

anecdotes.text: The shop and its owner Maekawa Hiroki were featured on TBS's nationally broadcast 'Matsuko no Shiranai Sekai' (Matsuko's Unknown World) onigiri episode that aired April 23, 2024.
Nara tea rice is described as a balanced staple of rice and soybeans with good satiety, and the shop pre-roasts the soybeans and cooks them with its own hojicha blend.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Nara tea-rice onigiri is offered in a soy-sauce version, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; takikomi-style rices such as the mackerel-and-ginger onigiri are also typically cooked with soy-sauce-based seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many of the daily varieties contain fish, egg, or meat (salt-koji grilled salmon, mackerel rice, tamagoyaki, fried chicken), and the complimentary dine-in miso soup is not documented as vegan, so plant-based items are prepared in the same shop alongside animal products.

positives.text: The salted Nara tea-rice onigiri (rice with roasted soybeans and hojicha) and the pickled-plum-and-shiso onigiri are plant-based in their core ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, friendly grab-and-go onigiri counter where ordering is simple by pointing, but staff communication is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help.

places.name: 大和野菜と串焼き やまと 近鉄奈良店 (Yamato Yasai to Kushiyaki Yamato Kintetsu Na)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a sake-focused izakaya built around grilled skewers, chicken hot pots and creative dishes that rely on soy-sauce-based tare and fish/chicken dashi; no gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Skewer glazes and most seasonings contain wheat-based soy sauce, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Primarily a meat- and chicken-centric izakaya (Yamato free-range chicken tataki, skewers, 100-hour chicken broth hot pot). It does showcase seasonal Yamato vegetables (e.g. grilled Yamato negi, Tsutsui lotus root) so a few vegetable dishes exist, but these are typically grilled with non-vegan seasonings or served alongside meat, and no vegan menu is offered. A vegan would have very limited, uncertain options and should ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 15:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Open year-round (年中無休).
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 大和野菜・創作串焼き居酒屋
places.notes: A second-floor izakaya in the Higashimuki shopping arcade just two minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station, built entirely around the flavors of Nara itself. The kitchen sources Yamato vegetables, Yamato free-range chicken (Yamato niku-dori) and prefectural produce such as Tsutsui lot

chef_bio.background: A second-floor izakaya in the Higashimuki shopping arcade just two minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station, built entirely around the flavors of Nara itself. The kitchen sources Yamato vegetables, Yamato free-range chicken (Yamato niku-dori) and prefectural produce such as Tsutsui lotus root and Yamato long onions, turning them into creative grilled skewers and regional hot pots.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let guests taste Nara in one sitting through its local sake, regional chicken and prefecture-grown vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative skewers of Yamato vegetables and Yamato free-range chicken, morning-fresh chicken tataki, a Yamato-chicken water hot pot built on a house broth simmered for 100 hours, and Asuka-nabe, a Nara specialty hot pot. The drinks list carries more than 20 Nara local sake at all times, with tasting flights so guests can compare regional brews.

anecdotes.text: The signature Yamato-chicken water hot pot is built on a house broth the kitchen simmers for 100 hours.
More than 20 varieties of Nara local sake are kept in stock at all times, served as tasting sets for exploring regional brews.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on grilled skewers and tare-glazed dishes plus soy-based seasonings typical of izakaya cooking; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free claims are made.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetables are grilled on the same skewer station and in shared hot pots with chicken and meat; the headline broths are chicken-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way; staff are used to serving Nara residents more than international guests.

places.name: たかばたけ茶論 (Takabatake Salon)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. This is a café serving coffee, tea, and homemade cakes/cookies — wheat-based baked goods. Nothing on the menu or any fetched source indicates gluten-free items or any allergy accommodation, and the kitchen is a small home operation, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan items are documented; the food (cheesecake, cookies, ice cream) is dairy/egg based, so only black coffee or plain tea would reliably suit a vegan — confirm any food on the day.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists Fri–Sun 14:00–18:00, closed Mon–Thu. Some tourism listings give 11:00–18:00. Hours appear limited and may vary seasonally; confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-chō (next to the former Shiga Naoya residence, to
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The café occupies a Western-style residence built in the Taishō era, set in a garden shaded by a Himalayan cedar more than a century old, right beside the former home of novelist Shiga Naoya. The house was inherited by Western-style painter Nakamura Kazuo from his father, and he

chef_bio.chef_name: Nakamura Kazuo (中村一雄) and his wife
chef_bio.background: The café occupies a Western-style residence built in the Taishō era, set in a garden shaded by a Himalayan cedar more than a century old, right beside the former home of novelist Shiga Naoya. The house was inherited by Western-style painter Nakamura Kazuo from his father, and he and his wife opened the family garden to the public as a café.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners hope to revive the spirit of the 'Takabatake Salon,' the gathering of writers and painters that formed around Shiga Naoya's residence in the early Shōwa period, wishing for new culture to take root in Nara once again.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee, café au lait, and tea served alongside homemade desserts — cheesecake, cookies, and ice cream — enjoyed at indoor tables or on the garden terrace.

anecdotes.text: In the early Shōwa period, many literary figures and painters gathered at Shiga Naoya's nearby residence in a circle known as the 'Takabatake Salon'; the painter-owners named their café in its memory and opened their own garden to continue that tradition.
The terrace looks out on a Taishō-era Provençal-style house and a Himalayan cedar over 100 years old, giving visitors a tangible sense of the area's history.

positives.text: A small, calm garden café with a relaxed atmosphere where a simple order of black coffee or plain tea is straightforward.

cultural_comfort.note: A tranquil family-run garden café in a century-old artist's residence; an English menu is unlikely, so pointing at items or a few Japanese phrases will help, and a visiting friend or guide makes it smoother.

places.name: ろくさろん (Rokusaron)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house whose menu centers on coffee, cakes, and light dishes such as pilaf sets — all wheat-containing or prepared in a shared kitchen. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of celiac-safe handling, so it is not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Offerings are typical kissaten fare (coffee, cakes likely made with dairy and eggs, pilaf), so a vegan would need to ask directly and options are likely very limited. A black coffee or tea is the safest bet.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち南部・京終／新薬師寺方面）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rokusaron sits in a quiet residential pocket of Takahata, on the path between Shin-Yakushiji Temple and Nara Park. It occupies a white-walled house that visitors compare to a private villa, with high ceilings, big windows, and a tree-filled courtyard. The interior is painted in w

chef_bio.background: Rokusaron sits in a quiet residential pocket of Takahata, on the path between Shin-Yakushiji Temple and Nara Park. It occupies a white-walled house that visitors compare to a private villa, with high ceilings, big windows, and a tree-filled courtyard. The interior is painted in warm greens with wooden touches, giving the feeling of a café tucked inside a forest.
chef_bio.philosophy: More a salon than an ordinary café: the name 'salon' is earned, as the space functions as a community gathering place rather than a quick coffee stop.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee and café au lait served in pretty patterned cups, cakes, and light meals such as pilaf sets, enjoyed at an unhurried pace.

anecdotes.text: The place draws many lively older regulars and hosts cultural gatherings such as classical-music talks, group singing, and piano recitals on its in-house piano, giving it the feel of a vibrant community salon.
A separate tea-room annex can be reserved in advance for a quieter, more private tea time.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an old-fashioned local salon frequented mostly by older Japanese regulars and run in Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases and a relaxed, respectful manner will make a foreign student feel most welcome.

places.name: CHAMI（チャミ） (CHAMI)
places.gf_detail: This is a coffee-and-pastry cafe; signature items such as the chouquettes, citrus tart, flaune, scones and canelés are all wheat-flour baked goods. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Drinks include dairy lattes and the sweets are butter/cream/egg-based French-style pastries; plant-milk substitution for coffee may be possible but is unconfirmed, so a vegan would need to ask in person.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・ならまち
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー・カフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] CHAMI is a self-roasting specialty coffee shop set in a bright, white-walled, glass-fronted building a short walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station, on the edge of the Naramachi old town. The second floor offers window-side seating overlooking the street, with an espresso machine vi

chef_bio.background: CHAMI is a self-roasting specialty coffee shop set in a bright, white-walled, glass-fronted building a short walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station, on the edge of the Naramachi old town. The second floor offers window-side seating overlooking the street, with an espresso machine visible at the entrance so guests can watch drinks being prepared.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on in-house roasting and pairing carefully chosen coffees with pastries — a 'journey of discovery' through specialty coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: In-house roasted specialty coffee (Geisha and single-origin beans plus the house CHAMI Blend) and a signature CHAMI Latte, alongside French-style sweets such as chouquettes, citrus tart, flaune, scones and canelés. Drink lineup also includes matcha and chocolate frappes, fruit sodas and coffee-based alcoholic drinks.

anecdotes.text: The cafe was created around world-class barista talent and is known locally for award-winning latte art and championship-level espresso work.

cultural_comfort.note: An English version of the website exists and the modern specialty-coffee setting is welcoming to foreign visitors, so ordering coffee and sweets here should feel comfortable for an international student.

places.name: ウエルト（huerto） (Huerto)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The house signature is the wife's handmade natural-yeast (wheat) sourdough bread, lunch is sandwich-based, and Spanish tapas commonly use flour and wheat-containing soy/sauces. No gluten-free accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen makes heavy use of home-grown vegetables (cauliflower potage, spinach Spanish omelet, pan con tomate), so some vegetable plates exist, but this is a Spanish bar built around jamón, seafood, paella and egg-based tortilla, plus wheat sourdough bread. There is no documented vegan menu; a strictly vegan diner would need to ask in advance and options would be limited.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi, alley off Mochiidono shopping street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Huerto is a tiny husband-and-wife Spanish bar hidden in a narrow back alley off Naramachi's Mochiidono shopping street. The owner-chef is reported to have trained at an established restaurant in Kyoto before opening this intimate spot in a renovated townhouse-style space. The hus

chef_bio.background: Huerto is a tiny husband-and-wife Spanish bar hidden in a narrow back alley off Naramachi's Mochiidono shopping street. The owner-chef is reported to have trained at an established restaurant in Kyoto before opening this intimate spot in a renovated townhouse-style space. The husband grows vegetables in the couple's own kitchen garden and the wife bakes all the bread in house.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name huerto is Spanish for 'vegetable garden,' reflecting a farm-to-table approach centered on the couple's own home-grown produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Spanish tapas and paella built around home-grown vegetables and house-baked natural-yeast bread. Documented dishes include pan con tomate with sheep's cheese and jamón, cauliflower potage, spinach Spanish tortilla, seafood paella (sea bream and clam, or red shrimp), roasted Spanish mackerel, venison steak with Spanish sauce, and a tableside-caramelized Catalan cream. Lunch is a weekly-changing sandwich set with soup and side dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'huerto' means 'vegetable garden' in Spanish, and the husband uses vegetables grown in the couple's own garden while the wife bakes the natural-yeast bread by hand.
The chef is reported to have trained at a noted Kyoto establishment before opening this nine-seat alley bar in a renovated townhouse setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-baked wheat sourdough bread is a centerpiece and lunch is sandwich-based, so flour is pervasive in the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes such as the Spanish tortilla contain eggs and pan con tomate is served with sheep's cheese and jamón, so vegetable plates are not reliably vegan.

positives.text: Cooking centers on home-grown vegetables, so genuine vegetable-forward dishes (cauliflower potage, spinach tortilla) are available.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny nine-seat husband-and-wife bar tucked in a back alley; staff communicate mainly in Japanese, so a foreign student should reserve ahead and be ready to order with simple Japanese or a translation app.

places.name: 旬菜と地酒 野良 (Shunsai to Jizake Nora)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a sake-and-seafood izakaya, dishes rely on fish dashi and Japanese soy sauce (typically wheat-based), and fried items such as アジフライ (breaded horse-mackerel fry) contain wheat. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The kitchen centers on fresh fish from the owner's family fishery and seafood dishes like sashimi; even the Yamato vegetable plates and small fridge appetizers are very likely prepared with fish dashi or soy seasonings. No vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 14:00–22:00 (per the shop's Instagram and Tabelog). Closed days: irregular (不定休).
places.neighborhood: 南市町／近鉄奈良駅 (Minami-Ichi-cho, a back street one block east of
places.cuisine: 居酒屋・おばんざい／地酒
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] After running a larger restaurant called Umasugi in the Shin-Omiya area, the owner wanted a smaller, more personal place and opened this little counter izakaya on July 24, 2025, in a quiet back street one block east of the Mochiidono arcade. The space had previously been a coffee

chef_bio.origin: Minami-Ichi-cho, central Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station
chef_bio.background: After running a larger restaurant called Umasugi in the Shin-Omiya area, the owner wanted a smaller, more personal place and opened this little counter izakaya on July 24, 2025, in a quiet back street one block east of the Mochiidono arcade. The space had previously been a coffee-and-bread shop called Hiruneko, and the new name Nora is said to nod to that predecessor.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately small, hands-on counter where the owner curates the fish and an idiosyncratic, personally chosen lineup of Japanese sake; the 2 p.m. opening invites unhurried afternoon drinking.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh fish delivered daily from Amakusa in Kumamoto, where the owner's family runs a fishing business, with roughly six varieties on hand at a time. Expect sashimi and grilled or fried fish (such as horse-mackerel fry), seasonal Yamato vegetables, and a self-serve fridge of small ¥300 appetizers that diners pick out and tally themselves.

anecdotes.text: The owner's family operates a fishery in Amakusa, Kumamoto, so the day's fish comes straight from the family boats — about six kinds in stock at any time.
The shop opened on July 24, 2025 in the former premises of the Hiruneko coffee-and-bread shop, on a lane just east of Mochiidono shopping street.
A small fridge of ready-made appetizers is priced at ¥300 each, which guests serve themselves and report on the honor system.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, cash-only counter izakaya run solo by its owner, with little to no English expected, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will go a long way.

places.name: 奈良の酒蔵全部呑み うまっしゅ (Nara no Sakagura Zenbu Nomi Umasshu)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a washoku izakaya built around sashimi, handmade otsumami, wagyu and seasonal vegetables, with food seasoned in the standard Japanese way — meaning wheat-based soy sauce and likely fish/kombu dashi throughout. While the local sake itself is naturally gluten-free, the kitchen is not set up for celiac-level safety and no gluten-free protocol is documented. A celiac traveler would need to ask carefully dish by dish and should not assume any cooked or sauced item is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu centers on sashimi, seafood, domestic black wagyu and handmade dishes, and most savory items rely on fish dashi and are not vegan. There are seasonal local-vegetable plates, but these are likely cooked with bonito/kombu dashi or soy seasoning unless requested otherwise.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon, Wed-Fri) 15:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 14:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Tuesdays (if Tuesday is a holiday, closed the following day instead); also closed Dec 31 and Jan 1.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki-Nakamachi, Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nar
places.notes: Umasshu is a Nara sake izakaya built around a single idea: let guests drink their way through every brewery in the prefecture. It pours local jizake from all 29 of Nara's sake breweries, offering pours in three sizes (50ml, 100ml and 130ml) so you can do a tasting flight of many

chef_bio.origin: Higashimuki-Nakamachi, in the covered arcade a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.background: Umasshu is a Nara sake izakaya built around a single idea: let guests drink their way through every brewery in the prefecture. It pours local jizake from all 29 of Nara's sake breweries, offering pours in three sizes (50ml, 100ml and 130ml) so you can do a tasting flight of many labels or settle in with a full glass of a favorite. The shop bills itself as an easy place to drop by after work and enjoy together with everyone.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house describes its cooking and service through the phrase 磨き抜かれた技とおもてなしの心 (refined skill and a heart of hospitality), pairing carefully sourced seasonal ingredients with the full spread of Nara's local sake at approachable prices.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade washoku otsumami built to pair with sake — sashimi and fresh seafood, seasonal Yamato/local vegetables, and domestic black wagyu beef, all prepared by trained cooks. A signature feature is the sake-brewery stamp card: collect stamps from the different breweries you drink, level up your card grade and earn perks as you go.

anecdotes.text: The concept is to be able to drink across all 29 sake breweries of Nara Prefecture in one place, with a brewery stamp card that upgrades in grade as you sample more.

Pours come in 50ml, 100ml and 130ml measures so guests can sample many labels kikizake-style or enjoy a fuller glass of a favorite, alongside handmade dishes from seasonal local vegetables, fresh seafood and domestic wagyu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a washoku izakaya serving sashimi and handmade dishes, the food is seasoned in the standard Japanese manner, which means wheat-based soy sauce and dashi are integral; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most savory dishes are built on fish/seafood and dashi; seasonal vegetable plates are not documented as vegan and likely use bonito or kombu dashi unless requested otherwise.

positives.text: The local Nara sake poured here is naturally gluten-free, and the small-pour tasting format (50/100/130ml) lets drinkers with dietary limits still enjoy the core sake experience.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local sake izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way; staff are used to guests sampling many sakes, so a curious foreign student can settle in comfortably even without fluent Japanese.

places.name: 酒と肴 なかむら (Sake to Sakana Nakamura)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional izakaya whose food relies heavily on soy sauce, sake-based seasonings, and fish/kombu dashi, plus signature spice-infused dishes; nothing on the documented menu (e.g. masala oden, grilled skewers) is presented as gluten-free, and there is no indication of allergen management. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: An izakaya focused on grilled meats/fish, sake snacks (sakana) and dashi-based dishes; most items contain animal products or fish dashi. There may be a few vegetable-based or spice-forward sides, but no vegan menu is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly about specific dishes.
places.hours_raw: Wed-Sat 17:00-22:00; Sun 11:00-22:00; closed Mon & Tue (lunch service ended in 2024, now evening-only except Sunday).
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅・南市町）
places.cuisine: 古民家居酒屋・肴／日本酒
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A nostalgic izakaya set in a renovated traditional machiya (kominka) in Naramachi, about a ten-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It opened on December 19 as an owner-run spot built around relaxed evenings of sake and well-chosen otsumami.

chef_bio.background: A nostalgic izakaya set in a renovated traditional machiya (kominka) in Naramachi, about a ten-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It opened on December 19 as an owner-run spot built around relaxed evenings of sake and well-chosen otsumami.
chef_bio.philosophy: The proprietor is a genuine lover of izakaya culture who emphasizes carefully crafted one-plate dishes and grilled items that highlight the quality of the ingredients themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Spice-forward sake snacks that you will not find elsewhere: a signature masala oden and spice-rubbed grilled chicken-skin skewers, alongside simple grilled dishes that let seasonal ingredients shine. The owner draws on years of spice research and study in India, weaving that knowledge into an otherwise classic izakaya menu. The drinks list pairs local Nara sake with selections from across Japan, plus things like Sapporo Sorachi draft and Hoppy.

anecdotes.text: The owner spent years researching spices, including time studying in India, and brings that experience back into the izakaya kitchen, creating spice-infused sake snacks such as the masala oden that distinguish the place from a typical neighborhood pub.
The shop opened on December 19 as a kominka izakaya in a renovated old townhouse in central Nara.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, dishes rely on soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings, which typically contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run kominka izakaya where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) helps, since it is a local spot rather than a tourist-oriented venue; reservations can be made by phone or LINE.

places.name: 大和野菜と地酒 大和乃山賊 三条通り店 (Yamato Yasai to Jizake Yamato no Sanzoku San)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a sake-and-hearth izakaya, the kitchen leans on soy-based seasonings and likely wheat-containing soy sauce and fried items, and the signature grilled chicken/fish dishes carry cross-contamination risk. No celiac-safe handling is advertised, so a strict gluten-free or celiac diner would need to ask in detail and treat it as unconfirmed.
places.vegan_detail: The concept centers on Yamato (Nara) vegetables, so vegetable-forward small plates and obanzai-style dishes exist, but this is a meat-and-fish izakaya: signatures are hearth-grilled chicken and seasonal-fish kamameshi, and most dishes use fish dashi, so they are not vegan. A vegan could likely assemble a few plant dishes by asking, but there is no dedicated vegan menu and no stated dashi-free option.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 16:30–23:00; Fri & day before holidays 16:30–00:00; Sat 16:00–00:00; Sun & holidays 16:00–23:00 (some listings show dinner to 24:00 daily). Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Aburasaka-cho / Sanjo-dori, by JR Nara Station east side
places.notes: This Sanjo-dori location opened on April 14, 2025, on the third floor of the Daiichi Nakamura Building, directly facing the scramble crossing about a one-to-two-minute walk from JR Nara Station. It is the second outpost of the locally popular Yamato no Sanzoku ("Yamato mountain b

chef_bio.origin: Nara City, near JR Nara Station east side
chef_bio.background: This Sanjo-dori location opened on April 14, 2025, on the third floor of the Daiichi Nakamura Building, directly facing the scramble crossing about a one-to-two-minute walk from JR Nara Station. It is the second outpost of the locally popular Yamato no Sanzoku ("Yamato mountain bandit") brand, whose original hearth-and-sake main shop operates nearby.
chef_bio.philosophy: A warm Japanese-modern space with private rooms built for gatherings, pairing Nara-grown vegetables and seasonal seafood with more than thirty kinds of local Nara sake chosen by the proprietor.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal-fish clay-pot rice (donabe / kamado-meshi) is the standout dish, prized for concentrated umami that pairs with sake; the kitchen is also known for bold hearth-grilled 'sanzoku' chicken and obanzai-style plates built around Nara's Yamato vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The 'mountain bandit' name evokes rustic, robust hearth cooking, and the headline dish is a hearty hearth-grilled chicken; the brand's original main shop nearby is a long-popular local spot.
The seasonal clay-pot rice is described as the most-ordered signature, made to draw out concentrated umami that complements the lineup of local sake.

positives.text: Built around Nara's Yamato vegetables with vegetable-forward obanzai plates, so plant-based diners can find some vegetable dishes to ask about.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively dinner-only izakaya right by JR Nara Station where a few words of Japanese help, though the picture-friendly menu and tourist-facing location make it manageable; note it is cash only.

places.name: さくらバーガー (Sakura Burger)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core product is a hamburger served on a wheat bun, and the house signature uses a smoked soy sauce (Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat). No gluten-free buns or dedicated preparation are advertised; not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The shop is built around house-smoked bacon, ham, 100% Japanese beef patties and other meat products, so it is not a vegan destination. A vegetarian menu option is listed, but there is no documented vegan (egg/dairy-free) preparation, and buns/sauces are not confirmed plant-based. Vegans should ask directly; expect very limited options.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); morning menu 9:00-11:00. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi / Higashimuki-kita (a few minutes' walk north of K
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sakura Burger grew out of a passion for additive-free, dry-cured bacon that the owners began curing in-house with spices and slow-smoking over cherry-wood chips. As demand for the smoked products grew, they set up their own dedicated smokehouse, Asuka Kunsei Kobo (あすか燻製工房), a dir

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi district, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: Sakura Burger grew out of a passion for additive-free, dry-cured bacon that the owners began curing in-house with spices and slow-smoking over cherry-wood chips. As demand for the smoked products grew, they set up their own dedicated smokehouse, Asuka Kunsei Kobo (あすか燻製工房), a directly run workshop in Asuka Village that supplies all the bacon, ham, sausage, salami and smoked chicken used in the shop and also sold at farmers markets and by mail order.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality and craftsmanship over volume: the owner has spoken about deliberately taking extended breaks to rest and to maintain the standard of the burgers ('I want to keep making delicious hamburgers, so I'm taking time to care for my body'), and recently replaced cooking equipment after about 15 years of use.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the namesake Sakura Burger, stacking thick-cut house-smoked cherry-wood bacon; the kitchen also grills 150g 100%-Japanese-beef patties on volcanic stone, finished with a house-made smoked soy sauce. Hot dogs, sausage plates, hot sandwiches, a morning menu and desserts round out the offering.

anecdotes.text: All the smoked meats are made at the shop's own subsidiary smokehouse in Asuka Village, where bacon is dry-cured with spices and slowly smoked over cherry-wood chips before being thick-sliced for the burgers.
The beef burgers use 150g 100%-Japanese-beef patties grilled on volcanic stone and seasoned with a house-made smoked soy sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Burgers are served on wheat buns and the shop is a bread-and-meat operation; no gluten-free preparation or separation is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature beef burger is finished with a house-made smoked soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat, so it is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on house-smoked bacon, ham, sausage and beef patties, so shared grill/equipment cross-contact with animal products is highly likely for any meat-free item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu page includes an allergen information sheet, indicating the shop documents allergens for its dishes.

positives.text: An allergen information chart is provided on the menu, so diners with allergies can review ingredient information on site.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, welcoming counter-and-table burger shop near the station where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get you by, though staff may have limited English.

places.name: 麺屋 K (Menya K)
places.gf_detail: This is a ramen shop. The noodles are wheat, the broth and tare use soy sauce (wheat-containing) and the tsukemen is finished with a fish-based broth. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A chicken-broth (鶏白湯) ramen specialist. Broths are built on poultry stock, often with seafood/fish accents, and toppings include pork shoulder chashu. There are no vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Thu/Fri 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:15); Tue 11:00-14:30; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00-16:00. Closes early when ingredients run out (材料無くなり次第終了).
places.neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（餅飯殿商店街）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained at the renowned Osaka ramen shop Ramen Jinsei JET (ラーメン人生JET) before opening Menya K in a former izakaya space along the Mochiidono shopping arcade in central Nara.

chef_bio.background: The owner trained at the renowned Osaka ramen shop Ramen Jinsei JET (ラーメン人生JET) before opening Menya K in a former izakaya space along the Mochiidono shopping arcade in central Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made in-house: noodles, broth and chashu are all prepared by the chef (麺・スープ・チャーシューすべて自家製).
chef_bio.specialty: A rich, concentrated chicken-paitan (鶏白湯) ramen whose poultry umami stays mellow rather than heavy, served with handmade medium-thick noodles, shoulder-loin chashu and crisp menma. The kitchen also turns out chicken-base tsukemen finished with a fish-broth rinse, mazesoba, seasonal curry ramen and a winter miso ramen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a small counter-and-table space carved out of a former izakaya in the covered Mochiidono arcade, drawing a steady stream of local regulars and curious visitors a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
The chicken broth is described as intensely concentrated with umami yet completely free of heaviness, leaving a clean, light aftertaste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle ramen shop with shared cooking water and equipment; the menu and broths are not designed for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and tare rely on soy-sauce seasoning, which typically contains wheat; the tsukemen is finished with a fish-based broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based, often with seafood accents, and toppings include pork chashu, so no dishes are vegan or vegetarian.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 11-seat counter ramen shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu go a long way, though staff are used to the international visitors who pass through the arcade.

noodles.notes: House-made medium-thick straight noodles milled with whole-grain (zenryufun) flour, giving a pronounced wheat aroma and a chewy, springy bite that clings to the thick chicken broth. Tsukemen uses a 250g portion of smooth, wheat-forward noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Ramen (chicken ramen) — a golden, emulsified chicken paitan crowned with thick braised pork chashu and a generous tangle of Kujo negi. The chicken tsukemen, finished with a fish-dashi wari-soup, is the other house pillar.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu), sliced to a moderate thickness — meltingly tender with a gentle, slow-released umami rather than a heavily seasoned punch.

gf.note: House-made wheat (whole-grain) noodles and a wheat-containing shoyu element place this outside celiac-safe territory; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a long-simmered chicken-bone broth, topped with pork chashu; no plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is a modern tori-paitan specialist: a golden, gently emulsified chicken-bone broth whose lineage is the new wave of Kansai chicken ramen rather than any single gotochi region. The soul is all poultry — deep and creamy in the mouth yet finishing clean, so the chicken umami lingers without weighing you down.

places.highlight: This is a modern tori-paitan specialist: a golden, gently emulsified chicken-bone broth whose lineage is the new wave of Kansai chicken ramen rather than any single gotochi region. The soul is all poultry — deep and creamy in the mouth yet finishing clean, so the chicken umami lingers without weighing you down.

places.name: 喫茶 スギ (Kissa Sugi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional kissaten built around toast, open sandwiches, and curry — all wheat-based. There is no gluten-free offering and no indication of allergen awareness, so it is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee and fresh-squeezed/mixed fruit juices are plant-based, but the food menu (egg-and-mayonnaise open sandwiches, buttered toast, curry, bento) relies on egg, dairy, and likely meat/fish. There is no dedicated vegan menu; a vegan visitor would be limited to drinks.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-14:00 daily (per Tabelog); local guide lists closed Sundays, Tabelog lists irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Sugigacho, central Nara, about a 5-7 minute walk from JR Nar
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Sugi is a long-running junkissa (pure coffee shop) that has stood on Sugigacho street near JR Nara Station since the early 1960s. Its storefront sign reads 珈琲と果汁 (coffee and fruit juice), and the classically styled interior has been described as a space where time seems to

chef_bio.background: Kissa Sugi is a long-running junkissa (pure coffee shop) that has stood on Sugigacho street near JR Nara Station since the early 1960s. Its storefront sign reads 珈琲と果汁 (coffee and fruit juice), and the classically styled interior has been described as a space where time seems to have stopped.
chef_bio.philosophy: It embodies the junkissa ideal of the simple luxury of lingering over a cup of coffee in an old-fashioned, well-loved room.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully brewed blend coffee balancing acidity, bitterness and body served in distinctive indigo cups; open sandwiches of toast topped with mayonnaise-dressed cucumber and soft scrambled egg; plus fresh juices, buttered toast, curry, and shokado bento-style lunches. The shop opens early at 7am, making it a classic morning spot.

anecdotes.text: The shop is noted as one of the rare surviving traditional pure-kissaten near Nara Station, where customers order items individually such as mixed juice and an open sandwich rather than a set morning.

cultural_comfort.note: A nostalgic old-school local coffee shop with no English signage or menu, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help you order comfortably.

places.name: koharu cafe（コハル カフェ） (koharu cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. This is a casual café whose signatures are flour-based cakes (New York and white-chocolate cheesecakes, chiffon cake) and curry served with rice but typically thickened/seasoned with wheat-containing elements. No celiac protocols, dedicated equipment, or GF menu are mentioned on any source. Not suitable for celiacs; a gluten-sensitive diner should ask in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. Signature dishes include dairy-heavy cheesecakes and a beef-tendon curry, so the core menu is not vegan. Seasonal-vegetable plate lunches exist but no source confirms they are free of dashi, dairy, or animal products. A vegan diner would need to call ahead (reservations are recommended anyway) and ask the solo owner directly.
places.neighborhood: きたまち / 東大寺転害門近く・近鉄奈良駅北側（Kitamachi）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] koharu cafe is a hidden-away café set inside a renovated 100-year-old machiya (traditional wooden house) in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, north of Kintetsu-Nara Station and near Todai-ji's Tegaimon gate. The owner runs the place single-handedly, and the interior is kept in sof

chef_bio.background: koharu cafe is a hidden-away café set inside a renovated 100-year-old machiya (traditional wooden house) in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, north of Kintetsu-Nara Station and near Todai-ji's Tegaimon gate. The owner runs the place single-handedly, and the interior is kept in soft white tones with a garden that lends a calm, forest-like stillness.
chef_bio.philosophy: Because the café sits in a spot that is genuinely hard to find, the owner says the aim is for every guest to leave smiling and feeling the trip was worth it (『ここまで来て良かった』).
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for a curry simmered over two to three days with beef tendon and aromatic vegetables, and for its cheesecakes — a simple New York style and a white-chocolate version made by melting white chocolate into the batter. Specialty coffee, including beans from Hokkaido roaster Atelier Morihiko, rounds out the menu alongside seasonal one-plate lunches and chiffon cake.

anecdotes.text: The owner notes the café is in a tucked-away location and works to make sure visitors feel their journey was rewarded.
The curry is prepared over two to three days using beef tendon and aromatic vegetables, and the cheesecakes come in New York and white-chocolate varieties.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet solo-run old-house café where a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps, since it is a tucked-away local spot and no English menu or English service is documented.

places.name: やちむんカフェ おことパーラー (Yachimun Cafe Okoto Parlour)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The shop centers on shaved ice topped with various sauces and syrups, plus cakes and tarts (which contain wheat). Shaved ice itself is naturally wheat-free, but toppings such as Okinawan senbei crackers contain wheat, and there is no allergen labeling or dedicated GF handling described. A celiac visitor would need to ask in person about each item.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented shop. Signature items use dairy custard, condensed-milk-style syrups, cheese/tiramisu elements and cakes, so most desserts contain dairy. Some plant-based Okinawan ingredients appear (island tofu sweets, purple sweet potato, fuuchiba/mugwort, fruit), but no item is confirmed vegan. A vegan visitor would need to ask which sauces and toppings are dairy-free.
places.hours_raw: Specific daily hours are not published on listing sites; the shop directs customers to its Instagram (@ocotoparlour) for current operating days and times.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（奈良市坊屋敷町）／近鉄奈良駅北側
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-feeling parlour in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner fell for Okinawan culture and built the shop around 'yachimun' — Okinawan pottery with roughly 700 years of history — personally traveling to Okinawa to buy pieces d

chef_bio.background: A small, family-feeling parlour in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner fell for Okinawan culture and built the shop around 'yachimun' — Okinawan pottery with roughly 700 years of history — personally traveling to Okinawa to buy pieces directly from about ten different kilns. Those thick, rounded, hand-painted bowls, said to reflect the warmth of Okinawan people, are the vessels the desserts are served in. Although the owner was a beginner at shaved ice when opening, they developed a signature fluffy, snow-soft texture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Recreate the feeling of sitting in a neighborhood Okinawan teahouse in the middle of Nara — gentle Ryukyuan music, handmade pottery, and island ingredients turned into playful, beautiful sweets.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative Okinawan-inspired kakigori served in handmade yachimun bowls: passion fruit and acerola from Motobu balanced with black sugar and custard, ice scented green with fuuchiba (Okinawan mugwort) for an aromatic, gently herbal note, and toppings like Okinawan senbei; seasonal cakes and fruit tarts round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 'Okoto' comes from the owner's beloved pet dog, who lives at the shop and greets guests — so visitors with dog allergies are asked to take care.
The owner personally visits Okinawa to source yachimun pottery directly from about ten kilns, choosing the thick, rounded, warmly-painted pieces the desserts are served in.
Inside, soft Ryukyuan music plays, giving the sense of being in a local Okinawan cafe rather than central Nara.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes wheat-based cakes and tarts, and shaved ice is topped with Okinawan senbei crackers (wheat); no allergen labeling or gluten-free handling is described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature desserts use dairy custard, cheese/tiramisu elements and cakes; most items are not dairy-free and there is no documented vegan preparation.

positives.text: Some plant-based Okinawan ingredients feature on the menu, including island-tofu-based sweets, purple sweet potato (ta'amu), fuuchiba (mugwort) and fresh fruit.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, owner-run little parlour with a resident shop dog and Okinawan music; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed pace will make a foreign student feel welcome, though English support is not advertised.

places.name: オテンキパーラー (OTENKI PARLOR)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The menu is built around wheat: morning toast/French toast and a bread-focused half-buffet, copper-plate hotcakes, and spaghetti (meat sauce, Neapolitan) at lunch. Kakigori syrups and the cloud-pudding kakigori include a house crumble. No celiac-safe options or allergen accommodation are mentioned anywhere; treat as not suitable for those needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan place. Core items are egg-, dairy-, and meat-based: scrambled eggs and bacon at breakfast, the signature 雲のプリン (cloud pudding made with eggs, cream, milk and caramel), hotcakes, and cream sodas (typically dairy ice cream). The only plant-leaning item documented is the monthly-rotating おてんきプレート lunch featuring fresh local Nara vegetables, but its vegan status is not confirmed (likely contains dashi/dairy/egg components). A salad bar exists at the morning buffet. Vegans should ask in advance; assume options are minimal.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅前（高天町）
places.notes: A modern take on the Showa-era 純喫茶 (classic Japanese coffee parlor), pairing retro charm with a pastel, photo-friendly interior.

chef_bio.origin: Opened on the ground floor of Henna Hotel (now Henna Hotel Premier Nara) near Kintetsu Nara Station, OTENKI PARLOR is run as a sister venture to the beloved Kanmaki-town cafe Nanatsu Mori (ナナツモリ), famous for its lemon cakes.
chef_bio.background: A modern take on the Showa-era 純喫茶 (classic Japanese coffee parlor), pairing retro charm with a pastel, photo-friendly interior.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe is designed to feel like a retro department-store rooftop, with sky-blue tile below, clean white above, and shelves that float like clouds. Its idea is that you can have a good time no matter the weather: 晴れの日も、曇りの日も、雨の日も楽しめる (enjoyable on sunny days, cloudy days and rainy days alike).
chef_bio.specialty: A weather- and sky-themed all-day cafe. Signatures include the 雲のプリン (cloud pudding, made with eggs, cream, milk and caramel) served both on its own and crowning a summer kakigori, a trio of sky-colored cream sodas named Aozora (blue sky), Yuuzora (evening sky) and Hoshizora (starry sky), nostalgic copper-plate hotcakes, a half-buffet morning set, and the おてんきプレート lunch built around local Nara vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The interior is split with sky-blue tile on the lower half and clean white above, with decorative shelves floating like clouds to create an otherworldly, sky-like feel; the cafe is positioned as a sister shop to Nanatsu Mori in Kanmaki, known for its weekend lemon cake.
Cream sodas come in three sky variations and the cloud pudding is made simply from eggs, cream, milk and caramel, alongside a monthly-rotating weather plate of fresh local Nara vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: A bright, photogenic hotel-lobby cafe right by Kintetsu Nara Station where a smile and a few Japanese words get you far, and pointing at the picture menu works fine for the cream sodas and pudding.

places.name: ノモケマナ (Nomokemana)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery. Every product is bread made from wheat flour, with all loaves incorporating whole-grain (全粒粉) wheat. There is no gluten-free option and the entire premises is a flour-heavy environment, so it is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten or with celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: A natural-yeast bakery. Some simpler hard breads (baguette, campagne, ciabatta) may be plant-based, but the lineup also includes croissants, melon pan, and butter/egg/dairy-rich French pastries such as kouglof, plus cheese breads — so most items are not vegan. No vegan labeling is documented; a vegan diner would need to ask which specific loaves contain no egg, butter, or milk.
places.hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source: a Tabelog listing shows 10:00–18:30 closed Mon & Sun, while local bakery guides and a visitor blog report 9:00–19:00 closed Tue & Wed. Hours and closed days for this owner-run shop should be confirmed directly before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良公園南側・近鉄奈良駅／春日大社方面）
places.cuisine: 天然酵母・全粒粉の自家製パン
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small artisanal bakery in Nara's quiet Takabatake district, on the south side of Nara Park near the temples and Kasuga Taisha approach. It opened in May 2009. The whimsical name 'Nomokemana' is the Japanese word for sloth, 'namakemono,' spelled backwards, and the shop carries a

chef_bio.background: A small artisanal bakery in Nara's quiet Takabatake district, on the south side of Nara Park near the temples and Kasuga Taisha approach. It opened in May 2009. The whimsical name 'Nomokemana' is the Japanese word for sloth, 'namakemono,' spelled backwards, and the shop carries a little sloth mascot to match — a nod to its slow, unhurried approach to bread.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bread made slowly and honestly: domestic wheat and house-cultured natural yeast, with whole-grain flour worked into every loaf for rustic, deeply aromatic results.
chef_bio.specialty: French-style hard breads and a signature pain au levain raised with wild yeast cultivated from Ikoma-grown grapes, giving it a gentle tang. Around 30 kinds of bread are baked daily, from baguettes and walnut campagne to Japanese favorites like anpan and melon pan and French sweets such as kouglof. All loaves are blended with whole-grain flour milled from organically grown, pesticide-free wheat.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained under a French chef, and beyond baguettes he puts real effort into traditional French confections like kouglof.
The shop's name is 'namakemono' (sloth) read in reverse, complete with a sloth mascot, reflecting its slow, careful style of baking.
Its popular natural-yeast levain is leavened with wild yeast cultured from grapes grown in nearby Ikoma, lending the hard loaf a mild sourness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: An all-wheat bakery where every product contains flour and loaves use whole-grain wheat; flour is airborne throughout, making it unsafe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bread range mixes plant-based hard breads with butter/egg/dairy items (croissants, melon pan, kouglof, cheese breads), all produced in one small kitchen, so cross-contact between vegan and non-vegan items is likely.

positives.text: Bread is made with house-cultured natural yeast and domestic, organically grown pesticide-free wheat milled into whole-grain flour — a transparent, additive-conscious approach to ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood bakery where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; you can point at the loaves you want, but don't expect much English, and bring cash.

places.name: ならまちパン工房 okage (Naramachi Pan Kobo okage)
places.gf_detail: A wheat bakery. Every product is made with domestic Japanese wheat flour, so nothing here is gluten-free and the shop is not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners. No gluten-free line is offered.
places.vegan_detail: The shop emphasizes carefully sourced domestic wheat and organic yeast, but no vegan labeling is published. Some breads (hard-type lean breads, focaccia) may be plant-based, while enriched items such as cream pan, melon pan and the salted-caramel nut bread typically contain dairy/eggs. A vegan visitor would need to ask about each item; there is no confirmed vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: 10:00～売り切れ次第終了（営業時間は売り切れ次第終了）
places.neighborhood: 杉ヶ町（JR奈良駅東・ならまち方面）
places.cuisine: 国産小麦・有機酵母を使ったオーガニックパンの小さな個人店
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent bakery that opened on May 1, 2021 on the main street toward Naramachi, a short walk east of JR Nara Station. It is run as a tiny owner-operated shop, baking everything by hand and selling through a small window counter.

chef_bio.background: A small independent bakery that opened on May 1, 2021 on the main street toward Naramachi, a short walk east of JR Nara Station. It is run as a tiny owner-operated shop, baking everything by hand and selling through a small window counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: '毎日食べるものだからこそ、体が喜ぶパンを届けたい' — because bread is eaten every day, the shop wants to deliver bread that makes the body happy, choosing every ingredient by origin and safety.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic-leaning breads made with all-domestic Japanese wheat and organic yeast — hard-type lean breads, bagels, the 'okage' loaf (okage食パン), stone-ground rustique (リュスティック石臼), and creative items like multigrain melon pan and a salted-caramel nut bread that fans call a blissful treat.

anecdotes.text: Each bread is hand-made from carefully vetted ingredients, with hard-type breads as a particular strength; a writer praised the salted-caramel nutty bread as a 'blissful creation' and admitted it was hard to resist overbuying.
Breads are sold from a window counter for hygiene and the shop simply closes once it sells out, so regulars line up soon after the 10:00 opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery; all products contain gluten and there is no gluten-free option, so it is unsafe for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan menu is published; enriched breads likely contain dairy and eggs, and there is no stated separation, so vegans should ask about each item.
