positives.text: Ingredients are carefully selected for origin and safety, using all-domestic Japanese wheat and organic yeast.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood bakery with simple point-and-buy window service, so a foreign student can manage easily, though staff likely speak little English and popular breads sell out by late morning.

places.name: ブーランジェリー リッシュ (Boulangerie Riche)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-based bakery. Its signature products are shokupan (milk bread), anpan, croissants, focaccia and sandwiches, all made from wheat flour, with no gluten-free line documented. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan labeling is documented. Most signature items (shokupan, anpan with condensed milk, croissants, cheese focaccia, mentaiko-potato croissant, fruit tarts) contain dairy, eggs, butter or fish. A strict vegan would need to ask item by item, and many options will be unsuitable. There is no confirmed vegan bread.
places.hours_raw: 8:30-19:00 (Sunday closed per listing; the bakery describes itself as essentially open daily with occasional irregular closures)
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・JR奈良駅周辺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Boulangerie Riche is a Nara bakery whose original shop sits just off Sanjo-dori, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. From this small storefront it has grown into a locally rooted brand with several locations around the city while keeping the honten as its heart.

chef_bio.background: Boulangerie Riche is a Nara bakery whose original shop sits just off Sanjo-dori, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. From this small storefront it has grown into a locally rooted brand with several locations around the city while keeping the honten as its heart.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is 見て満足、食べて満足 (satisfying to look at, satisfying to eat), aiming to be a warm space built on careful baking and good ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Bread baked fresh throughout the day. Its best-known items are a coffee anpan filled with coffee beans and whipped cream, a Yamato-tea condensed-milk anpan stamped with a deer motif, a salted-caramel melon bread, and the proprietary 豊潤食パン (Houjun shokupan), a chewy milk loaf made by a method used since the shop's founding that brings out wheat's natural sweetness. Savory choices include mentaiko-potato croissants and colorful vegetable-and-cheese focaccia, plus pastry-chef-made fruit tarts and baked sweets.

anecdotes.text: The honten is described as a small shop you reach by passing through Konishi Sakura-dori and crossing Sanjo-dori, where it stands on the right.
The deer-stamped Yamato-tea condensed-milk anpan ties the bakery's identity to Nara's local imagery and Yamato tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: An entirely flour-based bakery with no documented gluten-free area or products; airborne flour and shared surfaces make heavy cross-contamination unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy- and egg-containing doughs and fillings (milk bread, condensed-milk anpan, cheese focaccia, croissants) are made in the same kitchen, so any incidentally plant-based item would share equipment with animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly walk-up bakery where you point at what you want with a tray and tongs, so a smile and a konnichiwa are all you need, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: コンフェクション アーティザナル ブレッド&スイーツ (Confection Artisanal Bread & Sweets)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — breads, baguettes, croissants and shokupan are all wheat-based (the shop blends 15–16 kinds of wheat flour). There is no gluten-free baking and no dedicated GF area, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Mostly not vegan: croissants and pastries use French fermented butter, and the sweets/cheese breads contain dairy and eggs. The croissants are noted as egg-free, and a plain baguette may be vegan, but the shop publishes no vegan labeling — a vegan would need to ask item by item, and most of the range contains dairy.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Thu–Sun 08:00–17:00 (articles note up to 17:30); closed Tue & Wed plus occasional irregular closures (check Instagram). Most bread available after 11:00.
places.neighborhood: 京終／ならまち・近鉄奈良方面
places.cuisine: アルチザン系パン＆焼き菓子・ケーキ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in November 2020 in a townhouse in Nara's historic Naramachi quarter. The boulanger, Hiroshige Homma, honed his craft in France, Taiwan and China, where he ran the popular Shanghai bakery 'mbd'; he returned to open this shop together with a Nara-born supervisor who steered

chef_bio.origin: Japan, with a career building bakeries abroad
chef_bio.background: Opened in November 2020 in a townhouse in Nara's historic Naramachi quarter. The boulanger, Hiroshige Homma, honed his craft in France, Taiwan and China, where he ran the popular Shanghai bakery 'mbd'; he returned to open this shop together with a Nara-born supervisor who steered it toward local ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: Homma favors face-to-face selling and wants visitors to discover the quality of Nara's local ingredients through bread, rotating flours, ferments and seasonal regional produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Artisan breads built on 15–16 different wheat flours and house cultures: the 'Hirone' baguette of stone-milled French wheat blended with Japanese nishinokaori whole-grain flour, three contrasting styles of shokupan (chewy, crisp, moist), egg-free croissants made with French fermented butter, and Nara-rooted breads like hojicha (roasted Yamato tea) anpan and a cheese ciabatta studded with chopped Nara-zuke pickles.

anecdotes.text: The owner previously ran the well-known bakery 'mbd' in Shanghai before bringing his craft to Naramachi.
Breads showcase Nara producers — hojicha anpan uses Yamato roasted tea from local grower Daiwanoen, and a ciabatta is made with chopped Nara-zuke pickles and cheese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire shop is a wheat-flour bakery blending many wheat varieties, so flour and gluten are pervasive throughout the space.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pastries and croissants use French fermented butter and the range includes dairy and egg products, sharing the same prep area as any plant-based item.

positives.text: Croissants are made egg-free, which can help visitors avoiding eggs.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want and pay at the counter, so a few words of Japanese and a smile are all you need.

places.name: 酒処 蔵 (Sakedokoro Kura)
places.gf_detail: This is an oden and yakitori izakaya with no gluten-free accommodation. Oden is simmered in a soy-and-dashi broth (soy sauce normally contains wheat), and items such as fried tofu, gobō tempura and skewered cutlets are breaded/fried in wheat. The house sushi uses standard vinegared rice. There is no indication of allergen handling or a gluten-free menu, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish izakaya whose signatures are grilled chicken liver, yakitori and mackerel sushi. Even the tofu, daikon and vegetable oden items are simmered in a fish/bonito-based dashi, so they are not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Wed 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00); Thu-Fri closed; Sat 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) & 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00); Sun 11:30-14:30 & 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00). As of Oct 2025. Local guides also describe longer evening hours (around 17:00-22:30) with irregular closures, so hours shift — call ahead.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・餅飯殿（近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏）
places.cuisine: 居酒屋・おでん・焼き鳥
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A retro-feel drinking spot tucked just off the Mochiidono shopping arcade in Nara's old town, set in a converted kura (storehouse) with Showa-era signage, a vintage radio and a warm, regulars-loving atmosphere. Guests sit around a U-shaped counter looking straight into the simmer

chef_bio.origin: ならまち・餅飯殿の路地
chef_bio.background: A retro-feel drinking spot tucked just off the Mochiidono shopping arcade in Nara's old town, set in a converted kura (storehouse) with Showa-era signage, a vintage radio and a warm, regulars-loving atmosphere. Guests sit around a U-shaped counter looking straight into the simmering oden pot, with staff working the center.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious neighborhood sake bar focused on simple, comforting oden, good local sake and conversation across the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Oden is the heart of the place: about ten kinds always in the pot (egg, daikon, grilled tofu, fried tofu, burdock-root tempura and more), ordered one piece at a time. The other signature is kimoyaki, slow-grilled chicken liver paired with quail eggs and seasoned with sansho pepper, plus house-cured saba (mackerel) sushi.

anecdotes.text: When you order oden the staff ask how you like it: yoku shimita n ga ii? asai n ga ii? (deeply soaked in broth, or lighter?).
The kimoyaki, liver and quail egg cooked low and slow, is described as surprisingly sweet and richly concentrated, pairing with any drink, and is one of the most popular orders.
The little counter is so beloved it can fill completely within an hour of opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is simmered in a shared soy-and-dashi broth and the menu includes wheat-fried/breaded items (fried tofu, burdock tempura, skewered cutlets), so gluten exposure and cross-contact are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden broth is soy-sauce and dashi based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free alternative is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable items such as tofu, daikon and burdock are cooked in the same fish/bonito dashi oden pot used for meat and fish items, so they are not vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, regulars-heavy local counter bar where some Japanese helps, though staff are used to visitors and English menus have been mentioned; go early as the few seats fill fast.

places.name: 藍布 (Ranpu)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no GF program is documented. The kitchen leans on Asian seasonings (soy sauce, curries, etc.) that typically contain wheat, and there is no mention of wheat-free preparation or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner would need to ask in detail and treat it as not confirmed safe.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-forward by design: the seasonal wellness lunch and the signature chickpea curry are built on legumes, vegetables and whole grains, and the chickpea curry is described as vegetarian. However the menu also spans Asian home cooking that can include meat/fish (e.g. Filipino-style braised dishes noted on listings), and there is no documented confirmation that broths/seasonings are free of fish dashi or other animal products. Vegans should ask which dishes are fully plant-based on the day.
places.hours_raw: Thursday-Sunday 11:30-16:00 (lunch from 11:30, last order 14:30); closed Monday-Wednesday
places.neighborhood: きたまち (Hōren-chō, north of Kintetsu Nara Station)
places.notes: The owner previously ran an Asian home-cooking restaurant in Osaka's Tanimachi district, then studied herbal-medicine (yakuzen) theory and returned to their hometown of Nara to open Ranpu in a renovated tenement house that is roughly 100 years old.

chef_bio.background: The owner previously ran an Asian home-cooking restaurant in Osaka's Tanimachi district, then studied herbal-medicine (yakuzen) theory and returned to their hometown of Nara to open Ranpu in a renovated tenement house that is roughly 100 years old.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is shokuyaku-dogen — that food and medicine share the same origin — expressed through casual Asian home cooking from Thailand, Vietnam, China and Korea meant to keep both heart and body healthy by eating deliciously.
chef_bio.specialty: A weekly-rotating seasonal wellness lunch course built around legumes, vegetables and whole grains with a medicinal soup, an Asian dessert and a herbal tea; the chickpea curry lunch — chickpeas with root vegetables — is a signature, alongside Hong Kong-style yin-yang herbal drinks and date-and-nut herbal cakes.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor loves talking with guests about Asian food culture and medicinal cooking, and keeps a blog called Ranpu Tsushin with travel notes, herb explanations and home recipes.

positives.text: The signature chickpea curry and the seasonal lunch are centered on legumes, vegetables and whole grains, so plant-based diners have substantial options.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm owner-run lunch cafe where the host enjoys chatting about Asian food and medicinal cooking; some basic Japanese will help, but a friendly foreign student will feel welcome.

places.name: コージー カフェ (Cozy Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a home-style cafe whose signature is a weekly set lunch (hamburger, omelets, multigrain rice, etc.) and house-made cakes and tarts, all wheat- and flour-based. No celiac or cross-contamination handling is mentioned anywhere.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The weekly lunch is vegetable-rich but typically includes meat, fish and egg (e.g. mushroom-sauce hamburger, Spanish omelet, tuna-sesame spinach), and the sweets use dairy and eggs. Any plant-based dish would have to be asked about case by case; no vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-18:00 (Apr-Sep); 11:30-17:00 (Oct-Mar). Closed Wednesdays and Sundays, with occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi (Horen-cho), along Yasuragi-no-Michi near Nara Wom
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cozy Cafe sits along Yasuragi-no-Michi in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, near Nara Women's University. It is run by Cozy Planning, a local architecture and design firm, and the cafe doubles as a showcase of their craft: the furniture and chairs are handmade by the studio, and t

chef_bio.background: Cozy Cafe sits along Yasuragi-no-Michi in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, near Nara Women's University. It is run by Cozy Planning, a local architecture and design firm, and the cafe doubles as a showcase of their craft: the furniture and chairs are handmade by the studio, and the room is finished with a soft Scandinavian-style interior, dried flowers, and a little corner of curated goods. It opened in October 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on home-style, good-value cooking in a hand-built, comfortable space designed by the owners themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the weekly-changing 'ouchi-gohan' home-style set lunch (around 990 yen) built around seasonal vegetables and a hearty main such as mushroom-sauce hamburger, served with multigrain rice and free refills. Sets can be expanded with a drink and a house-made dessert (about 1,400 yen). The kitchen also turns out a rotating daily lineup of roughly six to eight handmade cakes and tarts.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is operated by the architecture and design firm Cozy Planning, and the tables and chairs guests sit in were handmade by the studio itself.
The daily dessert case rotates with the seasons, offering several house-made sweets at once such as pumpkin pudding, fruit tart, lemon cake and fig-topped cake.

positives.text: The set lunch is built around a rotating selection of seasonal vegetable side dishes, so vegetable-forward eating is easy here.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm neighborhood cafe where service is in Japanese; a foreign student would be comfortable with a translation app or by pointing at the day's set lunch, but should not expect English support.

places.name: アレ!!ル トレッフル (Allez!! Le Trèfle)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a wine bar built around charcuterie, cheese, and chef's small plates; bread, flour-based and breaded items, and likely wheat-containing seasonings (soy sauce, etc.) are part of the menu, and there is no indication of celiac-aware preparation. Not celiac-safe; assume cross-contamination in a single small kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented place. The menu centers on meat and fish — house-made bacon/charcuterie, gizzard and heart confit, braised beef tongue/cheek in red wine, seared ox tongue, oyster carpaccio — plus cheese. A few vegetable items exist (pickled vegetables, mixed nuts) and the chef cooks flexibly with seasonal ingredients, so a purely vegetable plate might be possible, but most dishes contain animal products and there is no documented vegan menu. A vegan would have very limited options and should ask in advance (some Japanese ability helps).
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu 17:00-23:00 (some sources list 15:00-24:00 with last order 23:00); Fri/Sat/Sun & holidays 15:00-23:00. Closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi), 北袋町 — north of Kintetsu Nara Station, abou
places.cuisine: Wine bar serving Japanese and French wines w
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Allez!! Le Trèfle is a small counter wine bar tucked into Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station, run by a couple — a veteran sommelier with more than 20 years of experience and a chef trained in French cooking. The sommelier was struck over the year

chef_bio.background: Allez!! Le Trèfle is a small counter wine bar tucked into Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station, run by a couple — a veteran sommelier with more than 20 years of experience and a chef trained in French cooking. The sommelier was struck over the years by how good Japanese wine had become, alongside discovering exceptional French bottles, and opened the bar to share those finds with the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed, friendly drinking experience pairing Japanese and French wines, with food designed around the wine rather than fixed menus. The owners openly say they want to cherish their local Kitamachi customers, choosing community over a busier high-street location.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully chosen Japanese wines (40-60 varieties, with a leaning toward Yamanashi producers) and French wines, poured with explanations for both novices and enthusiasts. The kitchen turns out roughly 30 'wine-conscious' small plates a day built around seasonal ingredients — house-made bacon and charcuterie, cheese boards, gizzard and heart confit, braised beef tongue and cheek in red wine, seared ox tongue with mushrooms, oyster carpaccio with raspberry-vinegar sauce, and brûlée-style desserts. Some dishes incorporate locally produced sake lees (酒粕).

anecdotes.text: The name carries the spirit of the place: 'Allez!!' is French for 'go for it!' or 'do your best!', and 'Le Trèfle' means clover — a symbol of happiness.
The sommelier draws on over 20 years in the trade and was inspired to open the bar after being surprised by the quality of Japanese wines while also seeking out outstanding French ones.
The owners say they want to value the local customers of Kitamachi, accepting a spot slightly off the main station route in favor of a quiet neighborhood setting.

positives.text: The chef cooks flexibly around seasonal ingredients rather than a fixed menu, so vegetable-forward plates can sometimes be requested, and there are some vegetable items such as pickled vegetables and nuts.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 12-seat neighborhood wine bar where the owners prize local regulars and explain wines in detail — warm but very Japanese in feel, so some Japanese (or a confident, friendly attitude) will make a solo foreign student feel most at home.

places.name: とおく＆らいぶcafé 葉音（ぱおん） (Tooku & Live cafe Paon)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a small fair-trade coffee café serving light food (e.g. croquettes are shown), which typically involves wheat-based items. There is no information about dedicated gluten-free preparation or cross-contamination control, so a celiac visitor would need to ask directly and treat it as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The café emphasizes fair-trade coffee and organic/natural foods, and supports ethical and social causes, but no fully vegan menu is documented. Light takeaway items like croquettes are not necessarily vegan. Plant-friendly options may exist given the natural-food focus, but a vegan visitor should confirm specific dishes with staff.
places.hours_raw: 水・木・金・土・日 11:30〜19:00（ライブ開催時は21:00頃まで延長することあり）。月・火 定休。
places.neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅北側）
places.cuisine: フェアトレード珈琲とライブ・カフェ、軽食
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Paon is a small, home-based café tucked into a private residence in Nara's historic Kitamachi district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station and near Nara Women's University. The space doubles as an intimate, home-theater-style venue where occasional live music and perform

chef_bio.background: Paon is a small, home-based café tucked into a private residence in Nara's historic Kitamachi district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station and near Nara Women's University. The space doubles as an intimate, home-theater-style venue where occasional live music and performances are held, giving the place its full name, 'Tooku & Live cafe Paon.'
chef_bio.philosophy: The café is built around fair trade, ethical sourcing, and community support. Beyond coffee it sells fair-trade goods, handwoven products, and items that support people with disabilities, reflecting a focus on social and environmental causes.
chef_bio.specialty: Fair-trade coffee is the heart of the menu, alongside drinks such as Umajimura (Maji Village) yuzu tea. The café offers natural and organic light food to take away, with croquettes among the items shown.

anecdotes.text: The café operates inside a private home arranged like a small home theater, hosting occasional live performances where closing time can stretch to around 21:00.
Alongside fair-trade coffee, Paon sells fair-trade goods, Tinara handwoven items, and products that support people with disabilities, tying the café to wider community causes.

positives.text: Focus on fair-trade coffee and natural/organic light foods suggests an ingredient-conscious kitchen, though no specific allergen handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny owner-run home café in a residential setting where communication is likely in Japanese, so a foreign student should be ready to use basic Japanese and a warm, polite, low-key approach.

places.name: 魚・創作料理 花しば (Uo Sosaku Ryori Hanashiba)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a Japanese seafood izakaya built around sashimi, grilled fish, and nabe, all of which lean heavily on soy sauce and fish dashi (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is fresh fish — sashimi, charcoal-grilled fish, and seasonal seafood hot pots — and broths/seasonings are fish-dashi and soy based. No vegan dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 18:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Sundays/holidays dinner only (18:00-24:00).
places.neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺・花芝町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner of Hanashiba was born and raised in a Nara fish shop that operated for more than 90 years, giving him a fishmonger's eye for quality seafood. He still goes to the market every day to hand-pick seasonal, wild-caught fish for the restaurant.

chef_bio.origin: Nara (central Nara, Kitamachi area near Kintetsu Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: The owner of Hanashiba was born and raised in a Nara fish shop that operated for more than 90 years, giving him a fishmonger's eye for quality seafood. He still goes to the market every day to hand-pick seasonal, wild-caught fish for the restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bring seasonal, wild-caught fish to the people of Nara, drawing out each ingredient's natural flavor through careful preparation.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh sashimi platters, charcoal-grilled fish, and seasonal hot pots. Signature dishes include seared fresh tuna (aburi maguro), assorted sashimi, and seasonal nabe such as conger eel (hamo) and new-onion hot pot. At lunch, generous grilled-fish sets — natural yellowtail and Spanish mackerel — come with rice, miso soup, side dishes and pickles, with refills offered.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer praised the grilled fish for its fluffy, fresh texture — well above the hard, crumbly quality of cheaper grilled fish — and noted the kitchen rounded out the lunch with complimentary rice and miso soup refills plus pound cake and coffee afterward.

The shop pairs its fish with sake from the regions where the catch was landed, and the seasonal hot pots rotate through the year — yellowtail and oysters in winter, firefly squid in spring, and conger eel from summer into autumn.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled-fish dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Hot pots and the kitchen broadly rely on fish dashi; the menu is fish-centric with no documented plant-based options.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, local counter-and-tatami fish izakaya where the owner and regulars speak Japanese; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the seasonal fish will go a long way.

places.name: へいぞう (Heizou)
places.gf_detail: This is a creative Chinese kitchen built around soy sauce, doubanjiang, gyoza and noodles, so it is not suitable for celiacs. Signature dishes — mapo tofu, thick-skinned boiled gyoza, dan dan noodles, stir-fried "stamina" ramen — all involve wheat (noodle/dumpling wrappers) and wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. The menu centers on pork, chicken (yodare-dori), and fresh seafood from Shimane, and even tofu dishes (mapo tofu) are typically prepared with meat and meat/fish-based stock. A vegan would need to ask whether any vegetable small plates can be made without meat, dashi, oyster sauce or egg, and confirmation is not documented.
places.hours_raw: Sun–Thu 18:00–21:30; Fri–Sat 18:00–22:00. Closed Mondays. (Local write-ups also describe a weekend/weekday lunch service around 11:30–14:00 that has run at times, but current listed hours are dinner-only — check ahead, as the owner posts the day's plan on social media.)
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono Shopping Street (near Kintetsu Nara Station arcad
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Heizou is a small, independent creative-Chinese restaurant whose name comes, endearingly, from the owner's pet dog. After more than two decades cooking at a Chinese restaurant in Ikoma, the owner struck out alone, building a devoted local following first in Nara's quiet Kitamachi

chef_bio.origin: Nara (relocated from Kitamachi to Mochiidono Shopping Street, central Nara)
chef_bio.background: Heizou is a small, independent creative-Chinese restaurant whose name comes, endearingly, from the owner's pet dog. After more than two decades cooking at a Chinese restaurant in Ikoma, the owner struck out alone, building a devoted local following first in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district before moving in spring 2023 into a characterful old building on the Mochiidono shopping street, just off the Kintetsu Nara arcade — taking over a former restaurant space and reopening with room for counter diners downstairs and tatami seating for groups upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than classic neighborhood 'machi-chuka', the kitchen folds Japanese and Western sensibilities into creative Chinese cooking, leaning on fresh seasonal seafood and a daily-changing lineup that the owner decides and shares on social media.
chef_bio.specialty: Mapo tofu is the heart of the menu — offered in several regional Sichuan styles (Chengdu, Deyang, Chongqing) with adjustable heat — alongside thick, chewy-skinned boiled gyoza served with either Sichuan sesame sauce or a Japanese-style dressing, dan dan noodles, mouth-watering 'yodare' chicken, and a rotating cast of dishes built around fresh seafood (often sourced from Shimane), including a multi-fish carpaccio that regulars rave about.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is named after the owner's dog, Heizou.

The owner cooked for over 20 years at a Chinese restaurant in Ikoma before opening their own place, which moved from Kitamachi to the Mochiidono shopping street in spring 2023 and sits opposite the popular kakigori shop Hoseki-bako.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around gyoza, noodles (dan dan, stir-fried ramen) and soy/doubanjiang seasonings, so shared wheat surfaces and sauces are inherent; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard Chinese/Japanese soy-based seasonings (gyoza dipping sauces, mapo tofu), which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even tofu dishes such as mapo tofu are prepared in the Sichuan style with meat, and the kitchen emphasizes pork, chicken and seafood, so vegan separation is not documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a warm, owner-run neighborhood spot where the daily menu is hand-chosen and posted in Japanese on social media, so some basic Japanese (or a translation app and a relaxed, curious attitude) will make ordering much smoother.

noodles.notes: Flat-cut (hira-uchi), chewy and springy noodles that hold up to the heavy sesame-and-chili dressing in the brothless version and the creamy sesame soup in the soup version.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (brothless dandan noodles) — chewy flat noodles tossed with sesame, fierce chili oil and tongue-numbing Sichuan peppercorn (sansho/huajiao), finished by spooning the leftover meat sauce over rice with a soft-boiled egg.

gf.note: The flat wheat-and-kansui noodles and seasoning sauces are not gluten-free; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Both styles are built on coarse-ground pork meat sauce and a sesame/soy-milk base; no documented plant-only bowl free of pork. Best to ask if a meatless preparation is possible.

ramen.pantheon: A devoted Sichuan dandan specialist tucked into Nara's Mochiidono arcade, where the soul lives in the brothless, Chengdu-faithful bowl: sesame richness lit up by huajiao numbness and a clean chili-oil burn rather than a long-simmered soup. The companion soup version softens that fire into a creamy soy-milk-and-white-sesame paitan, a gentler, Japanese-leaning take on the same lineage.

places.highlight: A devoted Sichuan dandan specialist tucked into Nara's Mochiidono arcade, where the soul lives in the brothless, Chengdu-faithful bowl: sesame richness lit up by huajiao numbness and a clean chili-oil burn rather than a long-simmered soup. The companion soup version softens that fire into a creamy soy-milk-and-white-sesame paitan, a gentler, Japanese-leaning take on the same lineage.

places.name: 美と和 (Bi to Wa)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation documented. The menu centers on coffee, tea and Japanese sweets (wagashi), which typically contain wheat/rice flour; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Drinks (coffee, tea, orange juice) may be vegan but the paired Japanese sweets are not confirmed dairy/egg-free. Vegan diners would need to ask about each item.
places.hours_raw: Approx. 11:00–18:00 (until 17:30 in winter); often opens around noon. Open Sat/Sun & holidays; Friday and some holidays operate irregularly. Closed Mon–Thu.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 美と和 (Bi to Wa) is a hidden antique-shop-and-cafe down a narrow alley in Naramachi, the old merchant quarter of central Nara. The ground floor is an antique shop selling old tools, Showa- and Taisho-era ceramics, obi sashes and Japanese tableware; the second floor is a retro tea r

chef_bio.background: 美と和 (Bi to Wa) is a hidden antique-shop-and-cafe down a narrow alley in Naramachi, the old merchant quarter of central Nara. The ground floor is an antique shop selling old tools, Showa- and Taisho-era ceramics, obi sashes and Japanese tableware; the second floor is a retro tea room of two connected tatami rooms with low tables and floor cushions, surrounded by antique clocks and period details.
chef_bio.philosophy: A quiet, nostalgic space evoking old Japan; the woman who runs it personally renovated much of the building, and guests remove their shoes to settle into the unhurried, period atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Sets pairing a drink (coffee, tea, orange juice) with Japanese sweets — for example a summer iced coffee with melon-sherbet ice set.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor, a woman, personally renovated much of the shop, giving the space its handmade, time-stepped-back feeling.
Reached down a narrow Naramachi alley near a veterinary clinic, south of Sarusawa Pond and Gangoji Temple; the first floor is an antique shop and the second-floor cafe requires removing your shoes.

cultural_comfort.note: A tucked-away, mostly Japanese-language family-run tea room reached down a narrow alley; you remove your shoes and sit on floor cushions, so a little Japanese and patience with irregular hours help.

places.name: リストランテ リンコントロ (Ristorante Lincontro)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen makes its own pasta and bread in-house, so wheat is central to the menu. A celiac diner would need to ask directly whether any courses can be prepared without wheat; no documented gluten-free protocol exists.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-forward Italian restaurant whose signature is wild game (venison, boar, duck) and house-cured prosciutto and salami. It is not vegan-oriented; while the chef works extensively with Yamato (local Nara) vegetables, there is no documented vegan menu. A vegan would need to ask in advance whether plant-only courses are possible.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 18:00-22:00 (lunch seatings 11:30/12:30; dinner seatings 18:00/19:00). Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Yakushido-cho, on the edge of Naramachi near Kyobate / Kinte
places.notes: Chef-owner Nishioka Masato opened Lincontro after first establishing his restaurant Crocetta several years earlier. He chose this corner of Naramachi because it sits beside his grandmother's former home and the neighborhood where he grew up, which gave the location a sense of per

chef_bio.background: Chef-owner Nishioka Masato opened Lincontro after first establishing his restaurant Crocetta several years earlier. He chose this corner of Naramachi because it sits beside his grandmother's former home and the neighborhood where he grew up, which gave the location a sense of personal destiny; the name 'L'incontro' is Italian for 'the encounter.' The premises were previously run by a fellow chef who passed the space to him.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nishioka is a licensed hunter who sources whole animals from producers and works with a zero-waste, nose-to-tail ethic, using everything down to the bones. He frames his cooking as treating ingredients with reverence and creating dishes you cannot find anywhere else.
chef_bio.specialty: Wild game (venison, wild boar, and duck), handmade fresh pasta, house-baked bread, and house-cured prosciutto, salami, and terrines, all built around Yamato vegetables and seasonal Nara ingredients.

anecdotes.text: He notes that summer male deer feed on green grass, giving a refreshing, approachable flavor ideal for newcomers to game, in contrast to the stronger gamey character of winter preparations; his venison roast is described as having cool-tasting fat and meat umami that expand with each bite.

He sources deer and boar from Nara's Tsuge area and wild duck from Kagoshima, then processes the animals himself into cured meats and terrines, including a terrine using whey-fed pork and guinea fowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen makes its own pasta and bread on-site, so flour is in heavy use and cross-contamination is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus center on wild game and house-cured pork products, and the chef processes whole animals in-house, so shared surfaces and equipment make strict vegan separation unlikely without prior arrangement.

positives.text: Small 15-seat, reservation-only course format means the chef can be told of dietary needs in advance and can tailor a meal around seasonal Yamato vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-based course restaurant where staff most likely operate in Japanese, so a foreign student should book ahead (ideally with some Japanese or a Japanese-speaking friend) and mention any dietary needs when reserving.

places.name: KAKIHA (KAKIHA)
places.gf_detail: The teas and powder-based drinks (persimmon-leaf tea, persimmon-vinegar drink, plum-syrup soda) are naturally wheat-free, but the shop also makes persimmon-leaf cake and persimmon-leaf udon (udon is wheat). No celiac/gluten-free protocol is documented, so a strictly gluten-free traveler should stick to the drinks and ask before ordering any sweets.
places.vegan_detail: Products are built around persimmon-leaf tea and persimmon-leaf powder and the official site states all products are plant-based and suitable for vegan diets. No fish dashi or meat is involved; drinks use persimmon-leaf powder, plum syrup and persimmon vinegar. Baked sweets/cake should still be confirmed for eggs/dairy on the day, but the core offering is genuinely plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00, 不定休（pandemic period merchandise-only sales noted）
places.neighborhood: JR奈良駅前（三条本町）／ビエラ奈良 2F
places.notes: KAKIHA started in 2014 as a tiny six-seat counter shop in Gojo City, southern Nara, devoted to the leaves of the persimmon tree. After a television feature brought overwhelming crowds, the original shop closed within about two years, and the team reopened in December 2019 as a st

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Gojo City origins; persimmon leaves grown on the company's own organic farm in Tenri City)
chef_bio.background: KAKIHA started in 2014 as a tiny six-seat counter shop in Gojo City, southern Nara, devoted to the leaves of the persimmon tree. After a television feature brought overwhelming crowds, the original shop closed within about two years, and the team reopened in December 2019 as a stand on the second floor of the Viela building at JR Nara Station so they could reconnect with customers in the city center.
chef_bio.philosophy: Because these are products people would drink and eat every day, the founders insisted on rigorous safety, seeking out clean farmland free from pesticide drift; their guiding idea is that every single customer matters and that spreading the everyday benefits of the persimmon leaf is worth doing slowly and carefully.
chef_bio.specialty: Organic persimmon-leaf tea and persimmon-leaf-powder drinks and sweets — KAKIHA tea (hot/iced), a plum-syrup persimmon-leaf soda, a persimmon-vinegar drink, plus persimmon-leaf cake and udon, with daily-rotating powder-based beverages and confections.

anecdotes.text: The persimmon leaves come from a roughly 36,530-square-meter organic farm in Takenouchi, Tenri City, reclaimed from abandoned land along Japan's oldest pilgrimage route; harvest-time leaves were found to carry exceptionally high vitamin C, which set the shop on its persimmon-leaf path.

The shop's first incarnation was a six-seat counter in Gojo City that closed after about two years when a TV appearance brought more customers than it could handle, prompting the 2019 reopening at JR Nara Station.

positives.text: All products are plant-based and described as suitable for vegan diets, built around organic persimmon-leaf tea and persimmon-leaf powder rather than animal ingredients or fish dashi.
Persimmon leaves are grown without pesticides on the company's own organic-certified farm, with cultivation through to product carried out consistently in-house.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny grab-and-go tea stand inside the JR Nara station building where you just point and pay; little English is likely but ordering a cheap persimmon-leaf tea or soda needs almost no conversation.

places.name: cafe zuccu（カフェ ズック） (cafe zuccu)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The signature offerings are wheat-based: house-made pasta, sandwiches on bread, toast, and fruit tarts/cakes. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented. A celiac traveler should avoid the food here and treat it as a coffee-only stop at best.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. Menus rotate seasonally and lean heavily on animal products — documented dishes include chicken-neck oil-sauce pasta, mackerel sandwiches with cheese, egg sandwiches, and cream/cheese fruit tarts. Coffee and tea are available, but no specifically vegan food items are advertised. A vegan would need to ask and may only be able to have black coffee or a plain drink.
places.hours_raw: Per the official site: standard 8:00-18:00 (food L.O. 16:30, drink L.O. 17:00); Wednesday 8:00-17:00 (earlier L.O.); Tuesday and Friday open from 11:00-18:00 (no morning service); closed Thursdays. Morning service is offered on opening-at-8 days until late morning/sold out. (Tabelog lists a simpler 8:00-18:00 with Wed shorter; the official site's day-specific hours are used here and may vary.)
places.neighborhood: 高畑町 (Takabatake-chō), southeast of Naramachi near Kasuga Tai
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] cafe zuccu sits in a wooden machiya townhouse built in the late Edo period, more than 160 years ago, in the tranquil Takabatake district southeast of Naramachi and close to Kasuga Taisha. The building has been gently renovated rather than gutted — original earthen walls were inte

chef_bio.background: cafe zuccu sits in a wooden machiya townhouse built in the late Edo period, more than 160 years ago, in the tranquil Takabatake district southeast of Naramachi and close to Kasuga Taisha. The building has been gently renovated rather than gutted — original earthen walls were intentionally left as they were to preserve the old house's character — and the rooms are filled with antique furniture along with magazines and novels that guests are free to browse, giving it the feel of settling into a friend's historic home.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café leans on locally sourced, seasonal Yamato vegetables and fruit, building a rotating menu around what's good that month rather than a fixed list.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made pasta featuring local seasonal vegetables (for example a chicken-neck, fresh-ginger and edamame oil-sauce pasta), seasonal sandwiches (such as mackerel with tomato, onion, cheese and cilantro), seasonal fruit tarts (e.g. bankan citrus, banana and cheese-cream), and coffee brewed from a Kyoto roastery's blend for a rich, classic kissaten flavor.

anecdotes.text: The owner is an antique enthusiast, and curated antiques and collectibles are displayed throughout the space, with books and magazines left out for guests to read freely.
Coffee is made with a blend from a Kyoto roastery, aiming for a deep, traditional coffee-shop taste that suits the old townhouse setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat-based foods — house-made pasta, bread sandwiches, toast, and tarts/cakes — so gluten is present throughout the food prep; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu items prominently use meat, fish, eggs, cheese and cream (e.g. chicken-neck pasta, mackerel-and-cheese sandwich, egg sandwich, cream tarts); no dedicated vegan prep is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, locally run townhouse café where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; English is likely limited and payment is cash only, so come prepared and you'll be warmly welcomed.

places.name: 茶房 暖暖 (Sabo Nonnon)
places.gf_detail: The signature chagayu is made from rice and tea, so the porridge base is naturally gluten-free, and many accompaniments (pickles, umeboshi, Yamato vegetables) are rice/vegetable-based. But this is a traditional Japanese kitchen with no celiac protocol: side dishes and the noppe stew very likely involve wheat-containing soy sauce and possibly dashi/seasonings with wheat, and warabi-mochi/kudzu sweets can carry cross-contact risk. Nothing is documented about dedicated GF preparation or cross-contamination control. Not safe for strict celiacs without detailed advance discussion in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: An English-language tourism listing notes that vegetarian options are available, and the kitchen leans heavily on Yamato (local Nara) vegetables — chagayu (tea rice porridge), pickled radish/vegetables, plums, and sweets such as warabi-mochi and shiratama. However, the noppe vegetable stew and the porridge accompaniments are very likely prepared with fish-based dashi in the traditional manner, and this has not been confirmed as fish-free. Sweets like warabi-mochi and kudzu drinks are typically plant-based. Vegans should ask in advance about dashi and whether a fully plant-based set can be arranged; it is not a dedicated vegan kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 11:30–14:00; Sat/Sun & holidays 11:00–14:00 (sources vary, with some listing L.O. up to 14:30–15:00 and seasonal variation). Closed Mondays (open if Monday is a national holiday).
places.neighborhood: ならまち (Naramachi), 西新屋町
places.cuisine: 町家甘味処・郷土料理
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sabo Nonnon occupies the main residence (omoya) of the former Matsuyama family, one of Naramachi's prominent merchant houses — a townhouse roughly 180 to 200 years old that carries cultural-property designation and is known today as the Nara Orient Hall (奈良オリエント館). The tearoom si

chef_bio.background: Sabo Nonnon occupies the main residence (omoya) of the former Matsuyama family, one of Naramachi's prominent merchant houses — a townhouse roughly 180 to 200 years old that carries cultural-property designation and is known today as the Nara Orient Hall (奈良オリエント館). The tearoom sits in the heart of the old merchant quarter of Naramachi, with tatami rooms looking onto a quiet interior garden.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Nara's centuries-old chagayu tradition and Yamato vegetables in the setting of a preserved merchant townhouse.
chef_bio.specialty: Chagayu — Nara tea rice porridge, a local dish said to have been enjoyed in the region for over 1,300 years. It is served bubbling in a cast-iron pot, with a choice of hojicha (roasted green tea) or azuki (red-bean) tea, and diners can shift the flavor partway through with salt or matcha. It comes as a set with Yamato (local) vegetable side dishes, dried radish, Nara pickles, umeboshi, and noppe vegetable stew. Sweets include Yamato-tea warabi-mochi, shiratama (rice-ball) zenzai, kudzu drinks, kurumi okowa, and seasonal shaved ice.

anecdotes.text: Guests are encouraged to change the taste of the tea porridge mid-meal by adding salt or matcha powder, and in summer chilled versions are offered.
The building was the main house of the wealthy Matsuyama merchant family and is roughly 180–200 years old with cultural-property designation, now operating as the Nara Orient Hall.

positives.text: An English menu is available and vegetarian options are noted by an English tourism listing.
The signature chagayu is a rice-and-tea porridge accompanied by Yamato vegetables, pickles, and plums, so the core dish is plant- and rice-based.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is offered and photography is welcome, so a foreign student can manage with a few words of Japanese and pointing, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 農園直送レストラン 古都華 (Nouen Chokuso Restaurant Kotoka)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The menu centers on Japanese set meals (tonkatsu, curry, hamburger rice bowl, tempura) that use wheat flour, soy sauce, and breading, and rice dishes are served with miso/dashi-based sides. Naturally there is an abundant raw/cooked vegetable buffet, but no celiac-safe handling or cross-contamination controls are documented, so a strict gluten-free diner would need to ask in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The restaurant's draw is Nara-prefecture meat (Yamato pork, Yamato beef, Yamato chicken) and eggs, and Japanese set meals typically use fish dashi. However the signature かご農膳 lunch includes an all-you-can-eat seasonal Yamato-vegetable buffet, so a plant-forward diner can eat well from the vegetable selection. Specific vegan-certified entrees and dashi-free preparation are not documented; confirm at the counter.
places.neighborhood: JR Nara Station building, Sanjo-honmachi, Nara City
places.cuisine: 大和野菜・奈良食材の農園直送定食。野菜ビュッフェ付き「かご農膳」、大和ポーク・大和牛・大
places.notes: Tucked inside the first floor of JR Nara Station, Kotoka is the dining room of the 'Nara no Umaimono Plaza,' an antenna shop run by the Nara Delicious Foods Association (a roughly 80-member group of local producers). Fresh vegetables, fruit and other ingredients are delivered str

chef_bio.origin: Nara City, inside JR Nara Station
chef_bio.background: Tucked inside the first floor of JR Nara Station, Kotoka is the dining room of the 'Nara no Umaimono Plaza,' an antenna shop run by the Nara Delicious Foods Association (a roughly 80-member group of local producers). Fresh vegetables, fruit and other ingredients are delivered straight from partner farms across the prefecture, many grown on soil that is strictly vetted, and served in the adjoining restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The association sets out to honor Nara's role as Japan's ancient capital and a cradle of its food culture while creating new local specialties, championing producer-direct ingredients and rare Yamato heirloom vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature lunch, the 'kago nouzen' (かご農膳), pairs a chosen main — Yamato pork tonkatsu, curry, beef hamburger rice bowl, shrimp tempura or katsuo tataki — with an all-you-can-eat buffet of seasonal Yamato vegetables. Rice is Nara-grown Hinohikari, and breakfasts feature tamago-kake gohan made with rich-yolked eggs from Gojo.

anecdotes.text: At lunch many guests pair the kago nouzen with a regional sake tasting set served with two small dishes, a nod to Nara being celebrated as a birthplace of Japanese sake — daytime drinking is openly welcomed.
All rice on the menu is Nara-prefecture Hinohikari, and the produce rotates with the season because it is sourced direct from member farms across the prefecture.

positives.text: The signature lunch comes with an all-you-can-eat buffet of seasonal Yamato vegetables, giving plant-forward diners a generous spread of fresh produce to build a meal from.
Ingredients are delivered direct from vetted prefecture farms, so the vegetable selection is fresh and seasonal.

cultural_comfort.note: Easy first stop: it sits right inside JR Nara Station with a casual counter-and-table setup, picture-friendly set meals and an open vegetable buffet, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with limited Japanese, though menus and staff are primarily Japanese.

places.name: はり新 (Harishin)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Yamato cuisine restaurant serving set meals (tempura set, bento, the Asuka milk hotpot) that rely on soy sauce, fish/chicken dashi and wheat-based items; the Asuka hotpot is even finished with udon (wheat). No gluten-free menu or allergy program is documented, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Dishes center on chicken, fish, dashi, eggs and the ancient dairy-based cheese 蘇 (su); the signature Asuka hotpot uses chicken broth and milk. While it features Yamato vegetables and Yoshino kuzu, no vegan or vegetarian menu is documented and most dishes use fish or chicken stock, so it is not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (last order 14:00), Dinner 18:00-21:00 (last order 19:30). Closed Mondays (or the following day if Monday is a holiday) and Thursday evenings.
places.neighborhood: ならまち (中新屋町)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Harishin occupies a roughly 250-year-old machiya townhouse, formerly a money-changer's (両替商) shop, in the historic Naramachi quarter on the west side of the World Heritage temple Gangoji. The house faces the ancient 'Kamitsumichi' road (上ッ道) that once linked Fujiwara-kyo and Heij

chef_bio.background: Harishin occupies a roughly 250-year-old machiya townhouse, formerly a money-changer's (両替商) shop, in the historic Naramachi quarter on the west side of the World Heritage temple Gangoji. The house faces the ancient 'Kamitsumichi' road (上ッ道) that once linked Fujiwara-kyo and Heijo-kyo and served as a pilgrimage and trade route during the Nara period.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking that draws on Nara's history and local Yamato ingredients, served in a preserved Edo-era merchant townhouse so guests experience old Naramachi alongside the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato regional cuisine highlighting Nara ingredients: the Kamitsumichi bento, the milk-based Asuka hotpot (飛鳥�, by reservation), tempura set meals, and the revived ancient cheese 蘇 (su), alongside Yamato vegetables like Yamato-mana and Yoshino kuzu and persimmon vinegar.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name and concept reference the Kamitsumichi, an ancient upper road that connected the old capitals and along which travelers set out with prepared meals toward the Ise shrines.
It serves the revived ancient dairy 'so' (蘇), an early Japanese cheese-like food, as a nod to Nara's classical heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Set meals include tempura (wheat batter) and the Asuka hotpot is traditionally finished with udon (wheat), indicating wheat is used throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Yamato cuisine here uses soy sauce (usukuchi/light soy in the hotpot), which normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes use chicken broth, milk, fish dashi, eggs and the dairy-based 蘇 (su); no vegan menu is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional set-meal restaurant in a 250-year-old townhouse where some menu items (like the Asuka hotpot) need a day-ahead reservation, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel comfortable.

places.name: かめや (Kameya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any form. This is an okonomiyaki, negi-yaki, yakisoba and teppanyaki shop — the core of nearly every dish is a wheat-flour batter or wheat noodles, finished with Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce that contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no separate prep, and the entire griddle is used for wheat-batter cooking. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not eat here.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The signature dishes are built on egg, and standard ingredients include squid, shrimp, beef, fish cake (chikuwa) and pork. The batter is mixed with fish/bonito dashi and dishes are commonly topped with bonito flakes (katsuobushi) and mayonnaise (egg). No documented vegan or vegetarian options exist.
places.neighborhood: 樽井町（猿沢池・三条通り近く）
places.cuisine: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き・焼きそば
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kameya is a long-running okonomiyaki and teppanyaki institution that opened in 1965, just a short walk from Sarusawa Pond along the approach toward Sanjo-dori. More than half a century on, it remains a family-style neighborhood spot, recognizable from the street by its big red la

chef_bio.background: Kameya is a long-running okonomiyaki and teppanyaki institution that opened in 1965, just a short walk from Sarusawa Pond along the approach toward Sanjo-dori. More than half a century on, it remains a family-style neighborhood spot, recognizable from the street by its big red lantern and packed with a mix of local families, after-work groups, and visitors who have wandered up from the pond.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its own draw simply as a distinctly flavored okonomiyaki and negi-yaki experience — straightforward griddle cooking meant to be enjoyed casually with a beer.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the Daibutsu-yaki ('Great Buddha grill'), a jumbo negi-yaki roughly 35-40cm across that has been on the menu for over 20 years. It is loaded with green onion, squid, shrimp, beef, seasoned konnyaku, fish cake and egg, then finished with mayonnaise piped into the characters 大佛 (Great Buddha) on top. Because the batter is spread thin rather than built up like a thick okonomiyaki, the giant pancake stays surprisingly easy to eat and is comfortably shared by two. Alongside it the kitchen turns out classic okonomiyaki, negi-yaki, yakisoba and teppanyaki, including crowd-pleasers like tonpei-yaki and buta-tama.

anecdotes.text: The Daibutsu-yaki is built around heaps of green onion with mayonnaise drawn into the characters for the Great Buddha; the thin batter means even one person can finish the roughly 40cm pancake, though it is sized to share.
Founded in 1965, the shop seats around 55 across tatami and table sections and draws families with children, reserved salaryman parties, and international tourists alike.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All core dishes (okonomiyaki, negi-yaki, yakisoba, teppanyaki) are wheat-flour batter or wheat noodles cooked on a shared griddle, with no gluten-free menu or separate preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with Worcestershire-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard ingredients across the menu include squid, shrimp, beef, fish cake and egg, with bonito/fish dashi in the batter; no vegan separation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the shop is used to international tourists, so a foreign student can order comfortably; griddle dishes are sometimes cooked at your table, which is part of the fun.

places.name: 海鮮料理 つじ平 (Kaisen Ryori Tsujihira)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation documented. This is a Japanese seafood izakaya built around soy sauce, fish dashi, tempura/fried fish, and simmered dishes — all typically wheat-containing — so it is not safe for celiac diners. Sashimi exists but is served with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce, and there is no dedicated GF handling.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood restaurant; nearly every dish centers on fish or uses fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented and the kitchen is not set up for plant-based dining.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); Sun closed. Closed also 1st & 3rd Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Sanjo-honmachi (1-minute walk from JR Nara Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing, family-run seafood spot tucked into the Sanjo-honmachi blocks barely a minute from JR Nara Station. It works like a neighborhood catch-of-the-day kitchen: the menu flexes with whatever fresh fish comes in that morning, so regulars come back to see what is good to

chef_bio.origin: Central Nara, near JR Nara Station
chef_bio.background: A long-standing, family-run seafood spot tucked into the Sanjo-honmachi blocks barely a minute from JR Nara Station. It works like a neighborhood catch-of-the-day kitchen: the menu flexes with whatever fresh fish comes in that morning, so regulars come back to see what is good today rather than to order a fixed list.
chef_bio.philosophy: Buy fresh, cook simply, change the menu with the day's catch — value and freshness over flash.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh fish in every form — generous sashimi, grilled and simmered fish, and fried-fish sets. Lunch is the local draw: an excellent-value daily set (around 1,000 yen) that can include a beef main, simmered sea bream, sashimi, small dishes, rice, soup and pickles, plus a famous Tuesday-only special seafood donburi (yellowtail and salmon in sesame sauce topped with flying-fish roe).

anecdotes.text: The kitchen builds its menu around fish caught that day, so the offerings shift with the daily catch and the lunch set changes regularly.
A standout is the Tuesday-lunch-only special seafood donburi (around 800 yen) — yellowtail and salmon in sesame sauce with flying-fish roe, served with sea bream broth, tofu and pickles.
The value daily lunch set (around 1,000 yen) has included a beef steak main alongside simmered sea bream, sashimi, two small dishes, rice, soup and pickles, and the place fills with weekday regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is heavy on tempura/fried fish, soy sauce and fish dashi; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contact with wheat is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled-fish dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no tamari/GF soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi and fish ingredients are central to nearly all dishes including soups and small plates; assume animal products throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local fish restaurant with no English noted, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, but the counter and set lunches make ordering approachable for a foreign student.

places.name: 上海楼 (Shanhairo)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Chinese restaurant whose menu is built around soy sauce, wheat-based seasonings, ramen and gyoza, and flour-thickened sauces. There is no indication of gluten-free awareness or accommodation, and shared woks and fryers make it unsuitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-focused Chinese kitchen (mapo tofu with pork, sweet-and-sour pork, shrimp chili, crab omelet, ramen). Sauces and stocks typically use meat and fish, and there are no documented vegan dishes or accommodations.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Thursdays. (Some listings note an earlier 10:30 opening.)
places.neighborhood: 東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shanhairo has been serving the Higashimuki shopping arcade just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station since 1945 (Showa 20), making it one of central Nara's longest-running Chinese restaurants. It keeps the unmistakable Showa-era machi-chuka character, with bright red tables on the gr

chef_bio.background: Shanhairo has been serving the Higashimuki shopping arcade just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station since 1945 (Showa 20), making it one of central Nara's longest-running Chinese restaurants. It keeps the unmistakable Showa-era machi-chuka character, with bright red tables on the ground floor and an upstairs banquet room that seats groups of up to about thirty.
chef_bio.philosophy: Straightforward, orthodox cooking that lets genuine skill speak through familiar dishes rather than flashy specialties.
chef_bio.specialty: Honest, classic Chinese home cooking: generous-value lunch sets, mapo tofu, sweet-and-sour pork, shrimp in chili sauce, crab omelet, egg fried rice, and almond tofu for dessert. Most of the regular menu (apart from the noodles) is also available to take out.

anecdotes.text: A local food writer notes the restaurant gets little media attention despite real skill in the kitchen, and praises its affordable lunch course as a quiet downtown gem.
A long-running set lunch (rice, soup, salad and a spring roll) has been offered at a famously low price, drawing families and regulars in the arcade.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on ramen, gyoza, fried items and flour-thickened sauces cooked in shared woks and fryers, so cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a classic Chinese kitchen, dishes rely heavily on regular (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use pork, shrimp and crab, and stocks/sauces are meat- and fish-based, with no separate vegan preparation noted.

cultural_comfort.note: A laid-back, family-friendly neighborhood Chinese spot where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo menu will get you a good meal, though English support is not guaranteed.

noodles.notes: Diners describe a generous portion of soft noodles that prove oddly addictive — the supple, yielding texture is part of the old-fashioned charm rather than a firm modern bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba (中華そば) — the classic clear shoyu bowl; or the chashu-men for more pork. The shark-fin ramen (ふかひれラーメン) is the house extravagance, a shoyu soup thickened with ankake to stay piping hot.
ramen.chashu: Not documented in detail; a sliced pork chashu accompanies the chashu-men in the traditional town-Chinese style.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and wheat-based shoyu seasoning; a wheat-thickened ankake on the shark-fin version. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is offered, so it is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soup and seasonings are built on animal stock and shoyu in classic Chinese-restaurant style; no documented plant-based broth bowl. Ask staff, but expect no vegan ramen here.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl from a 1945 family Chinese restaurant on Nara's covered arcade, this is the disappearing art of the chuka-soba: a clear, gentle shoyu soup whose soul is restraint, not richness. You taste the lineage of post-war neighborhood China-meshi — light, nostalgic, and made to be slurped beside fried rice and gyoza rather than to dazzle.

places.highlight: A bowl from a 1945 family Chinese restaurant on Nara's covered arcade, this is the disappearing art of the chuka-soba: a clear, gentle shoyu soup whose soul is restraint, not richness. You taste the lineage of post-war neighborhood China-meshi — light, nostalgic, and made to be slurped beside fried rice and gyoza rather than to dazzle.

places.name: シャトードール もちいどの店 (Chateau d'Or Mochiidono)
places.gf_detail: This is a bakery whose core offerings are bread, sandwiches and wheat-flour pizza, all gluten-containing. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and shared bakery/pizza-oven surfaces make heavy cross-contamination with flour likely. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling is documented. The menu centers on bread (typically containing egg/dairy/butter), cheese pizzas (e.g. Quattro Formaggi), egg-topped morning sets, seafood/chicken ajillo, beef curry donut and whitebait pizza. A traveler would need to ask, and plant-based options are not confirmed.
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono shopping arcade, central Nara (walking distance f
places.cuisine: Bakery cafe with wood-fired pizza, sandwiche
places.notes: An offshoot of Chateau d'Or, a Nara bakery that has been baking for more than fifty years. The shop sits in the Mochiidono shopping arcade in the former Mitsuhashi location and represents a newer all-day format that pairs a retail bread counter with a sit-down dining area.

chef_bio.background: An offshoot of Chateau d'Or, a Nara bakery that has been baking for more than fifty years. The shop sits in the Mochiidono shopping arcade in the former Mitsuhashi location and represents a newer all-day format that pairs a retail bread counter with a sit-down dining area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Positioned as a place to both shop for bread and sit down for a full meal from morning toast sets through lunch pizza to afternoon cafe time, welcoming visitors exploring central Nara.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizza such as the Quattro Formaggi and the light Chichinielli with whitebait, mizuna and parmesan; volume-packed sandwiches; the signature Nara shokupan; the deep-fried beef curry donut filled with chunky house-made curry; and the popular 200g Daikoku anpan, also offered with a Yamato sake-starter version.

anecdotes.text: The long, narrow space is laid out with a bread sales counter at the front, a central kitchen with a wood-fired pizza oven, and roomy table seating plus a bright terrace at the back.
The parent bakery has been a Nara institution for over fifty years and has grown to include other locations such as a branch in Yamatokoriyama and a hotel cafe.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is an active bakery and pizzeria where wheat flour is used throughout for bread and pizza dough, making airborne and surface flour cross-contamination very likely.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed bakery-cafe where you can order at the counter and point at displayed breads, so a few words of Japanese and gestures are enough for a foreign student to enjoy a meal here.

places.name: バンブーノ (Bambuno)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly: the signature dishes are wheat pasta (奈良町パスタ, パスタ大和) and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy protocol. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Primarily an omnivore kitchen built around Yamato pork, cream and cheese, so it is not a vegan destination. However, it leans heavily on its own organic Nara vegetables and the cafe-time vegetable shaved ice ('ベジ氷') is vegetable-forward; some vegetable dishes may suit vegetarians. Vegans should call ahead and ask, as no formally vegan menu is documented and dairy/animal stock are common.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) lunch / 14:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:00) cafe / 18:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30) dinner. Closed Tuesdays (irregular closures during event periods).
places.neighborhood: ならまち（今御門町・猿沢池近く）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Bambuno is a husband-and-wife-run creative Italian restaurant in a renovated 100-plus-year-old machiya townhouse in Nara's old Naramachi quarter, a short walk from Sarusawa Pond. It has been running for over a decade and operates in three parts through the day: lunch, an afternoo

chef_bio.background: Bambuno is a husband-and-wife-run creative Italian restaurant in a renovated 100-plus-year-old machiya townhouse in Nara's old Naramachi quarter, a short walk from Sarusawa Pond. It has been running for over a decade and operates in three parts through the day: lunch, an afternoon cafe, and dinner.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local sourcing and community: the kitchen builds dishes around organic vegetables from its own kitchen garden and Nara-prefecture ingredients, with the stated idea that local encounters turn into deliciousness that makes people happy.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature 'Naramachi Pasta' is a cream pasta made with sake lees from the local Harushika (Harushika Brewery) daiginjo, paired with Nara-brand Yamato Pork, seasonal local vegetables and grated ginger. 'Pasta Yamato' (with roasted-tea/hojicha flavor) and house-made bagna cauda also feature. During cafe hours the kitchen serves an unusual vegetable shaved ice known as 'Veji-gori' (ベジ氷), such as a tomato-and-apple shaved ice and a taro-and-ruby-chocolate version.

anecdotes.text: The Naramachi Pasta was developed in spring 2014 in collaboration with Harushika Brewery, folding the brewery's daiginjo sake lees into the cream sauce so diners can taste Nara in the dish.

The vegetable shaved-ice menu began when the owners inherited a Swan-brand shaved-ice machine that had been bought for their grandparents' food stand at the now-closed Nara Dreamland; rather than waste it, they turned it into a vegetable-based cafe specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes are wheat-based pasta; no gluten-free menu or cross-contamination protocol is documented, so the kitchen handles wheat throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An omnivore Italian kitchen using Yamato pork, cream and cheese; no documented separation for vegan diners, so cross-contact with dairy and meat should be assumed unless confirmed.

positives.text: Heavy use of the restaurant's own organic Nara vegetables and a fully vegetable-based cafe-time shaved ice menu offers some plant-forward options.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-run machiya cafe-restaurant where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably; staff are warm but English may be limited.

places.name: 玉ろ (Gyokuro)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional sushi restaurant where soy sauce (wheat-based) is used throughout, and the lunch menu prominently features udon (wheat noodles). Sushi rice is seasoned with vinegar but the prep, dashi-based simmered items (e.g. simmered anago, kōya-dōfu) and shared kitchen carry high gluten cross-contact risk. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. As a sushi restaurant, virtually every dish centers on raw and cooked fish/seafood, and broths/simmered items rely on fish dashi. Even the chirashi's tofu sits atop fish-based ingredients. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented.
places.hours_raw: Dinner: Tue–Sun 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Lunch around 12:00–14:00 but on irregular days only (お昼の営業は不定休). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Motorinoin-chō, Naramachi (near Mochiidono shopping arcade,
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established sushi shop tucked into the old streets of Naramachi, said to have been going for more than 70 years. The owner-chef works the counter alone, hand-pressing each piece and choosing the day's fish himself, while his wife runs the warm front-of-house service.

chef_bio.background: A long-established sushi shop tucked into the old streets of Naramachi, said to have been going for more than 70 years. The owner-chef works the counter alone, hand-pressing each piece and choosing the day's fish himself, while his wife runs the warm front-of-house service.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, ingredient-first counter where everything is made by one pair of hands — service can be slow at busy times precisely because the owner refuses to rush the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-pressed nigiri and an unusually generous chirashi (tuna, squid, yellowtail, shrimp, scallop, salmon, sea bream, eel — and, distinctively, simmered kōya-dōfu tofu over shiitake-and-egg-ribbon rice). The affordable lunch set of nigiri, chirashi, tekka-don and very cheap udon makes a famously old-school sushi experience accessible at midday.

anecdotes.text: The counter is a single handsome plank of wood, with a small sunken tatami room downstairs and a banquet room upstairs; live tanks of horse mackerel (aji) and filefish sit behind the counter.
Lunch sets are remarkably gentle on the wallet — nigiri ¥1,430, chirashi and tekka-don ¥1,750, and udon as little as ¥550–¥660 — an everyday way into a 70-year-old sushi house.
Described as a calm, grown-up establishment that nonetheless feels approachable thanks to the owner's gentle manner and the proprietress's warm service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Udon (wheat noodles) are a core part of the lunch menu and are prepared in the same small kitchen, creating gluten cross-contact risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard sushi-style soy sauce (made with wheat) is used throughout, as is typical for a traditional sushi-ya; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are built around fish/seafood and fish dashi; even the chirashi's tofu is served over and alongside fish, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional neighborhood sushi counter where the owner cooks alone and the proprietress serves; little to no English, so a few Japanese phrases (or pointing at the whiteboard lunch specials) will help, but the welcome is warm and unpretentious.

places.name: ピノキオ (Pinocchio)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The menu centers on wheat-based dishes (pasta, pizza, breaded/fried items) and omurice is made with egg over chicken rice; sauces such as demi-glace typically contain wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. Signature omurice uses egg and chicken; pasta, doria and most dishes rely on meat, seafood, dairy and egg. No plant-based menu found.
places.neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街
places.cuisine: 昔ながらの個人喫茶・洋食
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Pinocchio is a long-running, old-fashioned individual coffee shop (kissaten) in central Nara. It originally stood on Sanjo-dori, where it became known as a filming location for anime and dramas, before relocating into the historic Mochiidono shopping arcade a few minutes' walk fr

chef_bio.background: Pinocchio is a long-running, old-fashioned individual coffee shop (kissaten) in central Nara. It originally stood on Sanjo-dori, where it became known as a filming location for anime and dramas, before relocating into the historic Mochiidono shopping arcade a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Though it bills itself as a café, it is really a place to settle in for a proper meal and linger over coffee while watching the flow of people through the arcade.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the omurice: thin-cooked fluffy egg wrapped over chicken rice with onion and mushroom, served in classic, nostalgic style with variations such as cheese and seafood. The kitchen also turns out a wide range of hearty plates including curry doria, cod-roe cream pasta, hamburger rice bowls and pizza, plus homemade desserts like chiffon cake, baked and rare cheesecake, and chocolate cake.

anecdotes.text: Its former Sanjo-dori location gained a following as a filming spot for anime and television dramas before the shop moved into the Mochiidono arcade.
Although classed as a café, most customers come for the substantial rice-based meals, above all the omurice, rather than just drinks.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy old-school Japanese café where ordering in basic Japanese (pointing at the omurice menu) will go smoothly, but English support is unlikely.

places.name: cervo bianco（チェルボ ビアンコ） (cervo bianco)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core menu is wood-fired pizza (wheat dough), and there are no published gluten-free pizza, pasta, or dedicated-prep claims. A celiac traveler should not assume safe options here. Gelato may be naturally GF but no allergen or cross-contamination information is published; ask directly if attempting.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature pizzas rely heavily on cheese (mozzarella, gorgonzola, camembert, grana), and others use Yamato pork sausage or sea bream. Margherita/Marinara exist but Margherita has cheese; Marinara (tomato, garlic, oregano, no cheese) could potentially be vegan but this is not confirmed. Gelato is dairy-based (Uemura farm milk). Vegetarians have pizza options; strict vegans would need to ask about a cheese-free pizza.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (lunch/café); after 17:00 reservation-only by phone. Sources vary slightly (11:00–16:00 / 11:00–17:00). Pizza service noted as closed Thursdays; gelato has irregular breaks.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Takamikadomachi), central Nara near Sarusawa Pond
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] cervo bianco opened in June 2018 inside a renovated Edo-period machiya (traditional townhouse) in the heart of Naramachi. It was created by Ashibiya Honpo, a long-established Nara pickle (narazuke) maker founded in the late Edo period, as part of an effort to bring new life to th

chef_bio.background: cervo bianco opened in June 2018 inside a renovated Edo-period machiya (traditional townhouse) in the heart of Naramachi. It was created by Ashibiya Honpo, a long-established Nara pickle (narazuke) maker founded in the late Edo period, as part of an effort to bring new life to the old neighborhood. The wood-fired stone oven sits where the house's old bathhouse once stood, and its chimney is a surviving piece of that original structure — Western cooking quietly folded into a Japanese home.
chef_bio.philosophy: An 'Enjoy NARA Flavor' concept — celebrating local Yamato produce, dairy, and tea through Italian technique, with the white-deer motif (the name means 'white deer' in Italian, after the legend of a deity arriving in Nara on a white stag) running through the artwork and dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizza and house-made gelato built around Nara-prefecture ingredients.
