The signature pizza, named after the ashibi (馬酔木) plant, pairs local narazuke pickles with four cheeses (mozzarella, gorgonzola, camembert, grana). Other pizzas include a Yamato-pork salsiccia, a 'machiya' pizza with white miso and sea bream, plus Margherita and Marinara. The gelato is made daily from Uemura farm milk, Koto-hana strawberries, Nara figs, and Tsukigase Yamato tea, and is served with deer-shaped cookies.

anecdotes.text: The wood-fired oven was placed where the townhouse's former bath once stood; the old chimney remains as a trace of the bathhouse.
The shop's name and deer artwork nod to the legend that a deity rode a white deer to Nara, said to be the origin of the city's sacred deer.
Chef Hiroki Kawahashi trained in Italian and French cuisine with experience in Canada, which shapes the creative, locally-sourced pizzas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pizza is the core product and is made from wheat dough in a shared wood-fired oven; no gluten-free dough or separate prep is documented, so heavy gluten presence and cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cheese is central to most pizzas and the gelato is dairy-based (Uemura farm milk); shared oven and prep surfaces handle cheese and meat, so dairy cross-contact is likely.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local café in a historic townhouse where a foreign student can comfortably point at the pizza or gelato menu and get by with a few basic Japanese phrases.

places.name: おちゃのこ（御茶乃子） (Ochanoko)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature reicha (擂茶) is a blended powder of 20-plus beans, grains, and green tea — grains may include wheat or be cross-contaminated, and shaved-ice syrups and toppings (e.g., walnut mochi) are unverified for gluten. A celiac traveler must ask about each item; treat as not confirmed gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: Teas themselves (Yamato tea, Taiwanese tea) are plant-based, but the cafe's headline items are dairy-based: hojicha latte ice and strawberry-milk ice use milk, and toppings like ice cream and mochi vary. Plain tea is fine for vegans; the shaved-ice and latte menu generally is not unless a dairy-free option is confirmed by asking.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-19:00 (tea/cafe L.O. 18:00); Sat/Sun/holidays 10:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:00). (Some third-party listings cite 10:00-20:00.)
places.neighborhood: 小西町・小西さくら通り商店街（コトモール1F）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ochanoko is a small specialty tea shop on the Konishi-sakura shopping street in central Nara, tucked into the ground floor of the CoMoLLE (コトモール) building a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. It began as a venture connected to Nara's 'Yume Cube' (夢CUBE) startup-incubat

chef_bio.background: Ochanoko is a small specialty tea shop on the Konishi-sakura shopping street in central Nara, tucked into the ground floor of the CoMoLLE (コトモール) building a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. It began as a venture connected to Nara's 'Yume Cube' (夢CUBE) startup-incubator program and grew into a beloved neighborhood spot. Alongside its retail tea counter it runs a small cafe known for shaved ice served all year round.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on authentic, additive-free tea and on sharing reicha's tradition of hospitality, paired with shaved ice whose signature is an airy texture that 'melts away the moment you eat it.'
chef_bio.specialty: The shop's identity rests on three teas — locally grown Yamato (Nara) tea, Taiwanese tea sourced from leading Taiwan producers, and reicha (擂茶), a Hakka 'hospitality tea' blending more than twenty kinds of beans, grains, and green tea into a powdered drink, made additive-, color-, and fragrance-free. Its best-known cafe items are fluffy, quick-melting shaved ice with house-made grown-up syrups: original reicha ice, hojicha latte ice, and Asuka Ruby strawberry-milk ice, with add-ons like ice cream and walnut mochi.

anecdotes.text: Reicha (擂茶) is presented as a hospitality drink rooted in the Hakka people's tradition, with origins traced back to China's Three Kingdoms era; the shop sells it as an additive-free powdered blend of 20-plus beans, grains, and green tea.
Visitors repeatedly praise the shaved ice for being so light and fluffy that it seems to disappear the instant it touches your mouth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature reicha is a powdered blend of 20-plus beans and grains plus green tea, which may contain or be cross-contaminated with wheat; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Headline cafe items (hojicha latte ice, strawberry-milk ice) are dairy-based and toppings include ice cream and mochi, so vegan separation is not assured.

positives.text: Plain teas (Yamato tea, Taiwanese tea) are naturally plant-based options for vegan visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual local tea-and-shaved-ice counter where ordering is simple by pointing at the menu, though staff may speak limited English, so knowing a few Japanese words or a translation app helps.

places.name: イタリアン キャラベル (Italian Caravel)
places.gf_detail: The menu is built around fresh wheat pasta (signature thick, chewy "namapasta"), pizza, and dishes like hayashi rice — all wheat-based. No gluten-free options, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation are documented on any source. Not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivore Italian café/bar centered on egg, cream, cheese and meat dishes (carbonara, amatriciana with bacon, beef hayashi rice, mascarpone tiramisu). A margherita pizza exists but uses mozzarella. No vegan or documented vegetarian menu; would need to ask, and options would be very limited.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:30, 17:30-21:00 (Tabelog); official/shop directory lists lunch 11:30-15:00, café 15:00-18:00, dinner 18:30-21:30. Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町・もちいどのセンター街 (Mochiidono, central Nara)
places.notes: A tucked-away Italian spot on the second floor of a building along Nara's historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The café-bar sits within a select shop stocked with vintage clothing and homeware, so the dining room carries a warm, ret

chef_bio.background: A tucked-away Italian spot on the second floor of a building along Nara's historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The café-bar sits within a select shop stocked with vintage clothing and homeware, so the dining room carries a warm, retro feel filled with cute bric-a-brac.
chef_bio.philosophy: The stated idea is 'casual yet authentic Italian you can enjoy anytime' — approachable cooking and good wine in an unpretentious, welcoming room.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-style fresh pasta is the heart of the kitchen — thick, springy noodles in dishes like a rich carbonara with chunky bacon, cream and cracked pepper, and a spicy amatriciana. The menu also runs to creative pizzas such as a margherita, a slow-cooked beef hayashi rice over saffron rice, and a homemade mascarpone-heavy tiramisu.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant shares its floor with a select shop selling vintage clothes and goods, giving the café a lived-in, treasure-box atmosphere rather than a formal trattoria feel.
It is described as a relaxed hideaway that happily welcomes solo diners, including women eating alone.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, casual neighborhood pasta café in a shopping arcade that welcomes solo diners; a little basic Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English service is not advertised.

places.name: 魚万本店 (Uoman Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a fried fish-cake (satsuma-age/kamaboko) shop. Surimi-based fried products typically contain wheat starch/flour binders, are deep-fried in shared oil, and are commonly served with soy sauce (which contains wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer; not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire product line is fish-based (surimi kamaboko and satsuma-age made from ground fish). There are no vegan options; even the "butter potato" item is a fish-paste product. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町・もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Uoman was founded in 1901 (Meiji 34) and has operated for more than 120 years as a kamaboko and satsuma-age (fried fish cake) specialty shop in the heart of the Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. In landlocked Nara, the founding family built a

chef_bio.background: Uoman was founded in 1901 (Meiji 34) and has operated for more than 120 years as a kamaboko and satsuma-age (fried fish cake) specialty shop in the heart of the Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. In landlocked Nara, the founding family built a reputation for bringing the nutrition and honest flavor of fish to the local table.
chef_bio.philosophy: Balancing long tradition with playful innovation in fish-paste products, while honoring the value and taste of fish in a prefecture far from the sea.
chef_bio.specialty: Original surimi (ground-fish) creations: classic kamaboko and satsuma-age plus inventive fried items, most famously the 'butter potato' fish cake. The shop fries items fresh at an open counter so customers can eat them piping hot, and offers walk-and-eat bites like one-mouthful 'NeRi Ball'.

anecdotes.text: The shop's 'butter potato' fish cake became a local sensation after being featured on Japanese TV programs, blending a familiar Western flavor into a traditional satsuma-age.
Staff suggest enjoying the freshly fried cakes simply with grated daikon and soy sauce, or reheating them in a microwave to recapture that just-fried taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Products are deep-fried satsuma-age/kamaboko; surimi fish cakes typically contain wheat starch or flour as a binder, and frying is done in shared oil. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shop recommends eating the fried cakes with grated daikon and soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items are fish-based surimi products; there are no vegan items and everything shares fish-paste preparation and frying.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly neighborhood fish-cake shop with an open-counter where you can grab freshly fried snacks; a smile, a "konnichiwa" and pointing at what you want will carry you through, though menu detail is in Japanese.

places.name: KUMI茶菓 (KUMI Chaka)
places.gf_detail: The core products are wheat-flour cookies, icing cookies, and baked sweets, so this is not a gluten-free-safe stop for a celiac. The teas are naturally gluten-free, but everything edible made on site is wheat-based and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Baked goods are butter cookies and焼き菓子 (baked sweets) that almost certainly contain dairy (butter) and eggs, so they are not vegan. The Chinese and Taiwanese loose-leaf teas themselves are plant-based. No vegan baking is advertised — ask before assuming any item is dairy/egg-free.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 / 定休日 木曜日（不定休あり）
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町・もちいどのセンター街（夢CUBE 2F）
places.cuisine: 中国茶・台湾茶と手作り焼き菓子・アイシングクッキー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] KUMI茶菓 is a small independent tea-and-sweets shop that grew out of the Mochiidono arcade's 夢CUBE incubator program for new shops, graduating to its own space on the second floor of the Yume CUBE building in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai, the covered shopping street that link

chef_bio.background: KUMI茶菓 is a small independent tea-and-sweets shop that grew out of the Mochiidono arcade's 夢CUBE incubator program for new shops, graduating to its own space on the second floor of the Yume CUBE building in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai, the covered shopping street that links toward Naramachi a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop opened in 2021 and later moved to its current second-floor location.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner makes baked goods that are deliberately not too sweet — 甘すぎない手作りの焼き菓子 — so they suit people who do not like very sugary confections, and uses tea leaves generously so the sweets carry the aroma of the tea.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade baked sweets and butter cookies made with Chinese and Taiwanese tea leaves, decorative icing cookies finished with colorful sugar (made to order for special occasions), and a curated selection of Chinese and Taiwanese loose-leaf teas chosen by a certified tea master (茶藝師).

anecdotes.text: The shop's tea sweets have been served at tea gatherings beyond Nara, including the Yoshida-yama grand tea gathering in Kyoto and the association's 'Tenkuu' (sky) tea events, where they were well received.

Teas are described as carefully selected by a certified tea master (茶藝師), with pastries infused with a subtle tea fragrance, and the storefront is styled like a cute miscellaneous-goods or flower shop with products on display.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's production centers on wheat-flour cookies, icing cookies, and baked sweets, so a flour-heavy environment makes cross-contamination likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods are butter cookies and焼き菓子 typically containing dairy and eggs; no dairy-free or egg-free line is advertised, so vegan visitors should ask item by item.

positives.text: The loose-leaf Chinese and Taiwanese teas are naturally plant-based and gluten-free and can be bought separately from the baked goods.

cultural_comfort.note: A small one-person Japanese tea-and-sweets shop where English is unlikely; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the displayed cookies and teas will carry you through.

places.name: ごはんの間 (Gohan no Aida)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The daily set centers on tempura or spring rolls (wheat batter/wrappers), and Japanese set meals here use standard soy sauce and miso, so wheat and gluten are present throughout. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The single daily set typically includes meat, seafood (e.g., shrimp tempura) or fried dishes, and the miso soup and seasonings use fish-based dashi. There is one fixed menu daily with no documented vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (lunch only). Closed Wednesday and Friday per Tabelog; an owner-blog source lists 11:00-15:00 closed Wednesday only, so closing time and Friday closure may vary.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（花芝町・近鉄奈良駅 北へ徒歩約3〜5分）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ごはんの間 opened in February 2011 in Nara's Kitamachi (Hanashiba-cho) area, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. From the street it looks almost like an ordinary house, opening only at midday and offering just one thing: that day's set meal. The room is plain and narrow, runn

chef_bio.background: ごはんの間 opened in February 2011 in Nara's Kitamachi (Hanashiba-cho) area, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. From the street it looks almost like an ordinary house, opening only at midday and offering just one thing: that day's set meal. The room is plain and narrow, running long toward the back with two-person tables and counter-style communal seating that makes it easy to drop in alone.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately limited model -- lunch only, one set menu a day -- that lets the kitchen keep meals affordable and generous without cutting corners on quality or comfort.
chef_bio.specialty: The one daily-changing set meal (honjitsu no teishoku). The main dish rotates among home-style favorites such as vegetable and shrimp tempura or stuffed cabbage rolls, served with rice, miso soup, salad and a side. The rice is sourced from Tahara in Nara Prefecture and is singled out by diners as genuinely delicious, and the tempura oil is described as light and easy on the stomach.

anecdotes.text: Opened February 2011 with the simple promise of 'lunch only, one set meal a day,' served in a narrow room of about 30 seats that welcomes solo diners.
The set's rice comes from Tahara in Nara Prefecture and was described as 'genuinely delicious,' with a generous, vegetable-laden miso soup alongside it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals here use standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso); no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Miso soup and dishes use fish-based dashi typical of Japanese home cooking; the single daily set commonly includes meat or seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A simple local lunch counter with no English menu and a single daily set meal, so a few words of Japanese and a willingness to eat whatever is served that day will make the visit smooth.

places.name: かやく飯とお酒 かやく (Kayaku Meshi to Osake Kayaku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature kayaku rice is cooked in kombu-and-bonito dashi and seasoned with soy sauce (which contains wheat), and the izakaya-style dishes rely on standard Japanese seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no indication of celiac-level cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house dashi uses bonito (katsuo) and the kayaku rice and set meals center on fish/meat dashi and animal proteins (e.g., tender-simmered chicken thigh). No vegan menu is documented; a strict vegan would have very little to eat. At most one could ask, but expect fish dashi throughout.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30; lunch ends when the kayaku rice sells out). Dinner 17:30-22:00 (last entry 20:00, L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays and national holidays.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi area, near Kintetsu Nara Station (Kitahandanaka-ch
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 2012 in a renovated old machiya (traditional townhouse) in central Nara, with thick old beams that carry a sense of history, the place is built around a single beloved idea: rice done properly. The dining room is intimate, seating only around twenty, and the rhythm of t

chef_bio.background: Opened in 2012 in a renovated old machiya (traditional townhouse) in central Nara, with thick old beams that carry a sense of history, the place is built around a single beloved idea: rice done properly. The dining room is intimate, seating only around twenty, and the rhythm of the day is split in two — a relaxed, affordable kayaku-rice lunch and a quieter evening of sake and small dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: An obsession with rice quality — milling fresh for each batch and cooking it gently in dashi so the grains, not the seasoning, are the star.
chef_bio.specialty: Kayaku rice (mixed rice) is the centerpiece. The kitchen sources rice from a single farmer in Omihachiman, keeps it in a refrigerated case and mills only what is needed for each cooking so the rice is always fresh, then cooks it in a clay pot with kombu-and-bonito dashi. The result is described as elegant and subtle, never heavy, and is served in bent-wood boxes; lunch sets pair it with a protein such as tender-simmered chicken thigh and side dishes, while a popular dish is the kayaku-rice grilled rice ball served as ochazuke.

anecdotes.text: The rice comes from one specific Omihachiman farmer's 'enko' rice, kept chilled and milled only in the amount needed for each pot so it is always at fresh-milled quality.
Set in a renovated old townhouse near the prefectural government and NHK Nara, the room keeps its original thick beams and seats only about twenty for an intimate meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kayaku rice is cooked with kombu-and-bonito dashi and seasoned in the Japanese style; soy sauce (wheat-containing) and shared cooking are standard, so a gluten-free meal cannot be assured.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese-style seasoning typical of kayaku rice and izakaya dishes involves soy sauce, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free soy is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made with bonito (katsuo) as well as kombu, so even vegetable-forward dishes are prepared with fish stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional, locally-loved townhouse eatery with no indication of English support, so a few words of Japanese (and pointing at the kayaku rice set) will go a long way; come at lunch for the easiest, most affordable experience.

places.name: やまと庵 本店 (Yamato-an Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on Japanese washoku where soy sauce (typically wheat-based), miso, tempura, grilled Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles), and fried dishes are common. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation is documented, so it is not safe for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat- and fish-forward kyodo-ryori restaurant built around Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, Yamato pork, Nara game (venison/boar), and seafood; most dishes use fish dashi. A few items lean plant-based (e.g. local tofu, Yamato-vegetable dishes, daikon salad, kuzu mochi), but there is no dedicated vegan menu and dashi/animal stock is pervasive. Vegans would need to ask carefully and options would be very limited.
places.neighborhood: 三条町（JR奈良駅 徒歩3分／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩8分）
places.notes: Yamato-an Honten sits along the main Sanjo-dori approach a short walk from JR Nara Station, in a warm modern-Japanese space that celebrates Nara's regional food culture. The kitchen builds its menu around prized local ingredients: Yamato beef, Yamato chicken (Yamato niku-dori), Y

chef_bio.background: Yamato-an Honten sits along the main Sanjo-dori approach a short walk from JR Nara Station, in a warm modern-Japanese space that celebrates Nara's regional food culture. The kitchen builds its menu around prized local ingredients: Yamato beef, Yamato chicken (Yamato niku-dori), Yamato pork, and Yamato heirloom vegetables grown by nearby farmers. Since 2017 it has also served Nara game, with venison and wild boar delivered by hunters from the Yoshino mountains, prepared to keep the gaminess gentle.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames its cooking as respect for local producers, highlighting fresh vegetables grown with care by farmers and honoring Nara's historic role as the place tofu first entered Japan, using tofu made from 100% Hokkaido soybeans and natural nigari.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature offerings include Yamato beef sukiyaki, Yoshino handmade ham, boar hot pot, grilled Miwa somen, and yaki chagayu (grilled tea-rice porridge), paired with a deep list of Nara local sake. Multi-course meals with free-flow drinks include a course featuring Yamato beef and Yamato pork and a lighter small-plate course.

anecdotes.text: Since 2017 the restaurant has sourced venison and wild boar directly from hunters in Yoshino, serving Nara gibier prepared so the meat has almost none of the gaminess often associated with game.
The menu reaches back to Nara-period history, presenting tofu as an ingredient made with Hokkaido soybeans and natural nigari.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is washoku-based with wheat-containing soy sauce, miso, tempura/fried items, and grilled Miwa somen (wheat noodles); no gluten-free accommodation is documented, so cross-contamination risk is high for celiacs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese soy sauce used throughout washoku menus is typically brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat-, game-, and fish-forward kitchen where fish dashi is used broadly; plant-based items would share preparation surfaces with animal products.

positives.text: Some inherently lighter plant-based items appear on the menu, including local tofu, Yamato-vegetable dishes, a crisp daikon salad, and kuzu mochi.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local izakaya-style restaurant where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: 喫茶 黒猫 (Kissa Kuroneko)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is toast, hot sandwiches and morning sets, all built on wheat bread; no gluten-free options are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan options. Signature dishes feature ham, egg, dairy (coffee, cheesecake) and beef-shank curry; this is a conventional omnivore café with no stated plant-based menu.
places.hours_raw: 7:00–17:00（モーニングは開店から）。定休日：日曜・祝日。（食べログの表記では8:00開店となっている箇所もあり、開店時間は要確認）
places.neighborhood: 大宮町（新大宮駅近く・中心部西側）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent café in the Omiya-dori area near Shin-Omiya, opened in 2015 and run by an owner often described as a motorcycle- and cat-loving 'master.' The cat theme runs through the shop's identity, and the room keeps a gently retro mood with counter seats and a sofa corne

chef_bio.background: A small independent café in the Omiya-dori area near Shin-Omiya, opened in 2015 and run by an owner often described as a motorcycle- and cat-loving 'master.' The cat theme runs through the shop's identity, and the room keeps a gently retro mood with counter seats and a sofa corner.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is set up for unhurried time — free Wi-Fi, charging outlets and a shelf of magazines invite guests to linger.
chef_bio.specialty: Hot sandwich morning sets (three filling choices, the ham-and-egg version being the signature), served with mild blend coffee, chips and a mini salad; the kitchen also makes a beef-shank curry and house cheesecake.

anecdotes.text: The owner is affectionately known as a motorcycle-loving, cat-loving master, and the cat motif gives the café its name and character.
Mornings are the draw: toast and hot-sandwich sets are available from opening, paired with freshly brewed coffee.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood café with a laid-back retro feel; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese (and pointing at the morning-set menu) will go a long way.

places.name: なら酒蔵なべ (Nara Sakagura Nabe)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu centers on wheat-based items (kasu-udon noodles, fried tofu/abura-age) and soy sauce, and sake-lees broths plus typical Japanese seasonings carry gluten. No gluten-free program or dedicated handling is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-and-seafood izakaya. The signature sake-lees hot pot is built on chicken-bone dashi and includes Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, shrimp, and oysters, so it is not vegan. Vegetable components exist (watercress, napa cabbage, mushrooms, daikon) but no confirmed fully vegan dish; a strict vegan would need to ask, and broths/dashi make accommodation difficult.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (lunch until 14:00); closed Tuesdays. (One source lists closing at 23:00.)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Mochiidono, near Sarusawa Pond, about a 10-minut
places.notes: Nara Sakagura Nabe is a townhouse izakaya opened by the Sakaguranabe group in a renovated old machiya (wooden townhouse) in Naramachi, near Sarusawa Pond and across from Sarutahiko Shrine. The building keeps its traditional character with tatami zashiki rooms that turn into kotat

chef_bio.background: Nara Sakagura Nabe is a townhouse izakaya opened by the Sakaguranabe group in a renovated old machiya (wooden townhouse) in Naramachi, near Sarusawa Pond and across from Sarutahiko Shrine. The building keeps its traditional character with tatami zashiki rooms that turn into kotatsu seating in winter, table seating, and an upper floor, plus a basement cellar and attic used to store local sake, wine, shochu, and whisky. Each room has its own refrigerator from which guests serve their own drinks and settle up afterward.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on Nara local production for local consumption (chisan-chisho), pairing regional Yamato ingredients with the prefecture's sake and sake lees.
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship dish is the kasu-kuroto-nabe (sake-lees connoisseur's hot pot): a broth taken from Yamato chicken bones and gizzards enriched with sake lees from Nara's Yucho Shuzo 'Kaze no Mori' brewery, served with Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, shrimp, abura-age, watercress, napa cabbage, carrot, daikon, and shimeji. Oyster sets and a watercress-and-Yamato-pork shabu-shabu are also signatures, alongside an extensive selection of more than 40 Nara local sakes.

anecdotes.text: The renovated townhouse stores Yamato local sake in a hidden basement cellar and other spirits in the attic, and each dining room has its own fridge so guests pour their own drinks and pay at the end.
The connoisseur's sake-lees hot pot uses a dashi drawn from Yamato chicken bones and gizzards combined with sake lees from Yucho Shuzo's 'Kaze no Mori'.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu relies on wheat-based kasu-udon noodles, soy sauce, and sake-lees broths; no gluten-free handling documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese izakaya seasonings including soy sauce are used; no wheat-free soy sauce noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature hot pot broths are made from chicken bones and contain pork, chicken, shrimp, and oysters; shared nabe service makes vegan separation impractical.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers identify dishes and ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu and free Wi-Fi are available and the relaxed townhouse setting with private rooms makes it approachable for a foreign student, though staff English may be limited.

places.name: ならまちnakamuraya（ナカムラヤ） (Naramachi Nakamuraya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. As an Italian restaurant centered on pasta and wine, the menu is wheat-heavy and there is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation. Not suitable for celiac diners; a sensitive diner would need to ask directly in advance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Italian cooking here will typically involve meat, fish, dairy, and cheese, and the focus is on wine pairing. A vegan or vegetarian diner would need to inquire ahead about whether plant-based plates can be arranged; nothing specific is published.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:30-23:30 (L.O. 21:00); wine bar served through the day. (Official site. Tabelog lists slightly different times: lunch 11:30-15:30 L.O.13:30, dinner 17:30-22:00 L.O.20:00 — confirm on arrival.)
places.neighborhood: もちいどの（餅飯殿）商店街エリア・ならまち、近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約6分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Naramachi nakamuraya is a small, independent Italian restaurant tucked into a lane off the Mochiidono shopping street in Nara's old-town Naramachi district, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner-chef trained in Italy and is a certified sommelier, and he returns to It

chef_bio.background: Naramachi nakamuraya is a small, independent Italian restaurant tucked into a lane off the Mochiidono shopping street in Nara's old-town Naramachi district, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner-chef trained in Italy and is a certified sommelier, and he returns to Italy each year to research current regional cooking and bring those flavors back to Naramachi.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing the real flavors of Italy to Naramachi (本場イタリアの味をならまちに), built on seasonal, carefully chosen ingredients and genuine Italian technique paired thoughtfully with wine.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic regional Italian cooking paired with an extensive Italian wine list — around 200 Italian wines are kept on hand, each poured into glassware chosen to suit the variety. The venue works as both a relaxed wine bar and cafe and a more formal dining room, with a wine shop upstairs.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef travels to Italy every year to study the latest local cooking and flavors, then brings that back to the restaurant.
Roughly 200 Italian wines are stocked, with a wine shop on the second floor and glassware matched to each wine.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run Italian spot in the Naramachi lanes; staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will make ordering and discussing wine smoother.

places.name: キナリ ピッツァ (Kinari Pizza)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dough is made from wheat flour (Kumamoto-grown "Minami no Megumi"), so all pizzas contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or substitutions are documented, and the single shared wood-fired oven makes cross-contamination unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-forward — many pizzas (e.g. Pizza Ortolana Bianca with ~10 kinds of Nara vegetables) showcase produce from the owner's own farm. However, nearly all pizzas use dairy (fresh mozzarella, Grana Padano) and the menu also features Yamato pork, so it is not a vegan kitchen. A fully vegan order would require asking the staff to omit cheese, and no certified vegan option is documented. Vegetarians with dairy will find good choices.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O.); closes when the day's dough runs out. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday. Weather-dependent (open-air).
places.neighborhood: ならまち（西寺林町）、近鉄奈良駅徒歩約10分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kinari Pizza is the solo project of Miyaka Hisashi, who trained as a baker in France and Italy before opening this open-air pizzeria in the heart of Naramachi. The shop is built around a distinctive white kitchen truck fitted with a wood-fired oven, with terrace seating and cover

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi (Nishi-Terabayashi-cho), central Nara, a few minutes from Sarusawa Pond.
chef_bio.background: Kinari Pizza is the solo project of Miyaka Hisashi, who trained as a baker in France and Italy before opening this open-air pizzeria in the heart of Naramachi. The shop is built around a distinctive white kitchen truck fitted with a wood-fired oven, with terrace seating and covered tables tucked behind it.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner deliberately built the stand with no door, saying he wants people to drop by casually (「ラフに遊びに来てほしいから扉は作らない」). The dough uses less salt than authentic Neapolitan style to be gentler on the body, and the cooking puts local Yamato vegetables at the center rather than treating them as garnish.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizzas built on a low-salt, long-fermented, chewy dough made from Kumamoto wheat, topped with seasonal vegetables the owner grows on his own farm. Signatures include the Pizza Ortolana Bianca piled with around ten kinds of Nara vegetables, the Funghi with Nara-grown mushrooms, the classic Margherita, and a seasonal Strawberry Quattro Formaggi with Nara's Kotoka strawberries and honey.

anecdotes.text: The owner intentionally left the pizzeria doorless so guests feel free to wander in casually, reflecting the laid-back open-air concept.
Most of the vegetables on the pizzas are grown by the owner himself, with seasonal produce from Nara City's Tahara district featured as the main attraction.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All pizzas are baked from wheat dough in a single shared wood-fired oven, so flour cross-contamination is unavoidable; no gluten-free options are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most pizzas include fresh mozzarella and Grana Padano cheese, and the kitchen also handles Yamato pork, so vegetable pizzas share equipment with dairy and meat.

positives.text: Heavily vegetable-focused menu using produce from the owner's own farm; the Pizza Ortolana Bianca features around ten kinds of Nara vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, doorless open-air pizza stand where a friendly counter order works fine; basic Japanese greetings help but the casual, walk-up setting is easy for a foreign student.

places.name: キャラメル (Karamell)
places.gf_detail: This is a patisserie specializing in European-style cakes (Sachertorte, Schönbrunn torte, cheesecakes, tarts), which are wheat-flour based by nature. No gluten-free items or celiac accommodations are documented. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A dessert café whose menu centers on butter, cream, eggs, and dairy-rich pastries (cheesecakes, cream tortes, caramel-cream cakes). No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; closed Wednesdays; occasional irregular closures possible
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Minamichi-cho), down an alley off the Mochiidono
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Karamell opened in July 2015, run by patissier Shinsuke Muraoka and his wife Mitsuyo, who is also a pastry chef. Muraoka trained in France and built his career in hotel pastry kitchens, serving as head of the pastry department at the Rihga Royal Hotel Osaka and the ANA Crowne Pla

chef_bio.background: Karamell opened in July 2015, run by patissier Shinsuke Muraoka and his wife Mitsuyo, who is also a pastry chef. Muraoka trained in France and built his career in hotel pastry kitchens, serving as head of the pastry department at the Rihga Royal Hotel Osaka and the ANA Crowne Plaza Kobe, and teaching pastry at a culinary college in Hyogo before opening this small café in Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: The couple serves desserts only as plated compositions for eating in, rather than takeout, so guests experience the sweets at their peak; the name uses the German 'Karamell' spelling, nodding to the Viennese confectionery tradition rather than the French.
chef_bio.specialty: Viennese-style desserts served as plated dessert plates for dine-in only, so each sweet is enjoyed in its best condition. Signature items include the Schönbrunn torte (five layers of chocolate biscuit, caramel cream and fresh cream with caramel sauce, evoking the colors of Vienna's Schönbrunn Palace), Sachertorte, a double cheesecake with lemon sauce, and a caramel-apple-rum-raisin tarte paysanne.

anecdotes.text: The café sits down a quiet alley behind the Mochiidono shopping street in a renovated traditional wooden townhouse, with table seats, a street-facing counter, and tatami seating in the back where guests can watch the open kitchen.
One playful house creation is a chocolate-mousse dessert shaped in homage to Nara's Great Buddha.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A flour-based patisserie where wheat is used throughout the cake and pastry production; no separation or gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on dairy, eggs, butter and cream; no plant-based preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, hidden machiya café with no English presence documented, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the dessert plate menu will make ordering smooth.

places.name: なら和み館 (Nara Nagomi-kan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a souvenir-and-cafe complex serving Nara wagashi such as mikasa (dorayaki) and assorted sweets, which are wheat-based, plus a tour-group restaurant menu likely relying on wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi. Celiac travelers should treat it as not gluten-free unless staff can confirm specific items; ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The cafe and group restaurant serve general Japanese fare and sweets; most Japanese dishes here likely use fish dashi, and wagashi may contain egg. There is no published vegan offering, so vegans should ask staff about specific items rather than assume availability.
places.hours_raw: Souvenir space (みやげものスペース) approx. 9:30-16:30 per official site (Tabelog lists 10:00-19:00). Group restaurant 10:00-21:00 by full reservation only (完全ご予約制). Sake bar 地酒BAR"なら" 11:00-16:00, Tuesdays 11:00-15:00. Open year-round (no fixed closed day listed).
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち・奈良公園エリア）
places.cuisine: 和菓子・甘味・喫茶／お土産物店併設の団体レストラン
places.notes: Nara Nagomi-kan sits in the Takabatake quarter on the edge of historic Naramachi, near Nara Park. Built to blend with the old town, its exterior wears black plaster walls echoing the weathered ink-gray of traditional local houses, and the entrance is guarded by a striking dragon

chef_bio.origin: Operated by World Heritage Co., Ltd. (ワールド・ヘリテイジ), based in Nara.
chef_bio.background: Nara Nagomi-kan sits in the Takabatake quarter on the edge of historic Naramachi, near Nara Park. Built to blend with the old town, its exterior wears black plaster walls echoing the weathered ink-gray of traditional local houses, and the entrance is guarded by a striking dragon sculpture carved from three Japanese pine logs as a symbol of prosperity and welcome. Inside, it is one of the prefecture's largest souvenir halls, stocking roughly 1,500 to 3,000 items of Nara specialties and Japanese goods, alongside a large dining room for tour groups, a casual cafe, and a local-sake bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: To gather Nara's specialties and food culture under one roof and welcome travelers in a setting designed to harmonize with the historic townscape.
chef_bio.specialty: Nara confectionery, especially mikasa (the local name for dorayaki) and other regional wagashi and sweets, plus an in-house sake bar pouring Nara local sake.

anecdotes.text: The entrance is marked by a dragon carved from three Japanese pine logs, intended to symbolize prosperity and welcome visitors.
The building's black plaster walls were designed to mimic the aged ink-gray color of traditional Naramachi architecture so the modern facility blends into the historic town.

cultural_comfort.note: A large, tourist-oriented souvenir-and-cafe complex where dropping in to browse and grab sweets or a drink is easy and low-pressure for a foreign student, though it is geared to tour groups rather than intimate dining.

places.name: 日本料理かがりや（篝屋） (Nihon Ryori Kagariya)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant whose menu leans heavily on wheat-containing items: Miwa somen noodles (wheat), soy sauce, and fish/kombu dashi run through most dishes. Signature courses feature Yamato beef hitsumabushi and grilled meats served with sauces. No dedicated GF menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented gluten-free or celiac handling. A celiac traveler should treat this as unsafe unless they can arrange detailed accommodation in advance in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for strict vegans. Although there are seasonal vegetable sets (旬菜御膳 / 旬彩御膳) and Yamato-vegetable dishes, this is a meat- and fish-forward kaiseki kitchen where dashi (bonito/fish stock) is standard in simmered and seasoned dishes, and the signature courses center on Yamato beef, chicken, pork and duck. Plant-based components exist but are not prepared as a vegan menu, and no vegan-specific handling is documented. A vegan would need to ask in advance and may find options very limited.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. around 20:00-20:30). Closed Tuesdays (and per some listings Wednesdays); occasional irregular closures; year-end/New Year may be reserved for group bookings.
places.neighborhood: 奈良市油阪町（JR奈良駅・近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
places.notes: Kagariya is a Japanese kaiseki restaurant on the second floor of a hotel near Nara's main railway stations, devoted to local-ingredient cooking from Nara Prefecture. The name 篝屋 evokes the keepers who tended the bonfires that once lit castles, and the dining room carries that ima

chef_bio.background: Kagariya is a Japanese kaiseki restaurant on the second floor of a hotel near Nara's main railway stations, devoted to local-ingredient cooking from Nara Prefecture. The name 篝屋 evokes the keepers who tended the bonfires that once lit castles, and the dining room carries that imagery through warm wood and soft lighting meant to feel like a calm, healing retreat that nods to Nara's heritage of fine timber.
chef_bio.philosophy: To draw out the natural flavor of Nara's regional ingredients and present dishes refined in both appearance and taste, offering warm hospitality in a tranquil space.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki and set meals built around Nara produce: Yamato beef (大和牛), Yamato chicken (大和肉鶏), Yamato pork, Yamato duck, Yoshino kudzu (吉野葛) and Nara-grown vegetables. Signature dishes include a Yamato beef hitsumabushi set served to be enjoyed three ways, tasting courses pairing the local meats, and seasonal vegetable sets. Some courses also feature Miwa somen and traditional 'ancient' soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: The head chef trained in Kyoto and Nara before leading the kitchen, focusing on a broad command of Japanese cuisine refined in both look and flavor.
The restaurant name 篝屋 refers to those who guarded the beacon fires illuminating castles, and the interior uses wood and lighting to create a warm, healing space reflecting Nara's timber heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes wheat-based Miwa somen noodles and soy sauce, and traditional kaiseki preparation relies on shared dashi and sauces, so cross-contact with gluten is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Courses feature traditional soy sauce ('ancient soy sauce'), which is standard wheat-fermented Japanese shoyu unless otherwise specified.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a meat- and fish-forward kaiseki kitchen with signature courses built on Yamato beef, chicken, pork and duck, and dashi (fish stock) is standard in seasoned and simmered dishes, so vegan cross-contact is likely.

positives.text: Seasonal vegetable-focused sets featuring Nara-grown produce are available, giving non-strict diners some plant-forward options.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a polished hotel kaiseki restaurant where service and menus are primarily in Japanese, so a foreign student with dietary needs would do best bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or a written allergy card and reserving ahead.

places.name: 十割り蕎麦 つるや (Towari Soba Tsuruya)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are 中太の十割蕎麦 (100% buckwheat, no wheat blended into the noodle itself), but the broth/tsuyu uses soy sauce and dashi, dishes are prepared in a shared wheat-using kitchen, and there is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated equipment. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on chicken and beef soba (cold chicken soba, beef atsumori, ground-chicken soboro rice) and the broth is a dashi/poultry-based stock, so the food is not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: 火〜土 11:00-14:30 / 18:00-23:00、日 11:00-14:30、月曜定休（現金のみ）。Local press describes the soba lunch service as roughly 11:00-14:00/14:30; evening is the お茶屋BAR.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（元林院・今御門町）, central Nara
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuruya is run by Kikuno, an active geiko, and is described in local press as the only surviving ochaya (geisha tea house) in Nara, set in a historic building with a vermilion-lacquered counter and period furnishings. In mid-August 2025 Kikuno launched a daytime 100% buckwheat so

chef_bio.background: Tsuruya is run by Kikuno, an active geiko, and is described in local press as the only surviving ochaya (geisha tea house) in Nara, set in a historic building with a vermilion-lacquered counter and period furnishings. In mid-August 2025 Kikuno launched a daytime 100% buckwheat soba service to open the refined tea-house world to ordinary visitors, softening the traditional 'no first-time customers' image of the flower district.
chef_bio.philosophy: To lower the threshold of the tea-house world and let casual daytime guests experience the atmosphere of a working ochaya through approachable, affordable soba.
chef_bio.specialty: Medium-thick 100% buckwheat (juwari) soba. Signatures include a cold chicken soba (limited to about ten servings a day) topped with chicken char-siu, soft-boiled egg, scallion and nori and finished with black pepper at the table, and a warm-broth mori soba served Sakai-style with raw egg — said to be the only place in Nara serving it that way. There is also a daily rice dish with spiced chicken and egg yolk, and soba offered as 'soba-mae' nibbles with drinks.

anecdotes.text: Tsuruya is described as Nara's only remaining ochaya still run by an active geiko, who began serving 100% buckwheat soba by day in mid-August 2025 to make the tea-house experience accessible to everyone.
The warm-broth mori soba is served Sakai-style with a raw egg, a preparation the shop bills as unique to this location in Nara, while the cold chicken soba is capped at about ten servings a day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or cross-contamination protocol is documented; although the soba is 100% buckwheat, it is prepared in a shared kitchen and served with a soy-sauce/dashi broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba broth/tsuyu is a standard dashi-and-soy-sauce style stock (warm broth, dipping tsuyu), which normally contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen works with chicken and beef and a poultry/dashi-based broth, so vegan cross-contact is likely; no vegan accommodation is documented.

positives.text: The noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat (juwari) with no wheat flour blended in, which is unusual and relevant for those reducing wheat in the noodle (though the broth is not wheat-free).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 7-seat counter inside a working ochaya (geisha tea house) where the daytime soba service is welcoming and casual, but little English is likely, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: ごはん日和り (Gohan Biyori)
places.gf_detail: Not suitable for celiacs. Oden simmers many items in a soy-sauce-seasoned broth (wheat-containing soy sauce), and obanzai home-style dishes commonly use soy sauce, mirin and miso. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. Cross-contamination from the shared oden pot is likely.
places.vegan_detail: This is an oden, obanzai and sake spot where most dishes are built on fish dashi (katsuo/kombu), so the menu is not vegan-oriented. Some plant-based obanzai (seasonal vegetable side dishes) may appear day to day, but even those typically use fish-based dashi, so a strict vegan should ask carefully and expect very few safe options.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Lunch by reservation only. Closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 奈良市 東向北町（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約2分・東向北商店街）
places.cuisine: おでん・おばんざい・地酒
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gohan Biyori opened on September 15, 2023 on the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, about a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the former space of a French-regional izakaya that had closed the previous November.

chef_bio.origin: Nara (central Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: Gohan Biyori opened on September 15, 2023 on the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, about a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the former space of a French-regional izakaya that had closed the previous November.
chef_bio.philosophy: A female owner-cook prepares everything herself from scratch, aiming for simple, gentle, homey food and heartfelt hospitality — cooking with local ingredients so the dishes 'resonate with the heart and body.'
chef_bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a gentle, refined dashi (from around 200 yen per item) and daily-changing obanzai made with seasonal vegetables, paired with a generous lineup of Nara-area and seasonal Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over the premises of 'Kitamachi Su Magasin,' a French-regional-cuisine izakaya that closed in November 2022, reopening as an oden-and-obanzai house in autumn 2023.
The owner cooks every dish herself, leaning into a homestyle style that draws many solo diners (including women) as well as families, who can settle into the zashiki tatami area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden items share a single simmering pot of soy-sauce-seasoned dashi, so wheat-containing broth contacts everything cooked in it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden and home-style obanzai are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden and obanzai are built on fish dashi (bonito/kombu), so even vegetable dishes are unlikely to be free of animal-derived stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm counter-style local izakaya run by the owner herself; some basic Japanese (and pointing at the menu displayed outside) will go a long way, as English support is unlikely.

places.name: 四季彩菜 一条 東向通店 (Shikisaisai Ichijo Higashimukidori-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a Japanese izakaya built around sashimi, tempura/fritters, simmered dishes and sake — soy sauce (wheat-based), ponzu, batter-fried items and fish dashi run through the menu. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation is documented, and the kitchen is high-volume. A celiac traveler cannot eat safely here.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu centers on fresh fish and meat; vegetable items (salads, lotus-root dumplings, potato-mochi cheese fritters, seasonal vegetable dishes) exist but are typically seasoned with fish dashi, bonito, or contain dairy/egg. No vegan menu or labeling is documented. A vegan would be very limited and would need to ask, with no guarantee of dashi-free preparation.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–14:30 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:00–23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Open year-round (年中無休). Note: some listings show lunch 11:00–14:00 and dinner 17:00–23:00.
places.neighborhood: 東向南町（東向商店街）, central Nara, ~3 min walk / 186m from Kintetsu
places.notes: Head chef and manager Kira Shinichiro was born in Nara Prefecture in 1973 and first stepped into the 一条 (Ichijo) kitchen as a student working a part-time job at the group's original izakaya. That job turned into a calling: over roughly 32 years he worked his way through the group

chef_bio.background: Head chef and manager Kira Shinichiro was born in Nara Prefecture in 1973 and first stepped into the 一条 (Ichijo) kitchen as a student working a part-time job at the group's original izakaya. That job turned into a calling: over roughly 32 years he worked his way through the group's sister restaurants before taking the helm at the Higashimuki shopping-street location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kira's guiding idea is that a restaurant should move its guests through the sheer value it offers — delicious, honest Japanese food at prices ordinary people can enjoy, served in a polished space with genuine hospitality. The shop name 四季彩菜 ('the colorful vegetables of the four seasons') reflects a focus on seasonal produce alongside the seafood.
chef_bio.specialty: Reasonably priced fresh-fish sashimi sourced from the market daily, the seasonal 'Ichijo Bento Zen' (a lacquered multi-tier bento of sashimi, vinegared and simmered dishes served at lunch prices), and a curated lineup of Nara local sake such as Mimuro-sugi and Gozenshu.

anecdotes.text: The Ichijo name has long been known locally as a 'cheap but delicious' place, and the brand grew from its original Shin-Omiya store into several Nara locations.
A local blogger describes it as a grown-up's easygoing izakaya — a refuge from the tourist crowds with sashimi platters, scallop tsubo-yaki, cod-milt ponzu and a three-glass Nara sake tasting set.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on sashimi, fried/battered items (cartilage karaage, potato-mochi cheese fritters), soy-sauce and ponzu dressings — high gluten exposure with no GF protocol documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard izakaya soy sauce and ponzu (wheat-containing) are used throughout for sashimi and dishes; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes and salads are typical izakaya fare likely seasoned with fish dashi or bonito; no vegan preparation is documented and seafood dominates the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local izakaya where clientele is mostly Japanese and the menu is mainly in Japanese, so a translation app helps, though an English menu is reportedly available and card payment is accepted.

places.name: 和食cafe&bar Comfy (Washoku cafe & bar Comfy)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The kitchen builds everything around freshly drawn Japanese dashi and locally made soy sauce and miso, and the menu features fish, chicken, and seasonal vegetables on shared plates — all dishes that typically involve wheat-containing soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, no allergen handling, and no gluten-free menu were documented. A gluten-sensitive diner would need to ask directly, but cross-contact is likely given the dashi- and soy-sauce-heavy style.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly as a destination. While the kitchen sources many vegetables directly from farmers and serves around ten vegetable side dishes with the main set, the cooking is centered on fish dashi (stock drawn fresh each morning) and the set meals include fish and chicken, plus dairy (Nara-sourced milk) and eggs in desserts. Vegetable side dishes very likely use dashi and soy sauce, so even the vegetable-forward plates are not vegan. A vegan would need to ask whether anything can be prepared without fish stock; nothing specific is documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:00); Dinner 18:00-01:00 (next day). Café time roughly 15:00-18:00 between services. Closed Thursdays plus the 2nd and 4th Wednesdays (2-3 Wednesday closures per month). Hours and closures reported differently across sources (one source lists Mon-Tue closed, 18:00-22:00); the operator-managed listing gives Thursday + irregular Wednesday closures and late-night bar hours.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・元興寺周辺 / 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 和食カフェ＆ダイニングバー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Comfy is a small Japanese cafe and bar tucked into a former machiya (traditional townhouse) in the Nishiterabayashi-cho lane of Naramachi, a short walk south from the center of Nara past Sarusawa Pond. It opened in late December 2022. The kitchen is led by a woman who trained at

chef_bio.background: Comfy is a small Japanese cafe and bar tucked into a former machiya (traditional townhouse) in the Nishiterabayashi-cho lane of Naramachi, a short walk south from the center of Nara past Sarusawa Pond. It opened in late December 2022. The kitchen is led by a woman who trained at culinary school and then spent about ten years cooking in traditional Japanese ryotei before striking out on her own; her partner runs the front of house, drawn to hospitality and, in his words, the joy of seeing people's happy faces.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef works to a simple credo: cooking is the act of offering time and care. In practice that means drawing dashi from scratch every morning and putting in the invisible, unhurried effort needed to coax out the natural flavor of each ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: Lunch centers on beautifully composed set plates: the most generous 'flower plate' brings fish and chicken alongside roughly ten small vegetable dishes, with lighter fish-or-chicken sets also offered. Vegetables come straight from farmers in Gojo City, and the pantry leans on Nara-area makers — Inoue Honten soy sauce and miso, Uemura Pasture milk, and eggs from a Kashihara farm. House-made seasonal desserts include pudding, and in summer the shop serves carefully shaved Nara kakigori using ice from Hinode Ice. In the evening the room shifts into a quiet bar serving Japanese small plates such as braised yellowtail with daikon, sashimi, and sweet-potato chips.

anecdotes.text: Before opening Comfy, the chef trained at culinary school and then cooked for about ten years in traditional Japanese ryotei, and she describes her guiding belief as 'cooking is providing time and care,' beginning each dish by drawing fresh dashi rather than taking shortcuts.

The shop occupies a long, narrow machiya-style space with just five counter seats and ten table seats, opened in late December 2022 in a Naramachi back lane reached by walking south from Kintetsu Nara Station past Sarusawa Pond.
In summer Comfy offers a limited shaved-ice menu made with ice from Hinode Ice Manufacturing, prepared by trained staff during lunch and cafe hours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is built on freshly drawn Japanese dashi and locally made soy sauce and miso, so wheat-derived soy sauce and shared preparation make cross-contact likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses Inoue Honten soy sauce and miso (standard Japanese soy sauce, which normally contains wheat); no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi is drawn fresh each morning (fish-based) and set meals include fish, chicken, dairy and eggs, so vegetable side dishes are very likely cooked with fish stock and soy sauce; no vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Vegetables are sourced directly from farmers in Gojo City and a generous set includes around ten vegetable side dishes, so vegetable-forward eating is possible even if not certified vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and free Wi-Fi is offered, so a foreign student can order comfortably with a friendly greeting, though staff conversation will mostly be in Japanese.

places.name: 和食とお酒 やまと庵 近鉄奈良駅前店 (Washoku to Osake Yamatoan Kintetsu Nara Ekim)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a soy-sauce and dashi-heavy washoku izakaya; signature items include Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles), tempura, and persimmon-leaf sushi seasoned with soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination controls are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu centers on Yamato beef, Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, Yoshino venison and wild boar, and seafood. While vegetable dishes using Yamato vegetables and tofu (e.g., Yamato-cha tofu) exist, nearly everything is prepared with fish-based dashi and soy sauce, so plant-only options are minimal and not specifically labeled vegan. A vegan should ask, but expect very limited true options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Tabelog lists dinner until 23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅前 (Kintetsu Nara Station area, Higashimuki-nakamachi)
places.notes: Yamatoan is a Nara izakaya devoted to 'おいしい奈良' (delicious Nara) — celebrating Yamato regional cuisine made from local Nara prefecture ingredients. The Kintetsu Nara Ekimae branch sits one minute from Kintetsu Nara Station in the Higashimuki shopping street, alongside its honten (

chef_bio.background: Yamatoan is a Nara izakaya devoted to 'おいしい奈良' (delicious Nara) — celebrating Yamato regional cuisine made from local Nara prefecture ingredients. The Kintetsu Nara Ekimae branch sits one minute from Kintetsu Nara Station in the Higashimuki shopping street, alongside its honten (main store).
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames Nara as the birthplace of refined sake (清酒発祥の地) and pours an all-Nara sake list — labels like Hoshuku, Hyakurakumon, Ume-no-Yado, Suzakumon, Kinko, Mimuro-sugi, Harushika and more — served in half-, one-, and one-and-a-half-gō pours so guests can taste widely across local breweries.
chef_bio.specialty: Local proteins are the stars: Yamato beef rare steak, Yamato pork, Yamato chicken (Yamato niku-dori), and Nara game such as Yoshino venison and wild boar. Regional Nara classics round out the table: house-made persimmon-leaf sushi (kaki-no-ha-zushi), Miwa sōmen, and Yamato-cha (tea) tofu.

anecdotes.text: The sake program is exclusively Nara prefecture sake, with pours from 0.5 to 1.5 gō priced roughly 330 to 1,485 yen, letting diners sample many local breweries in one sitting.
Nara game (jibie) such as Yoshino wild venison and boar is described as a popular draw alongside the Yamato-branded meats.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu features wheat-based Miwa sōmen and tempura plus soy-sauce-seasoned dishes; no gluten-free handling or cross-contamination controls are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used throughout, including with persimmon-leaf sushi and grilled/braised dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on meat, game, and seafood with fish-based dashi; shared preparation makes incidental animal-product contact likely for any vegetable dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, tourist-accustomed izakaya right by Kintetsu Nara Station where a little Japanese helps but staff are used to visitors; menus showcase clearly labeled regional dishes and sake.

places.name: 小川又兵衛商店 ならまち店 (Ogawa Matabee Shoten Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: A sake and beer standing bar: beer contains barley/wheat gluten and there is no documented gluten-free program. Sake is rice-based and generally gluten-free, but no celiac-safe handling is described, so this is not a safe choice for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: This is a drinks-focused standing bar (local sake, craft beer, wine) rather than a kitchen; only light snacks may be available and none are documented as vegan. Sake and most beer are plant-based, but any food offerings and serving practices would need to be confirmed in person.
places.hours_raw: 平日 15:00-19:00 / 土日祝 10:00-19:00。営業は少し不定期。年始3日間のみ休み。
places.neighborhood: ならまち / 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 奈良の地酒を集めた酒場・角打ち
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1868 (Meiji 1), Ogawa Matabee Shoten is a long-established sake merchant standing in front of the World Heritage temple Gankoji in Nara's historic Naramachi quarter. Its storehouse is a nationally registered tangible cultural property and a designated Nara City landsca

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1868 (Meiji 1), Ogawa Matabee Shoten is a long-established sake merchant standing in front of the World Heritage temple Gankoji in Nara's historic Naramachi quarter. Its storehouse is a nationally registered tangible cultural property and a designated Nara City landscape-preservation building, and a roughly 60-meter rail track once used to move sake barrels still runs inside.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop playfully sums up its mood as nurui (lukewarm), yurui (loose) and ayashii (mysterious) — an unhurried oasis for travelers wandering Naramachi, where you can sip while gazing at the temple across the street.
chef_bio.specialty: A curated lineup of Nara local sake said to gather bottles from breweries across the prefecture (around 70 varieties), served by the glass alongside craft beer and wine. Notable pours include a revived Taisho-era Sakura Beer and craft-beer flights.

anecdotes.text: A resident dog named Fuku-chan has become a beloved neighborhood fixture, drawing visitors in on its own.
The semi-open shopfront runs a casual standing 'chokochoko cafe' where you drink looking out at Gankoji temple.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, casual standing sake bar in front of a temple where pointing at the day's bottles works fine, though staff likely speak limited English and there is little to no printed English menu.

places.name: とろけるハンバーグ 福よし ならまち店 (Torokeru Hamburg Fukuyoshi Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: The hamburg patty itself is notable: it is 100% domestic Japanese black wagyu ground without binders, so the official site and brand state it contains no wheat flour, egg, or dairy in the patty. However, this is NOT a gluten-free or celiac-safe restaurant. It is a hamburg steak house with no GF program: the patties are finished by diners on a shared-style tabletop iron plate, the accompanying steak sauce and the wasabi-soy-sauce condiment likely contain wheat/soy, garlic rice is cooked in the meat pan, and the menu directs guests to ask staff about any allergens not listed. A celiac traveler must confirm sauce ingredients and cross-contact directly with staff (Japanese helpful).
places.vegan_detail: A wagyu-beef hamburg specialist; the entire concept is built around 100% domestic black wagyu cooked at the table. There are no vegan or vegetarian main dishes, and even the rice is finished in the beef drippings. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: ならまち / 近鉄奈良駅
places.notes: Torokeru Hamburg Fukuyoshi began as a melting-hamburg specialist that grew from a single shop into a widely recognized brand, today running more than thirty locations across Japan and abroad in New York, Taiwan and Singapore. The Naramachi shop is the brand's first location in Na

chef_bio.origin: The melting-hamburg concept traces back to Sagamihara in Kanagawa, where it became a local specialty famous for its queues.
chef_bio.background: Torokeru Hamburg Fukuyoshi began as a melting-hamburg specialist that grew from a single shop into a widely recognized brand, today running more than thirty locations across Japan and abroad in New York, Taiwan and Singapore. The Naramachi shop is the brand's first location in Nara Prefecture, opened on June 30, 2025, near the lanes of the old merchant quarter a short walk from the city's main station, brought in by a local operator who was convinced the experience would resonate with Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: The brand frames itself around delivering an emotional dining experience rather than just a meal, aiming for a 'Chateaubriand-quality' hamburg and letting guests draw out the ideal final state of the meat themselves at the table.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the namesake torokeru ('melting') hamburg, made only from premium cuts of 100% domestic black wagyu, coarsely ground with no binders so you can both feel the grain of the meat and its melting softness. It arrives barely cooked and you finish it yourself on a small tabletop griddle to your preferred doneness; many diners then make garlic rice in the leftover meat juices with fried garlic, salt and pepper.

anecdotes.text: The hamburg is built to evoke the prized Chateaubriand cut, using steak-grade wagyu generously ground rather than trimmings, and is deliberately served nearly raw so diners cook it to taste on a solid-fuel-heated iron plate.

The tableside style is often compared to the beloved Shizuoka hamburg chain Sawayaka: the patty cooks about three minutes for a medium finish before being cut into bite-size pieces, then garlic rice is fried in the remaining pan drippings.
The Naramachi branch is Nara Prefecture's first Fukuyoshi, opened June 30, 2025, by a franchise owner who felt the brand 'would absolutely be accepted in Nara.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Diners finish cooking the wagyu patty themselves on a tabletop iron griddle, and garlic rice is fried afterward in the same meat juices/pan, creating shared-surface cross-contact risk for anyone avoiding gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A wasabi-soy-sauce condiment is offered alongside the hamburg, and a house steak sauce is served; soy sauce typically contains wheat and the wheat content of these sauces should be confirmed with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every main centers on wagyu beef and even the garlic rice is cooked in beef drippings, so there are no vegan-safe preparations.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official menu instructs guests to ask staff about any allergens not listed, indicating allergen handling is by direct inquiry rather than a published program.

positives.text: The hamburg patty is made from 100% domestic black wagyu with no binders, so the patty itself uses no wheat flour, egg, or dairy.

cultural_comfort.note: A bright, modern hamburg restaurant where staff bring the patty mostly raw for you to finish on a tabletop griddle, so a basic greeting and willingness to follow the cooking ritual go a long way even with limited Japanese.

places.name: アンヌ・マリーカフェ (Anne-Marie Cafe)
places.gf_detail: A British-style cafe whose menu centers on wheat-based items — scones, quiche, finger sandwiches, cakes. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu and no documented gluten-free offering, so this is not a safe choice for celiacs. The cafe states that allergy accommodations are possible (アレルギー対応も可), so a gluten-sensitive diner could ask, but no flour-free dishes are confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Baked goods (scones, cakes, quiche) and the afternoon tea set involve dairy (clotted cream, butter) and eggs, and the morning/lunch plates are not described as plant-based. The cafe notes that allergy accommodations are possible, so a vegetarian could inquire, but nothing fully vegan is confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri 8:30–18:00 (L.O. 17:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays 9:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Lunch approx. 11:00–14:00; afternoon tea from 14:00. Closed Mondays. Hours may vary.
places.neighborhood: 東向北町（きたまち入口）／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩1分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Anne-Marie Cafe is a long-running British-style cafe just a one-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, at the entrance to the historic Kitamachi quarter. It opened in 1999 and has been loved by both locals and visitors for over two decades. The interior is done in an English-cou

chef_bio.background: Anne-Marie Cafe is a long-running British-style cafe just a one-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, at the entrance to the historic Kitamachi quarter. It opened in 1999 and has been loved by both locals and visitors for over two decades. The interior is done in an English-countryside style, and the cafe deliberately keeps a quiet, settled atmosphere so that even someone dropping in alone feels at ease.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, welcoming English-style tearoom where guests — including those dining solo — can relax over scones and tea.
chef_bio.specialty: The house-made scones are the signature draw — served warm with clotted cream and jam, with a rotating range said to span dozens of seasonal varieties. Alongside the scones, the kitchen is known for its quiche, finger sandwiches, morning sets, and an afternoon tea set (from 14:00) that brings together sandwiches, quiche, pickles, cake, a scone of the day, marinated fruit, and a mini parfait.

anecdotes.text: Some customers travel from far away specifically for the scones.
The cafe has been loved by locals and tourists for over 22 years since opening in 1999.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around wheat flour (scones, cakes, quiche, sandwiches), so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods and the afternoon tea set use dairy (clotted cream, butter) and eggs; no plant-based preparation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe states that allergy accommodations are possible (アレルギー対応も可); diners with allergies should ask directly.

positives.text: Allergy accommodations are noted as available, so guests can discuss needs with staff before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood cafe where solo visitors are welcome; a little basic Japanese helps, and bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: 奈良三条カレー 本店 (Nara Sanjo Curry Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. Japanese curry roux is almost always thickened with wheat flour, and there is no indication this shop avoids it, so the menu should be treated as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: A beef-focused curry shop run with a steakhouse pedigree; signature dishes use wagyu beef, seafood, and egg (the fluffy omelet curry). No vegan or vegetarian option is documented, so it is not suitable for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00開店、無くなり次第close（月曜定休＋不定休あり）
places.neighborhood: 三条通り／近鉄・JR奈良駅 周辺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact second-floor curry shop on Sanjo-dori with roots in beef cookery: it is presented as a steakhouse's curry house, leaning on that experience in sourcing and handling its beef.

chef_bio.origin: Nara — on Sanjo-dori, the main approach street between Kintetsu/JR Nara stations and Kasuga, a short walk from the deer-filled park.
chef_bio.background: A compact second-floor curry shop on Sanjo-dori with roots in beef cookery: it is presented as a steakhouse's curry house, leaning on that experience in sourcing and handling its beef.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature plates are the wagyu fluffy omelet curry (和牛ふわふわオムカレー) — a soft, buttery plain omelet over rich curry — and a seafood curry. Curries come with an array of condiments to adjust the flavor as you eat.

anecdotes.text: Billed as 'the steakhouse's Nara Sanjo Curry,' the shop carries over a steak kitchen's beef know-how into its curries, with the wagyu fluffy omelet curry as the house favorite.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Japanese-style curry sauce is typically wheat-thickened and the shop documents no gluten-free preparation, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on wagyu beef, seafood, and egg; no separate vegan prep is indicated, so cross-contact with animal products is expected.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual lunch-only curry counter where pointing at a menu photo and paying works fine, but no English is advertised, so a foreign student should be ready to order in simple Japanese or by gesture.

places.name: ピークスベニエ (PEAKS BEIGNET)
places.gf_detail: Beignets are a fried wheat-flour dough (a doughnut-like pastry), so they are not gluten-free; one variety is even made with rye and whole grain. There is no documented gluten-free option, and items are fried in shared oil. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan claims are documented. Beignets are an enriched fried dough that typically contains dairy and/or egg, and flavored versions include cheese, white chocolate, caramel and nuts. The chicory coffee itself is plant-based and caffeine-free, but a vegan diner would need to ask whether any plain beignet is made without milk/egg.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-18:00 (sources also list 11:00-18:00); 不定休 (irregular closures announced via Instagram)
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, southeast Naramachi, near Kasuga Taisha and
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] PEAKS BEIGNET is run by the Okubo couple, who spent about two years selling their beignets at pop-up and temporary locations -- including 'Unso' on Mount Ikoma and the Nara venues 'Kore Kara' and 'SLOW HOUSE NARA' -- before opening this permanent shop in May 2021 in the quiet Tak

chef_bio.background: PEAKS BEIGNET is run by the Okubo couple, who spent about two years selling their beignets at pop-up and temporary locations -- including 'Unso' on Mount Ikoma and the Nara venues 'Kore Kara' and 'SLOW HOUSE NARA' -- before opening this permanent shop in May 2021 in the quiet Takabatake neighborhood of southeast Naramachi.
chef_bio.philosophy: A slow, unhurried cafe experience: they renovated the ground floor of a former vacant house into an intimate space and serve the beignets hot from the fryer so guests can enjoy that crisp-then-fluffy texture at its best.
chef_bio.specialty: Made-to-order beignets: a French-born, American-raised square fried pastry like a hole-less doughnut that puffs up balloon-like when fried, crisp on the outside and hollow and croissant-like inside. The standard plate is three beignets dusted with powdered sugar; varieties have included rye whole-grain, caramel and nut, lemon and white chocolate, cinnamon, coffee, and cheese-and-sugar versions. Their signature pairing is a caffeine-free chicory coffee made from roasted root.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies the renovated ground floor of a once-vacant house, and herds of Nara's famous deer sometimes wander right up to the storefront.

Staff suggest eating the three beignets in sequence -- the first for its crispness, the second for the balance of sweetness and chew, and the third to gather up the leftover powdered sugar and sauce -- picking up the square pastries by pinching a corner with two fingers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Beignets are a fried wheat-flour dough, with one variety made from rye and whole grain; no gluten-free option is documented and frying oil is shared.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavored beignets include cheese, white chocolate, caramel and nuts, indicating dairy and likely egg in the doughs; no vegan option is documented.
