places.gf_detail: Strongly gluten-free oriented. The signature focaccia is made from Hinohikari rice flour (no wheat), and the salad dressing uses gluten-free soy sauce. The owner built the cafe around the idea that people with gluten allergies can eat freely here. No documented dedicated separate fryer or formal cross-contamination protocol, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm preparation directly; but the GF intent is central and well-documented.
places.vegan_detail: Many dishes are plant-based: the seasonal vegetable salad set uses 10-15 vegetables with a vegan dressing (steamed red onion, rice oil, olive oil, gluten-free soy sauce, honey — note honey is not strictly vegan), and the seasonal anmitsu uses dairy- and egg-free brown-rice/soy/coconut-milk gelato from MINORI工房. The cafe explicitly accommodates vegetarians and vegans, but not every item is vegan (e.g., honey in dressing), so confirm specific dishes.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; closed Tuesdays and on alternating Wednesdays (隔週水曜日)
places.neighborhood: ならまち南部・西紀寺町（JR京終駅 徒歩約9分）
places.notes: café komorebi sits in a quiet machiya neighborhood in the southern part of Naramachi, about a nine-minute walk from JR Kyobate Station. Owner Hidetaka Hidaka created the cafe so that everyone could enjoy food freely, designing the menu to welcome people with gluten allergies as w

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara — a historic district of late-Edo and Meiji town houses.
chef_bio.background: café komorebi sits in a quiet machiya neighborhood in the southern part of Naramachi, about a nine-minute walk from JR Kyobate Station. Owner Hidetaka Hidaka created the cafe so that everyone could enjoy food freely, designing the menu to welcome people with gluten allergies as well as vegetarians, vegans, and those following halal and kosher diets.
chef_bio.philosophy: 誰もが自由に食を楽しめる — the belief that anyone, regardless of dietary or religious restrictions, should be able to sit down and enjoy a meal without worry.
chef_bio.specialty: A seasonal vegetable salad set built on 10-15 fresh vegetables sourced from local Nara and southern Kyoto farms and the cafe's own garden, paired with a chewy rice-flour focaccia made from Hinohikari rice flour and a soup. The menu rotates with the 24 solar terms (二十四節気). A standout dessert is the seasonal anmitsu topped with dairy- and egg-free plant-based gelato.

anecdotes.text: Although the owner himself has a dog allergy, he made the cafe pet-friendly in the afternoon as part of his commitment to being welcoming to everyone.
The cafe's name, komorebi (木漏れ日), refers to dappled sunlight; the high-ceilinged wooden room with skylights and a leafy front garden is designed to feel like light filtering through trees.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house salad dressing is made with gluten-free soy sauce rather than standard wheat-based shoyu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner built the cafe specifically to accommodate gluten allergies, vegetarian, vegan, halal and kosher diets, so staff are oriented around dietary needs.

positives.text: Signature focaccia is made from Hinohikari rice flour with no wheat.
Seasonal anmitsu uses plant-based gelato made without dairy or eggs (brown-rice/soy/coconut-milk base).
Profile states the cafe is gluten-free, halal and kosher friendly with fresh vegetables and herbs.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, inclusive cafe whose owner specifically wants people with dietary restrictions to feel at ease, so a foreign student can manage with a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 喫茶と軽食 佐保 (Kissa to Keishoku Saho)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop whose menu centers on wheat-based and dashi/soy-seasoned dishes — sandwiches, fried rice, tonkatsu (breaded pork) set meals, morning toast sets. No gluten-free menu, no celiac accommodation, and no information on cross-contamination control. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: An omnivorous kissaten built around meat and egg dishes (roasted-pork fried rice, tonkatsu sets, egg morning sets, sandwiches). While the owner grows fresh vegetables and fruit, there is no documented vegan menu and standard dishes use fish dashi, meat, eggs and dairy. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 8:00–17:00 (morning service until 11:30, lunch from 11:30 until sold out)
places.neighborhood: 奈良市法蓮町（国道24号沿い、一条高校東側／新大宮駅エリア）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 佐保 has been brewing coffee and serving light meals along National Route 24, just east of Ichijo High School, for about 42 years. Over four decades it has become a genuine fixture of the neighborhood — a place locals describe as a second living room, dropping in daily or several t

chef_bio.background: 佐保 has been brewing coffee and serving light meals along National Route 24, just east of Ichijo High School, for about 42 years. Over four decades it has become a genuine fixture of the neighborhood — a place locals describe as a second living room, dropping in daily or several times a week to read a manga over coffee or catch up with friends.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner has kept prices low for decades — barely raising them over the years — by growing the produce in-house, treating the shop as a welcoming everyday gathering place rather than a profit-driven business.
chef_bio.specialty: Honest, old-fashioned kissaten cooking at remarkably low prices: a ¥400 morning set, eight kinds of set meal at ¥600–700 (such as roasted-pork fried rice or tonkatsu), every drink at ¥350, plus homemade parfaits. The kitchen leans on vegetables and fruit the owner grows pesticide-free in the back garden.

anecdotes.text: The owner cultivates pesticide-free vegetables and fruit in the backyard, and that homegrown produce is the open secret behind the shop's unusually affordable menu.
After 42 years it works like a living room for the area — solo customers settle in with long-running comic series while groups linger over conversation.
A thoroughly Showa-retro coffee shop with vintage food-sample displays, manga and magazines to read, often described by visitors as a hidden gem.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, regulars-oriented neighborhood spot where little to no English is likely; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will make a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: さくらパン工房 (Sakura Pan Kobo)
places.gf_detail: A gluten-free rice-flour bread (米粉パン) is offered for customers concerned about gluten. However, this is a small wheat bakery whose core lineup is made from 100% domestic (Hokkaido) wheat, and no information about a separate gluten-free workspace, equipment, or cross-contamination controls is published. Not suitable for celiac or highly sensitive travelers; treat the rice-flour bread as a preference-level option, not a guaranteed gluten-free product. Note also that their "roasted rice flour roll" is a wheat-based bread that incorporates rice flour for texture — it is NOT gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: A standard Japanese bakery. Signature items (melon pan, salted-butter rolls, croissants) contain butter, eggs and/or dairy, so most of the lineup is not vegan. There may be incidental plant-based items among the ~30 daily breads, but nothing is marketed as vegan and ingredients are not labeled, so vegans must ask in person and inspect each product.
places.neighborhood: 四条大路 (Shijo-oji), near Amagatsuji Station, west of central N
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-style neighborhood bakery in the Shijo-oji area west of central Nara that has built a loyal local following over the years. Its breads are made from 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat with natural fermentation and no synthetic additives such as emulsifiers, yeast food o

chef_bio.background: A small, family-style neighborhood bakery in the Shijo-oji area west of central Nara that has built a loyal local following over the years. Its breads are made from 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat with natural fermentation and no synthetic additives such as emulsifiers, yeast food or dough improvers — a deliberately health-conscious, child-friendly approach.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is to always deliver soft, freshly-baked bread that is gentle on the body — favoring careful small-batch baking and natural ingredients over mass production.
chef_bio.specialty: Melon pan has been the flagship since opening, offered in several rotating styles: a plain version with a crisp biscuit top sweetened with raw cane sugar, an Earl Grey 'black-tea melon pan,' a caramel-sugar version, and a 'Wakakusa matcha melon pan' made with high-grade Nara-grown matcha. The roasted-rice-flour salted-butter roll is the bakery's most-repeated purchase, and cute animal-shaped breads are a signature draw. Around 30 kinds are baked daily.

anecdotes.text: The bakery collaborated with local second-graders to develop a palm-sized sandwich bread, reflecting its strong ties to the surrounding community and nearby schools.
Known locally as a relaxed hidden gem, it draws fans for inexpensive breads (such as a 90-yen item) and charming melon pan and turtle-shaped breads.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery using 100% domestic wheat for its main lineup; a gluten-free rice-flour bread is offered but no separate workspace, equipment or cross-contamination controls are documented, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature breads contain butter, eggs and dairy and are baked alongside all other products; no vegan-segregated handling is described.

positives.text: All breads are made with natural fermentation and without synthetic additives such as emulsifiers, yeast food or dough improvers, using 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat.
A gluten-free rice-flour bread is available for customers who are concerned about gluten.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly neighborhood bakery where you simply pick up a tray, choose your breads and pay at the counter, so a few words of Japanese and pointing are all you need; staff are unlikely to speak much English but the self-serve format makes it easy.

places.name: ミジンコブンコ (Mijinko Bunko)
places.gf_detail: Curries are served over rice (turmeric/saffron rice) and the menu leans on South Indian / Sri Lankan legume-and-vegetable dishes like dal and rassam, which are often naturally gluten-free. However, no gluten-free claim, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented, and spice blends and the tofu-based dessert could contain wheat-derived ingredients. Celiac travelers must ask directly and cannot assume cross-contamination control.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around vegetable-and-legume "spice obanzai" side dishes (dal, rassam, Bengali-style cabbage curry, vegetable sides) and a tofu gateau chocolat, so plant-forward eating is central. A vegetarian "whimsical" curry and the multiple vegetable sides are often vegan-friendly, but raita is yogurt-based and dishes like chicken and keema are meat. No formal vegan certification exists; confirm with the owner whether a chosen plate is fully plant-based (no dairy/ghee).
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Saturday 11:30–17:30 (last order 17:00). Closed Sunday and Monday. (One listing shows a shorter 11:30–14:30 service window; hours may vary, confirm before visiting.)
places.neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi)
places.cuisine: Book cafe serving South Indian / Sri Lankan-
places.notes: The owner, Hitomi Shuji, was once a biology researcher before deciding to build a life centered on the basics of clothing, food and shelter. He studied at an architecture school and apprenticed under a builder, then opened this cafe in 2013 inside a roughly 100-year-old machiya t

chef_bio.background: The owner, Hitomi Shuji, was once a biology researcher before deciding to build a life centered on the basics of clothing, food and shelter. He studied at an architecture school and apprenticed under a builder, then opened this cafe in 2013 inside a roughly 100-year-old machiya townhouse that he renovated himself, keeping its wide earthen-floor entrance and deep, nostalgic interior.
chef_bio.philosophy: More than a thousand books are arranged deliberately at random throughout the space so that wherever a guest sits, they encounter something unexpected and step beyond their own frame of reference, blending unhurried reading time with home-style spice cooking that lets vegetable and bean flavors come through naturally.
chef_bio.specialty: Original spice curries adapted from South Indian and Sri Lankan traditions, using twenty-plus spices per plate alongside ingredients like fenugreek and tamarind water. The signature Mijinko Plate pairs a chosen main curry with several rotating vegetable-and-legume side dishes such as dal, rassam and Bengali-style cabbage curry over turmeric rice; choices include a spicy chicken curry, a milder keema, and a daily 'whimsical' curry that changes with the weather and the owner's mood. A tofu gateau chocolat is a notable dessert.

anecdotes.text: Hitomi left a career as a biology researcher and retrained in architecture and building before opening the cafe in 2013, doing much of the townhouse renovation himself.
A visiting writer described the renovated century-old machiya as having a grandmother's-house nostalgia while the interior fills with exotic spice aromas.

positives.text: The cooking centers on South Indian and Sri Lankan legume-and-vegetable dishes (dal, rassam, vegetable curries) cooked from scratch with whole spices, so naturally plant-based and rice-based options are part of the regular menu rather than an afterthought.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, owner-run machiya book cafe where staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the curry menu) will make ordering smooth and relaxed.

places.name: お台所＆サロン かたつむり (Odaidokoro & Salon Katatsumuri)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The kitchen serves a single daily-changing home-style set meal, and documented dishes include spring rolls and okara (soy pulp) cake; miso and seasonings typically contain wheat, so the food is not celiac-safe. There is one cook preparing everything by hand, so a celiac diner would need to call ahead (0742-25-5366) and discuss specifics; nothing is dedicated or certified.
places.vegan_detail: The cooking leans plant-forward and home-style. A documented set meal included roasted squash and Brussels-sprout salad dressed with eggless vegan mayonnaise, spinach namul, and an okara cake, alongside non-vegan items such as pork-and-daikon miso. The menu is a single daily-changing set (本日の御膳, about 1,500 yen, plus 500 yen for dessert and drink), so vegan content varies by day and is not guaranteed; reserve and ask in advance. Fish dashi may be used in some dishes.
places.hours_raw: 土日のみの営業 11:00〜18:00（イベント等により変則的。インスタグラムで要確認）
places.neighborhood: きたまち（法蓮町）
places.cuisine: Gallery café in a renovated prewar townhouse
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The current owner reopened this café in June 2023, taking over a beloved prewar wooden townhouse in Nara's Kitamachi that had run for a decade as Gallery Café Katatsumuri. The host previously worked in the medical field and brought that experience into the kitchen.

chef_bio.background: The current owner reopened this café in June 2023, taking over a beloved prewar wooden townhouse in Nara's Kitamachi that had run for a decade as Gallery Café Katatsumuri. The host previously worked in the medical field and brought that experience into the kitchen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rooted in food-as-healing: the owner wants meals to nourish body and mind, creating a calm salon-like space where guests slow down and feel cared for, blending art and food under one roof.
chef_bio.specialty: A single daily-changing handmade set meal (本日の御膳) of warm home cooking, paired with homemade sweets and drinks; documented plates include pork-and-daikon miso, vegetable spring-roll soup, roasted squash and Brussels-sprout salad with eggless vegan mayonnaise, namul, and okara cake.

anecdotes.text: You leave your shoes at the door and settle into one of just a handful of two-person tables across two floors of the aged townhouse, giving the place the intimacy of being invited into someone's home rather than a restaurant.
The kitchen quietly works vegan touches into its everyday cooking, such as dressing a roasted-squash salad with eggless vegan mayonnaise.

positives.text: The kitchen prepares everything by hand and already uses eggless vegan mayonnaise in at least one salad, suggesting some willingness to accommodate plant-based eaters if asked in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny shoes-off home café run by one host with weekend-only, sometimes-irregular hours, so a foreign student should reserve ahead and be ready to communicate mostly in Japanese.

places.name: ならまちテラス食堂 蜜や (Naramachi Terrace Shokudo Mitsuya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Core menu items use wheat heavily — fried chicken batter, hamburger, curry roux, ramen and soba-style noodles, and standard soy sauce (which contains wheat). No gluten-free preparation or dedicated handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-forward set-meal diner whose signature dishes are fried chicken, pork steak (tonteki), Yamato beef hamburger and roast beef, and homemade roasted pork. Side dishes and sets rely on fish/meat dashi and miso, and no dedicated vegan menu is documented. A vegan diner would have very little to eat here; ask directly if you visit, but do not expect plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00; closed Wednesday and Thursday (per current Tabelog/Yahoo listings). An older 2020 cafe-launch article listed longer hours of ~11:30-19:00 (until ~20:00 weekends) with only Thursday closed; current sources show shortened daytime hours.
places.neighborhood: ならまち / 東寺林町
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mitsuya is a small cafe-style set-meal diner in Naramachi, run by an owner who grew up in a family of cooks and brings that home-kitchen sensibility to the menu.

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi, central Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.background: Mitsuya is a small cafe-style set-meal diner in Naramachi, run by an owner who grew up in a family of cooks and brings that home-kitchen sensibility to the menu.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carefully handmade home-style cooking, with key components like the roasted pork and miso made in-house from scratch.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty teishoku plates — salt-koji fried chicken with sweet-vinegar sauce, Yamato pork tonteki, Yamato beef hamburger and roast beef plates, omu-hayashi rice made with Nara tomatoes — alongside homemade items like roasted pork and miso, and a well-known generously-filled Chinese-style chimaki rice dumpling. The kitchen also serves house desserts such as hojicha-honey mitarashi dango.

anecdotes.text: The owner was raised in a chef's family and runs the place as an open, welcoming kitchen built around homemade dishes.
Set on a terrace-equipped cafe-style space, it became one of the most loved set-meal spots in Nara, with locals singling out plates like the salt-koji fried chicken and the Yamato pork tonteki.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried foods (karaage), hamburger, curry and noodle dishes are core menu items, so wheat is widespread in the kitchen and shared fryer/prep contamination is likely. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese set-meal diner, standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in seasoning and sauces (e.g. the sweet-vinegar sauce on the fried chicken); no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on meat (pork, chicken, Yamato beef) and homemade roasted pork, and sets use fish/meat dashi and miso, so plant-based meals are not separated and contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small family-run set-meal diner with mostly Japanese signage and menus and no reservations, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, but the casual cafe-style atmosphere is welcoming.

places.name: タリカロ3.5 (Tarikaro 3.5)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The kitchen serves South Indian-style spice curries (dal, shrimp, keema, chicken) over rice, but no information confirms whether spice blends, sauces, or sides are wheat-free, and there is no stated celiac handling. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The signature curry plate is built around shrimp, keema (minced meat), and chicken, with only the dal (lentil) curry being potentially plant-based — but there is no confirmation it is cooked without ghee, dairy, or animal stock. Yogurt and lassi are offered alongside. No vegan menu is documented, so a vegan visitor would need to ask whether a plant-only plate can be prepared.
places.neighborhood: 東向北商店街（カフェWAKAKUSA内）／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約3分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tarikaro began in Nara in 2010, moved to Tokyo's Nishi-Ogikubo, and closed in January 2025. A devoted longtime fan of the curry, violinist and whistle player Akio Nozawa took over the name through business succession and reopened it in June 2025 as Tarikaro 3.5, sharing a space w

chef_bio.origin: Nara (revival of a beloved local curry shop)
chef_bio.background: Tarikaro began in Nara in 2010, moved to Tokyo's Nishi-Ogikubo, and closed in January 2025. A devoted longtime fan of the curry, violinist and whistle player Akio Nozawa took over the name through business succession and reopened it in June 2025 as Tarikaro 3.5, sharing a space with Cafe Wakakusa on the Higashimuki-kita shopping street. He also directs an NPO that brings orchestra performances to children. He added '3.5' because he still considers himself only half-trained as a cook.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nozawa likens building a plate of spices to conducting an orchestra, with the cook deciding which flavors lead, aiming to deliver a complete experience of food, drink, atmosphere, and human connection rather than just a meal. He works to carry on the founders' balance of heat and umami.
chef_bio.specialty: A South Indian-style hot curry plate of four curries served left to right in rising heat: dal (lentil), shrimp, keema, and an ultra-spicy chicken, with yogurt and lassi offered to temper the fire.

anecdotes.text: When Nozawa learned the original shop had closed in 2025 he was devastated; days later he saw a post about the chance to carry it on, immediately traveled to Tokyo, and was chosen for his commitment to honoring the founders' vision.
The previous chef stressed that the balance of spice and umami is what defines Tarikaro's identity.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature plate is centered on shrimp, keema (minced meat), and chicken curries served on a shared plate, with only the dal potentially plant-based and no documented vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm owner-run counter shop sharing space with a cafe; a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way, and you should be ready for genuinely fierce spice levels.

places.name: HALAL & VEGGIE Restaurant ポンテロッソ (HALAL & VEGGIE Restaurant Ponte Rosso)
places.gf_detail: Curries are described as gluten-free, and a gluten-free naan can be made with advance notice (standard naan is wheat). The kitchen is not a dedicated gluten-free facility — wheat naan is prepared on-site, so cross-contact is possible. Celiac travelers should call ahead (0742-23-0808) to arrange gluten-free options and discuss preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Strong, clearly-labeled vegan offerings, not just incidental. There is a dedicated vegan dal bhat (around ¥1,738), vegan biryani, plant-based yogurt, and naan made from Nara-prefecture flour without egg, milk, or baking powder. The kitchen avoids animal fats and commercial oils, and the signature dal bhat is made free of wheat flour, plant oils, sugar, and dairy. Halal meat dishes are also served, so confirm the vegan version when ordering.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–21:50 (closed Wednesdays). Sources vary: some list a mid-afternoon break and slightly different closing times, so hours should be treated as approximate.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅エリア（小西さくら通り）
places.cuisine: インド・ネパール料理
places.notes: Set in a renovated roughly century-old townhouse just off a cherry-tree-lined shopping street in central Nara, this Indian and Nepalese kitchen was built around the owner's belief that, in an era of 100-year lifespans, good food is the foundation of a long, healthy life. That con

chef_bio.background: Set in a renovated roughly century-old townhouse just off a cherry-tree-lined shopping street in central Nara, this Indian and Nepalese kitchen was built around the owner's belief that, in an era of 100-year lifespans, good food is the foundation of a long, healthy life. That conviction shows up in an uncompromising approach to sourcing and cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: Additive-free, hand-made cooking with no commercial oils or additives, halal-certified meat, and sweets made with cane sugar instead of white sugar — food intended to be both authentic and genuinely nourishing.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dal bhat is the dish people come for, cooked free of wheat flour, plant oils, refined sugar, and dairy so the spices and vegetables carry the flavor; international guests have told the staff it is the best they have had. Other standouts include five kinds of biryani (chicken, lamb, cashew, seafood, and mixed vegetable) served generously, hand-made-from-the-skin soup momo, a chaat-masala vegetable salad, and naan baked from Nara-prefecture flour with no egg, milk, or baking powder.

anecdotes.text: The owner cooks by a '100-year-life' philosophy, holding that quality, additive-free food is essential to healthy longevity, which is why the kitchen refuses commercial oils and additives.
The vegan dal bhat is made free of the 'four poisons' — wheat flour, plant oils, sugar, and dairy — and international customers have told staff it is the best they have eaten.
Set inside a renovated townhouse roughly a century old, the restaurant serves halal-certified meat and makes its own sweets with cane sugar rather than white sugar.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Curries are labeled gluten-free and a gluten-free naan can be made on advance request, but standard wheat naan is baked on-site, so the kitchen is not a dedicated gluten-free environment and cross-contact is possible.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Halal meat dishes are prepared in the same kitchen as the vegan and vegetarian items, so diners should specify the vegan version (e.g., vegan dal bhat) when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen explicitly avoids animal fats, commercial oils, and additives, and offers dedicated halal, vegetarian, and vegan dishes, indicating staff routinely accommodate dietary needs; specific allergen protocols were not documented, so confirm by phone (0742-23-0808).

positives.text: Dedicated vegan dal bhat, vegan biryani, and plant-based yogurt are on the menu, plus naan made without egg, milk, or baking powder.
All meat is halal-certified, and sweets are made with cane sugar instead of white sugar.

cultural_comfort.note: Used to international and Muslim diners, with clearly labeled halal, vegan, and vegetarian options, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about ingredients.

places.name: 素味 スイーツ&コーヒー (Somi sweets&coffee) (Somi sweets & coffee)
places.gf_detail: Many gluten-free items use pesticide-free rice flour (rice-flour cookies, muffins, sponge cakes), but it is NOT a dedicated GF kitchen: the popular rum-raisin sandwich uses an ancient spelt-wheat (Shiga) sable, and some baked goods are "vegan or gluten-free" rather than both. Wheat flour is used and present in the small kitchen, so a celiac diner should ask which specific items are rice-flour-based and treat cross-contact as a real risk. Good for gluten-avoiders by choice; not confirmed celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Everything sold is plant-based. The owner, trained in vegan sweets and specialty coffee at a Kyoto cafe, makes a rotating selection of roughly 20 vegan sweets per week (cream sandwich cookies, tarts, rice-flour muffins, carrot cake, seasonal-fruit decoration cakes) using organic, transparently-sourced ingredients; creams are made from cashew and cacao butter rather than dairy. Coffee is specialty-grade. Reliably vegan throughout.
places.hours_raw: Most recent official-site info: open Saturdays and Sundays approx. 13:00-18:00 (may close earlier when sold out). Earlier listings showed 11:00-18:00 daily with irregular closures. Schedule is announced week-to-week on Instagram (@somi_narakitamachi).
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi (Horencho), near Todai-ji / Kintetsu Nara Station
places.cuisine: Organic vegan baked sweets and specialty cof
places.notes: Run by a woman who trained at a cafe in Kyoto, learning vegan sweets-making, specialty coffee, and organic sourcing before going independent. She opened Somi on December 7, 2017, in a renovated traditional machiya in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district.

chef_bio.origin: Kyoto cafe background; independent since 2017 in Nara Kitamachi
chef_bio.background: Run by a woman who trained at a cafe in Kyoto, learning vegan sweets-making, specialty coffee, and organic sourcing before going independent. She opened Somi on December 7, 2017, in a renovated traditional machiya in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district.
chef_bio.philosophy: She bakes with the conviction that good sweets should be 'not special treats for particular people, but approachable sweets for anyone,' choosing organic ingredients from producers whose backgrounds she knows.
chef_bio.specialty: A weekly-rotating lineup of about 20 vegan sweets. The signature is the rum-raisin sandwich: an ancient-spelt sable from Shiga filled with organic cashew-and-cacao-butter cream and rum raisins. Also beloved are the carrot cake (pesticide-free rice flour, organic carrots, lemon frosting) and rice-flour cookies paired with Nara green tea or domestic kinako and sesame. Seasonal-fruit decoration cakes can be ordered.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a cozy, old-fashioned converted townhouse with small tables and shelves lined with the week's vegan sweets, presided over by a notably warm, kind owner.
A Kyoto-cafe barista who went independent, she opened Somi on December 7, 2017, to showcase ingredient-driven baking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free facility: wheat (ancient spelt) is used for the popular rum-raisin sandwich sable, so rice-flour items are baked in a kitchen that also handles wheat. Celiac visitors should ask which items are rice-flour-based and weigh cross-contact risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire shop is plant-based, so there is no animal-product cross-contact concern; all sweets and the coffee program are vegan.

positives.text: All sweets are vegan and made with organic, transparently-sourced ingredients; many items are gluten-free rice-flour-based, giving plant-based and gluten-avoiding visitors plenty of reliable choices.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm owner-run sweets shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the display case go a long way; little English, but ordering takeout pastries is low-pressure for a foreign student.

places.name: じびえ井田 (Jibie Ida (Gibier Ida))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a multi-course game-meat tasting menu that relies on house-made miso, soy sauce (made from Daiteppo heirloom soybeans, which typically still contains wheat), and Japanese seasonings. There is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation. A celiac traveler would need to discuss needs in advance when booking; cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated wild-game (gibier) restaurant — the entire fixed menu is built around venison, wild boar, badger, bear and other meats. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options, and the cuisine is fundamentally meat-centered. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 19:00-22:00 (single simultaneous seating). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi / Sarusawa Pond area (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
places.notes: Chef Hiroshi Ida, born in 1975 in Tottori Prefecture, trained in the kitchens of major Osaka hotels including Hotel Agora and Hotel Seagull, and spent five years as executive chef at Garden City Club Osaka before becoming the inaugural head chef of the DINING ROOM IN THE NARAMACH

chef_bio.background: Chef Hiroshi Ida, born in 1975 in Tottori Prefecture, trained in the kitchens of major Osaka hotels including Hotel Agora and Hotel Seagull, and spent five years as executive chef at Garden City Club Osaka before becoming the inaugural head chef of the DINING ROOM IN THE NARAMACHI in 2018. He is a passionate game enthusiast who has been studying to become a licensed hunter so that he can one day serve game he has captured himself.
chef_bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is 'the more you know, the more delicious it becomes' (知ればもっと美味しくなる). Boar and deer culled to protect crops are usually discarded, so Ida treats game as a resource to be honored rather than wasted, talking diners through the meat, the tableware, the space and the drink pairings. In his words, expanding the uses and commercial distribution of game would make it a far more everyday ingredient and let food and life circulate with less waste.
chef_bio.specialty: A multi-course tasting menu built entirely around wild game paired with Nara's Yamato ingredients: charcoal-grilled citrus-fed wild boar with Yamato mustard-greens puree, Yamato venison sukiyaki, venison thigh steak served with house miso made from Daiteppo heirloom soybeans and Yamato green-onion paste, wild boar shoulder with house-smoked oil and Daiteppo soy sauce, and house-cured charcoal-grilled badger bacon. Game is sourced through the Totsukawa gibier culinary school, which specializes in the capture, processing and refinement of deer and wild boar.

anecdotes.text: Chef Ida is training to obtain a hunting license so that he can eventually source and serve game he has personally caught.
He notes that only around ten percent of culled wildlife currently becomes food, because of the cost and logistics of processing, which is part of why he champions dignified, low-waste use of game.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use house-made soy sauce and miso made from Daiteppo heirloom soybeans; Japanese soy sauce and miso of this kind typically contain wheat, so the menu should be assumed to contain gluten unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a dedicated wild-game restaurant where the entire menu is meat-based; there are no vegan or vegetarian dishes documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, high-end chef's-counter tasting experience conducted largely in Japanese, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with some Japanese ability or a Japanese-speaking companion, and should arrange any dietary needs when reserving.

places.name: 和風薬膳料理 紅茶 京小づち (Washoku Yakuzen Ryori Kocha Kyo Koduchi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a washoku kitchen that seasons with regular Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) and serves rice porridge and bento with many components; there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated handling, or cross-contamination control. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
places.vegan_detail: Despite the heavy use of seasonal Yamato vegetables and herbs, this is not a vegan destination: dishes are cooked in katsuo (bonito) and kombu dashi, so the broths and most savory items contain fish. Signature items include ancient cheese ("so"/蘇), a dairy product, and the medicinal porridge uses domestic chicken broth. A vegetable-forward eater could find some plant-heavy plates, but a strict vegan cannot reliably eat here without confirming dashi-free preparation, which is not documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-19:00 (official site; reservations prioritized, closes when sold out). Tabelog lists 11:30-21:00. Closed Thursdays (open if Thursday is a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Kintetsu Nara Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Naramachi restaurant devoted to washoku yakuzen — Japanese-style medicinal cuisine — built around the idea of creating new Nara flavors from historically significant ingredients. The kitchen leans on locally grown seasonal Yamato vegetables and accessible Chinese herbal i

chef_bio.background: A small Naramachi restaurant devoted to washoku yakuzen — Japanese-style medicinal cuisine — built around the idea of creating new Nara flavors from historically significant ingredients. The kitchen leans on locally grown seasonal Yamato vegetables and accessible Chinese herbal ingredients and herbs, cooking them gently in bonito-and-kelp dashi so the food is meant to be both tasty and kind to the body.
chef_bio.philosophy: Eat well to feel well from the inside: choose immunity-supporting ingredients, prepare them in a familiar Japanese style so anyone can enjoy them, and let history and local produce shape the plate.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature Yakuzen Naramachi Bento brings together around 40 ingredients, including ancient red rice introduced to Japan in the Nara period and 'so' (蘇), an ancient cheese, with each ingredient's wellness effect noted on the menu. Other standouts are the medicinal soup porridge (yakuzen zosui) made with domestic free-range chicken broth and Chinese herbs such as cinnamon, star anise and bay leaf, and dishes featuring Yamato toki (Japanese angelica) leaves — minced into a house tomato sauce or fried as tempura when the fresh leaves arrive.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant revives Nara-period foods that almost no modern menu carries, serving 'so' (ancient cheese) and 'raku' (an ancient yogurt-like dairy) alongside ancient red rice.
It is recognized as one of the eateries that lets diners taste Yamato toki (Japanese angelica) leaf, a Nara medicinal-herb specialty, used in its house tomato sauce and as tempura.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese washoku style; regular soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the norm and no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked in katsuo (bonito) and kombu dashi, so even vegetable dishes are prepared in a fish-stock kitchen; the porridge uses chicken broth and the signature bento includes ancient cheese.

positives.text: Menus describe each ingredient and its wellness effect, so diners can see what is in each component of the bento and course.
Heavy use of fresh seasonal Yamato vegetables and herbs means plenty of plant-forward components on the plate.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, traditional Japanese medicinal-cuisine spot where staff likely speak limited English, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help you order and ask about ingredients.

places.name: ぐるめ 藤 (Gourmet Fuji)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese home-style teishoku/izakaya. The kitchen relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and miso, fish dashi, and dishes such as tonkatsu (breaded/fried) and spaghetti, so it is not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is built around fish and meat set meals (e.g. grilled hokke or tonkatsu) with fish-dashi-based miso soup and standard seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian set is documented; not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 / Dinner 18:00-22:00 (some sources list evening service running later, up to around 23:00). Hours may vary.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small family-run eatery a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station in the Naramachi quarter. By day it is a humble teishoku diner serving daily set lunches; by night it turns into a neighborhood izakaya. The proprietress (okami-san) cooks the home-style dishes herself, and the lun

chef_bio.background: A small family-run eatery a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station in the Naramachi quarter. By day it is a humble teishoku diner serving daily set lunches; by night it turns into a neighborhood izakaya. The proprietress (okami-san) cooks the home-style dishes herself, and the lunch crowd is largely local regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: Warm, gentle home cooking meant to satisfy both stomach and heart.
chef_bio.specialty: The daily set lunch (around 800 yen, coffee included) with a choice of a fish or meat main — for example grilled hokke or tonkatsu, served with sides like hijiki, simmered greens, pickles and miso soup.

anecdotes.text: The daily lunch changes day to day and comes with after-meal coffee; a red banner advertising the day's set effectively serves as the shop sign.
Described as having a warm, gentle flavor that satisfies both stomach and heart, drawing loyal local regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried breaded items (tonkatsu) and wheat-based dishes; no separation or gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese home cooking; assumes ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce and miso. No wheat-free soy sauce documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sets center on fish and meat with fish-dashi miso soup; no vegan preparation documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, regulars-driven local diner run by the proprietress with no English menu, so a few words of Japanese and an easygoing attitude will go a long way.

places.name: オステリア ユルリ (Osteria Yururi)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-heavy Italian osteria: the menu centers on pasta and the kitchen serves bread and Italian dishes, so it is not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. A coeliac traveler should not rely on this place.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen showcases seasonal Nara (Yamato) vegetables and offers vegetable-forward dishes, but the restaurant is built around charcoal-grilled branded pork, fish and meat, plus pasta likely made with egg and animal-fat sauces (house-made pancetta noted). No vegan menu is documented; a strict vegan would need to ask the chef in advance, ideally when reserving.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-14:30, 18:00-21:30 (last entry 20:00); closed Wednesdays; irregular closures possible
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: Osteria Yururi is a small, one-person-run Italian osteria tucked on the second floor of a building along the Higashimuki/Mochiido shopping street near Kintetsu Nara Station, on the edge of the historic Naramachi district.

chef_bio.background: Osteria Yururi is a small, one-person-run Italian osteria tucked on the second floor of a building along the Higashimuki/Mochiido shopping street near Kintetsu Nara Station, on the edge of the historic Naramachi district.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking leans on seasonal vegetables sourced directly from local Nara farms and on carefully chosen brand meats and seafood, letting good ingredients speak for themselves through simple charcoal preparation.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled cooking is the heart of the kitchen: signature plates include Azumi pastured pork (あづみ放牧豚) grilled slowly over charcoal for about 40 minutes and finished simply with salt, alongside charcoal-grilled fish. The other house signature is clay-pot rice (土鍋ご飯) cooked in traditional Iga-ware using the chef's own reduced-pesticide rice. Pasta dishes round out the menu, such as tomato-sauce pasta with fried eggplant and an oil-based short pasta with green beans, spinach and house-made pancetta.

anecdotes.text: The grilled Azumi pastured pork is cooked low and slow over charcoal for around 40 minutes, then served with just salt so the meat's natural flavor comes through; a reviewer found the accompanying chewy short pasta especially good.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pasta and Italian dishes are central to the menu, so wheat is used heavily throughout the kitchen and cross-contact is likely; not suitable for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around charcoal-grilled pork, fish and meat plus house-made pancetta, so shared charcoal grill and surfaces make cross-contact with animal products likely for vegetable dishes.

positives.text: Seasonal vegetables are sourced directly from local Nara farms and the chef grows reduced-pesticide rice used for the clay-pot rice, so plant ingredients are fresh and traceable.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, reservation-only chef-run Italian counter where some Japanese (and advance booking by phone) will help, as there is no indication of English service.

places.name: ふじ門 製麺 (Fujimon Seimen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The shop's entire identity is house-made wheat noodles (jikasei men), and the only menu is ramen built on those noodles. Both the pork-bone/chicken broth and the fish (kombu/mackerel) broth are served over wheat noodles. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of celiac-safe handling. Unsuitable for anyone avoiding gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The two broths are a pork-bone-and-chicken blend and a fish dashi (kombu plus mackerel), and bowls are topped with chashu pork. Both base broths are animal-derived, so there is no vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 月・水〜金・祝前後: 10:50-13:50 / 土・日・祝: 10:50-13:30 / 火曜定休（祝日でも休み、臨時休業はHPで告知）。ランチ営業のみ。
places.neighborhood: 馬場町 / along Yasuragi-no-Michi between Kintetsu Nara and JR N
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Fujimon Seimen opened in the summer of 2013 on Yasuragi-no-Michi, the quiet through-road between Nara's two main stations, a short walk from the Mochiidono shopping arcade. It is a small, husband-and-wife counter shop with its own noodle-making room, and the couple put their nood

chef_bio.background: Fujimon Seimen opened in the summer of 2013 on Yasuragi-no-Michi, the quiet through-road between Nara's two main stations, a short walk from the Mochiidono shopping arcade. It is a small, husband-and-wife counter shop with its own noodle-making room, and the couple put their noodle equipment first when setting up the place.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on the ingredients themselves: the pork broth is made without chemical seasonings or added salt, and the noodles are made in-house from selected flour without preservatives, kneaded with care daily.
chef_bio.specialty: Two house-made wheat noodles (a thin and a medium-thick cut) paired with a choice of two broths: a creamy pork-and-chicken broth (using pork plus Mikawa red chicken) and an elegant, milder fish dashi built on kombu and mackerel. Bowls come generously topped with melt-in-the-mouth chashu, fried burdock, lotus root, scallion and greens.

anecdotes.text: The couple prioritized buying noodle-making equipment when they opened, building the shop around a visible in-house noodle room.
Opened July 2013 along Yasuragi-no-Michi, roughly a ten-minute walk from both JR and Kintetsu Nara stations, and quickly became known for lines at its tiny counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a noodle maker producing wheat noodles in-house, so flour and wheat are pervasive throughout the kitchen; cross-contact would be unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both broths are animal-based (pork/chicken or fish dashi) and prepared in the same small kitchen, so any item would share equipment with meat and fish stocks.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only counter ramen shop run by a couple with no reservations and lunch-only hours; a foreign student should be ready to order in Japanese, pay cash, and eat fairly quickly when there's a line.

noodles.notes: Self-made (jika-sei) noodles using inner-Mongolian kansui and rock salt. Diners choose between a medium-thick cut (#18) and a thin cut (#26).

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta-dashi ra-men (pork-broth ramen) — the rich, foam-whipped pork-and-chicken bowl; the deluxe version adds an extra thick black-pepper chashu slice.
ramen.chashu: Slow-braised, melt-in-the-mouth rolled pork (toro-toro); the deluxe bowl features a thick slab finished with cracked black pepper.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles are house-made; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Both broths are animal/fish-based — the light bowl uses mackerel-bushi and niboshi, the rich bowl uses pork and chicken bone. No documented plant-only broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Nara original rather than a regional throwback: this tiny noodle-house lets you pick its soul, either a clear, kombu-and-mackerel sea broth that whispers of refined gyokai dashi, or a creamy pork-and-chicken soup whipped to a fine foam so it drinks rich yet clean. Both lean on real ingredients over additives, and the house-pulled noodles tie the whole bowl to its own seimen (noodle-making) craft.

places.highlight: A modern Nara original rather than a regional throwback: this tiny noodle-house lets you pick its soul, either a clear, kombu-and-mackerel sea broth that whispers of refined gyokai dashi, or a creamy pork-and-chicken soup whipped to a fine foam so it drinks rich yet clean. Both lean on real ingredients over additives, and the house-pulled noodles tie the whole bowl to its own seimen (noodle-making) craft.

places.name: 粉もん屋 八 奈良餅飯殿店 (Konamonya Hachi Nara Mochiidono)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core product is takoyaki, made from a wheat-flour batter. The batter is also enriched with a fish-based broth, and the standard topping is wheat-based takoyaki/okonomiyaki sauce. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Takoyaki centers on octopus, the batter uses a fish-based dashi broth and typically egg, and toppings include bonito flakes and mayonnaise. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 食べログ: 10:00～19:00（木曜定休、臨時休業有） / もちいどの商店街公式: 11:00～20:00
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿センター街（もちいどの商店街） / 近鉄奈良駅
places.notes: Konamonya Hachi is a takoyaki and konamon (Osaka-style flour-food) brand that was born in Nara and has been operating for around 18 years, growing from a local stand into a chain of more than 40 shops across Nara, Osaka, Kyoto, Shiga, Saitama, Tokyo and Hokkaido. The Mochiidono b

chef_bio.background: Konamonya Hachi is a takoyaki and konamon (Osaka-style flour-food) brand that was born in Nara and has been operating for around 18 years, growing from a local stand into a chain of more than 40 shops across Nara, Osaka, Kyoto, Shiga, Saitama, Tokyo and Hokkaido. The Mochiidono branch sits inside the OK広場 open space in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, serving takeout and a handful of terrace seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its approach as serving customers with 元気と笑顔 (energy and smiles), and the company has also done volunteer relief cooking, including in Ishinomaki after the 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami.
chef_bio.specialty: Takoyaki in a wide range of varieties, plus small plates and drinks. The octopus balls are cooked on highly heat-conductive iron plates to get a crisp shell and a creamy molten center, with a fish-based secret broth worked into the batter.

anecdotes.text: Won first place in the konamon (flour-food) category of a Nara gourmet ranking chosen by 10,000 local residents, and took the top prize at one of Nara Prefecture's largest B-grade gourmet contests in August 2011.
The Mochiidono branch is presented as a B-grade-gourmet-winning shop offering a wide variety of takoyaki along with a small dish and drinks, with terrace seating in the arcade plaza.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Takoyaki batter is wheat flour cooked on shared iron griddle plates; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard finishing sauce on takoyaki is a wheat-based takoyaki/okonomiyaki-style sauce; no allergen labeling is provided.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Batter is made with a fish-based broth and the dish features octopus and typically egg, with bonito flakes and mayonnaise as common toppings.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up takoyaki stand in a covered shopping arcade where you can point at the menu and pay by cash or QR code, so a foreign student can order easily even with little Japanese.

places.name: 味泥棒 TaBaSa (Ajidorobo TaBaSa)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese izakaya whose cooking relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, fish dashi, and fried/grilled dishes (e.g. chicken steak). There is no gluten-free menu or any documented allergy accommodation, and no dedicated fryer. It is not suitable for anyone with celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional izakaya built around meat, fish, and dashi-based home-style cooking; the kitchen prepares dishes to budget but there is no documented vegetarian or vegan offering. Vegans should not rely on this place.
places.hours_raw: 14:00-24:00（もちいどのセンター街の店舗紹介より）。原則、年中無休。実際の営業は夕方頃からとの記載もあり。
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町・もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, family-style izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in the historic Mochiidono covered shopping arcade, about a three-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. With just thirteen seats, it is the kind of intimate neighborhood spot where you come to drink, eat, an

chef_bio.background: A small, family-style izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in the historic Mochiidono covered shopping arcade, about a three-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. With just thirteen seats, it is the kind of intimate neighborhood spot where you come to drink, eat, and sing.
chef_bio.philosophy: The spirit of the place is simple and welcoming: professionals cook for you according to your budget so you can drink, sing, and eat from around 2,500 yen in a fun, relaxed setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style izakaya cooking made to order; the kitchen is known for a carefully prepped and grilled chicken-thigh steak, with the proprietor preparing dishes to suit each guest's budget.

anecdotes.text: The shop's own arcade listing sums up the experience as 'drink, sing, and eat from 2,500 yen — a fun place,' with professionals cooking to your budget.
Diners single out the chicken-thigh steak, praising the careful preparation and grilling technique behind it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a standard izakaya cooking on shared equipment with wheat-based seasonings, cross-contamination with gluten should be assumed throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Home-style izakaya dishes here rely on standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around meat and fish (e.g. grilled chicken-thigh steak) and dashi, so vegan cross-contact is expected.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, locally-run izakaya with karaoke where the owner cooks to your budget, so some Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help a foreign student feel at home.

places.name: ピーワイハラールキッチン (PY Halal Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Core menu is wheat-heavy: naan, roti, and noodle/udon dishes all contain wheat, and curries are not prepared as celiac-safe. No dedicated gluten-free practices documented. Rice-based dishes such as biryani may suit a wheat-avoider, but cross-contamination cannot be assumed and no allergen handling is documented; ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: As a halal kitchen the menu is heavily meat-focused (chicken, lamb, beef biryani, tandoori), but vegetarian options are available and the owner caters to Muslim and vegetarian travelers from South/Southeast Asia. There is no documented fully-vegan menu; vegetarian dishes may contain dairy (cheese naan, butter chicken-style sauces, lassi) or egg, so strict vegans should confirm specific dishes and preparation. Ask staff for plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00; closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 橋本町 / もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅）
places.notes: PY Halal Kitchen is run by a devout Muslim owner from Bangladesh who opened a fully halal restaurant in the heart of Nara, just off the Mochiidono covered shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station. The spacious dining room seats around 70 and is built to welcome Muslim travelers

chef_bio.background: PY Halal Kitchen is run by a devout Muslim owner from Bangladesh who opened a fully halal restaurant in the heart of Nara, just off the Mochiidono covered shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station. The spacious dining room seats around 70 and is built to welcome Muslim travelers visiting the ancient capital, with a children's playroom for families.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything on the menu is halal and no alcohol is served, reflecting the owner's faith and creating a comfortable space for Muslim visitors from Southeast Asia, South Asia and the Middle East. The menu leans into à la carte exploration, with photo illustrations to help guests try unfamiliar dishes.
chef_bio.specialty: Lamb biryani, tandoori chicken, and cheese naan are the standout dishes. The kitchen also serves a wide pan-Asian range, including a PY chicken laksa in a coconut-milk spiced broth with springy noodles, fried tofu and boiled egg, butter chicken, and chicken wings in a sweet-savory glaze.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is designed around hospitality for Muslim tourists exploring Nara, even setting aside a playroom so families can relax over a long meal.
Beyond Indian curry, the kitchen offers dishes spanning several Asian cuisines, from biryani to laksa-style noodles, aimed at the diverse home flavors of its international guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on naan, roti and noodle/udon dishes that contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Primarily a meat-based halal kitchen (chicken, lamb, beef); vegetarian dishes may share equipment and often include dairy or egg, so vegan separation is not assured.

positives.text: Fully halal kitchen with no alcohol and no pork, run by a Muslim owner; vegetarian options are available for diners who avoid meat.
Photo-illustrated menus make it easier to identify dish contents and ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An international halal restaurant used to welcoming foreign Muslim travelers, with photo menus and a relaxed multicultural atmosphere, so a foreign student should feel comfortable ordering here.

places.name: 晴朗邸 勝手口 (Seiroutei Katteguchi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a home-cooking izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on dashi and Japanese seasonings; signature plates such as the Kansai-style meat-and-tofu stew, sautéed greens with fried tofu (油揚げ), and charred pork loin involve soy sauce, fried tofu, and likely wheat-containing soy sauce/mirin. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no gluten-free claims, and no indication of cross-contamination controls. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. It is a meat-and-fish izakaya whose cooking is built around homemade dashi (typically fish/kombu based), so most dishes — even vegetable ones — are unlikely to be vegan. There are vegetable-forward small plates (e.g., sautéed greens with fried tofu) but they use dashi and soy seasonings. A vegan would need to ask directly, and options would be very limited. Best treated as ask/limited at most.
places.hours_raw: 17:00～22:00（火～日・祝日営業）／定休日 月曜日
places.neighborhood: 餅飯殿町（もちいどのセンター街） / 近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約7分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, tucked-away izakaya run by a married couple on the second floor of a building above a food supermarket, partway down Mochiidono Center Street about a seven-minute walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station. It built a loyal following as one of the early local spots to focus its dri

chef_bio.origin: Nara, in the Mochiidono Center Street arcade that leads toward the old Naramachi district
chef_bio.background: A small, tucked-away izakaya run by a married couple on the second floor of a building above a food supermarket, partway down Mochiidono Center Street about a seven-minute walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station. It built a loyal following as one of the early local spots to focus its drinks list almost entirely on Nara regional sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is handmade and gentle on the body, letting the broth and the natural flavor of the ingredients lead; the room is intimate and home-like, the kind of place regulars return to for the warmth of the couple who run it.
chef_bio.specialty: Honest, homemade home-cooking built on carefully made dashi — Kansai-style meat-and-tofu stew with Yamato pork, sauteed greens with thin fried tofu, and charred pork loin — paired with a rotating lineup of Nara local sake, including occasional rare bottles and a local-sake tasting set.

anecdotes.text: The drinks list is devoted to Nara local sake, and a tasting set of regional sake is a favorite with guests.
Dishes are described as homemade and gentle, made with the owners' own carefully prepared dashi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Home-cooking dishes such as meat-and-tofu stew and sautéed greens with fried tofu rely on Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, mirin) that typically contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is built on homemade dashi, which at a fish-and-meat izakaya is typically fish/kombu based, so vegetable dishes are unlikely to be vegan.

positives.text: All dishes are made by hand using the owners' own dashi, described as gentle and homemade — a small kitchen where you can ask the couple directly about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, regulars-heavy couple-run izakaya up a staircase off the arcade — warm and welcoming, but little to no English, so basic Japanese or a translation app will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: シャンブル (Chambre)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional kissaten whose menu is built around wheat: honey toast, toast morning sets, sandwiches, pasta, and cakes are all wheat-based, and there is no documented gluten-free offering or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs. If you need gluten-free, you would have to ask, and nothing safe is confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature items are egg- and dairy-heavy (egg-filled hot dog morning, omelette rice, pudding, ice-cream-topped honey toast, floats). House-made fruit juices such as banana juice may be plant-based but milk content is not confirmed. A vegan diner would need to ask, and few if any dishes are likely to qualify.
places.hours_raw: 8:00～18:00 (opens 8:00; official site lists 8:00-18:00)
places.neighborhood: 高天町, just outside Kintetsu Nara Station (about 79m, north si
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chambre is a long-running pure kissaten tucked just outside Exit 6 of Kintetsu Nara Station. Its exact founding date is not recorded, but locals note it has been there since at least their childhood, marking it as a genuine neighborhood institution. After the previous owner retir

chef_bio.background: Chambre is a long-running pure kissaten tucked just outside Exit 6 of Kintetsu Nara Station. Its exact founding date is not recorded, but locals note it has been there since at least their childhood, marking it as a genuine neighborhood institution. After the previous owner retired, the café was renovated yet deliberately kept its Showa-retro mood — sofa seats, newspapers, and an unhurried atmosphere — making it a quiet refuge in an otherwise busy tourist district.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop aims to preserve the calm, nostalgic spirit of a traditional kissaten — a place to slow down over a leisurely morning rather than rush.
chef_bio.specialty: The morning service is the draw: fragrant toast (or the signature honey toast crowned with a half-scoop of vanilla ice cream and a drizzle of honey) and an egg-filled hot dog, served with coffee. Other beloved plates include a compact but filling omelette rice with big chunks of chicken (choose ketchup or demi-glace), the firm, smooth 'Chambre pudding,' sandwiches, daily lunch specials, parfaits, floats, and house-made fruit juices like banana juice.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer recalls the café has been part of the neighborhood since their childhood; it was renovated after the former owner retired but kept its Showa-retro character intact.
The honey toast morning is remembered for crispy-yet-chewy toast topped with vanilla ice cream and honey, paired with an egg-heavy hot dog and coffee for around 500 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on toast, sandwiches, pasta, and cakes, so wheat flour is pervasive and cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes rely on eggs and dairy (egg-filled hot dog morning, omelette rice, pudding, ice-cream honey toast); shared surfaces and grills make plant-based separation unlikely.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed old-school neighborhood café where a foreign student can comfortably point at the morning set or honey toast and get by with a friendly greeting and simple Japanese; staff are unlikely to speak much English.

places.name: 銀杏や (Ichouya (Patisserie Kozue))
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-based patisserie: shortcakes, baked donuts,焼き菓子 (baked sweets) and similar items contain flour, and the kitchen handles wheat throughout. Macarons are almond/egg-white/sugar based and naturally wheat-free, but they are made and displayed in a shared bakery environment with no documented gluten-free protocol, so this is not safe for celiacs. No dedicated gluten-free offering.
places.vegan_detail: A Western-style cake and macaron shop relying on eggs, butter, dairy and chocolate (the signature "ショコラノアール" macaron uses Hokkaido butter and Belgian chocolate). No vegan items are documented, and coffee drinks are not noted as having plant-milk options.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-19:00 (sources also list to 20:00); open daily
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping street (about 126m from Kintetsu Nara S
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 銀杏や is the in-house café of Patisserie Kozue (パティスリーこずえ), a small independent sweets shop tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Behind a pastel-colored storefront, the counter displays colorful macarons, shortcakes, meringues and bak

chef_bio.background: 銀杏や is the in-house café of Patisserie Kozue (パティスリーこずえ), a small independent sweets shop tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Behind a pastel-colored storefront, the counter displays colorful macarons, shortcakes, meringues and baked donuts, and the attached café lets you sit down with your sweets and a coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: Macarons are the signature — the flagship is the 'ショコラノアール' (Chocolat Noir) macaron made with Hokkaido butter, almond powder and Belgian chocolate. The shop also sells shortcakes, seasonal fresh cakes, meringues and baked donuts, and serves kakigōri (shaved ice).

anecdotes.text: At the end of 2020 the shop installed an unusual macaron vending machine at the entrance, offering around 11 flavors (pistachio, strawberry, blueberry, matcha, chocolate and more) dispensed in clear cylindrical cases with a cooling pack — a quirky local landmark in the arcade.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based baked goods (shortcakes, baked donuts, baked sweets) are produced and displayed alongside the macarons in a shared patisserie environment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Products rely on eggs, butter, dairy and chocolate; the signature macaron is made with Hokkaido butter and Belgian chocolate, so cross-contact with animal ingredients is pervasive.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed counter-style sweets shop and café where you point at what you want in the case and pay cash, so a foreign student can manage easily even with little Japanese — though no English menu is documented, so a few phrases or pointing help.

places.name: 鶴 (Tsuru)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi izakaya that relies heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and fish dashi for nigiri, sashimi, small plates, and hot-pot dishes. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no statement about handling wheat allergies. A celiac traveler should treat this place as not safe; gluten-free dining cannot be relied upon here.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on market-fresh fish — sushi, sashimi, and seafood small plates — and standard Japanese cooking that uses fish dashi. There is no documented vegan offering, and even side dishes are very likely prepared with fish-based stock. Not a viable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:50), 16:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun: 16:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); after 20:00 enter via the JR 2F entrance. Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: JR Nara Station area (Silkia Nara / Hotel Nikko Nara buildin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuru is the second shop of a long-established sushi restaurant of the same name in Tenri that has been going for around forty years. This Nara-city outpost sits right at JR Nara Station, tucked into the Silkia Nara building, and brings the parent shop's everyday-sushi spirit to

chef_bio.background: Tsuru is the second shop of a long-established sushi restaurant of the same name in Tenri that has been going for around forty years. This Nara-city outpost sits right at JR Nara Station, tucked into the Silkia Nara building, and brings the parent shop's everyday-sushi spirit to commuters and visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: An easygoing day-into-evening sushi tavern: it is just as happy to serve someone a quick lunch as it is to pour beer or sake for someone lingering over sashimi and a few small plates, so the room is usually split between the two.
chef_bio.specialty: Market-fresh seasonal fish served as sushi and sashimi, plus seafood small plates and hot-pot dishes; lunchtime sets are a draw, with several set varieties to choose from.

anecdotes.text: Roughly half the room is people eating a quick lunch and half is people unwinding over sashimi and small plates with a beer or sake, which gives the place its relaxed all-day rhythm.
It is the second location of the original Tsuru sushi shop in Tenri, an establishment that has been operating for about forty years.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi house, dishes are served and seasoned with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fresh fish and seafood, and Japanese sides are typically made with fish dashi, so vegan diners face pervasive animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local sushi-and-drinks spot right by the station where a little Japanese helps; you can simply sit at the counter, point to the seasonal fish, and order a lunch set, but expect Japanese-only menus and casual local regulars.

places.name: そば切り 川名 (Sobakiri Kawana)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. While the headline soba is 十割 (100% buckwheat), the menu also offers 二八 (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) noodles and tempura, and standard soba tsuyu dipping sauce typically contains wheat-based soy sauce. There is no documented dedicated buckwheat-only kitchen or allergen protocol, and cross-contamination with wheat is likely in a shared soba kitchen. Celiac diners should not assume this is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Soba tsuyu and broths are made with fish-based dashi (bonito/anchovy), the lunch set includes tempura and anchovy-pepper rice, and signature items include 鴨汁そば (duck-broth soba). Plain zaru soba might be possible if requested without the dashi-based tsuyu, but no vegan options are documented; a vegan would need to ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O.), Dinner approx. 17:15–20:00 (L.O.); closes when the day's soba sells out. Closed Tuesday and Monday evenings; open on public holidays; irregular closures also occur. (A secondary listing gives 11:30–14:00 / 17:30–20:30, closed Mondays — hours vary by source.)
places.neighborhood: 芝辻町（新大宮駅北側）／Shibatsuji-cho, north of Shin-Omiya Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] そば切り 川名 is a small, couple-run soba house tucked a few minutes north of Shin-Omiya Station in Nara's Shibatsuji-cho neighborhood. Behind a quiet beige building, the room is unexpectedly refined — a polished counter and two large shared tables, with jazz playing softly, giving it

chef_bio.origin: 茨城常陸産そば（Ibaraki Hitachi-grown buckwheat）, stone-milled fresh in-house daily.
chef_bio.background: そば切り 川名 is a small, couple-run soba house tucked a few minutes north of Shin-Omiya Station in Nara's Shibatsuji-cho neighborhood. Behind a quiet beige building, the room is unexpectedly refined — a polished counter and two large shared tables, with jazz playing softly, giving it the feel of a calm jazz bar where even solo diners feel at ease.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, full soba experience: the shop encourages lingering over sobagaki and grilled miso with sake before closing the meal with a bowl of freshly cut noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut buckwheat noodles made from Ibaraki Hitachi-aki-soba buckwheat that is stone-ground and milled in-house, served as thin noodles with a firm, satisfying chew. Diners can choose 二八 (80% buckwheat) or upgrade to 十割 (100% buckwheat) for a small surcharge. The signature lunch set pairs the soba with tempura, soba tofu, salad, pickles and anchovy-pepper rice, finishing with a dessert of soba shiratama dumplings and red beans. Other favorites include the spicy grated-radish soba and the duck-broth soba.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat is stone-ground and hand-milled fresh in-house daily from Ibaraki Hitachi-aki-soba, then hand-cut into thin, strongly chewy noodles offered in both 8:2 and 100% buckwheat ratios.
The room is described as having a refined counter and two large tables with jazz BGM, an elegant hideaway atmosphere just a couple of minutes' walk north of Shin-Omiya Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu offers both 十割 (100% buckwheat) and 二八 (20% wheat) noodles from the same kitchen, plus tempura, so wheat is present and cross-contamination is likely. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Lunch set and broths use fish dashi and include tempura, anchovy-pepper rice, and duck-broth soba; no documented vegan-safe preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, calm couple-run soba spot with a refined jazz-bar atmosphere; menus and service are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help, but ordering set lunches is straightforward.

places.name: 明倫館 (Meirinkan)
places.gf_detail: A classic yoshoku menu centered on hamburger, demi-glace, hayashi rice, doria/gratin, fried cutlets, and curry roux, all of which typically contain wheat (flour-based sauces, breading, and roux). There is no gluten-free program documented and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused Western diner whose signature dishes are black wagyu steak, hamburger, hayashi rice, and curry, all built around beef, dairy, and likely meat-based stocks. No vegan offerings are documented and the cuisine is not suited to plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Official site: Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00. Closed Mondays (open if a national holiday, closed the following day).
places.neighborhood: Minami-Kyobate / southern Naramachi, near Kyobate Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Meirinkan opened in 1987, starting life as a mountain-lodge-style coffee shop before growing into the Western-cuisine restaurant it is today. The owner-chef took the name from the Meirinkan, the historic domain school of the old Hagi clan associated with figures like Yoshida Shoi

chef_bio.background: Meirinkan opened in 1987, starting life as a mountain-lodge-style coffee shop before growing into the Western-cuisine restaurant it is today. The owner-chef took the name from the Meirinkan, the historic domain school of the old Hagi clan associated with figures like Yoshida Shoin and Katsura Kogoro, and built a school-like Western-style building to suit it, with high ceilings and soft natural light.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen prepares each dish carefully by hand, using no frozen foods, no retort/ready-made foods, and no chemical seasonings.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cooked yoshoku: black wagyu loin steak (the centerpiece of the Meirinkan course), the broadly loved black-beef hamburger with a choice of demi-glace, white, or tartare sauce, plus hayashi rice, pilaf, curry, doria, and sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name was borrowed from the Meirinkan domain school of the former Hagi clan, tied to historical figures such as Yoshida Shoin and Katsura Kogoro; what began in 1987 as a rustic lodge-style cafe was rebuilt as a school-style Western building to live up to that name.
The owner-chef serves the black wagyu loin steak as the main of the Meirinkan course, while the tender, juicy black-beef hamburger is a favorite across all ages.

positives.text: The kitchen states it uses no frozen foods, no retort/ready-made foods, and no chemical seasonings, preparing each dish carefully by hand.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running family Western-food restaurant aimed at locals; menus and service are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student order comfortably.

places.name: 洋食 ふじい (Yoshoku Fujii)
places.gf_detail: This is a yoshoku (Western-style) diner built around wheat-heavy dishes: breaded fried cutlets (tonkatsu, chicken/shrimp fry), hamburg steak with mushroom or demi-glace-style sauce, and omurice. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy handling is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish-centered Western diner — signature items are pork cutlets, hamburg steak, chicken sauté, omurice (egg/chicken) and seasonal fish. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and dishes rely on egg, dairy, meat and likely fish/meat-based stocks. Not a vegan-friendly option.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 18:00-21:00; closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Sugichō, near JR Nara Station (approx. 382m)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run Western-style eatery tucked into the Sugichō neighborhood a short walk from JR Nara Station. Despite the Western menu, the dining room keeps a homey, distinctly Japanese touch — a floor/tatami-style cushion seating area that makes it comfortable for fam

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run Western-style eatery tucked into the Sugichō neighborhood a short walk from JR Nara Station. Despite the Western menu, the dining room keeps a homey, distinctly Japanese touch — a floor/tatami-style cushion seating area that makes it comfortable for families rather than a stiff formal restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Approachable Western cooking at neighborhood prices — generous lunch sets in the affordable range and a varied menu meant for both a quick midday meal and a relaxed dinner.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty, affordable yoshoku: a hamburg steak you can have with Japanese-style or mushroom sauce, omurice, and crisp tonkatsu made with Yamato pork loin, plus a rotating roster of daily lunch specials such as shrimp fry with tartar sauce and chicken cutlet in tomato sauce, each served with salad, rice and soup. Evenings add small plates like potato-and-bacon galette and garlic-olive-oil octopus.

anecdotes.text: The tonkatsu is praised for its crispy coating and tender meat, made with locally raised Yamato pork; omurice and chicken doria appear only on weekends.
