Unusually for a Western restaurant, it offers floor-style tatami seating with cushions, giving it a family-friendly, casual feel.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded, deep-fried cutlets, hamburg steak and omurice; no separate fryer or gluten-free preparation is documented, so heavy wheat cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is centered on meat and fish; shared surfaces and likely meat/fish-based stocks make this unsuitable for strict vegans, and no plant-based dishes are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood spot a few minutes from JR Nara Station; an English menu is not documented, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, but the casual family-friendly atmosphere makes it approachable.

places.name: サルマメ咖喱 (Sarumame Curry)
places.gf_detail: A notable plus for gluten-sensitive diners: the kitchen states it does not use wheat flour (小麦粉不使用) or chemical seasonings, building its curries on dashi, salt and fermentation instead. However, this is a small shop with no documented dedicated gluten-free protocol or allergen labeling, and fermented/seasoning ingredients (e.g. sake lees, soy-based condiments, dashi) may still carry gluten or cross-contact. Not verified celiac-safe — coeliacs should ask directly about each day's ingredients.
places.vegan_detail: There is a permanent bean curry (豆カレー), but the rotating weekly menu is largely non-vegan — documented options include chicken-and-sake-lees curry, squid/octopus curry, and an optional spiced soft-boiled egg, and the cooking is built around dashi (likely fish-based). A vegan could potentially eat the bean curry on its own but should confirm the dashi and accompaniments; the menu is not designed as vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (last order ~14:30); lunch only. Sources vary between 11:00 and 11:30 opening. Irregular days off, with frequent weekday closures — check Instagram before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi, Nara (Horencho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sarumame Curry opened in March 2022 in a renovated old wooden long-house (古民家・長屋) in Nara's Kitamachi district, sharing the building with neighbors like the Asian medicinal-food spot Ranpu (藍布) and Chitehako confectionery. Before settling into this storefront, the operation built

chef_bio.background: Sarumame Curry opened in March 2022 in a renovated old wooden long-house (古民家・長屋) in Nara's Kitamachi district, sharing the building with neighbors like the Asian medicinal-food spot Ranpu (藍布) and Chitehako confectionery. Before settling into this storefront, the operation built a devoted following through pop-up events and rented-kitchen (間借り) appearances, and it is run as a small husband-and-wife operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is 'おいしくて、うれしくて、ちからになる' (delicious, joyful, and strengthening). The cooking deliberately avoids wheat flour and chemical seasonings, instead drawing depth from good dashi, good salt and fermentation combined with the goodness of spices — food meant to be gentle on the body while still full-flavored.
chef_bio.specialty: Weekly-rotating spice curry plates served as a one-, two-, or three-curry 'aigake' combination, with a permanent bean curry alongside changing options such as chicken-and-sake-lees curry, squid/octopus spicy curry, and sides like potato poriyal, sansho leaf, sprouts and tomato-coconut salad, plus an optional spiced soft-boiled egg.

anecdotes.text: The eatery grew out of a popular pop-up and rented-kitchen curry venture before opening its permanent home in the Kitamachi long-house in March 2022.
Diners take off their shoes and settle onto floor cushions at low tables in the relaxed, homey townhouse interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small home-style kitchen with no documented dedicated gluten-free preparation or allergen separation; even though no wheat flour is used in the curries, fermented and seasoning ingredients may still introduce gluten or cross-contact, so coeliacs should ask about each day's specific dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curries are built on dashi and the rotating menu features chicken, seafood and egg, so vegan diners cannot assume shared pots or stocks are plant-based — confirm the bean curry and its dashi before ordering.

positives.text: The kitchen explicitly does not use wheat flour or chemical seasonings, relying instead on dashi, salt, fermentation and spices — a meaningful starting point for gluten-sensitive diners (though not a verified celiac-safe environment).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual old-house spot where you remove your shoes and sit on floor cushions; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way, and checking their Instagram before you go is wise since opening days are irregular.

places.name: 奈良 而今 (Nara Nikon)
places.gf_detail: A traditional Japanese kaiseki / omakase house. Courses rely heavily on fish dashi and soy sauce, both of which typically contain wheat, so this is not a celiac-safe destination. No dedicated gluten-free program is documented; a celiac diner would need to discuss needs in detail (in Japanese) well before the visit, with no guarantee.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-forward kaiseki restaurant (fish shipped directly from Akashi) where dishes are built on fish dashi, so it is not vegan-oriented. As a fully reservation-based omakase, the chef may be able to accommodate dietary requests if arranged far in advance, but nothing vegan is documented and it should not be relied upon.
places.hours_raw: Tue/Thu/Fri 18:00-22:00; Wed/Sat 12:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00; Mon & Sun closed. Completely reservation-only.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約4分）
places.notes: A Nara native, chef Shimizu Shojiro trained at the historic Nara ryotei Kikusuiro and at the Kyoto kaiseki restaurant Gion Nishikawa before opening his own counter-centered hideaway in the Naramachi area in 2016.

chef_bio.background: A Nara native, chef Shimizu Shojiro trained at the historic Nara ryotei Kikusuiro and at the Kyoto kaiseki restaurant Gion Nishikawa before opening his own counter-centered hideaway in the Naramachi area in 2016.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 而今 (Nikon) comes from Zen Buddhist language meaning to treasure the present moment; the chef chose it as a positive name reflecting his wish to honor each dining occasion as it happens.
chef_bio.specialty: Monthly-changing omakase kaiseki built on seasonal seafood (fish shipped directly from Akashi) and seasonal vegetables, prepared with classic technique and a personal, creative twist.

anecdotes.text: Rather than following tradition rigidly, Shimizu applies what he calls a change-up: for his sweetfish (ayu) dish he leaves the crisped scales on the outside instead of concealing them, and he chooses specific daikon varieties that hold up well.
He has rethought the conventional caution against pairing strongly flavored sake with refined cuisine, finding that thoughtfully matched they need not overshadow one another.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kaiseki kitchen, dishes rely on soy sauce and fish dashi, which conventionally contain wheat; no gluten-free sourcing is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is seafood-forward with fish dashi as a base, so vegan diners cannot assume any course is free of animal products.

positives.text: Completely reservation-only omakase format means the chef may be able to tailor a course to discussed dietary needs if arranged well in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: An intimate, reservation-only counter kaiseki house where some Japanese ability (or a Japanese-speaking companion) will make booking and the omakase experience far smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 喫茶バオバブ (Kissa Baobab)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The menu centers on hotcakes and French toast, which are wheat-based, in a small shared kitchen. The okara cake replaces wheat flour with okara (soybean pulp), but okara contains soy and there is no documentation of gluten-free handling or cross-contamination control, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. It is a traditional kissaten serving hotcakes, French toast, coffee and tea, which typically involve eggs and dairy. One signature item, an "okara cake," swaps wheat flour for okara (soybean pulp), but there is no documentation that it is egg- or dairy-free, so anything would need to be confirmed in person.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi (north side of Kintetsu Nara Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A warm, low-key Japanese coffee shop tucked into the Imanishi Building on the north (Kitamachi) side of Kintetsu Nara Station, an area known for quiet, independent cafes.

chef_bio.background: A warm, low-key Japanese coffee shop tucked into the Imanishi Building on the north (Kitamachi) side of Kintetsu Nara Station, an area known for quiet, independent cafes.
chef_bio.philosophy: A place built around an unhurried, leisurely pace — somewhere visitors can settle in and spend time as they wish over carefully made drinks and desserts.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade sweets paired with specialty coffee or fragrant tea — including heart-shaped plain hotcakes served as a coffee set, caramelized French toast, and a distinctive okara cake that uses soybean pulp in place of wheat flour, offered as part of an afternoon snack set.

anecdotes.text: Featured in a Nara Kitamachi guide to cozy cafes worth visiting for a coffee break, described as a warm-atmosphere spot with wooden tables and gentle-tasting handmade sweets.
Its okara cake is made with okara (soybean pulp) instead of wheat flour and is served on an afternoon snack set with the day's sweet plus coffee or tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based hotcakes and French toast are core menu items prepared in the same small kitchen, so gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Typical kissaten desserts here (hotcakes, French toast) generally use eggs and dairy; no animal-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: An okara cake uses soybean pulp instead of wheat flour, which may interest those reducing wheat — though it is not verified as gluten-free or vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood Japanese coffee shop with no documented English support, so a foreign student is best going with a guide or some basic Japanese and an easygoing, quiet manner.

places.name: 珈琲風雅 ぷろばんす (Coffee Fuga Provence (Purobansu))
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The kitchen's signature items are built on wheat: house-made bread, French toast, sandwiches, spaghetti in the lunch set, and homemade cakes. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented. A celiac traveler could in principle order black coffee only, but cross-contamination from a wheat-heavy bakery kitchen is unaddressed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. This is a coffee-and-cake café; the food (French toast, sandwiches, cakes, bread, lunch plate with side dishes and spaghetti) typically involves dairy, eggs and animal products, and Japanese café side dishes commonly use fish dashi. Black coffee and straight/blend coffees are plant-based, but anything beyond plain coffee should be confirmed with staff. Treat as ask-only for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 8:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); some listings note 8:00 opening. Closures irregular.
places.neighborhood: Kashiwagi-cho, Nara (north-west of Kintetsu Nara Station, ou
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Provence (ぷろばんす) is a long-standing, owner-run garden coffee house on the outer edge of Nara's Kitamachi area, in Kashiwagi-cho. The shop pairs European café culture with Japanese aesthetics: guests sip coffee while looking onto a hand-built Japanese garden planted with seasonal

chef_bio.background: Provence (ぷろばんす) is a long-standing, owner-run garden coffee house on the outer edge of Nara's Kitamachi area, in Kashiwagi-cho. The shop pairs European café culture with Japanese aesthetics: guests sip coffee while looking onto a hand-built Japanese garden planted with seasonal trees such as crape myrtle and maple.
chef_bio.philosophy: A lifestyle-oriented café built around a relaxed, stress-free atmosphere where Western and Japanese elements harmonize, with handmade food and carefully extracted coffee at the center.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is coffee drawn with a 'light siphon' that uses a halogen lamp as the heat source instead of an alcohol flame or gas burner; the steady heat is said to produce a smooth, mellow cup. The owner curates a deep coffee list of more than 20 to 40 beans, including house blends and a lineup of straight coffees marketed under poetic names (e.g. a '六歌仙' blend and a '十二珈撰' straight-coffee selection). On the food side, everything is made in-house: bread, French toast, sandwiches and cakes, plus a daily lunch plate.

anecdotes.text: The shop brews with a 'light siphon' that swaps the usual alcohol or gas flame for a halogen lamp, giving steadier heat and a rounder flavor.
Coffee is poured against the backdrop of a hand-made Japanese garden with seasonal plantings like crape myrtle and maple, and all bread and cakes are made by the owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on house-baked bread, French toast, sandwiches, cakes and a spaghetti-inclusive lunch plate, so wheat is pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed local garden kissaten where a little Japanese and a calm, unhurried manner go a long way; English menus or service should not be expected.

places.name: にこちゃん堂 (Nicochan-do)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no gluten-free program is documented. Rice (ancient/red rice, brown rice) is a staple here, which is naturally GF, but the side dishes and home-style seasonings almost certainly involve wheat-based soy sauce and other wheat-containing condiments, and the all-homemade sweets are not stated to be flour-free. There is no information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control, so a celiac diner cannot assume safety and would need to ask in detail.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is strongly vegetable-centered and a vegetarian menu is noted, with plates built around seasonal root and field vegetables served over ancient/red rice or brown rice. However the menu is not exclusively meat-free (a reviewer described a vegetable-heavy plate served with a hamburger steak), and as a typical Japanese home-style café the seasoned dishes very likely rely on fish dashi, soy sauce, and other animal-derived seasonings. A strict vegan should ask which specific dishes can be prepared without dashi, meat, or dairy before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Lunch & café 11:00-19:00; occasional irregular night service (announced via SNS). Closed Tuesdays. (Tabelog lists 11:00-22:00 with evening hours subject to change; official site lists day service to 19:00.)
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Tsubaichō), west side of Mochiidono Center-gai, n
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nicochan-do opened in 2006 inside a roughly 160-year-old Meiji-era rice warehouse in the heart of Naramachi, just south of Kintetsu Nara Station. The old wooden storehouse was converted into a rambling one-house café, with a loft, a gallery, a studio, and a terrace tucked into it

chef_bio.background: Nicochan-do opened in 2006 inside a roughly 160-year-old Meiji-era rice warehouse in the heart of Naramachi, just south of Kintetsu Nara Station. The old wooden storehouse was converted into a rambling one-house café, with a loft, a gallery, a studio, and a terrace tucked into its multiple levels, and a big elephant painted on the tin roof as its landmark.
chef_bio.philosophy: A healthy, well-balanced, vegetable-centered table in a retro, lived-in setting — sweets are all made in-house with restrained sweetness, and the space doubles as a gallery and event venue, welcoming families with nursing and changing facilities so guests feel as if they are relaxing at a friend's home.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward lunch plates piled with seasonal root and field vegetables and served over ancient red rice or brown rice, alongside entirely homemade, lightly sweetened original sweets such as ice-cream parfaits, and specialty coffee roasted at Nara's Ikoma Eleven roastery.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies a roughly 160-year-old rice warehouse from the Meiji era, with a large elephant painted on its tin roof serving as the building's landmark.
Lunch centers on a plate generously loaded with seasonal root and field vegetables, with coffee from Nara's Ikoma Eleven roastery and all sweets made in-house, lightly sweetened and original.
A reviewer described the meal as a vegetable-heavy salad with ancient rice, in one case served alongside a hamburger steak, and the staff as friendly and pleasant.

positives.text: The kitchen is built around vegetables and seasonal produce, and a vegetarian menu is available, so plant-leaning diners have real choices.
Ancient red rice and brown rice are used as the standard staple, which are naturally gluten-free bases.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-and-solo-friendly neighborhood café where staff are described as warm and easygoing, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably even if no English is offered.

places.name: くるみの木 一条店 (Kuruminoki Ichijo-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is not a gluten-free or celiac-safe restaurant. The kitchen makes no gluten-free claims and there is no allergen labeling. The signature seasonal lunch and menu include clearly wheat-based items (udon, toast, cakes/tarts, doria with white sauce, and wheat gluten "fu" in the miso soup), and Japanese home-style cooking here relies on soy sauce and likely fish dashi. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish is safe; no dedicated GF prep is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant, though it is a vegetable-forward, health-minded kitchen using lots of seasonal Nara/Yamato vegetables. The rotating lunch is built around vegetable side dishes (おばんざい) but typically includes a meat/fish main (e.g., hamburger steak, spring rolls) and uses dashi, soy sauce, and dairy (cheesecake, cream cakes). No vegan menu or labeling is published. A vegetarian could find some side dishes, but cross-use of fish dashi means even those are not reliably vegan unless confirmed with staff.
places.neighborhood: きたまち・法蓮町（一条通り、近鉄奈良駅北側）
places.cuisine: 町家風カフェ・自然派ランチ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kuruminoki began in 1984 when Yukiko Ishimura, then in her early thirties, opened a tiny twelve-seat café with an attached general-goods shop in a quiet house in northern Nara. The story goes that she first fell for the place a year earlier, drawn in by hydrangeas blooming in the

chef_bio.origin: Founded in Nara; the owner is originally from Takamatsu, Kagawa.
chef_bio.background: Kuruminoki began in 1984 when Yukiko Ishimura, then in her early thirties, opened a tiny twelve-seat café with an attached general-goods shop in a quiet house in northern Nara. The story goes that she first fell for the place a year earlier, drawn in by hydrangeas blooming in the garden of a building beside a railway crossing. The café still occupies that original house, surrounded by greenery, and has kept its first furnishings, including large wooden tables and chairs that once came from a church chapel. As the business grew it opened a second location and the original was renamed the Ichijo branch; today it is widely regarded as a pioneer of the Japanese cafe-plus-lifestyle-shop concept and draws visitors from far beyond Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ishimura describes her approach as proposing a mindful, enriched everyday life; the cooking is meant to feel like wholehearted, health-conscious home cooking made for family.
chef_bio.specialty: A rotating seasonal lunch built around home-style vegetable side dishes (おばんざい) and mixed rice using Nara-area produce — a representative plate has included a tofu-bound hamburger steak with hijiki, yam and carrot, chickpea-and-millet rice, miso soup with wheat gluten, kabocha salad and spring rolls. The café is also loved for homemade cakes and tarts such as a yuzu/citrus lemon rare cheesecake and a pineapple Victoria sponge.

anecdotes.text: Ishimura was drawn to the building in 1983 by hydrangeas blooming in its garden beside a railway crossing, and opened the original 12-seat café there in 1984.
The café still uses its original large wooden tables and chairs that once served in a church chapel, and the atmosphere has been kept largely unchanged for over forty years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu and lunch include multiple wheat items (udon, toast, cakes/tarts, doria) and wheat gluten (fu) in the miso soup, in a shared home-style kitchen; no gluten-free prep is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese home-style おばんざい cooking here relies on standard soy sauce (wheat-containing); no tamari/GF soy is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The seasonal lunch typically includes meat/fish mains and dairy desserts, and Japanese side dishes commonly use fish dashi, so vegetable dishes are not reliably vegan.

positives.text: The kitchen is genuinely vegetable-forward and health-minded, using ample seasonal Nara/Yamato vegetables, so vegetarians can usually find vegetable side dishes to enjoy.

cultural_comfort.note: A very popular sit-down café where seating is first-come walk-in order (sign-in from 10:30) and the lunch is a fixed seasonal set with no English menu or dietary labeling, so a foreign student with allergies should ideally come with a Japanese-speaking friend or be ready to ask carefully about ingredients.

places.name: cafe 卑弥呼 (cafe Himiko)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program found. This is a Japanese home-style café whose signature plates lean on cutlets/hamburger, pasta, soy-based seasonings and dashi-flavored sides (e.g., dashi-flavored konnyaku, sake-lees soup), so wheat and barley-derived soy sauce are routine. Nothing on the official site or listings indicates celiac-safe handling, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce. Not suitable for strict gluten-free/celiac diners without direct confirmation in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The kitchen emphasizes vegetables and "no chemical seasonings," following the マゴワヤサシイ balance (which includes fish), and the signature Himiko-gohan typically features meat (pork/chicken cutlets) and fish/dashi-based sides. There is no documented vegan menu; a plant-based diner would need to ask in advance whether any meat-, fish-, or dashi-free plate can be arranged.
places.hours_raw: 10:00～20:00（ラストオーダー19:30）。予約や希望があれば22:00まで営業。月曜・火曜定休。
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅／やすらぎの道沿い（徒歩約8分）, 法蓮町
places.cuisine: 夫婦経営のダイニングカフェ。化学調味料不使用・野菜多めの手作り健康ごはん
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in December 2021 along Yasuragi-no-Michi, a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, this cozy dining café is run by a husband-and-wife team, the Fukudas. The room is filled with personal touches — handcrafted cut-glass pieces drawing on Mrs. Fukuda's family background in gl

chef_bio.background: Opened in December 2021 along Yasuragi-no-Michi, a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, this cozy dining café is run by a husband-and-wife team, the Fukudas. The room is filled with personal touches — handcrafted cut-glass pieces drawing on Mrs. Fukuda's family background in glasswork, and a treasured Hakata doll of the ancient queen Himiko after whom the café is named.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wholesome, health-minded home cooking: no chemical seasonings, plenty of vegetables, and a kitchen where everything from the pre-meal vinegar to the soups and dressings is made in-house, guided by the マゴワヤサシイ balance of beans, seeds, seaweed, vegetables, fish, mushrooms and root vegetables.
chef_bio.specialty: The daily-changing Himiko-gohan set is the heart of the menu: a main such as pork-and-chicken cutlet alongside several rotating vegetable side dishes, soup, salad with homemade carrot dressing, and locally grown Hinohikari multigrain rice. Other regulars include a homemade-sauce hamburger lunch, pasta, and Thai-style gapao rice.

anecdotes.text: The café takes its name from the legendary queen Himiko; the owner, drawn to a Hakata doll of Himiko and even told in a fortune-telling that she was Himiko's reincarnation, bought the doll and displays it in the shop.
The owner says the greatest joy is 'seeing customers smile after their first bite' — お客さんが一口食べて『おいしい！』と喜んでくれる姿を見るのがうれしい.
Small homemade details define the food — the chicken uses jam as a secret tenderizer, and the sake-lees soup uses lees from Toyosawa Brewery with red-yuzu miso.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu relies on Japanese seasonings and dashi-flavored items (e.g., dashi-flavored Ōmi red konnyaku, sake-lees soup), implying standard wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable-forward but not vegan: signature plates feature meat and fish/dashi sides, with no documented separation for plant-based diners.

positives.text: Everything from the pre-meal vinegar to soups and dressings is made in-house and the kitchen uses no chemical seasonings, so ingredients are largely from scratch — helpful for asking about specific items.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, small couple-run neighborhood café where staff and menus are Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: アストラルレイコーヒー (Astral Ray Coffee)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The food menu centers on house-made bread, toast, financiers, apple pie and other wheat-based baked goods, so it is not a celiac-safe destination. Black coffee and plain lattes are naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF preparation or cross-contamination controls are described.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Drip coffee and black coffee are vegan; the many latte options and house sweets/breads typically use dairy and eggs. A non-dairy milk substitution may be possible but is not confirmed, so a vegan visitor would need to ask.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-20:00; closed Thursdays and the 1st & 3rd Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Hōren-chō, along Ichijō-dōri in the Kitamachi area north of
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Astral Ray Coffee was opened on June 20, 2022 by two women who had both come from careers in education and childcare: one was a former high school teacher and the other had worked in early-childhood care. Drawing on their experience nurturing others, they set out to build a small

chef_bio.background: Astral Ray Coffee was opened on June 20, 2022 by two women who had both come from careers in education and childcare: one was a former high school teacher and the other had worked in early-childhood care. Drawing on their experience nurturing others, they set out to build a small, warm coffee stand that could serve as a gathering place in the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name comes from 'Astral' (of the stars) and 'Ray' (light): the owners believe that each person shines on their own, and that connecting those individual lights forms something larger, like a constellation, expanding everyone's possibilities. The shop is designed as a place where customers can simply be themselves (自分らしくいられる).
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee with five or more bean varieties on hand at any time, roasted and ground to suit each customer; alongside drip coffee they offer a wide range of lattes, their signature Astral Blend, and house-made sweets and specially developed bread such as financiers, apple pie, flan and toast. Everything is available for takeout.

anecdotes.text: The two owners left careers in high-school teaching and childcare to create what they describe as a 'place in the town' (まちの居場所) where anyone can feel at ease.
The café was conceived around the idea of 'being able to be yourself,' with an open, accessible space welcoming families and solo visitors alike.

positives.text: All coffee and food items are available for takeout, which can help a traveler with dietary restrictions stick to simple, naturally compatible drinks like black drip coffee.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, welcoming neighborhood coffee stand run by two friendly owners where a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; the menu is simple coffee-and-sweets so ordering is easy even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 珈琲豆蔵 (Kohi Mamekura)
places.gf_detail: Black coffee is naturally gluten-free, but this is not a gluten-free establishment. Food offerings center on pizza toast and cookies (wheat), so there is no celiac-safe kitchen and no documented gluten-free handling. Coffee should be fine; avoid the food unless you can confirm.
places.vegan_detail: Primarily a coffee shop; black coffee and single-origin pour-overs are naturally plant-based, but food items (pizza toast, cookies, hot chocolate, coffee jelly with vanilla) and milk-based drinks are not vegan. No documented vegan menu, so vegans should ask about ingredients and stick to black coffee.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-19:00, closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi / Horen-cho, north of Kintetsu Nara Station
places.cuisine: Owner-run self-roasted coffee shop and quiet
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by a proprietor who left a corporate career to open a coffee shop, choosing to focus on craft and quality over convenience.

chef_bio.origin: Horen-cho, on the north side of central Nara near Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.background: The shop is run by a proprietor who left a corporate career to open a coffee shop, choosing to focus on craft and quality over convenience.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality over convenience: meticulous bean-by-bean hand selection, roasting tailored to each bean, and brewing one cup at a time to draw out a deep, nuanced flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted, hand-picked coffee. The owner painstakingly hand-picks (ハンドピック) the green beans to remove defective ones, then roasts each origin to suit its character and brews each cup to order. Signature pours include the house Mamekura Blend (balanced acidity and depth) and the lighter Shin-Mamekura Blend, plus single origins like Mandheling Sinar and Tanzania (with cassis-like fruity sweetness). Light fare such as pizza toast and a coffee jelly with vanilla and chocolate chips round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: A career-changer who left corporate work opened the shop out of a commitment to doing coffee right, hand-picking beans individually before roasting.
A second cup of blend coffee is offered at half price, encouraging guests to linger in the calm, wood-floored room.

positives.text: Black coffee, single-origin pour-overs, and roasted beans are naturally free of gluten and animal products, so a plain coffee is a safe choice for most travelers.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, owner-run neighborhood coffee shop where a little basic Japanese will help you order, though pointing at the bean list and menu works fine for a relaxed solo coffee.

places.name: やさいパンのみせ まちのカフェVIVO (Yasai Pan no Mise Machi no Cafe VIVO)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery cafe whose entire concept is bread and sandwiches, so it is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners. No gluten-free bread or menu is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A few plant-leaning items exist — notably the "VIVOバーガー" with a teriyaki-flavored tofu patty and plenty of vegetables, and breads with vegetable paste kneaded in. However most sandwiches use egg (from a named farm) and meats like chicken, and there is no documented vegan menu, vegan labeling, or confirmation that breads/sauces are free of egg, dairy, or honey. A vegan diner would need to ask item-by-item.
places.hours_raw: 月～金 10:30～17:00（売り切れ次第終了の場合あり）／土・日・祝 定休
places.neighborhood: きたまち寄り・法蓮町（近鉄新大宮駅北側、国道24号沿い）
places.cuisine: 野菜パンのベーカリーカフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] VIVO is a small bakery cafe run by a Nara nonprofit, NPO Msねっと, at its base in Horen-cho just off Route 24, a short walk north of Kintetsu Shin-Omiya Station. The cafe and its companion bakery double as a community gathering spot and a workplace where people of different ages and

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Horen-cho, near Shin-Omiya), opened by NPO Msねっと.
chef_bio.background: VIVO is a small bakery cafe run by a Nara nonprofit, NPO Msねっと, at its base in Horen-cho just off Route 24, a short walk north of Kintetsu Shin-Omiya Station. The cafe and its companion bakery double as a community gathering spot and a workplace where people of different ages and abilities can find a role, reflecting the group's mission of helping diverse people live authentically within the neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: Body-friendly, handmade food in a bright, casual room with colorful furniture and houseplants — a place meant to feel welcoming and to connect people in the community.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable breads made with vegetable paste kneaded into the dough, fresh-baked anpan and rolls, and made-to-order sandwiches. A popular pick is the steamed-chicken, kinpira and keema-curry sandwich; the signature VIVO burger uses a teriyaki tofu patty piled with vegetables. Coffee is sourced locally.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is operated by NPO Msねっと as part of its child-rearing support and employment-support work, so eating here also supports an inclusive community project.
Vegetable sandwiches use bread with vegetable paste kneaded in, the egg sandwich uses eggs from a named farm, and the VIVO burger features a teriyaki-flavored tofu patty with lots of vegetables.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working wheat-flour bakery: bread and sandwiches are the core product, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively certain. Not suitable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg- and meat-based sandwiches (e.g. egg sandwich, steamed-chicken sandwich) are prepared in the same small kitchen as the tofu-patty and vegetable items, with no documented separation.

positives.text: Emphasis on body-friendly, handmade food using vegetable-paste breads and a tofu patty, and a few plant-leaning options exist for those who can ask in person.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, warm neighborhood cafe run by a local nonprofit — staff likely speak mainly Japanese, but ordering counter-style bread and sandwiches is easy and pointing works fine for a foreign student.

places.name: 喫茶 佐保 (Kissa Saho)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop centered on toast/sandwich morning sets, fried-rice set meals and coffee. Bread, sandwiches and fried rice with seasonings all involve wheat, and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The owner grows pesticide-free vegetables and fruit in the backyard, so produce is fresh, but the menu is a standard kissaten lineup (morning toast sets, sandwiches with egg, yakitori fried rice, coffee). Most dishes involve egg, meat or dairy and there is no documented vegan menu. A plant-only meal would have to be requested directly and is not guaranteed.
places.hours_raw: 8:00-17:00 (morning service until 11:30, lunch from 11:30). Closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Horen-cho (Kitamachi area, along the Saho River, north of Ki
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Saho is a retro coffee shop that has been part of the Horen-cho neighborhood along Route 24, just east of Ichijo High School in northern Nara, for more than 40 years. It is a genuine old-school Showa-era kissaten that has resisted price increases and still serves much of it

chef_bio.background: Kissa Saho is a retro coffee shop that has been part of the Horen-cho neighborhood along Route 24, just east of Ichijo High School in northern Nara, for more than 40 years. It is a genuine old-school Showa-era kissaten that has resisted price increases and still serves much of its menu at remarkably low prices.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeping a neighborhood gathering place affordable and welcoming, holding prices down by growing produce in-house.
chef_bio.specialty: Inexpensive morning service (around 400 yen), a rotating selection of home-style set meals such as yakitori fried rice (around 600-700 yen), and coffee for about 350 yen. The kitchen leans on pesticide-free vegetables and fruit the owner grows in the backyard garden, which keeps prices low and the cooking fresh; surplus garden vegetables are sometimes sold to customers.

anecdotes.text: The owner cultivates pesticide-free vegetables and fruit in the back garden, and this home-grown produce is the cornerstone that lets the cafe keep its whole menu unusually cheap even after 42 years in business.
Regulars treat it like a living room, with solo customers settling in to read through the shop's comic-book collection while groups chat over coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around bread, sandwiches and fried rice; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard kissaten dishes use egg, meat and dairy; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Vegetables and fruit are grown pesticide-free in the owner's own backyard garden, so produce is fresh and locally sourced.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, deeply local Showa-era coffee shop run by its owner for decades; there is no English support and regulars set the relaxed, neighborhood tone, so a foreign student would feel most comfortable visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or someone familiar with kissaten etiquette.

places.name: そば処 南郷 (Soba-dokoro Nango)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The soba is made with an 8:2 wheat-to-buckwheat (so-called nihachi) ratio, so the noodles contain a large proportion of wheat flour. Tempura is wheat-battered and soba is served with a wheat-soy-based broth. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The kitchen centers on soba served with fish-based dashi broth, plus signature eel (unagi) dishes and tempura that may include animal-derived items; no documented vegan menu. A diner could ask about a plain zaru soba but the noodles are wheat-heavy and the broth is fish-based.
places.hours_raw: 11:30～15:00 (lunch only; no dinner service)
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho (Naramachi east / Gangoji / Kyobate Station a
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A handmade soba shop that opened in 2021 in Nara's quiet Takabatake neighborhood, just south of Nara Park near the Fukuchiin area. It occupies a small space arranged with a little waiting nook at the entrance and a bamboo-blind-screened counter dining area, giving it the feel of

chef_bio.background: A handmade soba shop that opened in 2021 in Nara's quiet Takabatake neighborhood, just south of Nara Park near the Fukuchiin area. It occupies a small space arranged with a little waiting nook at the entrance and a bamboo-blind-screened counter dining area, giving it the feel of a tucked-away hideaway.
chef_bio.philosophy: Run quietly by a husband-and-wife team in an at-home, unhurried atmosphere focused on a short, carefully made menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba served chilled or hot, the spicy grated-daikon soba (karami daikon soba) and tempura zaru being favorites, alongside the standout unagi seiro rice set in which fluffy steamed eel is layered over sauce-soaked rice and served with a small portion of soba. A small selection of finishing salts (such as seaweed, yuzu, and matcha) is offered alongside the broth.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 2021 as a small handmade soba shop in the Takabatake district south of Nara Park.
The unagi seiro rice, a dish in the style of a Fukuoka regional specialty, is regarded as a signature item served alongside the soba.
Diners are offered table-side finishing salts such as seaweed, yuzu, and matcha to vary the flavor of the broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles are made with an 8:2 wheat-to-buckwheat blend, so they are not gluten-free; tempura is wheat-battered and prepared in the same small kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional dashi-and-soy broth, which is typically wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is fish-based dashi and the kitchen centers on eel and tempura, so vegan cross-contact is likely; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, calm counter-only soba shop run by a couple where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the simple menu) will go smoothly; little to no English is documented, so a few words of Japanese help.

places.name: 衣掛茶屋 (Kinugake Chaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is wheat-based udon and soba (Japanese soba is typically blended with wheat flour), and broths use standard fish/soy-based dashi. No gluten-free accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature dishes include meat udon and meat soba, and noodle broths are made with fish dashi. Kuzu mochi and some drinks may be plant-based but are served at a shop where fish stock and meat are standard; no vegan menu is documented.
places.neighborhood: Sarusawa Pond, central Nara (near Kofuku-ji / Kintetsu Nara
places.cuisine: Kissaten / soba & udon / sweets tea house
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kinugake Chaya is a tea house that has stood for over a hundred years at the foot of the fifty-two stone steps descending toward Sarusawa Pond, just below Kofuku-ji. It appears in old picture postcards of Nara and has served visitors continuously through generations, blending int

chef_bio.background: Kinugake Chaya is a tea house that has stood for over a hundred years at the foot of the fifty-two stone steps descending toward Sarusawa Pond, just below Kofuku-ji. It appears in old picture postcards of Nara and has served visitors continuously through generations, blending into the historic pondside landscape with its traditional architecture, coffered ceiling, and vintage furnishings. From the window seats, light reflecting off the pond's surface dances across the ceiling, and across the water you can see the five-story pagoda of Kofuku-ji and Mt. Wakakusa.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking aims for honest, accessible, everyday flavors rather than refined technique — a relaxed stop to rest beside the pond.
chef_bio.specialty: Simple, comforting plates of warm meat udon and grated-radish meat soba (around 700 yen), alongside cold noodles, ramune soda, sweet amazake, kuzu mochi and warabi mochi. The shop also sells deer crackers for the resident deer.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits at the base of the well-known fifty-two stone steps leading down to Sarusawa Pond, with seating that extends toward the water's edge and views of the Kofuku-ji pagoda and Mt. Wakakusa across the pond.
It has operated for more than a century and appears in old postcards of Nara, retaining vintage interior details like a coffered ceiling.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on wheat udon and soba cooked in shared water; high gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodle broths and seasonings rely on standard fish/soy-based dashi, which typically contains wheat-derived soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and fish dashi are used in the signature noodle dishes, served from the same kitchen as any plant-based sweets.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, century-old pondside tea house where you order simple noodles and sweets at the counter; little to no English, but pointing and basic Japanese will get you by.

places.name: vegan soba 楽夢咲 (Vegan Soba Ramusa)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The shop offers handmade soba as either 100% buckwheat (十割) or an 80/20 buckwheat-wheat blend (二八), and tempura is part of the standard set, so wheat is present in the kitchen. No gluten-free or celiac-safety claims are documented, and there is no mention of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Travelers with celiac disease should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. The shop is explicitly a vegan soba restaurant, run by the owner of a weekday vegan cafe (楽夢菜). The signature soba set (¥1,650) is handmade soba served zaru or kake style with a small side dish, pickles, vegetable tempura, and seasoned rice, all prepared vegan. Note that one source described the rice as recently changed to a "seasoned beef rice" style; given the all-vegan concept this is most likely a plant-based mock-meat preparation rather than actual beef, but the exact rice topping is worth confirming on arrival.
places.hours_raw: 土日 12:00-15:00（ランチ営業のみ、週末のみ営業）
places.neighborhood: 中院町・ならまち
places.cuisine: ヴィーガン蕎麦・プラントベース
places.notes: vegan soba 楽夢咲 opened on May 3, 2025, in a quiet alley one street back from Naramachi's main lane, in a renovated traditional townhouse that fits the neighborhood's old-town aesthetic. It is a one-person weekend project: the same owner runs the weekday vegan cafe 楽夢菜 in Takabatak

chef_bio.background: vegan soba 楽夢咲 opened on May 3, 2025, in a quiet alley one street back from Naramachi's main lane, in a renovated traditional townhouse that fits the neighborhood's old-town aesthetic. It is a one-person weekend project: the same owner runs the weekday vegan cafe 楽夢菜 in Takabatake-cho and devotes Saturdays and Sundays to soba craft here.
chef_bio.philosophy: An entirely plant-based take on traditional soba, made by hand and aimed at both committed vegans and soba lovers.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade soba served as a set meal (そば定食, around ¥1,650): zaru or kake soba made from northern Hokkaido buckwheat flour, with a small side dish, pickles, vegetable tempura, and seasoned rice. The 野菜天ざる蕎麦 (vegetable tempura zaru soba) is a highlight.

anecdotes.text: The owner splits the week in two: running the vegan cafe 楽夢菜 on weekdays and switching to the role of a soba craftsperson on weekends at this shop.
The soba is made from buckwheat flour sourced from northern Hokkaido, with tempura prepared in a light, clean style.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is offered as a 100% buckwheat (十割) or 80/20 buckwheat-wheat (二八) blend, and the set includes wheat-battered vegetable tempura, so wheat is present and cross-contamination is likely.

positives.text: The entire shop is plant-based, so vegan diners can order the full menu without modification.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny weekend-only townhouse soba shop run by one chef; expect a Japanese-language, cash-only setting, so bring cash and a few basic Japanese phrases, though the all-vegan menu makes ordering simple.

places.name: 轉害坊（転害坊） (Tengaibou (Cafe Gallery Tengaibou))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature offering is a New York-style cheesecake, which is wheat-based, and there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation. Drip coffee is naturally gluten-free, but no allergen handling is documented, so this is not a destination for strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. The cafe is known for its NY-style Meyer lemon cheesecake (contains dairy and wheat) paired with freshly roasted coffee. Black coffee itself is naturally vegan, but any plant-milk option is undocumented — ask on arrival. The owner separately runs "raw sweets" (ロースイーツ) classes, suggesting some familiarity with plant-based baking, but this is not confirmed as a regular cafe menu offering.
places.hours_raw: 営業時間 13:00～17:00（金・土・日のみ営業）／定休日 月～木。焙煎体験教室は10:00～12:00・要予約。公式サイトでは「カフェ完全予約制」と案内。
places.neighborhood: 今在家町・きたまち（東大寺転害門近く / near Todaiji Tegaimon, Kitamachi）
places.cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒー・町家カフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 轉害坊（転害坊）occupies a roughly 90-year-old machiya townhouse near Todaiji's Tegaimon gate that was once a long-established Chinese-medicine (kampo) pharmacy dating back generations. The current owner bought the aging building, renovated it, and revived the shop's old name, 転害坊, turni

chef_bio.background: 轉害坊（転害坊）occupies a roughly 90-year-old machiya townhouse near Todaiji's Tegaimon gate that was once a long-established Chinese-medicine (kampo) pharmacy dating back generations. The current owner bought the aging building, renovated it, and revived the shop's old name, 転害坊, turning it into a small cafe and gallery. Antique tools that the former pharmacy actually used are kept on display inside, so guests sip coffee surrounded by the building's apothecary history.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on roasting beans fresh daily and drawing out coffee's flavor, while preserving and showcasing the heritage of the former pharmacy building.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly roasted coffee made from beans roasted that same day (around 550 yen), paired with the house Meyer-lemon New York cheesecake (around 500 yen). The owner hand-roasts high-grade green beans from around the world and also sells original goods.

anecdotes.text: The building was a long-standing kampo (Chinese-medicine) pharmacy from the Edo period; after about 90 years the current owner purchased and renovated it and inherited the former shop name, 転害坊, with the old pharmacy's antique tools still displayed inside.
The owner hand-roasts world-class green coffee beans and runs coffee-roasting workshops where visitors can roast their own beans (10:00-12:00, reservation required).

positives.text: Coffee is roasted in-house from green beans daily and served fresh, so the core drink is a simple single-ingredient preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat owner-run townhouse cafe now operating largely by reservation, so a foreign student should arrange a booking in advance (a Japanese-speaking friend or translation help is useful) and expect a quiet, personal, mostly-Japanese-language setting.

places.name: 御菓子司 萬勝堂 (Okashitsukasa Manshodo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional wagashi shop whose core products are wheat-based: mikasa (a dorayaki-style cake), chestnut manjū, and other baked/steamed confections all contain wheat flour. The signature mitarashi dango is glazed with a soy-sauce-based syrup that typically contains wheat. Some individual items (kudzu noodles/kuzukiri, wasanbon rakugan, azuki-based sweets) are naturally wheat-free, but there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation, no allergen labeling documented, and high risk of cross-contact in a small shared confectionery kitchen. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan offering. Many traditional wagashi here are plant-based by nature (sweet azuki bean paste, kudzu, wasanbon sugar, rice flour, mitarashi dango), which can suit a flexible plant-based diet. However the shop's signature summer kakigōri includes a dairy item (大和茶ミルク金時 / Yamato-tea milk), and there is no vegan menu, labeling, or confirmation that bean pastes/sweets avoid all animal ingredients. A strict vegan should ask item by item before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (kakigōri/shaved ice served until 17:00). Open year-round (年中無休). Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00; official site lists 10:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki-naka-machi / Higashimuki Shopping Street, near K
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Manshodo is a long-standing Nara confectionery founded in 1890 (Meiji 23), and has spent more than 130 years preserving the city's tradition of fine Japanese sweets. It sits just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station along the lively Higashimuki shopping street, where it has become a

chef_bio.background: Manshodo is a long-standing Nara confectionery founded in 1890 (Meiji 23), and has spent more than 130 years preserving the city's tradition of fine Japanese sweets. It sits just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station along the lively Higashimuki shopping street, where it has become a familiar neighborhood fixture — a working retail sweets shop out front with a small tea-rest space tucked at the back.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes making its sweets by time-honored hand techniques ("昔ながらの手法"), aiming to breathe life and spirit into each confection so that it reflects Nara's historic legacy rather than simply being eaten.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-glazed mitarashi dango sold at the storefront, mikasa (a dorayaki-style filled cake), chestnut manjū, kuzukiri (kudzu noodles), Nara meika such as the wasanbon rakugan, and seasonal fresh sweets (jō-namagashi). In summer the shop is best known for kakigōri offered in just two carefully made versions: a Yamato-tea milk kintoki and a pure fresh-strawberry, each built around the shop's refined house-made azuki paste.

anecdotes.text: Order the shaved ice and the bowl holds a quiet surprise: what looks like a plain white ceramic vessel gradually reveals hand-painted Nara-e folk imagery on the inside as you eat your way down.

The kakigōri's sweetness comes from the confectioner's own azuki paste with a subtle hidden note of wasanbon sugar lifting the Yamato matcha — the kind of refinement that comes from a dedicated sweets specialist rather than a cafe.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small traditional wagashi shop with a shared kitchen producing wheat-based cakes, manjū and dango alongside any wheat-free items; cross-contact with flour is likely and no allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature mitarashi dango is finished with a soy-sauce-based mitarashi glaze, which in standard form contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seasonal kakigōri includes a dairy (milk) version (Yamato-tea milk kintoki), prepared in the same small space as other items.

positives.text: Several traditional items are naturally plant-based and wheat-free in concept (kuzukiri kudzu noodles, wasanbon rakugan, azuki-bean sweets), giving flexible diners some options if confirmed at the counter.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional family-run sweets shop where staff likely speak limited English, but pointing at the displayed dango or shaved ice and a friendly konnichiwa will get you served comfortably.

places.name: らつく (Ratsuku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Sukiyaki and shabu-shabu sets are served with udon noodles (wheat) and use soy-sauce-based broth/dipping sauce (warishita, ponzu), which contain wheat. The kakigori toppings include cookie pieces (Oreo, wheat) on some menus. No gluten-free accommodations are documented; celiac travelers should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The core menu is beef sukiyaki and shabu-shabu (A5 wagyu, domestic beef, Yamato pork). The kakigori is dessert-focused but uses dairy (milk cream/espuma, custard, matcha ice cream). No documented plant-based dishes.
places.hours_raw: Official site: weekdays 12:30-20:30 (last order), weekends 12:00-20:30 (last order), closed Mondays. Tabelog and a local guide instead list 11:00-21:30 daily (無休). The official ryokan site is treated as authoritative; hours and closures vary seasonally (kakigori is added in summer).
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki-Nakamachi / Higashimuki shopping arcade, near Ki
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ratsuku occupies the ground floor of the Tenpyo Ryokan, a long-running family inn that has hosted visitors to Nara for decades, tucked into the covered Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station. The restaurant is the inn's dining room turned independent

chef_bio.background: Ratsuku occupies the ground floor of the Tenpyo Ryokan, a long-running family inn that has hosted visitors to Nara for decades, tucked into the covered Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station. The restaurant is the inn's dining room turned independent eatery, serving travelers and locals alike with English- and Chinese-language menus.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name reflects a wish to 'create Nara' (ならを創る) — a small place tying the old inn to the everyday life of the shopping street.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef sukiyaki and shabu-shabu featuring A5-grade Japanese black wagyu, domestic beef loin, and local Yamato pork, with sets that come with vegetables, tofu, udon, and a small dessert. In the warm season the shop becomes known for fluffy shaved ice, with seasonal creations like a black-syrup-and-kinako espuma and a berry-custard-Oreo bowl built on milk cream and milk espuma.

anecdotes.text: Though primarily a hot-pot restaurant, Ratsuku puts out a sign warmly welcoming customers who come in just for a bowl of shaved ice, and is relaxed about single-item orders.
Its shaved ice is praised as fluffy and clean-scooping, with restrained sweetness — a berry-custard-Oreo bowl layered strawberry sauce, milk espuma, mild custard, Oreo pieces, and blueberries.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki and shabu-shabu are prepared with soy-sauce-based broth/dipping sauces, which in Japan are standardly brewed with wheat; no wheat-free alternative is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: Used to international visitors with English and Chinese menus, so a foreign student can order comfortably, and shaved-ice-only customers are explicitly welcomed.

places.name: 糀料理とビオスチームのお店 えん (Koji Ryori to Bio Steam no Omise En)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire concept is built on koji and house-made fermented seasonings (shio-koji, amazake, miso, soy-based condiments), which are typically made with wheat/barley, plus barley-rice dishes (麦こがし) and amazake scones made with flour. No celiac accommodation documented; treat as not safe for gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The signature koji plate features meat and fish marinated in koji and miki alongside the vegetable, tofu and koji-natto sides, and soups use fermented seasonings. There are genuine plant-based elements (organic local vegetables, salt-koji tofu, anti-aging salad, amazake sweets, the miki probiotic drink), so a vegetarian could likely be accommodated by skipping the meat/fish items, but this should be arranged by asking in advance; default dishes are omnivorous.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Cafe 14:00-17:00, Dinner 17:00- (reservation only). Tabelog lists 11:30-21:00. Closed Sunday and Monday (Monday salon/biosteam only).
places.neighborhood: 白毫寺町（ならまち/京終エリア、新薬師寺・白毫寺の近く、近鉄奈良駅から南東）
places.notes: Tanigawa worked as a nurse and health advisor before opening En, and that medical background shaped her conviction that food is the foundation of health. She had been a staff member at the previous occupant of this space, the koji-and-vegetable restaurant 花 (Hana); after Hana clo

chef_bio.background: Tanigawa worked as a nurse and health advisor before opening En, and that medical background shaped her conviction that food is the foundation of health. She had been a staff member at the previous occupant of this space, the koji-and-vegetable restaurant 花 (Hana); after Hana closed she reopened the same renovated, roughly 80-year-old farmhouse on her own as En.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every basic seasoning is replaced with a house-made fermented one — miso, umeboshi, shio-koji, amazake — so that each dish delivers fermentation bacteria and enzymes, with the aim that 'food brings joy to body and spirit.' She uses organic, pesticide-free local vegetables and makes miki, a lactic-acid-rich traditional drink from the Amami Islands, encouraging guests to make it at home too.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 糀とミキのえんプレート is a round plate of about seven seasonal side dishes with rice, served with a koji-salt soup and miso soup — including salt-koji-marinated tofu, sweet koji natto, an 'anti-aging' salad, and meat and fish marinated in koji and miki. Sweets center on amazake: small azuki amazake douhua, amazake and sake-kasu scones, and a seasonal lemon amazake lassi. The menu rotates every two months.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies the same renovated farmhouse where the earlier koji-and-vegetable restaurant 花 once operated; Tanigawa worked there as staff and reopened the space as her own shop after it closed.
Tanigawa tells guests that miki, the fermented Amami Islands drink she serves, can be made easily at home and encourages them to try incorporating it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Concept is entirely koji/fermentation-based with house-made fermented seasonings (shio-koji, amazake, miso) and flour-based scones, so gluten-containing ingredients are pervasive throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use house-made fermented seasonings including miso and koji condiments, which in Japanese cooking are typically wheat/barley-inclusive; specific gluten-free formulation is not documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature plate includes meat and fish marinated in koji and miki served alongside the vegetable and tofu dishes, so animal products are prepared in the same kitchen and on the same plate.

positives.text: Uses organic, pesticide-free local vegetables and offers genuinely plant-based items such as salt-koji tofu, an 'anti-aging' salad, amazake sweets, and the miki probiotic drink.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, tatami-seated farmhouse cafe run by a single owner where you remove your shoes; menus and conversation are in Japanese, so come with a translation app and the patience for a slow, home-style meal.

places.name: 大和茶大福専門店 GRANCHA（グランチャ） (Yamatocha Daifuku Senmonten GRANCHA)
places.gf_detail: The headline daifuku are mochi (glutinous rice), which is naturally wheat-free, and zenzai and warabi mochi are also rice/starch-based, so several core items contain no wheat by recipe. However, this is not a gluten-free shop: there is no allergen program, the daifuku contain matcha cream and bean paste (check for barley/wheat in hojicha-rice items and any cookie/Montblanc/soft-serve toppings), and there is no separate prep area. A celiac traveler must ask item-by-item and treat cross-contact as unmanaged.
places.vegan_detail: Some items are plausibly plant-based (mochi, anko, warabi mochi, plain matcha), but the signature daifuku use matcha cream and the parlor serves dairy soft-serve and Montblanc, so dairy is common and ingredients are not labeled. No documented vegan menu; a vegan visitor should ask which items contain milk/cream.
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki-nakamachi (Higashimuki shopping arcade, just sou
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] GRANCHA opened in 2019 in the Higashimuki shopping arcade just south of Kintetsu Nara Station as a specialty shop devoted to Yamato tea, the variety often called the root of Japanese tea. Yamato tea has more than 1,200 years of history in Nara, grown in the high-altitude Uda regi

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Yamato tea from the Uda region of Nara Prefecture)
chef_bio.background: GRANCHA opened in 2019 in the Higashimuki shopping arcade just south of Kintetsu Nara Station as a specialty shop devoted to Yamato tea, the variety often called the root of Japanese tea. Yamato tea has more than 1,200 years of history in Nara, grown in the high-altitude Uda region above 300 meters, giving it a high fragrance with sweetness lingering beneath gentle astringency. The makers worked through trial and error to express that tea in daifuku at graded strengths of matcha and roasted tea.
chef_bio.philosophy: To spread the appeal of historic Yamato tea and let visitors enjoy it through all five senses; the shop frames its daifuku with the wordplay kou-fuku-mochi (mouth-fortune-rice cake), aiming to deliver smiles. It uses additive-free mochi made fresh daily.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato-tea daifuku in five graded matcha intensities (the strongest billed as possibly Japan's most concentrated matcha daifuku), built in layers from three Yamato teas with matcha cream, salted bean paste, matcha mochi and dusted matcha; plus hojicha daifuku, matcha and hojicha kakigori, zenzai, warabi mochi, and Yamato-tea Montblanc soft serve. A self-prepared set of Yamato matcha with tea daifuku is a popular way to taste in the lounge.

anecdotes.text: Despite a bright, Western-pastry-shop look, the space is filled with the aroma of tea so that customers experience Yamato tea through all five senses.
The shop ties Yamato tea to Nara's role as the cradle of Japanese tea culture, noting that Japanese tea began when monks returning from Tang China brought back tea seeds.

positives.text: Mochi (daifuku), zenzai and warabi mochi are rice- and starch-based, so the core sweets are naturally wheat-free by recipe.
Mochi is made additive-free and fresh daily.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, walk-in sweets shop and tea stand in the main arcade where pointing at the colorful daifuku display works fine, though menu and signage are mostly in Japanese.

places.name: 京終駅舎カフェ ハテノミドリ (Kyobate Ekisha Cafe Hateno Midori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The food menu centers on wheat-based items (toast, pizza toast, pan gratin) and scones, with seasonal soup lunches that are not documented as GF. Coffee, tea and shaved ice (kakigori) may be safer in principle, but there is no stated celiac-safe handling or dedicated prep, so this is not a celiac-safe destination.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature items use dairy and eggs (custard pudding, cheesecake, rum-milk and yogurt shaved ice, gratin), and the weekly soup may contain animal-based stock. Drip coffee and some teas are likely vegan-friendly, but a plant-based eater would need to ask about each dish; nothing is confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: 平日 11:00-18:00 / 土日祝 10:00-18:00（出典により11:00-18:00表記あり）。変則営業あり（SNSで告知）。定休日：水曜（火曜は不定休）
places.neighborhood: 南京終町（JR京終駅舎内、ならまち南端）
places.cuisine: 復元された旧京終駅舎を活用した地域密着の小さなカフェ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hateno Midori occupies the restored Kyobate station building on the JR Manyo-Mahoroba (Sakurai) Line, at the southern edge of Nara's historic Naramachi district. The aging, unmanned wooden station had fallen into disrepair until a local NPO of young volunteers, working with the K

chef_bio.background: Hateno Midori occupies the restored Kyobate station building on the JR Manyo-Mahoroba (Sakurai) Line, at the southern edge of Nara's historic Naramachi district. The aging, unmanned wooden station had fallen into disrepair until a local NPO of young volunteers, working with the Kyobate community development council and the city, revitalized the building and opened this cafe inside it in February 2019. It doubles as a neighborhood gathering place and informal tourist information point for visitors arriving by train.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe's guiding theme is 'water' — offering orthodox, unhurried hospitality and a calm space that does not chase passing trends. Its name, 'Hateno Midori,' plays on the 'bate/終' of Kyobate and carries the wish that, like a single drop of water nurturing the area's greenery into endless verdure, the cafe might help people grow fond of Kyobate as a place worth returning to.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully hand-dripped coffee (light, medium and dark roasts from local roasters), a weekly-changing soup lunch, handmade custard pudding, seasonal pan gratin, scones, and a cheesecake supplied by a local bento-shop owner. In summer the shaved ice (kakigori) is a draw, with flavors such as Japanese black tea with rum and rum milk over tea jelly, and homemade grapefruit syrup with tangy yogurt and crumble.

anecdotes.text: The cafe was born from a community effort to save a deteriorating, unmanned historic station building, reopening it in February 2019 as a place for both residents and travelers.
The name 'Hateno Midori' draws on the '終 (bate)' of Kyobate, expressing the hope that a single drop of water can nurture the area's green into endless verdure.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen routinely prepares wheat-based toast, pizza toast, scones and pan gratin, so flour is present in the small open kitchen; no celiac protocols are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items rely on dairy and eggs (custard pudding, cheesecake, rum-milk/yogurt shaved ice, gratin); no separate vegan prep is documented.

positives.text: Hand-dripped coffee and some teas are served and are likely suitable for plant-based diners, though ingredients should be confirmed with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, locally-run community cafe where a smile and a little basic Japanese go a long way; English is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.

places.name: 和廣飯店 (Wako Hanten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any way. This is a classic town Chinese restaurant whose core menu is built on wheat: ramen, gyoza (wheat-flour wrappers), and fried rice and stir-fries seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication staff accommodate celiac needs. Not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature gyoza are filled with ground pork, set meals center on meat, egg, and seafood (sweet-and-sour pork, shrimp dishes, chive-and-egg stir-fry), and ramen broth and stir-fries rely on animal-based stocks. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00 - 19:00（ラストオーダーは閉店30分前）／不定休
places.neighborhood: 紀寺町・京終エリア（ならまち南）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run Chinese diner that opened in 1977 in a quiet residential pocket of Nara's Kiji-machi neighborhood, just south of Naramachi. The shop's name comes from the owner's eldest son's name. Inside, a red-toned room holds a short counter and two round tables, with a worn, vin

chef_bio.background: A family-run Chinese diner that opened in 1977 in a quiet residential pocket of Nara's Kiji-machi neighborhood, just south of Naramachi. The shop's name comes from the owner's eldest son's name. Inside, a red-toned room holds a short counter and two round tables, with a worn, vintage feel and an old TV in the corner — a genuine slice of Showa-era Japan that draws a loyal crowd of local regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: Holding prices steady and the recipes unchanged for more than four decades — affordable, consistent, everyday food for the neighborhood.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the gyoza: ten crisp-edged pork-and-vegetable dumplings that have famously stayed at 300 yen since the day the shop opened. Beyond that, the kitchen turns out stamina ramen, fried rice, mapo-tofu rice bowls, sweet-and-sour pork, shrimp dishes, and around eleven affordable set meals served with rice and miso soup — the broad, comforting repertoire of a classic Japanese town Chinese restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The 10-piece gyoza have stayed at 300 yen ever since the shop opened in 1977, making them a flagship the owners have kept unchanged for over 40 years.
The restaurant is named after the owner's eldest son, read phonetically.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the menu (ramen noodles, gyoza wrappers, soy-sauce-seasoned fried rice and stir-fries), so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable in this kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Stir-fries and fried rice are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature gyoza are pork-filled and most dishes use meat, egg, or seafood, with broths based on animal stock; vegan separation is not practiced.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, cash-only Showa-era neighborhood spot full of local regulars where almost everything is in Japanese, so it suits a student comfortable pointing at the menu and using a few Japanese phrases.

noodles.notes: Standard machi-chuka egg noodles; specific gauge/shape not documented, so treated as the typical medium wavy noodle of a neighborhood Chinese kitchen.

ramen.signature_bowl: Stamina Ramen (スタミナラーメン) - a sour-and-spicy garlic-shoyu noodle bowl piled with cabbage, garlic chives, bean sprouts, carrot and pork simmered together in the soup; finish it with the table-side sansho (花椒) for extra lift.
ramen.chashu: No braised or grilled chashu - the protein is thin pork slices stir-simmered with the vegetables directly in the seasoned broth, served as part of the topping mound rather than as a separate cut.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat-based) tare and doubanjiang make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Built on a chicken-bone (gara) broth with pork and a soy/chili-bean tare; no documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: This is the noodle soul of an old-school neighborhood Chinese kitchen rather than any regional gotōchi style: a clear chicken-gara shoyu broth turned fiery and tangy with heaps of garlic and chili-bean paste, then crowned with vegetables and pork simmered right in the pot. You taste the wok-driven machi-chuka lineage in every sour-spicy, vegetable-laden spoonful - a distinctly different beast from Nara's better-known stamina-ramen camps.

places.highlight: This is the noodle soul of an old-school neighborhood Chinese kitchen rather than any regional gotōchi style: a clear chicken-gara shoyu broth turned fiery and tangy with heaps of garlic and chili-bean paste, then crowned with vegetables and pork simmered right in the pot. You taste the wok-driven machi-chuka lineage in every sour-spicy, vegetable-laden spoonful - a distinctly different beast from Nara's better-known stamina-ramen camps.

places.name: まんぎょく (Mangyoku)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese izakaya whose menu is built on fish/kombu dashi, soy sauce, and dishes such as dashi-rolled egg and sōmen (a wheat noodle). Nothing on the pages reviewed indicates gluten-free preparation, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce. Not suitable for a strict celiac diner.
places.vegan_detail: Although there are vegetable-forward small plates (e.g., root-vegetable shira-ae with lotus root, burdock and carrot; roasted eggplant), the kitchen is described as "particular about fish" and the cuisine relies on fish/bonito dashi. Signature items include crab dumplings, dashi-rolled egg, steamed chicken and beef stew. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented, so it is not a vegan destination; a vegan would need to ask and options would be very limited.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-23:00; closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Motorinoin-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Mangyoku occupies one of the oldest surviving townhouses in Naramachi, a building dating to 1742 that once housed the geisha establishment known as Bangyokuro (萬玉楼) in the old Motorinoin-cho flower district. The wooden structure survives largely in its original form, and the inte

chef_bio.background: Mangyoku occupies one of the oldest surviving townhouses in Naramachi, a building dating to 1742 that once housed the geisha establishment known as Bangyokuro (萬玉楼) in the old Motorinoin-cho flower district. The wooden structure survives largely in its original form, and the interior keeps period touches such as antique water shelves and tansu chests, giving the small izakaya a quietly historic, grown-up atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: A refined, calm space for adults to linger over good sake and carefully made seasonal dishes within a centuries-old machiya.
chef_bio.specialty: Home-style Japanese small plates and izakaya fare meant to pair with sake: fluffy crab dumplings in a gentle broth, a soft dashi-rolled egg infused with shiitake and kombu, root-vegetable shira-ae (lotus root, burdock, carrot), roasted eggplant, steamed Hakata-style chicken, uni tofu and beef stew. The kitchen is noted for caring about its fish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is set inside the former Bangyokuro (萬玉楼), a geisha house built in 1742 in the Motorinoin-cho district, and is considered among the oldest townhouses in Naramachi, preserved in close to its original state.
Local writeups describe it as an izakaya in a former okiya (geisha lodging house) of old Nara town, prized for its antique atmosphere.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, traditional evening izakaya where Japanese is the working language, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese or come with a Japanese-speaking friend and bring cash.

places.name: 料亭 菊水楼 (Ryotei Kikusuiro)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free oriented. This is a traditional kaiseki house where dishes lean heavily on soy sauce, miso, and fish/kombu dashi, so wheat (and gluten via soy sauce) is present throughout the menu. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu and no documented celiac protocol. The restaurant does state that allergy and dietary requests must be communicated at least 3 business days in advance, so a strict gluten-free traveler should arrange this far ahead and not assume safety. Treat as not celiac-safe without direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian by default. The kaiseki courses are built around seasonal seafood (including whole roasted sea bream in celebration courses) and almost certainly fish/kombu dashi, so standard courses are not vegan. Goma-dofu (sesame tofu) is a documented signature, but that alone does not make the menu plant-based. The kitchen accepts dietary/allergy requests with at least 3 business days' notice and builds each day's menu to order, so a vegetarian or vegan accommodation may be possible if arranged well in advance, but nothing vegan-specific is documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (last entry 13:00), 17:00-20:30 (last entry 18:00). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday.
places.neighborhood: Nara Park / in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taish
places.notes: Kikusuiro is a long-established ryotei founded in 1891, set just before the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha at the edge of Nara Park. For more than 130 years it has welcomed prominent figures, intellectuals, and literary writers, becoming one of Nara's most storied dining house

chef_bio.background: Kikusuiro is a long-established ryotei founded in 1891, set just before the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha at the edge of Nara Park. For more than 130 years it has welcomed prominent figures, intellectuals, and literary writers, becoming one of Nara's most storied dining houses. Its formal gate, relocated from Enjo-ji temple and dating to the Edo period, is a Registered Tangible Cultural Property, so guests step through a piece of preserved architectural history on their way in.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hospitality and flavor refined through tradition, with care taken so that a meal can be enjoyed with all five senses - from delicate presentation down to the lingering aromas of each dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki with no fixed menu - the head chef composes a unique course each day around the season and the guests. Signature touches include a carefully hand-ground sesame tofu (goma-dofu) and, for celebration courses, a whole roasted sea bream symbolizing good fortune, all made with Nara's seasonal produce.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's entrance gate was relocated from Nara's Enjo-ji temple and dates to the Edo period; it is designated a Registered Tangible Cultural Property.
Founded in 1891, the house has long been favored by notable figures and literary writers over its 130-plus year history.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional kaiseki ryotei, dishes rely on soy sauce, miso, and dashi; no gluten-free or wheat-free menu is documented, so wheat-derived seasonings should be assumed present.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses center on seasonal seafood (including whole sea bream) and likely fish/kombu dashi, so standard menus are not vegan and shared kitchen prep is expected.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks that allergy and dietary restrictions be communicated at least 3 business days in advance.

positives.text: Menus are made to order each day by the head chef, which means dietary requests arranged well ahead may be accommodated.
