cultural_comfort.note: This is a formal, high-end traditional ryotei requiring advance reservation and a special-occasion budget, so a foreign student should book ahead, communicate any dietary needs at least three business days early, and be ready for a refined Japanese-language setting.

places.name: ラ・リベルテ (LA LIBERTE)
places.gf_detail: This is a flour-based patisserie and bakery; cakes, breads, and baked goods are made with wheat flour and there is no documented gluten-free offering. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional Western-style bake shop whose cakes, breads, and baked sweets rely on butter, eggs, and dairy. No vegan or plant-based items are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:30 (January-February 10:00-16:00). Closed Monday and Tuesday; Sunday irregular. Cake and bread quantities are limited and may sell out before midday.
places.neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Cake & Bread Cafe tucked into Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by a woman who grew up in Kitamachi and returned to open her shop in the very neighborhood she calls home. Before baking professionally she worked as a

chef_bio.background: A small Cake & Bread Cafe tucked into Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by a woman who grew up in Kitamachi and returned to open her shop in the very neighborhood she calls home. Before baking professionally she worked as a kindergarten teacher, then became a cooking-school instructor and ran her own dessert classes, eventually turning that passion into this little storefront.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner likens making cakes to classical music, a quiet passion that deepened over the years, and says her aim is simply to offer sweets that bring a smile to every customer, each one made carefully and by hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade cakes and baked goods made one at a time, with a rotating daily selection of roughly five to seven cakes, a few kinds of焼き菓子 (baked sweets) and breads, plus black tea, flavored teas and herbal teas you can enjoy in the small cafe space. Signature pieces include seasonal whole cakes, decorative flower cakes, macarons, and a tea-flavored chiffon cake.

anecdotes.text: The owner grew up in Kitamachi and considers it an honor to run her shop in her home neighborhood; she came to baking after careers as a kindergarten teacher and a cooking-school instructor.
Cakes, baked sweets and breads are made in small daily batches and sometimes sell out before midday, and the cafe space doubles as the venue for her sweets lessons.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny owner-run neighborhood bake shop where everything is in Japanese, so a few polite phrases and pointing at the cakes in the case will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: ヤマトモダン JR奈良駅前店 (Yamato Modern JR Nara Ekimae-ten)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. The menu is wheat- and soy-heavy (pasta, curry, breaded mille-feuille cutlet, chicken nanban, teriyaki/soy-glazed pork) and most savory dishes rely on fish/soy-based seasonings, so it is not celiac-safe. The rice-flour (米粉) pancakes are naturally lower in wheat but are made in a shared kitchen with no documented allergen controls — treat as not safe for celiac without asking in person.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. It is an omnivore Nara-ingredient dining bar built around Yamato pork, Yamato beef and chicken. There are vegetable side-dish platters and a daily vegetable assortment, and the official site notes "healthy menu" options can be requested, but vegetable and broth dishes almost certainly use fish dashi or soy-based seasoning. A vegan would need to ask the staff directly; nothing is documented as confirmed vegan.
places.neighborhood: 三条町（JR奈良駅前、徒歩約3〜4分）
places.cuisine: 和ダイニング＆カフェ・洋食
places.notes: ヤマトモダン JR奈良駅前店 is a modern Japanese dining bar and cafe just a few minutes' walk from JR Nara Station, set in a townhouse-style space (it previously operated under the name 大和CAFE). It is part of a small family of Nara restaurants alongside やまと庵 and 大和野菜と串焼きやまと, all built on the

chef_bio.background: ヤマトモダン JR奈良駅前店 is a modern Japanese dining bar and cafe just a few minutes' walk from JR Nara Station, set in a townhouse-style space (it previously operated under the name 大和CAFE). It is part of a small family of Nara restaurants alongside やまと庵 and 大和野菜と串焼きやまと, all built on the same idea of cooking with local Nara-prefecture ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is local-ingredient cooking by day and a casual bar by night ('夜はバル'), pairing Nara produce, pork and beef with regional sake such as Harushika from the Imanishi Seibei brewery.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen centers on Nara brand ingredients: Yamato pork spare ribs glazed in a sweet teriyaki sauce, Nara-style chicken nanban topped with pickled daikon, a Yamato beef mille-feuille cutlet, hand-made curry pizza, and an eggplant-and-red-miso bolognese. A daily vegetable platter showcases Yamato vegetables, and the daytime cafe is known for fluffy rice-flour (米粉) pancakes served scone-style with maple syrup, fresh cream and plenty of fruit.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's rice-flour pancakes are described as scone-like, served with maple syrup and fresh cream and topped generously with fruit.
By night the venue rebrands as a bar, leaning into the pairing of Nara ingredients with local Harushika sake.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes use teriyaki/soy glazes (Yamato pork spare ribs) and breaded cutlets, indicating standard wheat-containing soy sauce and flour are in regular use.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site notes a 'healthy menu' can be requested, but no specific allergen, gluten-free or vegan handling protocol is documented.

positives.text: A daily vegetable platter featuring Yamato vegetables and rice-flour pancakes are on the menu, giving some lighter or lower-wheat starting points to discuss with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, casual townhouse-style dining cafe near JR Nara Station where a foreign student can comfortably point at the menu; some basic Japanese helps but it is welcoming and unintimidating.

places.name: カフェ ワカクサ (cafe WAKAKUSA)
places.gf_detail: The house specialty is crepes made from 100% domestic wheat flour (a blend of bread flour and cake flour); pasta is also on the menu. These are wheat-based, so the cafe is not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten-free diners. No gluten-free options are documented.
places.vegan_detail: The signature crepes are made with milk, eggs, and unsalted butter, and the sweet menu is dairy- and chocolate-based, so the core offerings are not vegan. No vegan menu or plant-based substitutions are documented.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon-Tue 08:00-19:00; Wed 09:00-17:00; Thu closed; Fri-Sun 08:00-18:00. (Some local listings note 9:00-18:00 and a Tuesday closing — hours appear to vary, confirm directly.)
places.neighborhood: 東向北町（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run two-story cafe tucked into the Higashimuki-kita covered shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. The first floor has a counter where guests can chat with the owner, and the upstairs room offers relaxed sofa seating. It shifts gracefully through t

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run two-story cafe tucked into the Higashimuki-kita covered shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. The first floor has a counter where guests can chat with the owner, and the upstairs room offers relaxed sofa seating. It shifts gracefully through the day as a morning spot, a lunch cafe, and a casual evening place for a drink.
chef_bio.philosophy: An additive-free, made-from-scratch approach to the crepe batter, aiming for honest, body-friendly food served in a warm, conversational setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made crepes are the heart of the menu, with more than ten savory and more than ten sweet varieties. The batter is made only from a blend of domestic bread and cake flour, milk, eggs, unsalted butter, and granulated sugar, with no additives, giving it a chewy texture. The fruit-laden dessert crepes (mango, kiwi, strawberry, banana) are a favorite, and lunch sets pair a crepe or short pasta with salad, soup, and a mini dessert. Hojicha and matcha lattes round out the drinks.

anecdotes.text: The owner also works as a local tour guide and welcomes foreign visitors with English-language support, making it an easy, friendly stop for travelers.
The crepe batter is described as using 100% domestic wheat flour blending bread and cake flours with only milk, eggs, unsalted butter, and sugar, with no additives, for a chewy texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the kitchen: crepes are made from a domestic wheat-flour blend and pasta is served, so flour handling pervades the cooking and there is high risk of gluten cross-contact.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core items are built on milk, eggs, and butter, with dairy- and chocolate-based desserts, so plant-based diners cannot avoid animal ingredients in the signature menu.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner also works as a local tour guide and offers English-language support, so a foreign student can order and chat comfortably.

places.name: ベーカリーポポロ (Bakery Poporo)
places.gf_detail: The shop is known for rice flour (米粉) breads such as rice flour sandwiches, rice flour red bean butter, and rice flour cream breads, which makes it appealing for those reducing wheat. However, this is a conventional bakery that also produces large amounts of wheat bread on the same premises and equipment, so it is NOT a dedicated gluten-free facility and cross-contamination with wheat flour is highly likely. Celiacs should not assume safety; you would need to ask staff directly about ingredients and shared equipment.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan labeling is documented. Many items contain dairy (cream cheese, milk chocolate cream, butter), and savory breads include meat and fish (sausage, smoked chicken, salted mackerel). Some plain breads may be incidentally egg/dairy-free but this is not confirmed; you would need to ask about each item.
places.hours_raw: 7:50-18:00 (closes earlier when sold out). Closed Saturdays, Sundays, and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Nabeyacho, near Kintetsu-Nara Station and Nara Women's Unive
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Bakery Poporo is a long-running neighborhood bread shop in central Nara, tucked into the Nabeyacho district a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station and just north of Nara Women's University. With its tidy, recently refreshed storefront, it serves a steady stream of students, loca

chef_bio.background: Bakery Poporo is a long-running neighborhood bread shop in central Nara, tucked into the Nabeyacho district a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station and just north of Nara Women's University. With its tidy, recently refreshed storefront, it serves a steady stream of students, local office and government workers, and residents who file in from early morning.
chef_bio.philosophy: The fillings for its rice flour breads are said to be inspired by restaurant dishes, giving everyday bakery items a more composed, meal-like character at very approachable prices.
chef_bio.specialty: Around twenty kinds of sandwiches alongside a wide range of sweet and savory rolls. The shop is especially known for its rice flour (komeko) breads, which have a soft, slightly chewy mochi-like texture; popular examples include rice flour avocado smoked-chicken and rice flour red bean butter, plus crowd favorites like curry bread, cream cheese rolls, raisin Danish, and French-bread an-pan.

anecdotes.text: The fillings for the popular rice flour breads are reportedly drawn from restaurant menus, giving the lineup unusual variety for a small bakery.
Students, government workers, and locals arrive continuously from morning, and by early afternoon the selection has noticeably thinned, so popular breads sell out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a conventional bakery producing wheat bread on the same premises and equipment as its rice flour breads, so airborne flour and shared-surface cross-contamination is highly likely; no dedicated gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and animal products (cream cheese, milk chocolate cream, butter, sausage, smoked chicken, salted mackerel) are used throughout the lineup, so vegan items, if any, would be prepared in a non-vegan environment.

positives.text: A range of rice flour (komeko) breads is a regular part of the menu, offering a wheat-reduced option for those who are not strictly celiac.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood bakery where you point at the bread you want and pay in cash, so a friendly greeting and basic Japanese (or just gestures) will get you through comfortably.

places.name: ダイニング螺鈿 (Dining Raden)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes are hand-pulled somen and udon (both wheat noodles), and the creative Japanese/izakaya menu relies on soy sauce and miso. No gluten-free accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu centers on local-vegetable dishes, somen/udon and an Asuka-style miso soup made with milk, and most dishes use fish dashi, so they are not vegan as served. A vegetable-forward set might be adaptable but you would need to ask in advance; no vegan menu is documented.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（三条通り／ひがしむき）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Dining Raden opened in late July 2024 near Kintetsu Nara Station, billing itself as a 'Nara soul food' dining spot. By day it serves customizable lunch sets and noodles; in the evening it shifts into a sake-and-craft-beer bar.

chef_bio.background: Dining Raden opened in late July 2024 near Kintetsu Nara Station, billing itself as a 'Nara soul food' dining spot. By day it serves customizable lunch sets and noodles; in the evening it shifts into a sake-and-craft-beer bar.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to showcasing Nara's ingredients across the whole menu, sourcing rice, noodles and vegetables from villages such as Soni and Kawakami to deepen appreciation of the region's culinary heritage.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-pulled somen and nyumen noodles from the Okuyoshino/Kawakami area, locally grown Yamato vegetables, Nara-rice onigiri, and a distinctive Asuka-style miso soup blended with milk, paired with Nara craft beer and local sake.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a Nara City tourism ambassador who also works as a tour guide, regional PR filmmaker and facility planner, and channels that varied experience into a menu built around local Nara produce.
By day it operates as the lunch concept '螺鈿' and by night transforms into a sake-and-oden bar with a curated, owner-selected sake and craft-beer lineup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat somen and udon are core menu items prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Creative Japanese and izakaya dishes use standard soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most dishes use fish dashi and the signature miso soup is made with milk, so vegan-safe preparation is not assured without asking.

positives.text: Lunch sets are customizable with choices across proteins, sides and rice, so there is some flexibility to assemble a more vegetable-forward plate if you ask.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way; the owner is a Nara tourism ambassador and welcoming to visitors.

places.name: 珈琲屋 エルムンド (Kohiya El Mundo)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house, not a gluten-free destination. Documented food items are toast (wheat bread) and cheesecake, both of which contain gluten. There is no indication of gluten-free offerings, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs beyond plain black coffee.
places.vegan_detail: A drinks-focused kissaten. Coffee can be taken black without dairy, and there are juices and non-caffeinated teas. However, the documented food (buttered toast, rare cheesecake) and arranged coffee drinks contain dairy. No vegan dishes are documented; vegans are limited to black coffee, plain juices, and non-caffeinated tea.
places.hours_raw: 火～日 10:30-18:30（月曜定休）。資料により11:00-18:00との記載もあり。
places.neighborhood: 東向北商店街 (Higashimuki-Kita shopping street), 2 minutes north o
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in 1974 on the second floor of a building in the Higashimuki-Kita shopping street, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The ground floor is an antique/used bookstore, and a Nipper dog figurine at the foot of a steep staircase marks the way up. The name El Mundo mea

chef_bio.background: Opened in 1974 on the second floor of a building in the Higashimuki-Kita shopping street, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The ground floor is an antique/used bookstore, and a Nipper dog figurine at the foot of a steep staircase marks the way up. The name El Mundo means "world" in Spanish. The room is dressed in nostalgic European style: retro furniture, stained-glass lamps, paintings on the walls, antique European cups and saucers on the shelves, and a red carpet leading in.
chef_bio.philosophy: Conceived as a quiet retreat where guests can briefly step out of daily life and let coffee and music carry them somewhere else.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee above all, with more than 20 varieties spanning blends, single-origin, and arranged drinks. The house El Mundo Blend is mild, while a charcoal-style roast is described as aromatic with a slightly robust character for a mature palate. The rare cheesecake and buttered toast are favorite accompaniments, and non-caffeinated teas and juices are also served.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run by a refined woman regulars call "the madame," who is welcoming and deeply versed in classical music. Records play on an analog turntable, and guests can request a piece as loosely as "anything by Mozart" or "any violin concerto" and she will choose a fitting recording, gently guiding even those new to classical music.
A writer who first visited as a student returned decades later to find the place essentially unchanged, a testament to how faithfully it has preserved its atmosphere over the years.

positives.text: Coffee, non-caffeinated teas, and juices can be ordered as plain drinks without dairy or wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, intimate Showa-era coffee house run by a gracious madame where you order in simple Japanese; comfortable for a foreign student who enjoys calm spaces, though there is no indication of English service or menus.

places.name: おんどり (Ondori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional yakitori shop where skewers are basted in a wheat-based tare (soy sauce) and the menu includes fried chicken (kara-age, wheat batter) and other dishes that use soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation is documented and the lone master cannot be assumed to handle celiac-level needs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The shop is built almost entirely around chicken (skewers, liver, gizzard, skin, tsukune, chicken-and-egg dishes) and eggs; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sun approx. 14:00-22:00 (last order ~22:00); closed Monday. Reported open time varies by source (14:00-14:30); some listings show evening-only hours, so hours are not strictly fixed.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishi-Terajibayashi, off Mochiido shopping street
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ondori is a longstanding yakitori shop founded in 1982 (Showa 57), tucked down a narrow alley off the Mochiido shopping street in Naramachi. It is run single-handedly by one quiet, veteran master who has worked the counter for over forty years, grilling to order in front of a han

chef_bio.background: Ondori is a longstanding yakitori shop founded in 1982 (Showa 57), tucked down a narrow alley off the Mochiido shopping street in Naramachi. It is run single-handedly by one quiet, veteran master who has worked the counter for over forty years, grilling to order in front of a handful of regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, old-school neighborhood izakaya experience: the master lets his grilling and timing do the talking while strangers at the tightly packed counter end up chatting like old friends.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken skewers of local jidori (skin, gizzard, liver, thigh-and-leek, tsukune) basted in a house tare, alongside crowd-favorite fried chicken and a much-praised dashimaki tamago (rolled omelet). Skewers start around 150 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is famously cramped: with about 10 people the counter is shoulder-to-shoulder, and patrons cooperate to shuffle and make room for newcomers.
The walls are decorated with Audrey Hepburn photos, and the restroom is reached by such a steeply sloped staircase that customers grip a rope to climb it - a running talking point among regulars.
The master is known for being almost completely silent ('the quiet master'), yet he reads the room and serves each customer's needs without a word.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Skewers are basted in a house tare (sweet soy-based sauce), which is standard wheat-containing soy sauce; dishes such as ponzu-dressed items and fried chicken also rely on soy sauce and wheat batter.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is dedicated to chicken and egg dishes grilled on a shared charcoal grill, so there is no separation for plant-based diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, packed counter run by one quiet master with regulars and no reservations or English menu, so a few Japanese phrases and a relaxed, go-with-the-flow attitude will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: ニュー大黒 (New Daikoku)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a classic yoshoku diner built around breaded-and-fried items (shrimp fries, tonkatsu, beef cutlet), flour-thickened demi-glace and curry, hamburg steak, spaghetti (Napolitan), udon/noodles and katsu-don — virtually everything contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergy handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is centered on meat and seafood (hamburg steak, fried shrimp, pork/beef cutlets, beef bowls) with sauces and broths that rely on meat and fish dashi, plus egg and dairy. No vegan options are documented, and staff are not set up to accommodate plant-based requests.
places.neighborhood: 法蓮佐保山 (Hōren-Sahoyama), north of Kōnoike Sports Park, northe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running family-style Western diner in northern Nara that has been serving the neighborhood for more than 50 years. Tucked north of Kōnoike Sports Park, it looks from the outside like a slightly weathered roadside drive-in, and inside it preserves a thoroughly Showa-era caf

chef_bio.background: A long-running family-style Western diner in northern Nara that has been serving the neighborhood for more than 50 years. Tucked north of Kōnoike Sports Park, it looks from the outside like a slightly weathered roadside drive-in, and inside it preserves a thoroughly Showa-era cafeteria feel — old food-sample displays at the entrance, sturdy tables, sofa seating and bottles of sauce on every table. It's the kind of place described as the original blueprint of the Japanese family restaurant, and it draws families, regulars, and even passing taxi and truck drivers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keeping the comforting, nostalgic flavors of traditional Japanese-Western home cooking alive, with hand-made hamburgers and demi-glace and retro touches like a slice of pineapple on the plate.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty old-school yoshoku plate lunches. The most popular is the B Lunch — a mini hamburg steak in house-made demi-glace paired with two butterflied fried shrimp, ham and salad — with a 'Special B' upgrade serving a full-size hamburg on a sizzling iron plate with potatoes and broccoli. The A Lunch pairs a pork cutlet with ketchup spaghetti, and the kitchen also turns out beef bowls, katsu-don, Napolitan, beef cutlet sets, jumbo fried shrimp, and nostalgic desserts like a banana sundae.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers say stepping inside feels like time-slipping into the Showa era, comparing the cafeteria atmosphere to a scene out of Chibi Maruko-chan, and note the hand-made hamburger is tender with a real beefy flavor.
It is a working-people's favorite as well as a family spot — taxi and truck drivers are regulars, and at peak lunch times waits can reach about 30 minutes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded deep-fried items (fried shrimp, tonkatsu, beef cutlet) sharing fryer oil, plus flour-based demi-glace and curry, so wheat cross-contact is pervasive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on meat and fish-dashi-based dishes; no separation for plant-based diets is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local Showa-era diner where ordering is in Japanese and a little pointing at the food-sample display or photo menu will get you through, but no English or allergy support should be expected.

places.name: 洋食お米料理 Emiru (Yoshoku Okome Ryori Emiru)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu is built around wheat-heavy Western dishes — omurice, doria (cheese/gratin rice), risotto with cream sauces and demi-glace, plus risotto croquettes (breaded/fried). Demi-glace and many sauces typically contain wheat. No dedicated GF menu, no documented allergen handling, and no source confirms celiac-safe preparation. Not suitable for those requiring strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. Signature dishes rely on egg (omurice), cheese (four-cheese doria, gorgonzola risotto), seafood, and meat/demi-glace. A plain tomato-sauce or vegetable risotto might be adaptable, but nothing is documented as vegan and dishes commonly use dairy, egg, and likely fish/meat stock. A vegan diner would need to ask directly, and options would be very limited at best.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30), open Monday and Wednesday–Sunday and holidays; closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: 京終・近鉄奈良駅エリア（高畑町／ならまち寄り）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small Western-style restaurant that opened in mid-January 2026 in Nara's Takabatake district, a quiet area between the temples east of the city and the old machiya streets of Naramachi. As its name 'お米料理' (rice cuisine) promises, it is built around one simple idea: make the ric

chef_bio.background: A small Western-style restaurant that opened in mid-January 2026 in Nara's Takabatake district, a quiet area between the temples east of the city and the old machiya streets of Naramachi. As its name 'お米料理' (rice cuisine) promises, it is built around one simple idea: make the rice the star of the plate rather than a side.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to let the character of the rice come through, balancing it with carefully measured sauces so that, as one writeup put it, guests are surprised first by how good the rice itself tastes.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-centered Western dishes — omurice in several styles (garlic-tomato sauce, demi-glace), doria (baked gratin rice such as four-cheese and a margherita-style), and risotto (seafood, gorgonzola, and risotto croquettes). Affordable lunch sets come with salad and soup, and there are children's plates available any time.

anecdotes.text: Coverage of the opening described it as a rare 'rice-first' Western restaurant where diners are struck by the quality of the rice before anything else, with daily-rotating lunch sets of omurice, doria, and risotto.

A local Nara food blog called it a genuinely unusual rice-dish Western eatery, praising the creamy gorgonzola risotto and four-cheese doria, and noting a chic, calm interior that makes solo diners comfortable.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, casual neighborhood spot with picture-friendly rice dishes and a friendly welcome for solo diners, though menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app helps.

places.name: 洋風処こん (Yofu-dokoro Kon)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional yoshoku (Western-style) restaurant centered on a house-made demiglace sauce, hamburg steak, gratin, doria, fried shrimp and fried chicken — dishes that rely heavily on wheat flour (roux, breading, batter). Not suitable for celiac diners; no dedicated preparation or allergy protocol is mentioned anywhere on the official site or listings.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. The menu is built around meat and seafood (hamburg steak, fried chicken, fried shrimp, chicken/shrimp gratin) and dishes use meat-based demiglace; nothing on the official site or articles indicates plant-based dishes.
places.neighborhood: 新大宮・奈良駅エリア (Shin-Omiya, Nara City)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yofu-dokoro Kon is a small owner-run Western-style eatery in Nara's Shin-Omiya district, a short walk from Shin-Omiya Station. Owner-chef Kunihiro Konoki has worked in food service since his student days and opened this place to serve approachable, affordable comfort food for din

chef_bio.background: Yofu-dokoro Kon is a small owner-run Western-style eatery in Nara's Shin-Omiya district, a short walk from Shin-Omiya Station. Owner-chef Kunihiro Konoki has worked in food service since his student days and opened this place to serve approachable, affordable comfort food for diners of all ages, in the spirit of an old-fashioned neighborhood yoshoku shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Konoki keeps to the image of a traditional Western-style diner (昔ながらの洋食屋さん), focusing on honest, carefully made home cooking that everyone can afford and enjoy, with the aim of bringing daily joy to his customers.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made demiglace sauce is the heart of the kitchen, served over hamburg steak and omelet rice. Other signatures include chicken gratin, shrimp gratin and doria, plus fried shrimp and fried chicken; seasonal touches like young-corn tempura appear with the seasons.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe it as a reimagined old-fashioned yoshoku shop that delivers properly delicious comfort food through quiet, careful technique rather than trendy presentation, with families and regulars gathering in a warm, homey room.

cultural_comfort.note: A homey neighborhood Western-food diner where the menu and service are in Japanese; a little basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smooth and comfortable.

places.name: 植村牧場カフェレストランいちづ (Uemura Bokujo Cafe Restaurant Ichizu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The Western-style menu centers on wheat-heavy dishes — cream croquettes, hamburg steak, omelet rice and the farm's own cakes, cookies, madeleines and financiers. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no documented allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a dairy-farm restaurant whose entire identity is built around its own fresh milk, plus eggs and meat (hamburg steak, cream croquettes, omelet rice, parfaits, ice cream and milk-based sweets). Dairy and animal products are central to nearly every item; there are no documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (14:00–17:00 cafe service only; lunch orders generally until ~14:00). Closed Wednesdays. (Sources also list 11:00–16:00; afternoon hours are cafe-only.)
places.neighborhood: Kintetsu Nara Station / Kitamachi area, beside Hannyaji Temp
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] いちづ is the cafe-restaurant of Uemura Bokujo, said to be Nara's oldest dairy farm, run by the Uemura family since the Meiji era (founded in the 1880s). Sitting directly across from Hannyaji Temple in the quiet Kitamachi area north of central Nara, the roughly 1,800-tsubo farm stil

chef_bio.background: いちづ is the cafe-restaurant of Uemura Bokujo, said to be Nara's oldest dairy farm, run by the Uemura family since the Meiji era (founded in the 1880s). Sitting directly across from Hannyaji Temple in the quiet Kitamachi area north of central Nara, the roughly 1,800-tsubo farm still keeps around 30 dairy cows alongside miniature horses, and delivers some 1,500 bottles of low-temperature pasteurized milk across Nara each day — including to the historic Nara Hotel.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 'Ichizu' means devoting oneself single-mindedly to dairy farming — a one-track dedication to good milk and the food made from it.
chef_bio.specialty: Western-style home cooking made with the farm's own fresh milk: cream croquette lunch, hamburg steak lunch, a mixed lunch, omelet-rice sets, and milk-based sweets — parfaits, ice cream sandwiches, cookies, madeleines and financiers from the farm shop. The kitchen is led by a chef with experience at the Nara Hotel.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name 'Ichizu' was chosen by Oscar-honored, Nara-born filmmaker Naomi Kawase, with the signboard calligraphy by the artist Shishu (紫舟), known for NHK historical-drama title work.
Diners look out from the windows onto Hannyaji Temple while eating dishes built around the farm's own daily-fresh milk, in a country atmosphere with cow sculptures dotted around the grounds.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-run countryside cafe where a guidebook or pointing at the menu is enough; staff likely speak little English, but the simple Western-style dishes make ordering easy.

places.name: にぼしこいし (Niboshi Koishi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen and tsukemen noodles are wheat-based (the house noodles use a blend of two Hokkaido wheat varieties with tapioca starch), and the broth is built on fish niboshi and chicken with seasonings that typically include wheat-containing soy sauce. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu is built on a double broth of dried fish (large sardine, urume, aji) and chicken paitan, topped with pork chashu and egg. There are no vegan options.
places.neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: 煮干しラーメン・つけ麺
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run niboshi (dried-fish) ramen specialist tucked into central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and about ten minutes from JR Nara. As the name suggests, dried fish is the heart of everything here.

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run niboshi (dried-fish) ramen specialist tucked into central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and about ten minutes from JR Nara. As the name suggests, dried fish is the heart of everything here.
chef_bio.philosophy: A single-minded focus on dried fish — every bowl is designed to deliver a powerful, unmistakable niboshi punch.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a richly concentrated niboshi ramen built on a double broth that marries dried fish from across Japan — large sardine, urume, and aji — with a chicken paitan base. The deluxe special bowl (特製にぼこいラーメン) piles on six slices of shoulder-roast chashu, a soft-boiled egg, menma, scallion and naruto, with fish powder over the nori to push the dried-fish character further. The shop also serves tsukemen (dipping noodles) in 200g, 300g and 400g portions, with house-made noodles.

anecdotes.text: One diner described the dried-fish flavor as hitting powerfully on the first taste — "食べた瞬間、煮干しの風味がガツンときました。こんなに煮干しを感じたのは初めてです" — calling it an unprecedented level of niboshi intensity.
The thick, chewy house noodles are made from a blend of two Hokkaido wheat varieties combined with tapioca starch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core product and are boiled in shared water; broth and tare contain fish and likely wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Niboshi/chicken ramen and tsukemen are seasoned in the standard Japanese ramen style, which uses wheat-containing soy sauce-based tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are made from dried fish and chicken; toppings include pork chashu and egg. No vegan items.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighborhood ramen counter where ordering is simple, cash-only, and a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the menu will see you through comfortably.

noodles.notes: House-made chewy straight noodles blending two Hokkaido wheat varieties with tapioca flour for a mochi-mochi bite; a thinner noodle is offered for kaedama and can be requested.

ramen.signature_bowl: Special Nibokoi Ramen — the rich niboshi-and-chicken-paitan double soup, fully loaded
ramen.chashu: Pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu), braised and sliced — the special bowl piles on roughly six slices

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-and-kansui (here even blended with tapioca) and the tare is shoyu/shio; nothing celiac-safe is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is dried-sardine dashi plus chicken paitan, with pork chashu and animal-based tare; no plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern niboshi house near Kintetsu Nara that pushes dried-sardine ramen to its richest extreme — fistfuls of iwashi, urume and aji niboshi married to a velvety chicken paitan for a double soup that is at once bitter-deep sea umami and creamy poultry body. You can dial it from a clean, clear assari niboshi up to the thick, almost gravy-like Nibokoi, tasting the sardine lineage hit harder with every step.

places.highlight: A modern niboshi house near Kintetsu Nara that pushes dried-sardine ramen to its richest extreme — fistfuls of iwashi, urume and aji niboshi married to a velvety chicken paitan for a double soup that is at once bitter-deep sea umami and creamy poultry body. You can dial it from a clean, clear assari niboshi up to the thick, almost gravy-like Nibokoi, tasting the sardine lineage hit harder with every step.

places.name: おか田 (Okada)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki house with no documented gluten-free program. The cuisine relies on a katsuobushi-and-kombu dashi and seasonal courses that routinely include wheat-based elements such as Miwa sōmen noodles and soy-sauce-seasoned dishes, so it is not safe for celiacs. A diner with a strict gluten allergy would need to discuss it directly at the time of reservation, and even then accommodation is not confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The omakase courses are built around a fish-based dashi (Makurazaki katsuobushi and kombu) and feature animal proteins such as Yamato beef, so the standard menu is not vegan or vegetarian. As an omakase counter that changes monthly with seasonal Yamato vegetables and kudzu, the chef might be able to adapt dishes if asked well in advance at booking, but no vegan offering is documented.
places.neighborhood: 今御門町（ならまち・猿沢池近く）
places.notes: Chef-owner Okada Naoki grew up in a farming family in Nara, so he knew the flavor of the region's produce from childhood. After culinary school he spent sixteen years training at the Osaka restaurant Shinsaibashi Masuda before returning home, opening his own small counter near Sa

chef_bio.background: Chef-owner Okada Naoki grew up in a farming family in Nara, so he knew the flavor of the region's produce from childhood. After culinary school he spent sixteen years training at the Osaka restaurant Shinsaibashi Masuda before returning home, opening his own small counter near Sarusawa Pond in September 2019.
chef_bio.philosophy: Okada wants guests to enjoy themselves through refined tableware, meticulously prepared dishes, and sincere hospitality, expressing the turning of the seasons through Nara's ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: An eight-course seasonal omakase centered on a rotating hassun (assorted seasonal appetizer platter) that showcases Nara's bounty — Yamato beef, traditional Yamato vegetables, kudzu, and Miwa sōmen. The dashi is drawn Osaka-style from Makurazaki katsuobushi and kombu.

anecdotes.text: Okada studied the tea ceremony so that he can personally whisk and serve matcha to close each meal — a deliberate gesture of hospitality.
Raised in a Nara farming family, he draws on a lifelong familiarity with local Yamato ingredients, which he calls part of Nara's abundance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Courses include wheat-based items such as Miwa sōmen noodles and soy-seasoned dishes, with no documented gluten-free protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine uses Japanese soy sauce and a dashi base typical of kaiseki, both of which involve wheat and fish; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is made from katsuobushi (bonito) and kombu and the menu features Yamato beef, so vegan diners cannot rely on the standard courses.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, upscale counter where reservations are essential and the chef serves a set omakase course; a foreign student would have a smoother experience with some basic Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion, and should communicate any dietary needs at booking.

places.name: 喫茶 Rich（リッチ） (Kissa Rich)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Western-style kissaten whose core menu is wheat-heavy and not suitable for celiacs. Signature dishes include omurice (ketchup rice with a demi-glace or white sauce — both typically thickened with wheat flour), curry, pasta/spaghetti, doria and gratin, plus morning toast, sandwiches and hot dogs. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no documented allergy handling. Not a gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional omurice-and-coffee shop with no vegan offerings. Egg- and dairy-based dishes dominate (omurice, egg sandwiches, doria/gratin), and savory items use beef and demi-glace. No plant-based options are documented. Not suitable for vegans; a vegetarian could potentially find plain toast or coffee but nothing is confirmed vegan.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 7:00–18:30 (morning service 7:00–11:00, lunch 11:30–15:00); Sundays & holidays 7:00–17:00; last order ~30 min before close. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 大宮町 / 三条通り, near JR Nara & Kintetsu Nara Stations
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running classic kissaten near JR and Kintetsu Nara Stations, Rich has been pouring coffee and serving Western-style comfort food for around 40 years, occupying the ground floor of the Tokyu Dwell Nara Park Village building near the former Nara Girls' High School along Sanj

chef_bio.background: A long-running classic kissaten near JR and Kintetsu Nara Stations, Rich has been pouring coffee and serving Western-style comfort food for around 40 years, occupying the ground floor of the Tokyu Dwell Nara Park Village building near the former Nara Girls' High School along Sanjo-dori.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unhurried, at-home (アットホーム) atmosphere that keeps decades of regulars coming back while staying easy for first-time visitors to drop into.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is a fluffy omurice — soft, half-set scrambled egg over ketchup rice with beef or mushrooms, finished with a choice of sweet demi-glace or white sauce. The other star is the coffee: a house blend of several beans brewed by siphon for a smooth, mellow, mild cup. Morning sets (egg sandwiches, toast, hot dogs) and lunch sets of curry, doria, gratin and pasta round out the menu, each lunch coming with salad, a mini dessert and a drink.

anecdotes.text: The coffee is a multi-bean house blend brewed in a siphon, described as smooth and mellow with no harshness.
Open from 7:00 in the morning with a dedicated morning service of egg sandwiches, toast and hot dogs, reflecting its role as an early-day neighborhood gathering spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (omurice sauces, curry, doria, gratin, pasta, toast, sandwiches) are wheat-based; no gluten-free menu or separate preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on egg, dairy and beef dishes; no vegan options or plant-based preparation are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, regulars-filled neighborhood kissaten where the menu and service are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or pointing at the omurice will help; warm and welcoming once you're seated.

places.name: すき焼きと牛まぶし ももしき (Sukiyaki to Gyumabushi Momoshiki)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature sukiyaki broth is made with aged soy sauce (which contains wheat) plus shiitake and kombu, and gyu-mabushi is served over rice with soy-based seasonings. No gluten-free accommodations are documented, so celiac diners should avoid it.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Yamato wagyu beef specialist built around sukiyaki and beef rice bowls; the broth and core dishes are meat- and fish-based, and no vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00) daily; no regular closed day
places.neighborhood: Higashimuki-Nakamachi, near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: A Yamato-beef sukiyaki house created to turn a trip to Nara into lasting memories, conceived around the idea of reimagining the dining of Japan's ancient capital — when the region was called Yamato — in a contemporary way.

chef_bio.origin: Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station in the Higashimuki shopping arcade area
chef_bio.background: A Yamato-beef sukiyaki house created to turn a trip to Nara into lasting memories, conceived around the idea of reimagining the dining of Japan's ancient capital — when the region was called Yamato — in a contemporary way.
chef_bio.philosophy: To convey the charm of Nara through sukiyaki, expressing respect for the region by showcasing local Nara ingredients in cuisine that echoes ancient imperial dining traditions.
chef_bio.specialty: Premium Yamato beef sukiyaki using A4-ranked female cattle from a heritage Nara breed documented for centuries, simmered in a house-made broth of aged soy sauce, dried shiitake and kombu; and gyu-mabushi beef rice bowls featuring seared beef tataki, shigure-ni style, and grilled loin. Seasonal dishes pair the beef with local Nara produce such as Momotaro tomatoes.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sources what it calls 'Nara Star Ingredients' — local vegetables and seafood from regional producers — to complement its Yamato beef.
A sister location, Momoshiki Hanare, opened in July 2025, extending the same Yamato-beef sukiyaki concept nearby.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature sukiyaki broth is made with aged soy sauce, which contains wheat, so the core dishes are not safe for those avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on wagyu beef with meat- and fish-based broths and seasonings; no vegan preparations are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished but welcoming wagyu restaurant a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station with online booking and English-friendly tourist traffic; a few words of Japanese and a reservation make a visit smooth for a foreign student.

places.name: 田舎料理 畔（ホトリ） (Inaka Ryori Hotori)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The rotating lunch set centers on fried mains (e.g., fried horse mackerel, white fish, shrimp-and-shiitake croquettes) that use wheat batter/breading, plus miso soup and pickles that typically involve wheat-based miso and soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling is documented. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward but not vegetarian or vegan. The kitchen follows the "mago wa yasashii" principle, in which the "sa" stands for fish (魚), and the menu explicitly includes fish and meat dishes such as fried horse mackerel, seared tuna, pork mille-feuille, and braised hamburger. Side dishes and miso soup almost certainly use fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian set is documented; a vegan would need to ask in advance and likely could not be accommodated.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (lunch only). Open Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat; closed Wed, Thu, Sun. Food quantities are limited, so advance inquiry/reservation is recommended.
places.neighborhood: きたまち / Kawakami-cho, along the Saho River behind Todai-ji, w
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner spent about 35 years working as a childcare worker before opening this lunch spot to fulfill a long-held dream. She describes it modestly as a completely amateur venture devoted to vegetable-centered home cooking.

chef_bio.origin: The restaurant occupies a renovated old farmhouse on the bank of the Saho River, behind Todai-ji in the quiet Kawakami-cho area; its name, Hotori, means 'the banks (of the river).' The space is filled with old farm tools and antique furniture, evoking the feeling of visiting a grandmother's house in the countryside.
chef_bio.background: The owner spent about 35 years working as a childcare worker before opening this lunch spot to fulfill a long-held dream. She describes it modestly as a completely amateur venture devoted to vegetable-centered home cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking follows the Japanese 'mago wa yasashii' principle of balanced eating across beans, seeds, seaweed, vegetables, fish, mushrooms, and root vegetables. The owner uses homegrown produce and seasonal mountain greens, and sun-dries ingredients like daikon and taro to concentrate their nutrition, aiming for simple, gentle flavors that are kind to the body.
chef_bio.specialty: A single daily lunch set (with a smaller-portion option). It pairs a choice of rotating mains with five small vegetable side dishes, miso soup, pickles, and locally grown Hinohikari rice with free refills. Mains rotate among fried and braised dishes such as fried horse mackerel, white fish fries, shrimp-and-shiitake croquettes, seared tuna, pork mille-feuille, and braised hamburger.

anecdotes.text: Rice is the local Hinohikari variety grown with spring water from near the World Heritage Kasuga shrine area, and rice refills are free.
Vegetables such as daikon and taro are dried before cooking to enhance their nutritional value, reflecting the owner's focus on health and traditional preservation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Set lunches regularly feature deep-fried mains (fried horse mackerel, white fish fries, croquettes) made with wheat batter/breading, with no indication of a separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes include soy-marinated and braised items (e.g., soy-soaked tuna, braised hamburger) and miso soup, which in standard Japanese home cooking use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen's 'mago wa yasashii' approach intentionally includes fish, and mains include fish, tuna, pork, and beef, with side dishes and miso soup likely made with fish dashi.

positives.text: Cooking is strongly vegetable- and produce-forward, using homegrown and seasonal Yamato-area vegetables and a balanced, health-minded menu philosophy.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny owner-run farmhouse lunch spot where almost everything is in Japanese and portions are limited, so calling ahead in Japanese to reserve and being comfortable with a simple set menu will make the visit much smoother.

places.name: みりあむ (Miriam)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings or accommodations are documented. The kitchen centers on curry served over rice plus homemade Western-style cakes and pastries (gateau chocolat and other baked goods), all of which are wheat-based. No celiac or cross-contamination handling is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature curry is built on slow-simmered beef brisket and is served with plain yogurt; menus also feature a creamy chicken stew and dairy-rich homemade cakes. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Ratatouille appears as a seasonal summer item but its preparation is not described as vegan.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–19:30; closed Monday and Tuesday
places.neighborhood: Takabatake (near Nara University of Education, walkable from
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-loved retro coffee house in Nara's quiet Takabatake neighborhood, near Nara University of Education and within reach of Kasuga Taisha and Shin-Yakushiji. The cafe was founded decades ago by a Japanese proprietor named Sakamoto, who built its reputation on a distinctive cur

chef_bio.background: A long-loved retro coffee house in Nara's quiet Takabatake neighborhood, near Nara University of Education and within reach of Kasuga Taisha and Shin-Yakushiji. The cafe was founded decades ago by a Japanese proprietor named Sakamoto, who built its reputation on a distinctive curry. After his passing, his French wife, Miriam — for whom the shop is named — has continued to run it, keeping its character intact inside a hand-renovated old wooden house.
chef_bio.philosophy: A deliberately tranquil room with vintage furnishings and pendant lamps and no background music, made for slow, unhurried meals.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is a fragrant Indian-style beef curry: chunks of beef brisket simmered with eight spices for about three hours and then rested a full day, served over rice with raisins and a side of plain yogurt that diners can stir in to soften the heat. A creamy chicken stew is also offered. Miriam bakes homemade Western-style cakes and pastries daily — including gateau chocolat — which draw their own devoted regulars, with seasonal touches like ratatouille in summer.

anecdotes.text: The curry recipe calls for eight spices simmered for three hours and then left to rest a full day; raisins are offered alongside to act like a palate-cleansing accent and yogurt to mellow the spice.
The husband traditionally made the curry while his French wife baked the cakes and pastries; after his passing she carries the shop on, and her understated baked goods bring visitors in on their own.

positives.text: Yogurt is served on the side of the curry so diners can adjust the spice level to taste.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, calm family-run cafe where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the owner is French, so basic communication is easy, but it is a quiet local spot rather than a tourist-oriented place.

places.name: あるるかん (Arurukan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional Western-style diner whose core dishes — hamburg steak with demi-glace sauce, beef cutlet (katsu), and curry rice — are built on wheat. Demi-glace and curry roux are flour-based, and cutlets are breaded; there is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on meat dishes (hamburg steak, beef filet cutlet, steak) and the lunch sets include consommé and coffee; sauces are meat- and dairy-based demi-glace. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Thursdays, with occasional irregular closures. Note: the shopping-arcade and lunch listings cite lunch running to 15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner L.O. 20:40, so the lunch cutoff may extend later than the Tabelog figure.
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara St
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing, family-run Western-style restaurant tucked inside the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The dining room is built in a cozy mountain-cabin (yamagoya) style with a retro, old-fashioned atmosphere that feels worlds away from

chef_bio.origin: Nara, in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai arcade leading toward the old Naramachi district.
chef_bio.background: A long-standing, family-run Western-style restaurant tucked inside the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The dining room is built in a cozy mountain-cabin (yamagoya) style with a retro, old-fashioned atmosphere that feels worlds away from the tourist bustle outside.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand — 'all-handmade, warm-hearted cooking' built around quality meat and seasonal ingredients, with wine and other drinks recommended as pairings.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made hamburg steak in an authentic demi-glace sauce, served as a set with salad, consommé, rice and coffee; also known for beef filet cutlet, steak, and homestyle curry rice.

anecdotes.text: Visitors note that the demi-glace sauce on the hamburg steak is surprisingly authentic and delicious for a spot in a tourist area, served over a roughly 150g patty in a tidy, welcoming room.
The place appears to be run by the family themselves, which gives it a warm, familiar feeling for solo diners and couples alike.
Described on the local arcade's own page as an old-fashioned European-Western eatery with a calming mountain-cabin interior, emphasizing fully handmade dishes and quality meat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes rely on flour-based demi-glace sauce and breaded cutlets, so wheat is pervasive across the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is meat- and dairy-centric (hamburg steak, demi-glace, consommé, beef cutlet); no vegan options or separate preparation are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-run neighborhood diner where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and ordering are simple, but English support is unlikely, so bring cash and a little patience.

places.name: ハチノス（8nosu） (8nosu (Hachinosu))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is published. It is a soup curry rather than a thick wheat-roux curry, but there is no documented information on whether the spice blend, soup, or sides are wheat-free, and no cross-contamination details. A celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume it is safe.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetable-only curry exists (about ¥880-¥950), but the soup base is made with chicken-bone and dried-fish (niboshi) dashi, and the curries contain honey, so even the vegetable option is not vegan or strictly vegetarian. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Sources conflict. Tabelog: 11:30-15:00 and 17:30-20:00, closed Thursday. Official jimdo site: lunch 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:30), dinner 17:30-20:00 (LO 19:30), closed Fridays, additional closures via Instagram, closes when sold out. Narakko: weekdays 11:00-15:00 & 17:00-20:00, weekends/holidays 11:00-17:00, closed Tuesdays and 2nd & 4th Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi, Nara City (about 8 min walk from Kintetsu Nara St
places.notes: 8nosu is a small spice-curry-and-honey shop tucked into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's Naramachi district, a short walk south of Sarusawa Pond and Kofuku-ji Temple. It is run by a certified yakuzen (medicinal-herb cooking) coordinator who built the menu around the idea

chef_bio.background: 8nosu is a small spice-curry-and-honey shop tucked into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's Naramachi district, a short walk south of Sarusawa Pond and Kofuku-ji Temple. It is run by a certified yakuzen (medicinal-herb cooking) coordinator who built the menu around the idea of food as everyday wellness.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the motto 食べて元気になる - eat and become well - applying medicinal-herb principles so a casual curry lunch doubles as nourishment.
chef_bio.specialty: Stone-bowl soup curry built from about 20 spices and seasonal medicinal-herb vegetables. Diners choose a base (vegetable-only, soft chicken, or slow-braised pork), a soup style (original, coconut, or shrimp), and a spice level. House drinks include lassi, masala chai, and a honey-lemon squash made with domestic lemons and pure honey from Nara's Uda area (Ito-yu Beekeeping), which is also sold by the jar.

anecdotes.text: The curries are sweetened in part with honey, so the shop notes the dishes are not suitable for infants under one year old.
The honey served and sold is pure domestic honey from Ito-yu Beekeeping in the Uda area of Nara Prefecture.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The soup base for all curries, including the vegetable-only option, is made with chicken-bone and dried-fish (niboshi) dashi, so vegetable dishes share an animal-derived broth.
Curries contain honey.

positives.text: A vegetable-only medicinal-herb soup curry is offered as a standing menu choice (though it is not vegan due to the broth and honey).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, friendly Naramachi cafe used to tourists and international visitors; menu is build-your-own (base, soup, spice level, rice), so a few words of Japanese or pointing will go a long way.

places.name: メインダイニングルーム「三笠」（奈良ホテル） (Main Dining Room Mikasa (Nara Hotel))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. Classic French cuisine relies on flour-thickened sauces, bread, and pastry, and the kitchen states all dishes are prepared in the same kitchen so cross-contamination is possible. The hotel does offer allergy accommodation if you notify them well in advance of your reservation, so a celiac or gluten-avoidant diner should call ahead (0742-24-3044) and discuss specific needs, but this is not a celiac-safe environment by default.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant advertises ovo-lacto vegetarian course options (which include eggs and dairy, so not vegan) and builds menus around seasonal Yamato vegetables and local Nara produce. There is no documented fully vegan menu; French cooking here uses butter, cream, dairy and likely meat/fish stocks. Strict vegans should contact the restaurant in advance to ask what can be prepared, but should not assume vegan dishes are available off the standard menu.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良ホテル本館内）／奈良公園・荒池近く
places.notes: The main dining room of the Nara Hotel, which opened in 1909 in the Takabatake area beside Nara Park. The dining room has served guests since the hotel's founding and has hosted dignitaries and celebrities from Japan and abroad in a preserved Meiji–Taisho era setting.

chef_bio.chef_name: 乾 洋介 (Yosuke Inui), head chef
chef_bio.origin: Nara (奈良市高畑町), within the historic Nara Hotel
chef_bio.background: The main dining room of the Nara Hotel, which opened in 1909 in the Takabatake area beside Nara Park. The dining room has served guests since the hotel's founding and has hosted dignitaries and celebrities from Japan and abroad in a preserved Meiji–Taisho era setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: The head chef describes the goal as French cuisine that brings smiles and emotion befitting the tradition and history of the Nara Hotel (伝統と歴史を培った奈良ホテルに相応しい笑顔と感動を与えるフランス料理).
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional French cuisine built on seasonal Yamato vegetables and local Nara ingredients, with recipes handed down through successive head chefs since the hotel opened.

anecdotes.text: The dining room has been part of the Nara Hotel since its 1909 founding and carries on traditional French cuisine inherited from successive head chefs, served in a preserved Meiji–Taisho-era room.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All dishes are prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contamination is possible.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian offering is ovo-lacto (includes eggs and dairy) and shares the same kitchen as meat/fish dishes, so cross-contact is possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodation is available but requires advance notice when booking.

positives.text: Offers ovo-lacto vegetarian course options and menus featuring seasonal Yamato vegetables.

cultural_comfort.note: As the flagship dining room of a historic international hotel, the staff are used to overseas guests and can handle English, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably — though it is a formal, special-occasion-priced setting rather than a casual everyday spot.

places.name: ナカムラヤ (Nakamuraya)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The menu centers on Italian cooking with house-made pasta such as tortelloni, so most dishes contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is documented; a strictly gluten-free or celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safe options.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented restaurant. The cuisine is meat-, fish-, and dairy-forward (game terrines, acqua pazza fish, butter-sauce pasta, cheese). No vegan menu is documented; a vegan diner would need to call ahead, and options would likely be very limited.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 20:00); wine bar/café service throughout. Irregular closing days (不定休).
places.neighborhood: もちいどの商店街エリア／ならまち（near Kintetsu Nara Station, ~6 min walk）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Kohei Nakamura trained at a Tokyo restaurant before apprenticing in Italy, where he learned cooking across both mountainous central regions and the coastal north. He is also a certified sommelier and returns to Italy each year to research current flavors and trends so

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Kohei Nakamura trained at a Tokyo restaurant before apprenticing in Italy, where he learned cooking across both mountainous central regions and the coastal north. He is also a certified sommelier and returns to Italy each year to research current flavors and trends so he can serve them in Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: To bring the true taste of Italy to Nara's old town - 'to provide the taste of the authentic place as much as possible.' The venue is split into a casual front wine bar and cafe and a more formal dining room in back, letting guests choose their experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Authentic regional Italian cooking using carefully chosen seasonal ingredients: house-made tortelloni in sage butter, fresh fish acqua pazza, steamed squid, and winter game such as a young-wild-boar and foie gras terrine made with meat from Tsuge Village near the Nara-Mie border, marinated in Italian dried-grape spirit and served with red-wine gelee and walnuts. Around 200 Italian wines, including Piedmont Barolo and Tuscan Brunello, served in glassware matched to each pour.

anecdotes.text: The front bar functions as a wine bar and cafe with an Italian barista, Andrea Picchioni, manning the espresso counter, while the back room serves refined course meals on Limoges porcelain with Riedel glassware and Baccarat tumblers.
For winter dishes the chef sources game meat from Tsuge Village near the Nara-Mie prefectural border, reflecting a blend of authentic Italian technique and local Nara ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm but refined Italian spot where a few words of Japanese help; an Italian barista works the front bar, so basic communication is manageable, but the dinner courses run higher in price than a casual student meal.

places.name: ペロンチョ (PE LONCHO)
places.gf_detail: This is a teppan hamburger and steak house. Hamburger patties contain binders and the dishes are served with onion/red-wine sauces; no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no documented celiac protocols. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-focused restaurant whose entire concept is coarse-ground beef/pork hamburger steak and rare beef steak. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily except Tuesday; Dinner 18:00–21:30 (L.O. 20:30) Wed–Sun & holidays. Monday is lunch only. Tuesday closed. Some irregular/event closures and year-end/New Year holidays.
places.neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち東側・奈良公園寄り） / Takabatake, near Naramachi and Nara Par
places.cuisine: 洋食・ステーキ・粗挽きハンバーグ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] PE LONCHO opened in December 2019 in the Takabatake area at the eastern end of Naramachi-dori, closer to Nara Park. It is a small, independent teppan (iron-griddle) restaurant built around two signature plates: a thick coarse-ground hamburger steak and a thick-cut, rare beef stea

chef_bio.background: PE LONCHO opened in December 2019 in the Takabatake area at the eastern end of Naramachi-dori, closer to Nara Park. It is a small, independent teppan (iron-griddle) restaurant built around two signature plates: a thick coarse-ground hamburger steak and a thick-cut, rare beef steak, each served sizzling on a hot iron plate.
chef_bio.specialty: Coarse-ground teppan hamburger steak and rare beef steak (around 250g portions). The hamburger blends Yamato beef, chuck roll and Kazaguruma pork in a secret ratio, finished with a sauce built on plenty of onions and red wine.

anecdotes.text: The signature hamburger recipe is said to have been handed down from a well-known Italian-cuisine chef in the Sangenjaya area of Tokyo.
The kitchen serves a limited number of meals per day (around 50), reflecting a small, hands-on operation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hamburger steak is a core dish and typically contains wheat-based binders; sauces are onion/red-wine based with no stated gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a beef/pork teppan concept (hamburger and steak); no vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local teppan spot where ordering is smoother in Japanese, and because mains must be reserved in advance to secure stock, a foreign student should phone or message ahead (ideally with a Japanese-speaking friend).

places.name: toi印食店 (toi Inshokuten)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no celiac protocol is documented. The meals plate centers on rice (jasmine/Nara rice) and many spice-based vegetable sides, which can lean naturally wheat-free, but fried items such as pakoda fritters and papad (lentil wafers) may contain or be cross-contacted by wheat, and the daily curries are made in a shared kitchen with no dedicated equipment noted. A celiac traveler must ask in person about each component; nothing specific is published.
places.vegan_detail: The fixed meals format pairs a choice of curries (which on most days include meat/fish options such as fish, mutton, chicken or pork curries) with a large spread of roughly eight vegetable sides — e.g. coconut-stir-fried carrots, mustard-simmered yam and taro, leafy-green and red-radish kachumbar salad, dal, achar pickles and chutneys. A vegetable curry is usually available so a largely plant-based meals plate can be assembled, but the kitchen has not published whether dals/sides use ghee, dairy or any fish/animal stock, and it is not a dedicated vegetarian/vegan kitchen. Vegans should confirm dairy (ghee/yogurt) and stock in person.
places.neighborhood: きたまち / 花芝町 (near Kintetsu Nara Station north side)
places.cuisine: South/North Indian spice curry and meals
places.notes: The owner-chef honed his craft at established Nara Indian and curry kitchens — including time as chef at an Indian cafe-restaurant in Tomigaoka and a monthly pop-up at a spot in Ayameike — and made a multi-week journey through India to deepen his feel for regional cooking before

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef honed his craft at established Nara Indian and curry kitchens — including time as chef at an Indian cafe-restaurant in Tomigaoka and a monthly pop-up at a spot in Ayameike — and made a multi-week journey through India to deepen his feel for regional cooking before opening his own place in February 2021 in the Hanashiba-cho area just north of Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Indian food adapted to Nara's climate and local produce, plated like a jewel box but meant to be mixed and eaten by hand — the chef describes treating the plate as a palette and adjusting the flavors with your fingers as you go.
chef_bio.specialty: A daily-changing 'meals' (ミールス) plate: a choice of one to four curries that rotate each day — spanning South Indian chicken, Kerala-style seafood, vindaloo and vegetable curries — served around rice with about eight sides such as coconut-stir-fried carrots, mustard-simmered yam and taro, dal, kachumbar salad, achar pickles, chutneys, pakoda fritters and crisp lentil papad.

anecdotes.text: The chef frames authentic Indian dining as hands-on: '皿をパレットに見立て、指で調理しながら食べるのがインド流' — treat the plate as a palette and season as you eat with your fingers.
Local curry writers greeted the 2021 opening as 'a supernova born in Nara,' noting the chef's pedigree at earlier Nara kitchens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The plate includes fried pakoda fritters and crisp lentil papad/appalam, which commonly involve wheat or shared frying oil; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curries on most days include fish, mutton, chicken or pork options served from the same daily lineup as the vegetable curry; sides' use of ghee/dairy or any stock is not published.

positives.text: The fixed meals format includes a wide range of vegetable sides (coconut carrots, mustard yam and taro, dal, kachumbar salad, pickles, chutneys) so a largely plant-based plate can usually be assembled.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, popular counter shop with no reservations — write your name in the entry ledger and wait; a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, and the hand-eating Indian style is welcomed.

places.name: THE BURGER STAND MAVERICK（マーベリック） (The Burger Stand Maverick)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core product is a burger on a natural-yeast wheat bun (baked in a stone oven), and there is no documented gluten-free bun or accommodation. The signature Luther Burger uses a wheat donut in place of the bun. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A grass-fed beef burger shop; the menu is built around beef patties, bacon, cheese and egg. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); may close early when sold out
places.neighborhood: 京終〜近鉄奈良駅エリア（奈良女子大学北側・北袋町）
places.cuisine: グラスフェッドビーフのクラフトハンバーガー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent burger stand that began near Fukuchiin Temple in the southern part of Naramachi and in early 2025 relocated to a spot on the north side of Nara Women's University, a short walk from the heart of central Nara. The interior leans full Americana: counter seats an

chef_bio.origin: Naramachi (Fukuchiin-cho), then relocated to Kitabukuro-cho, Nara City
chef_bio.background: A small independent burger stand that began near Fukuchiin Temple in the southern part of Naramachi and in early 2025 relocated to a spot on the north side of Nara Women's University, a short walk from the heart of central Nara. The interior leans full Americana: counter seats and a couple of tables, oldies playing, and American-themed décor on the walls.
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to lean, firm grass-fed beef and from-scratch buns, served in an unpretentious, all-American counter setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Craft hamburgers built on patties made exclusively from grass-fed beef, served on natural-yeast buns slow-baked in a stone oven until the surface turns crisp. Standouts include the Cheese Burger, the Bacon Egg Cheese Burger, and the playful Luther Burger, which swaps the bun for a house-made glazed donut layered with bacon and cheese for a sweet-and-savory bite. Burgers come in a classic style with lettuce and tomato or a bread-and-meat-forward American style, and arrive with a side salad.

anecdotes.text: The shop's mascot, Maverick-kun, is said to have a hobby of collecting insects.
The Luther Burger sandwiches the patty between a self-made sugar-glazed donut with bacon and cheese, and reviewers describe its sweet-savory balance as addictive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Teriyaki Burger uses a Japanese soy-based sauce, which typically contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: An easygoing American-style burger stand with an English menu, so a foreign student can order comfortably; just bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: カフェ タンネ（Cafe TANNE） (Cafe TANNE)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. Some menu items lean naturally lower-gluten — buckwheat (soba) pancakes and sprouted brown rice bread — but the café is wheat-using overall (apple pie crust, pastries, pasta), buckwheat pancakes/galettes in Japan are commonly blended with wheat flour, and there is no documented dedicated prep, gluten-free flour sourcing, or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner would need to ask directly about each item; assume shared surfaces and possible wheat in batters.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The kitchen uses animal products throughout — grilled fish and miso soup in the Japanese morning set, ice cream/whipped cream with pancakes, and butter/eggs/dairy in the apple pie and baked sweets. No documented vegan menu or vegan-specific items. A plant-based diner would need to ask what, if anything, can be prepared without animal products; options are likely very limited.
places.hours_raw: 8:00–18:00 (morning service 8:00–10:30, lunch from 11:00). Closed Thursdays per the local shopping-street listing; some listings note irregular closures and year-round operation, so confirm before a special trip.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・下御門町（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe TANNE is a townhouse café on the Shimomikado shopping street, on the edge of Nara's historic Naramachi district. Its owner spent about 17 years living in Togakushi in Shinshu (Nagano) before returning to their Nara hometown to open the café. The name 'Tanne' means fir tree a

chef_bio.origin: Nara (owner's hometown), with strong ties to Togakushi/Shinshu, Nagano
chef_bio.background: Cafe TANNE is a townhouse café on the Shimomikado shopping street, on the edge of Nara's historic Naramachi district. Its owner spent about 17 years living in Togakushi in Shinshu (Nagano) before returning to their Nara hometown to open the café. The name 'Tanne' means fir tree and carries the owner's affection for Togakushi, described as a second home.
chef_bio.philosophy: Gentle, body-friendly cooking built around seasonal ingredients — many sourced from Nagano — served in a warm, relaxed space aimed at adults who want a calm, nourishing meal or coffee break.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade health-conscious sweets and light meals: a Shinshu apple pie made with Kogyoku (Jonathan) red apples, pancakes made with sprouted brown rice, hot sandwiches on sprouted brown rice bread, and a set-style 'Tanne Lunch.' The sweets menu runs to roughly fifteen items.

anecdotes.text: The owner lived in Togakushi, Shinshu for about 17 years before returning to their Nara hometown to open the café; 'Tanne' means fir tree and expresses their lasting affection for Togakushi as a second home.
Mornings offer a homestyle Japanese set with freshly cooked rice, vegetable miso soup, braised vegetables, grilled fish and pickled plum, alongside Western morning options.

positives.text: Some menu items are built on naturally lower-gluten bases such as buckwheat (soba) pancakes and sprouted brown rice bread, which may give flexible (non-celiac) gluten-avoiders something to work with — confirm ingredients with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, owner-run neighborhood café where a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way; English support is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.

places.name: こはく。 (Cohcu (Kohaku))
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery. Bread is the core product (hard-crust loaves, filled breads, bagels, sandwiches), so it is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners. No gluten-free items are documented.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional bakery whose best-known items contain dairy and egg (the signature cream pan "Kobako" is filled with cold custard cream; other items include curry bread and sandwiches). Plain hard-crust breads may be egg/dairy-free but this is not confirmed; a vegan diner would need to ask about specific loaves. No explicitly vegan items are documented.
places.hours_raw: 8:00頃～売り切れ次第終了（営業時間・在庫はSNS等で告知）。定休日：水曜、火曜は不定休。
