places.neighborhood: Horen-cho, Nara (near Ichijo High School, north of Shin-Omiy
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent neighborhood bakery tucked into a quiet residential block of Horen-cho in northern Nara, near Ichijo High School and a short walk from Shin-Omiya Station. The plain, unassuming storefront barely looks like a bakery from outside, but inside it is packed with a

chef_bio.background: A small, independent neighborhood bakery tucked into a quiet residential block of Horen-cho in northern Nara, near Ichijo High School and a short walk from Shin-Omiya Station. The plain, unassuming storefront barely looks like a bakery from outside, but inside it is packed with around thirty kinds of bread laid out invitingly each day.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's own writing stresses making bread 'humbly and earnestly' (謙虚にそして真摯に) out of genuine love for the bread itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Hard-crust, dough-focused breads alongside generously filled breads. The signature item is a cream pan called 'Kobako' (こはく。のこばこ): the cold custard cream is piped in fresh at the moment of purchase, and it is meant to be eaten right away while the cream is still cold. Other beloved items include a local Yamato-beef curry bread and a sweet-potato cream Danish.

anecdotes.text: The bread is made in limited daily quantity and is famous for selling out fast, sometimes by just after midday, so regulars line up early.
The signature 'Kobako' cream pan has its cold cream added after you order, and is recommended to be eaten immediately while cold.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working wheat-flour bakery; flour is used throughout, making cross-contamination unavoidable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature and popular items contain dairy and egg (custard cream pan, curry bread, sandwiches); shared bakery equipment means egg/dairy contact is likely across products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood takeout bakery where ordering is simple point-and-pay; a friendly greeting in Japanese is plenty, though English is unlikely and items sell out by early afternoon.

places.name: ノール コーヒー ロースターズ (NORR Coffee Roasters)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The food menu centers on wheat-based baked goods such as financiers, pound cake, and Breton galettes, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners. No allergen handling or gluten-free items are mentioned on any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. As a coffee shop the black filter coffees and coffee tonic soda are plant-based, but milk-based drinks (cafe latte) and the baked sweets (financier, pound cake, galette) typically contain dairy and eggs. A vegan visitor could likely have a black coffee but should ask about everything else; no plant-milk options are confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Sundays only, approx. 13:00-18:00 (last order around 16:45). The owner works on weekdays, so the shop opens only on Sundays; an "öppet" (Swedish for "open") sign is displayed when trading. Note: an older listing shows weekday 11:00-16:30 and Sunday 11:00-17:00 hours, which appears to be outdated. Confirm via the shop's social media before visiting.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（北半田中町）
places.cuisine: Specialty coffee roastery and gallery cafe
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] NORR Coffee Roasters is a small self-roasting specialty coffee shop tucked into the quiet Kitamachi residential area in northern central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner keeps a weekday job and opens the shop only on Sundays, hanging out an 'öppet' sign —

chef_bio.background: NORR Coffee Roasters is a small self-roasting specialty coffee shop tucked into the quiet Kitamachi residential area in northern central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner keeps a weekday job and opens the shop only on Sundays, hanging out an 'öppet' sign — Swedish for 'open' — when trading. The name NORR means 'north' in Swedish, a nod to the shop's location in the northern Kitamachi district and its Nordic-inflected sensibility.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uses 100% specialty-grade beans, roasting to emphasize each coffee's natural sweetness, and runs the space as both a roastery cafe and a rotating art gallery for a slow, leisurely atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted single-origin specialty coffees (four varieties available hot or iced, from origins such as Brazil, Honduras, Guatemala, Indonesia, El Salvador, Tanzania, Rwanda and more) plus a house 'NORR Blend' roasted to bring out natural sweetness. Also coffee tonic soda and frozen coffee, alongside homemade-style baked sweets such as financiers, pound cake and Breton galettes.

anecdotes.text: The space doubles as a gallery, with rotating artwork, three single seats by the entrance, a mezzanine with tables upstairs, and a semi-underground terrace facing a courtyard.
Limited-edition coffee packages are released to celebrate local Nara events such as the Todaiji water ceremony and the Wakakusayama grass-burning festival.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, relaxed Nordic-themed coffee shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff may not speak much English and the Sunday-only hours mean you should check before making the trip.

places.name: 地酒BAR"なら" (Jizake BAR "Nara" (Sake BAR NARA))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. Sake itself is rice-based and naturally wheat-free, so the core tasting flights are fine for someone avoiding wheat. However the food snacks are a different matter: cured/smoked items and seasonings commonly involve soy sauce (which contains wheat) and there is no allergen labeling or celiac handling here. Treat it as a drinking spot where the sake is the safe item and any food should be questioned individually. Not a celiac-safe kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Primarily a sake bar; the food leans heavily on game meat (house-made venison jerky, wild boar smoked ham) and other animal products (smoked eggs, sweetfish). A few plant-based snacks exist — smoked tofu and pickled vegetables (漬物) — but these are likely seasoned with non-vegan dashi or soy-based marinades and are not advertised as vegan. The sake flights are the main vegan-friendly draw. Suitable for a vegan only as a place to drink with a token plant snack, not for a meal.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (Tuesdays 11:00–15:00). Hours have varied over time (earlier listings showed 10:00–18:00 / 11:00–17:00); confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake (near Nara Hotel), Nara
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This standing sake bar sits on the ground floor of Nara Nagomi-kan, a souvenir hall facing the historic Nara Hotel in the Takabatake district. The hall once thrived on bus tours and school excursions, but when the pandemic erased that crowd in 2020, the director, Yamaji Junichi,

chef_bio.background: This standing sake bar sits on the ground floor of Nara Nagomi-kan, a souvenir hall facing the historic Nara Hotel in the Takabatake district. The hall once thrived on bus tours and school excursions, but when the pandemic erased that crowd in 2020, the director, Yamaji Junichi, shrank the gift-shop floor and reinvented the space as a place to drink. The bar opened on April 29, 2021, drawing on know-how from a sister project, a sake bar in Tohoku that had been running since 2013 as part of the region's earthquake-recovery efforts.
chef_bio.philosophy: The bar deliberately serves two crowds at once: an everyday neighborhood stop for locals, and a way for travelers to taste Nara's breweries and make a memory of the trip.
chef_bio.specialty: Nara and Tohoku local sake tasting flights

anecdotes.text: The bar is a COVID-era reinvention: with tour buses and school trips gone, Nagomi-kan downsized its souvenir section and opened a standing sake bar in its place in spring 2021.
The chef hunts the game himself, turning venison and wild boar into house-made jerky and smoked ham served as sake pairings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Snacks include cured, smoked, and pickled items typical of a sake bar, which commonly use soy-sauce-based seasonings (soy sauce normally contains wheat); no gluten-free claims are made.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food program centers on house-made venison and wild boar, so plant snacks like smoked tofu and pickles are prepared in a meat-handling environment.

positives.text: Sake is rice-based and naturally free of wheat and animal products, so the tasting flights are inherently safe for both wheat-avoidant and vegan drinkers.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual standing sake bar inside a tourist-friendly souvenir hall near Nara Hotel where tasting sets are priced for walk-ins, so a foreign student can comfortably point at a flight and enjoy it with a few words of Japanese.

places.name: 七福食堂／喫茶古白 (Shichifuku Shokudo / Kissa Kohaku)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The monthly lunch sets are built around dishes such as tempura, chilled wheat noodles, fu (wheat gluten / seitan) and seasonal Japanese home cooking, and the menu uses standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, etc.) that contain wheat. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free preparation. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian place. The monthly teishoku centers on meat and fish — e.g. pork Yanagawa-style hotpot, shrimp/mushroom chirashi sushi, mozuku vinegar — and most dishes use fish dashi. Vegetable side dishes (namul, seasonal Yamato/local produce) and the parfaits/coffee exist but are not prepared as guaranteed vegan, and dashi/honey/dairy are likely present. A vegan traveler would have very limited safe options and should ask directly.
places.hours_raw: Lunch (set meals) 11:30–15:00; cafe 11:30–17:30 (L.O. around 17:00). Thursdays cafe only. Reported closed days vary slightly between sources (Tue/Wed, or Tue/Wed/Thu), with additional irregular closures announced on social media. Open on holidays.
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (ならまち), Narukawa-chō, in front of Tokuyū-ji temple
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sakai grew up in Saitama with little exposure to temples until a middle-school trip to Kyoto, where the fierce, dynamic Buddhist statues at Toji (the Five Great Wisdom Kings) lit a lifelong fascination. He chose Nara University specifically for its cultural-heritage program and i

chef_bio.origin: Saitama Prefecture; moved to Nara in 2007 to study Buddhist art at Nara University
chef_bio.background: Sakai grew up in Saitama with little exposure to temples until a middle-school trip to Kyoto, where the fierce, dynamic Buddhist statues at Toji (the Five Great Wisdom Kings) lit a lifelong fascination. He chose Nara University specifically for its cultural-heritage program and its closeness to countless Buddhist statues, then decided to make Nara his permanent home. He runs the guesthouse 古白 (Kohaku, started 2015), which offers Buddhist-statue talks and sukiyaki dinners, added a cafe and renamed it 古白 in 2018, and opened 七福食堂 in 2020 to reach a wider audience.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sakai talks about wanting to 'light the spark' that draws people into Nara's charm, treating a lodging, a cafe or a plate of food as an inviting entry point. He even compares a beautiful parfait to a Buddhist statue: both are tangible, attractive forms that can lead someone toward a deeper appreciation of Nara's culture.
chef_bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating teishoku (set lunch) of seasonal Japanese home cooking — past menus have featured pork Yanagawa-style hotpot with a soft-set egg, shrimp-and-mushroom chirashi sushi, tempura, miso-glazed fu, chilled noodles, mozuku vinegar and colorful namul — alongside seasonal parfaits (such as rum-raisin and Japanese-chestnut Mont Blanc, or caramel-nut and baked-apple) and house-roasted drip coffee.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a renovated former printing workshop, with a high ceiling, a glass frontage, antique tansu chests and old dishware that give the small room a calm, lived-in feel.
Sakai compares an enticing parfait to a Buddhist statue — a visible 'entry point' meant to spark curiosity in people (even high-schoolers) who might otherwise never engage with Nara's heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Lunch sets regularly include wheat-based items (tempura, chilled wheat noodles, miso-glazed fu/seitan), so wheat is handled throughout the small kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso glaze) which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is meat- and fish-centered (pork hotpot, shrimp chirashi, mozuku) and most dishes use fish dashi, so vegan separation is not maintained.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes seasonal local and Yamato/Nara-area vegetables (e.g. produce from a farm in Ikoma), so plant-forward side dishes are part of the meal even though they are not certified vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run shokudo-cafe in old Naramachi where the menu changes monthly and is mostly posted in Japanese on Instagram, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing and a friendly konnichiwa) will go a long way.

places.name: しかさんしょこら（Shika san chocolat） (Shika san chocolat)
places.gf_detail: The shop's signature and essentially sole product is a gateau au chocolat made deliberately without wheat flour or rice flour, so it is naturally gluten-free. The official site and a local news feature both describe it as グルテンフリー / made with no flour. This is not a certified celiac-dedicated facility and there is no published statement about cross-contamination controls, so highly gluten-sensitive visitors should still confirm in person, but the core product genuinely contains no gluten grains.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The gateau au chocolat is made with butter, fresh cream, and eggs (chocolate cake), so it contains dairy and egg. No plant-based version is documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri); 10:00-19:00 (Sat, Sun). Closed Thursdays. (Official site lists 10:00-19:00; Tabelog lists 10:00-18:00 weekdays / 10:00-19:00 weekends.)
places.neighborhood: Mochiidono-cho / Mochiidono Center-gai (Naramachi area), abo
places.notes: Shika san chocolat opened in late 2023 as a one-product specialty stand inside the Yume CUBE, the entrepreneur-support corner of Nara's historic Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade. Its owner runs it as a second venture alongside Milleturu, a women's tailoring atelier based in

chef_bio.background: Shika san chocolat opened in late 2023 as a one-product specialty stand inside the Yume CUBE, the entrepreneur-support corner of Nara's historic Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade. Its owner runs it as a second venture alongside Milleturu, a women's tailoring atelier based in nearby Yamato-Koriyama, and spent more than a decade perfecting a single idea: a gateau au chocolat unlike any other.
chef_bio.philosophy: The maker set out to create 'a new standard no one has tasted before,' framing the chocolate-to-sansho transition as a journey ending in Nara, the terminus of the Silk Road. The flourless approach is central to the recipe rather than an afterthought.
chef_bio.specialty: A sansho (Japanese mountain pepper) gateau au chocolat — a rich-but-clean-finishing, fluffy-yet-moist chocolate cake in which the chocolate slowly gives way to bright Japanese-pepper notes. It is made entirely without wheat flour or rice flour. Sold by the cut, half, and whole cake; a plain version without sansho is also offered for those who prefer it.

anecdotes.text: The signature sansho gateau au chocolat was developed over roughly 15 years of trial and error, using powdered Japanese pepper, before the shop opened in 2023.
The owner also runs a women's tailoring business, Milleturu, in Yamato-Koriyama, and treats the chocolate shop as a parallel creative project.
Staff are known for offering free taste samples and explaining the cake to passers-by at the stall.

positives.text: The signature gateau au chocolat is made with no wheat flour and no rice flour, so the core product is naturally gluten-free by recipe.
A local news feature independently confirms the cake is made without wheat or rice flour and is naturally gluten-free.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, friendly takeout stall where staff happily offer free samples and explain the cake, so a few words of Japanese and pointing will carry you through a quick, low-pressure purchase.

places.name: 懐石料理 円 (Kaiseki Ryori En (YEN))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature dish is sake-lees tempura (酒粕の天ぷら — wheat batter), and summer menus feature sōmen (wheat) noodles. Cooking is built on fish/kombu dashi and soy-based seasonings. No documented gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free menu. Celiac travelers should treat it as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is vegetable-centric and the official site explicitly advertises vegetarian guidance, so plant-forward meals are possible with advance reservation, but the default kaiseki uses fish dashi and includes fish/egg dishes; vegans must call ahead to confirm.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（もちいどの・下御門商店街2階／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約8分）
places.notes: A small, independent kaiseki house in Nara's Naramachi district run by its proprietor for more than 30 years, on the second floor of a building along the Shimo-mikado arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.

chef_bio.background: A small, independent kaiseki house in Nara's Naramachi district run by its proprietor for more than 30 years, on the second floor of a building along the Shimo-mikado arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Letting Nara's seasonal vegetables and local Yamato specialties shine; menus rotate with the seasons. The owner recently added a weekend breakfast in response to demand from nearby guesthouses.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward seasonal kaiseki at an approachable price. Two signature dishes are sake-lees tempura and miso-marinated tofu. It is also one of the few places in Nara still serving the local specialty chagayu (tea-steeped rice porridge), offered as a set and as a swap for plain rice at breakfast.

anecdotes.text: A diner praised the smooth feel of the tea porridge and the careful knife work in the thoughtfully cut vegetables.
The breakfast was introduced in response to requests from nearby guesthouse guests wanting an early seasonal Nara meal.
Described as a calm, hidden upstairs space with no background music and traditional decor including armor displays and fresh flowers, with windows over Naramachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dish is sake-lees tempura (wheat batter) and summer menus include somen (wheat noodles); cooking relies on soy-based seasonings and fish/kombu dashi. No gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard kaiseki includes fish dishes (salted grilled mackerel) and dashi/egg; the kitchen is not vegan by default, so vegans must request plant-only preparation in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site advertises guidance for vegetarian guests; reservations by phone with menu choice stated in advance are recommended, allowing accommodation to be discussed ahead.

positives.text: Vegetable-centric seasonal cooking and explicit willingness to provide vegetarian guidance, with advance phone reservation, make plant-forward meals feasible.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, traditional family-run kaiseki spot where the proprietor cooks and serves personally; phone ahead in basic Japanese to reserve and state your menu choice, and be comfortable in a calm, mostly Japanese-speaking setting.

places.name: 転害坊 (Tengaibou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The signature offerings include a New York-style cheesecake and other homemade baked sweets, which are wheat-based, and there is no documented allergen handling, so this is not a celiac-safe destination. A gluten-free traveler would need to ask in person (in Japanese) and treat anything baked as containing wheat.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. The focus is hand-roasted coffee plus homemade cakes such as a New York cheesecake (dairy/egg), so default options are not vegan. Black coffee is the only obviously plant-based item; anything else would need to be confirmed in person.
places.hours_raw: Thu–Sun 11:00–17:00; closed Mon, Tue, Wed (per Tabelog). A secondary write-up listed Fri–Sun 13:00–17:00, so day/time can vary — confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi / Imajikecho (near Todai-ji Tengaimon Gate)
places.cuisine: Self-roasted specialty coffee and homemade s
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tengaibou occupies a roughly 90-year-old machiya townhouse near Todai-ji's Tengaimon Gate that was originally a Chinese herbal-medicine pharmacy. The current owner bought and renovated the old building and revived its historic name, 転害坊, turning it into a quiet single-owner coffe

chef_bio.origin: Imajikecho, in Nara's Kitamachi district just north of the Todai-ji precinct.
chef_bio.background: Tengaibou occupies a roughly 90-year-old machiya townhouse near Todai-ji's Tengaimon Gate that was originally a Chinese herbal-medicine pharmacy. The current owner bought and renovated the old building and revived its historic name, 転害坊, turning it into a quiet single-owner coffee house.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sweets and drinks made with natural ingredients, served in a calm, unhurried space.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee from beans roasted in-house on the same day, drip-brewed to order, paired with a homemade Meyer lemon New York-style cheesecake designed to bring out the coffee's flavor. The shop also runs a hands-on session where visitors can roast green beans into their own coffee.

anecdotes.text: Although it sits close to busy Nara Station and the Nandaimon area, the interior is a surprisingly quiet, peaceful room that is uncrowded on weekdays, making it a good place to spend time alone with a book.

The owner inherited the historic shop name 転害坊 from the building's past life as a Chinese-medicine pharmacy and renovated the roughly 90-year-old townhouse to serve day-roasted coffee and a Meyer lemon NY cheesecake.

positives.text: Coffee is roasted in-house and brewed to order, and sweets are made by hand with natural ingredients, so the owner can describe exactly what is in each item if asked in person.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 8-seat owner-run machiya café where Japanese will carry you furthest; it is calm and welcoming, but bring cash and don't expect an English menu or English-speaking staff.

places.name: かき氷＆ウイスキー Gentry（ジェントリー） (Kakigori & Whisky Gentry)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature shaved ice is layered with components such as lemon jelly, azuki red beans, condensed milk and matcha meringue, with no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. Toppings and accompanying sweets are not verified as wheat-free, and there is no information about cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The famous shaved ice relies on dairy (condensed milk, white chocolate, custard, milk-tea cheese foam, puddings) and the matcha-meringue version is brûléed and finished with flambéed Cointreau. No plant-based versions are documented. As a whisky/cocktail bar, drinks are the other focus. Not a vegan destination.
places.neighborhood: 小西町 / Konishi-chō, central Nara near Kintetsu Nara Station (
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gentry first opened in April 1984 as an old-school jazz bar in central Nara, spinning jazz on vinyl and hosting occasional live performances. Over four decades it became beloved less for its records than for an unlikely signature: an elaborate flambéed shaved ice. After closing i

chef_bio.background: Gentry first opened in April 1984 as an old-school jazz bar in central Nara, spinning jazz on vinyl and hosting occasional live performances. Over four decades it became beloved less for its records than for an unlikely signature: an elaborate flambéed shaved ice. After closing its long-time home in the Shimomikado shopping street in April 2024 to the sadness of many regulars, it returned in October 2025 a few steps from Kintetsu Nara Station, now billed as Kakigori & Whisky Gentry, still built around its Swan ice shaver and jazz-bar mood.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is yaki-gōri, baked shaved ice: layers of azuki beans, lemon jelly and condensed milk under a matcha meringue that the owner blowtorches to a caramelized crust, then douses with flambéed Cointreau so the mountain of ice briefly catches fire, a nod to Nara's Wakakusa-yama grass-burning festival. Seasonal creations rotate through pistachio and white chocolate, pumpkin with pumpkin pudding, strawberry, and custard brûlée. By night it pours whisky, wine and cocktails.

anecdotes.text: The signature baked shaved ice is set alight tableside with flambéed orange liqueur, an edible homage to the Wakakusa-yama mountain-burning that lights up the Nara skyline each winter.
Founded in April 1984, the shop closed its original location in April 2024 and reopened on 23 October 2025 nearer the station; the owner is remembered by visitors for a bright, courteous manner.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, owner-run jazz bar where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo menu will get you the famous flambéed shaved ice; the master is known for being friendly and polite, so a beginner can manage comfortably.

places.name: 寧楽料理 こくとぅーら (Nara Ryori Coctura)
places.gf_detail: This is a creative izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on soy sauce, miso and dashi, plus cured meats, smoked items and pasta — all typical sources of wheat and gluten. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented anywhere. The kitchen is run single-handedly in a 9-seat counter space, so dedicated allergen control is unlikely. Not safe for celiacs; a gluten-sensitive diner would need to confirm directly in Japanese with the owner.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat, fish and seafood — Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, Yamato beef, homemade prosciutto and cured meats, smoked fish, squid, oysters and scallops — and seasoning relies on dashi (fish stock), soy sauce and miso. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu. A plant-based diner would have very limited options and should ask the owner directly.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri) 17:00-22:00; Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 15:00-22:00. Last order: food 21:00, drinks 21:30. Closed Wednesdays.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner ran a creative izakaya near JR Nara Station for roughly six years before relocating in January 2024 to a quietly renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) in the Naramachi district, near the Harushika sake brewery. He runs the entire counter single-handedly.

chef_bio.chef_name: 廣岡夏主 (Hirooka Natsushi, who goes by the nickname "Nassh")
chef_bio.background: The owner ran a creative izakaya near JR Nara Station for roughly six years before relocating in January 2024 to a quietly renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) in the Naramachi district, near the Harushika sake brewery. He runs the entire counter single-handedly.
chef_bio.philosophy: He builds dishes on a French, Italian and Spanish base and fuses them with Japanese seasonings — dashi, soy sauce and miso — describing his aim as expanding 'new possibilities in food,' an idea he links to the cultural mixing of the Silk Road that historically reached Nara.
chef_bio.specialty: Cold-smoking (冷燻): the homemade smoked assortment — which can include prosciutto, quail egg, oyster and scallop — is the most-ordered dish. Other signatures pair Nara produce with European technique, such as cold peach pasta with Yamato beef prosciutto, young-corn fritters with white-miso aioli, and a 'wa-hijo' (Japanese-style ajillo) of firefly squid and rape blossoms.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes a point of using Nara-region ingredients — Yamato pork, Yamato chicken and Yamato beef — alongside local sake and hand-pulled Miwa somen noodles from the Yamamoto producer.
An earlier black-rice curry of the owner's took second place in a 2010 curry competition.
The current space is a renovated townhouse with a single wooden-slab counter, sitting near the Harushika brewery and a well-known soba restaurant.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking is explicitly built on dashi, soy sauce and miso as its Japanese seasoning base, all of which typically contain or accompany wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 9-seat counter run solo by the owner in a renovated townhouse; basic Japanese helps a great deal as English service should not be assumed, and the intimate setting rewards diners comfortable with a quiet, local atmosphere.

places.name: カフェ酒場SomechoA (Cafe Sakaba SomechoA)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu centers on Korean dishes that are inherently wheat-heavy: yangnyeom chicken has a fried wheat-flour batter, tteokbokki and seafood pancakes (haemul pajeon) use wheat, and Korean sauces typically contain wheat-based soy sauce and gochujang. No dedicated fryer or allergy handling is documented, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (Korean fried chicken, seafood pancakes), and even sides like tteokbokki commonly use fish/anchovy-based broth and dairy (their tomato-cream tteokbokki). A vegan would need to ask in person, but options appear minimal and cross-contact with meat/seafood is likely.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Fri (and weekdays before holidays): 11:30–14:30 (lunch, L.O. 14:00), 17:00–00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30). Sat & holidays: 11:30–00:00 continuous. Closed Sun & Mon. Irregular closures occur — check Instagram.
places.neighborhood: 三条町（JR・近鉄奈良駅間、三条通エリア）
places.cuisine: カフェ・ダイニングバー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] SomechoA is a small Korean-style day cafe and night bar tucked along Sanjo-dori between JR and Kintetsu Nara stations. It is run by three siblings, and the name itself reflects that: in Korean 'Sam' means 'three' and 'choA' means 'like/love,' so the place is built around the trio

chef_bio.origin: Korea-inspired (Korean cafe and izakaya culture), in central Nara
chef_bio.background: SomechoA is a small Korean-style day cafe and night bar tucked along Sanjo-dori between JR and Kintetsu Nara stations. It is run by three siblings, and the name itself reflects that: in Korean 'Sam' means 'three' and 'choA' means 'like/love,' so the place is built around the trio and the things they love.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed hideaway that doubles as a lunch and night cafe and an evening drinking spot, filling the room with the siblings' favorite Korean food, sweets, and drinks.
chef_bio.specialty: Korean fried chicken (yangnyeom) with a crispy batter and house-made yangnyeom sauce, creamy tomato tteokbokki, seafood pancakes, and night-cafe desserts including handmade gateau chocolate and New York cheesecake sold from the entrance showcase. A signature playful item is the Nara-themed 'deer pudding,' a local twist on Korea's animal-shaped puddings.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from Korean: 'Sam' (three) plus 'choA' (like/love), referring to the three siblings who run it and pack it with their favorite things.
They make a Nara-exclusive deer-shaped pudding, a regional riff on the trendy animal-shaped Korean puddings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu items use wheat: yangnyeom fried chicken has a fried wheat batter, and seafood pancakes (pajeon) and tteokbokki are wheat-based, sharing a kitchen with no documented separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Korean sauces such as the house yangnyeom sauce typically rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and gochujang; no gluten-free sauces are indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (Korean fried chicken, seafood pancakes) and desserts use dairy/eggs, so vegan cross-contact is likely and unmanaged.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, youthful Korean cafe-bar run by young owners where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though detailed allergy questions may be hard without some Japanese.

places.name: サバスカフェ (SAVAS CAFE)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu centers on wheat-based dishes: gratin served over pasta or inside a baguette, sandwiches, and chiffon cakes (made with wheat flour). No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented, and this is not a celiac-safe destination.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Signature items use dairy and animal products: gratin includes cheese, cream/bechamel, salmon, chicken or hamburger, while chiffon cakes use eggs and dairy. A plant-based eater would have very limited options and should ask in advance; no vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-19:00; closed Wednesdays (open if Wednesday is a public holiday)
places.neighborhood: Motokomorichō, central Nara — along Yasuragi-no-michi, next
places.cuisine: Small independent one-house cafe known for h
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] SAVAS CAFE sits in a crisp white one-house building along Yasuragi-no-michi, right beside Isagawa Shrine and just a few minutes' walk from the bustle of Kintetsu Nara Station. The cafe spreads over two floors: a takeout and retail counter on the ground floor, and an airy upstairs

chef_bio.background: SAVAS CAFE sits in a crisp white one-house building along Yasuragi-no-michi, right beside Isagawa Shrine and just a few minutes' walk from the bustle of Kintetsu Nara Station. The cafe spreads over two floors: a takeout and retail counter on the ground floor, and an airy upstairs dining room with high ceilings, white walls, cheerful red accents on the window frames and furniture, and big windows looking out over the street. A ceiling fan keeps the room comfortable even in summer.
chef_bio.philosophy: The place is run with a clear emphasis on comfort and relaxation — staff will even suggest the best seat for the temperature that day, a small touch that captures the cafe's attentive, unhurried spirit.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is built around two homemade specialties: gratin and chiffon cake. The gratin comes either baked in an ovenproof dish over pasta or tucked inside a baguette, with fillings that rotate through combinations like salmon and corn, spinach and chicken, or hamburger and broccoli, typically served as a set with salad and a drink. The chiffon cakes are the other star — light, finely textured and only gently sweet, offered in around five rotating varieties such as matcha, cocoa-banana, plain, apple rum-raisin and seasonal flavors, sold by the slice or as whole cakes. A roasted-tea jelly parfait made with Yamato tea and fruit-and-custard parfaits round out the dessert menu.

anecdotes.text: Staff proactively recommend the most comfortable seat depending on the temperature, reflecting the cafe's relaxed, guest-first attitude.
The chiffon cakes draw on a deep repertoire — described as rotating through well over a hundred recipes across the year, including seasonal flavors.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through; bring cash and don't expect English support.

places.name: なら泉勇斎 (Nara Izumi Yusai)
places.gf_detail: This is a sake tasting bar, not a kitchen. Sake (junmai/ginjo) is brewed from rice, water and koji and is generally gluten-free, so the core offering of by-the-glass tastings is broadly suitable. However, this is not a celiac-focused venue and there is no published allergen handling. Accompanying small plates include items such as hishio (a koji-based fermented seasoning), aged sake-lees cream cheese and Nara pickles (narazuke) — narazuke and some seasonings are cured in sake lees / may involve soy or barley-derived components, so a sensitive celiac should ask about or avoid the food side and confirm before consuming.
places.vegan_detail: Drinks-focused venue. Most sake is vegan, though sake filtration/processing is not disclosed here. Food is limited to a few snacks: Nara pickles (narazuke) and koji-based hishio are plant-based, but the aged sake-lees cream cheese is dairy. There is no dedicated vegan menu — vegans can taste sake and choose pickle-type snacks but should ask about each item.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–20:00, closed Thursdays (open if Thursday falls on a national holiday); occasional temporary closures announced on the official site.
places.neighborhood: Nishiterabayashi-cho, off the Mochiidono / Higashimuki shopp
places.notes: The shop opened in April 2012 in a white-walled traditional townhouse just off the Mochiidono arcade, on a site that had previously been the sake shop Sakagura Sasaya. It occupies a historic merchant quarter near Todai-ji, Kofuku-ji and Gangoji. The proprietor, Shinsuke Yamamoto,

chef_bio.background: The shop opened in April 2012 in a white-walled traditional townhouse just off the Mochiidono arcade, on a site that had previously been the sake shop Sakagura Sasaya. It occupies a historic merchant quarter near Todai-ji, Kofuku-ji and Gangoji. The proprietor, Shinsuke Yamamoto, is a former researcher and director at Nara's prefectural industrial technology center with a background in fermentation chemistry — expertise he drew on, including work connected to reviving the ancient monk-brewed Bodaisen sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: Celebrates Nara's claim as a birthplace of clear refined sake, giving visitors an approachable, low-commitment way to explore the prefecture's breweries (such as Harushika and Hatsugasumi) and discover a favorite with staff guidance.
chef_bio.specialty: Paid by-the-glass tasting of over 120 regional sake from breweries across Nara Prefecture, poured in small 50cc glasses from around 150–200 yen, with comparison sets (pure-rice, ginjo, dry) and the option to buy a bottle of anything you like. Snacks lean on Nara's food heritage — hishio (a revived ancient koji seasoning), sake-lees cream cheese and Nara pickles.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former director of Nara's prefectural industrial technology center with a fermentation-chemistry background, connected to the revival of the ancient monk-brewed Bodaisen sake.
Set in a historic white-walled townhouse off the Mochiidono shopping street, the compact space has high ceilings and a bright, refined feel.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Snacks include hishio (a koji/soy-type fermented seasoning) and Nara pickles cured in sake lees; these can involve wheat/barley or soy-derived components and are not confirmed gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Among the snacks, sake-lees cream cheese is dairy; pickles and hishio are plant-based but no vegan handling is documented.

positives.text: Sake is poured by the glass and bottles are visible in the counter fridge, so you can see exactly what you are served and ask staff about each one.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly counter where staff happily help you pick sake; a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the fridge go a long way, and inexpensive 50cc pours make it easy to try without commitment.

places.name: 中華処 青天 (Chuka-dokoro Seiten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a Chinese kitchen built around gyoza (wheat wrappers), wheat-based soy sauce, and likely flour-thickened/wok dishes; there is no dedicated gluten-free handling and cross-contact is unavoidable. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The kitchen centers on Yamato pork, Yamato beef and seafood; signature dishes (gyoza, mapo tofu, vinegar chicken) are meat/fish-based. Even the locally made tofu dishes are cooked in meat- or fish-derived sauces. The gyoza being garlic-free is a flavor choice, not a vegan one. Vegans would need to ask, and options would be very limited at best.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:00/14:10); Dinner 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Tuesdays (plus occasional irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: 東向南町・東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, hidden-gem Chinese restaurant tucked down a narrow alley off the Higashimuki shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run almost single-handedly by its owner-chef, who has relocated the shop at least once over the years.

chef_bio.background: A small, hidden-gem Chinese restaurant tucked down a narrow alley off the Higashimuki shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run almost single-handedly by its owner-chef, who has relocated the shop at least once over the years.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef prizes freshness over speed, cutting vegetables to order and avoiding chemical seasonings, building a 'light, gentle and colorful' style of Chinese cooking that leans on Nara-grown ingredients — Yamato pork, Yamato beef, Yamato vegetables and local tofu — to create a distinctly Nara take on Chinese food.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled gyoza filled with Yamato pork, Chinese cabbage and cabbage (kept juicy with added tofu) and made without garlic; and the house mapo tofu, made with locally grown soybean tofu and seasoned with kombu dashi and two kinds of fragrant Japanese pepper for a savory, brightly numbing heat. Vinegar chicken and a seafood-and-vegetable chanpon are also local favorites.

anecdotes.text: The standard menu is constantly refreshed with new dishes, so regulars find something different to try almost every visit.
It is known among locals as 'the Chinese place where mapo tofu lovers gather,' pairing especially well with aged Nara sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza and other dishes are made with wheat wrappers and almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce in a shared single-cook kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant, dishes are seasoned with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even tofu and vegetable dishes are cooked alongside and in sauces derived from pork, beef and seafood; vegan separation is not offered.

positives.text: The signature gyoza are made without garlic, which can help diners avoiding garlic specifically (note: this is not related to gluten or vegan needs).

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run Chinese spot down a side alley with no English menu, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will help; a friendly greeting goes a long way in a place this small.

places.name: 元喜神 奈良店 (Genkishin Nara)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any meaningful way. The core menu is wheat ramen and tsukemen — the shop makes its own noodles from domestic wheat flour (and mineral-rich whole-grain wheat flour for the tsukemen), serves gyoza (wheat wrappers), and uses chicken/vegetable/kombu broth typically finished with standard seasonings. No celiac-safe options and no dedicated handling reported.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a chicken-paitan (tori paitan) specialist — broths are simmered from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato chicken, and the signature dishes include char siu, soft-boiled egg, shrimp and gyoza. Even the "soymilk" ramen (飛鳥豆乳ラーメン) is built on a chicken paitan base. No vegan menu documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 16:00), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 20:00). Note: some listings show continuous service to ~21:30-22:00; hours vary by source/season.
places.neighborhood: 東向商店街 / 東向南町 (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン・つけ麺・餃子
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A chicken-paitan ramen and tsukemen shop tucked into the Higashimuki covered arcade, a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run as part of a small local restaurant group (which also operates the nearby Chinese kitchens Hiten and Hiten Sange), and the Nara branch is d

chef_bio.origin: Nara (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
chef_bio.background: A chicken-paitan ramen and tsukemen shop tucked into the Higashimuki covered arcade, a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run as part of a small local restaurant group (which also operates the nearby Chinese kitchens Hiten and Hiten Sange), and the Nara branch is descended from an earlier ramen shop on the site once known as 'Nara Ramen Aoniyoshi,' rebranded around the end of 2011.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on a long-simmered, made-from-scratch chicken paitan base and self-made noodles, paired with local Nara rice.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen builds everything on a milky tori paitan broth simmered for hours from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato local chicken with vegetables and kombu. The flagship is the chicken-paitan tsukemen, with house-made noodles from domestic wheat (a mineral-rich whole-grain flour for the dipping noodles) and rice from Nara-grown organic Hinohikari. Tourist-friendly specials round out the menu, including the lavish Daibutsu (Great Buddha) ramen topped with shrimp tempura, fried mochi, char siu and egg, and the lighter Asuka soy-milk paitan ramen.

anecdotes.text: Each table is fitted with an IH burner so diners can finish the meal by turning the leftover dipping broth, rice and a raw egg into a savory zosui rice porridge or risotto at the table — many regulars say the rice finish is the best part.
The original site once operated as 'Nara Ramen Aoniyoshi' and was known for its Daibutsu (Great Buddha) ramen, which still survives on the current menu after the rebrand to Genkishin.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central and unavoidable here: house-made wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles, wheat gyoza wrappers, and shared cookware make this unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (tori paitan from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato chicken); toppings include char siu, egg and shrimp. No vegan preparation reported.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual ramen shop right in the tourist-heavy Higashimuki arcade that sees lots of international visitors, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you a comfortable, no-pressure meal.

places.name: すするか、すすらんか。 (Susuruka, Susuranka.)
places.gf_detail: This is a ramen shop whose entire concept is a wheat-noodle dish. The signature mapo tofu ramen uses standard medium-thick wheat noodles, and the mapo sauce relies on doubanjiang and fermented soybeans (typically wheat-containing) plus a chicken-bone broth. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling information. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The core and signature dish is mapo tofu ramen built on a chicken-bone broth with minced meat and wheat noodles. No vegan or vegetarian version is documented. Tofu is present but the dish is meat- and animal-broth based, so it is not vegan or vegetarian.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅エリア）／ Naramachi
places.cuisine: 麻婆豆腐ラーメン専門店
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Two friends and Kinki University students (Faculty of Agriculture, Department of Aquaculture) started the shop during the 2020 pandemic. Nishi, a former aspiring drummer who felt adrift after giving up that dream, spent the COVID lockdown visiting local Nara business owners to ex

chef_bio.chef_name: 西 奈槻 (Nishi Natsuki) & 奥野 亮太郎 (Okuno Ryotaro)
chef_bio.origin: Nara, Japan; founded by Kinki University students
chef_bio.background: Two friends and Kinki University students (Faculty of Agriculture, Department of Aquaculture) started the shop during the 2020 pandemic. Nishi, a former aspiring drummer who felt adrift after giving up that dream, spent the COVID lockdown visiting local Nara business owners to explore his future. On his third visit, the owner of a Naramachi halal ramen shop offered to hand over the space; Nishi immediately called Okuno, a friend known for his cooking and obsession with mapo tofu, who agreed on the spot. They first opened as 'jinniyah / 奈KAMA' in October 2020, then rebranded to 'すするか、すすらんか。' on June 6, 2021.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name means roughly 'Shall we slurp, or shan't we?' — a play on the founder's mantra '結局やるか、やらんかやで' ('In the end it comes down to whether you do it or not'). They see the shop as more than a restaurant: a way to show young people in Japan that they have choices ('日本の若者に選択肢を'), running initiatives like inviting young people to submit recipes and hosting one-day managers to spark conversations about life paths.
chef_bio.specialty: Their flagship is the mapo tofu ramen called '革命物語' (Kakumei Monogatari) — a custom mapo tofu developed specifically for ramen, layering the heat of doubanjiang and fermented soybeans with the richness of a chicken-bone broth over medium-thick smooth noodles. It was described as an unprecedented bowl for Nara Prefecture at the time.

anecdotes.text: The shop took shape through a public consulting session with Nakagawa Atsushi, chairman of Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten, which helped the founders crystallize their mission of giving young people more choices.
The 奈KAMA name combined Nishi's character '奈' with Okuno's nickname 'Kamakiri' before the pair rebranded to the current playful name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the foundation of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; the mapo sauce uses doubanjiang and fermented-soybean pastes that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mapo tofu base relies on doubanjiang and fermented soybeans, which are conventional wheat-containing fermented seasonings; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on a chicken-bone broth with minced meat, so all preparation surfaces and broth contact animal products; no vegan option is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, lively local ramen counter where a basic Japanese greeting and pointing at the signature bowl will get you a long way; the young owners are open-minded and welcoming, but expect Japanese-only ordering and a short wait at busy times.

places.name: LUU's BAO（ルーズバオ） (LUU's BAO (Ruuzu Bao))
places.gf_detail: This is a bao (steamed bun) specialist and the buns are made from organic WHEAT flour, so the core product is not gluten-free and the shop is not celiac-safe. Donuts are also wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or wheat-free options are documented. A celiac traveler should not rely on this shop.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan / 100% plant-based — the storefront is marked "100% PLANT-BASED" and the shop is described across multiple sources as a 100％ヴィーガン bao specialist. Around 11 varieties of vegan bao (Taiwanese-style burgers, e.g. plant-meat with BBQ sauce and ample vegetables), plus fries sets, vegan donuts, smoothies, matcha drinks, and ice cream. Uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil. No animal ingredients used.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sun 10:00–16:00 (last food order 15:30); closed Mondays and Tuesdays. One local listing notes 11:00–16:30; the Tabelog listing states 10:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:30).
places.neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏）
places.notes: LUU's BAO is a 100% plant-based bao specialist that began in Osaka's Nipponbashi (Nihonbashi/Ebisucho) area and relocated to the historic Naramachi district of Nara on March 15, 2026, settling into a quiet machiya streetscape between Amenokaku Coffee and across from Kasugaan. The

chef_bio.background: LUU's BAO is a 100% plant-based bao specialist that began in Osaka's Nipponbashi (Nihonbashi/Ebisucho) area and relocated to the historic Naramachi district of Nara on March 15, 2026, settling into a quiet machiya streetscape between Amenokaku Coffee and across from Kasugaan. The storefront announces '100% PLANT-BASED,' and the shop set out to prove that a fully vegan menu can still be hearty and satisfying.
chef_bio.philosophy: Wholesome, body-conscious plant-based eating: the dough is made from organic wheat and steamed in bamboo baskets like a traditional pork bun, fillings are entirely plant-derived, and the kitchen uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil while aiming for filling, satisfying portions without animal products.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan 'bao' — the steamed split bun known as 割包 (guabao), often called a Taiwanese burger. About 11 varieties are offered, including a plant-meat burger with barbecue sauce piled with vegetables, available with a fries set. Sweets include freshly fried vegan donuts, smoothies, matcha drinks, and ice cream.

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated from Osaka's Nipponbashi to Naramachi and opened in Nara on March 15, 2026, choosing a calm machiya building that suits the area's traditional architecture, with an outdoor bench for casual stops.
It is described as a 'hidden celebrity' sweets spot, with freshly fried donuts and ice cream alongside its roughly 11 bao varieties.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire core product line is wheat-based (steamed bao buns and donuts made from organic wheat flour), so the kitchen is a wheat-heavy environment and is not suitable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is described as 100% plant-based across all sources, so animal-product cross-contact risk is inherently low; no specific cross-contact procedures are documented.

positives.text: Marked '100% PLANT-BASED' on the storefront and consistently described as a fully vegan bao specialist, so all menu items are plant-based.
Uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil with organic wheat and plant-based fillings.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual, takeout-friendly counter shop with a clearly visible "100% PLANT-BASED" sign and picture-friendly bao, so a foreign student can order comfortably with basic Japanese greetings and pointing, though staff English is not documented.

places.name: Vegan Ramen by Playpen Friends（ヴィーガンラーメン専門店） (Vegan Ramen by Playpen Friends)
places.gf_detail: This is a ramen shop; the noodles are wheat-based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free and the kitchen is not celiac-safe. One reviewer mentioned a "gluten free" option, but this is unconfirmed by the shop and there is no documented dedicated gluten-free preparation. A celiac traveler should not rely on it; ask directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: A vegan-ramen-focused shop with around six clearly labeled vegan ramen styles (vegan miso, soy sauce, tomato, vegan "tonkotsu"-style). The vegan miso uses a domestic miso base with vegetables and spices stir-fried in plant oil, topped with broccoli, corn, fried tofu, soy meat, several seaweeds, wood-ear mushroom, green onion and chili threads. Note that the same kitchen also serves genuine pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken paitan ramen, so this is a vegan-friendly shop rather than a 100% vegan kitchen; strict vegans should confirm separate handling.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (closed Tuesdays). One local listing lists 10:00-17:00 — confirm on arrival.
places.neighborhood: 登大路町（奈良公園・奈良国立博物館前、近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
places.cuisine: ヴィーガンラーメン専門
places.notes: Opened on July 29, 2024 in Noborioji-cho, on the second floor of a building between the Shizuka park branch and Mamejika Shokudo, directly across from the Nara National Museum and a step from Nara Park. The space previously housed a Japanese-tea cafe, Chahua.

chef_bio.origin: Second outpost of a vegan ramen concept that started in Osaka's Nishi Ward (Kita-Horie area), where it built a loyal following before expanding to Nara.
chef_bio.background: Opened on July 29, 2024 in Noborioji-cho, on the second floor of a building between the Shizuka park branch and Mamejika Shokudo, directly across from the Nara National Museum and a step from Nara Park. The space previously housed a Japanese-tea cafe, Chahua.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring approachable, clearly labeled plant-based ramen to one of Japan's most-visited tourist areas so international and vegan diners can eat ramen overlooking the deer of Nara Park.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based ramen built around a vegan miso bowl: a domestic-miso base with vegetables and spices stir-fried in plant oil, generously topped with broccoli, corn, fried tofu, soy meat, several kinds of seaweed, wood-ear mushroom, green onion and chili threads. Other vegan styles include soy-sauce, tomato and a vegan tonkotsu. The shop also cooks non-vegan pork-bone and chicken-paitan bowls.

anecdotes.text: Window counter seats look straight out over Nara Park, so diners can watch the famous deer while eating; reviewers repeatedly note the generous, colorful assortment of toppings and friendly staff.
Sits between the Shizuka park branch and Mamejika Shokudo, opposite the Nara National Museum, in a spot that previously held the Japanese-tea cafe Chahua.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen noodles are wheat-based and the menu is wheat- and broth-heavy; no dedicated gluten-free handling is documented, so the kitchen should be treated as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-sauce and miso ramen styles are offered; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat and no wheat-free version is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same kitchen cooks genuine pork-bone and chicken-paitan ramen alongside the vegan bowls, so shared-equipment cross-contact is possible; strict vegans should confirm handling.

positives.text: Around six clearly labeled vegan ramen options make it easy for plant-based diners to order without ambiguity.
Vegan miso bowl is built on a domestic miso base with vegetables stir-fried in plant oil and plant-based toppings (fried tofu, soy meat, seaweed, vegetables).

cultural_comfort.note: Very foreigner-friendly with clearly labeled vegan options and a heavily international clientele, so a foreign student can order comfortably with little Japanese.

noodles.notes: Plant-based wheat noodles; thick wavy cut on the miso bowl with substantial volume, while the vegan tonkotsu and shoyu-tonkotsu use different (finer) thicknesses to match their creamier broths.

ramen.signature_bowl: Vegan Tonkotsu Ramen — a creamy, emulsified plant-based broth built to echo the richness of pork-bone paitan without any animal products; the vegan miso (kokumi-rich, with vegetables and spices stir-fried in plant oil) is the other star.
ramen.chashu: No pork or chicken; the protein is plant-based — soy meat (daizu) and thick deep-fried tofu (atsuage) stand in for chashu.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based and miso/shoyu tares contain wheat, so standard bowls are not gluten-free. As a plant-focused specialist they may accommodate questions, but no documented rice/konjac noodle or GF tare option exists — ask before ordering.

vegan.note: A dedicated plant-based menu of roughly six bowls (vegan tonkotsu, miso, shoyu, tomato) uses vegetable/plant-oil broths with soy meat and tofu instead of any pork, chicken or fish dashi. Note the kitchen also serves real tonkotsu and chicken paitan, so confirm your bowl is from the vegan line.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, original plant-based ramen house rather than a regional descendant — its soul is the alchemy of making a vegetable-and-plant-oil broth taste as creamy and full-bodied as pork-bone paitan, proving that the emulsified richness usually born from bones can be coaxed entirely from plants. Beside it sits a spice-laced vegan miso whose depth comes from vegetables and aromatics seared in oil.

places.highlight: A modern, original plant-based ramen house rather than a regional descendant — its soul is the alchemy of making a vegetable-and-plant-oil broth taste as creamy and full-bodied as pork-bone paitan, proving that the emulsified richness usually born from bones can be coaxed entirely from plants. Beside it sits a spice-laced vegan miso whose depth comes from vegetables and aromatics seared in oil.

places.name: 三笠観光会館 (Mikasa Kanko Kaikan)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu is built on wheat-heavy dishes (tonkatsu, katsu curry with roux, hamburg, udon) and Japanese set meals that rely on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, separate fryers, or allergy handling, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The official menu lists a Buddhist-vegetarian (精進料理 / shōjin) set at roughly 1,650–2,350 yen, which is plant-forward by tradition, but its vegan status is not confirmed — shōjin sets in Japan commonly include items prepared with non-vegan dashi or other animal-derived seasonings, and no allergen/vegan information is published. A traveler must confirm directly. The rest of the menu (tonkatsu, hamburg, curry, kamameshi, teppanyaki) is omnivore. The black-soybean soy-milk vegan soft serve mentioned in some listings could not be verified on the official site or any source and should be confirmed on arrival.
places.neighborhood: Wakakusayama foothills, Nara Park area (Zōshichō), east of K
places.notes: Mikasa Kanko Kaikan sits at the foot of Wakakusayama in the heart of Nara Park, a short walk east of the city center toward Kasuga Taisha. It is a long-running tourist hall that pairs a ground-floor souvenir shop with sit-down dining spread across two floors — table seating downs

chef_bio.background: Mikasa Kanko Kaikan sits at the foot of Wakakusayama in the heart of Nara Park, a short walk east of the city center toward Kasuga Taisha. It is a long-running tourist hall that pairs a ground-floor souvenir shop with sit-down dining spread across two floors — table seating downstairs and a mix of tables and tatami-mat rooms upstairs — making it a common stop for school groups and sightseeing travelers exploring the deer park.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty Nara-style set meals and bento: the Sōhei (warrior-monk) bento, tonkatsu and Nanto set meals, kamameshi, teppanyaki sets, katsu curry and hamburg plates, alongside a Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set. Bundles of deer crackers (shika-senbei) are sold out front for feeding the park's free-roaming deer.

anecdotes.text: The hall is named after Mikasayama (the older name for Wakakusayama, the grassy hill it sits beneath), the same mountain that lent its name to Nara's famous Mikasa sweet — a dorayaki-style confection whose shape evokes the rounded mountain.
It functions as a one-stop tourist rest house — souvenirs and packaged Nara sweets downstairs, full meals upstairs, and deer crackers for sale at the entrance for visitors feeding the park deer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by deep-fried and wheat-based dishes (tonkatsu, katsu curry, hamburg, udon) served in a shared high-volume kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese set meals and bento are served, which in Japan typically use wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set is offered, but it is prepared in the same kitchen as meat and fish dishes and its dashi/seasoning ingredients are not disclosed, so vegan suitability and cross-contact cannot be confirmed.

positives.text: A dedicated Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set meal is available on the regular menu, giving plant-based diners a starting point to ask about.

cultural_comfort.note: A large, tourist-facing dining hall and souvenir shop in Nara Park where ordering is straightforward by pointing at set-meal photos; basic greetings are enough, though detailed dietary questions may need patience or a translation app.

places.name: 春日大社 カフェ・ショップ鹿音 (Kasuga Taisha Cafe Shop Kaon)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. The menu centers on wheat-based items: Miwa sōmen / nyūmen and ramen are wheat noodles, pancakes and cookies contain wheat flour, and there is no dedicated fryer or allergen handling documented. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: No confirmed vegan items. The documented soft serve flavors (matcha, matcha-milk, chocolate, mango-milk, etc.) are all dairy-based, and meal sets use rice/sōmen typically with fish dashi. Despite a claim that a vegan soft serve is offered, no source I checked lists a ヴィーガン/植物性 (plant-based) item, so a plant-based eater would need to ask directly.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–17:00 (L.O. 16:30); rurubu lists 10:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Follows the Kasuga Taisha National Treasure Hall schedule.
places.neighborhood: 春日野町（春日大社境内・奈良公園内）
places.notes: The space is built to carry the feeling of Kasuga's primeval forest indoors: its counter is fashioned from a Kasuga cedar that grew on the mountain and was felled by a lightning strike in the Showa era, and other wooden fittings are made from nagi, a tree designated a natural mon

chef_bio.origin: Opened in autumn 2015 alongside the renovated National Treasure Hall (Kokuhoden) of Kasuga Taisha, this cafe and shop sits on the first floor of the museum building within the shrine's sacred grounds in Nara Park.
chef_bio.background: The space is built to carry the feeling of Kasuga's primeval forest indoors: its counter is fashioned from a Kasuga cedar that grew on the mountain and was felled by a lightning strike in the Showa era, and other wooden fittings are made from nagi, a tree designated a natural monument. A display of an 800-year-old cedar stump anchors the room.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let visitors to the shrine taste Nara's local produce and feel the atmosphere of the sacred Kasuga mountain forest within the cafe itself.
chef_bio.specialty: Soft serve made with Nara-grown matcha is the signature, offered in five flavors, alongside sweets like black-honey-and-kinako pancakes with warabi mochi and the Shiki Gozen seasonal set pairing Nara rice bowls with Miwa somen.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's counter table is cut from a cedar that once stood on Mount Kasuga and was struck by lightning during the Showa period, having been stored at the old treasure house before being repurposed here.
Original goods and confections are themed on cats found depicted in the scabbards of national-treasure swords held by the shrine.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soft serve flavors documented are dairy-based (matcha-milk, chocolate-milk, mango-milk), so plant-based options are not confirmed and any would share equipment with dairy.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed museum cafe inside a major shrine's National Treasure Hall, used to tourists and easy to order at by pointing, though staff English may be limited so a translation app helps.

places.name: aonocafe（アオノカフェ） (aonocafe)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no GF accommodation is documented. The base food is rice (onigiri), and a plain-salt onigiri (こだわりの塩) exists, but the lunch sets come with a rolled egg, a hearty pork soup (tonjiru) that almost certainly contains wheat-based miso/seasoning, and fillings such as seared cheese-mentaiko, tuna mayo, and roast beef that may use soy sauce or other wheat-containing seasonings. There is no mention of dedicated GF handling or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner would need to ask in detail and likely cannot eat the standard set safely.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The signature lunches center on fish, meat, and dairy fillings (tuna mayo, mentaiko, seared cheese, prosciutto with cream cheese, roast beef) and include a rolled egg and a pork-based soup. A plain-salt or kombu onigiri could in principle be vegan if ordered à la carte, but the set meals are not, fish dashi is likely in the soup, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan would need to ask whether individual non-animal onigiri can be ordered separately.
places.neighborhood: 恋の窪 (Koinokubo), northwest of Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A photogenic onigiri café that opened on March 25, 2024 in the Koinokubo area northwest of central Nara. The shop is built around handmade rice balls and a relaxed, welcoming lunch atmosphere.

chef_bio.background: A photogenic onigiri café that opened on March 25, 2024 in the Koinokubo area northwest of central Nara. The shop is built around handmade rice balls and a relaxed, welcoming lunch atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated draw is offering unique rice-ball varieties you can't find elsewhere, served generously and affordably for a satisfying everyday lunch.
chef_bio.specialty: Onigiri lunch sets: diners choose three rice balls from around fifteen original fillings. The playful menu splits into an 'Ao' (blue) line of classics like tuna-mayo and mentaiko, and a 'Shiro' (white) line of creative choices such as seared cheese-mentaiko, house-made roast beef, kombu, and prosciutto with cream cheese. Each set comes with a generous, vegetable-packed pork soup, a rolled egg, and small sides, with takeout also available.

anecdotes.text: Opened on March 25, 2024 and quickly drew attention as a 'photogenic onigiri shop,' with a full lunch set built around three chosen rice balls and a hearty pork soup offered around the 1,000-yen mark.
Visitors describe a warm, busy little café with the rice-ball sets praised as filling yet never monotonous thanks to the vegetable-forward pork soup.

positives.text: The core dish is rice-based onigiri, and a simple salt onigiri (こだわりの塩) and kombu onigiri are on the filling list, so plain rice-ball options exist for diners avoiding meat or fish if ordered carefully.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly local lunch café where a smile and a little basic Japanese go a long way; the menu is simple (pick your rice-ball fillings), so a foreign student can manage comfortably even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 日々ムラカミ (Hibi Murakami)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery whose entire product line is built on domestic wheat flour (plus rice and other grains), so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free products are documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Documented items include shokupan, melon pan with almonds, anko-and-butter, and curry pan, several of which clearly contain dairy/egg. Some simple hard-crust breads may be free of animal products, but ingredients are not published, so a vegan diner would need to ask item by item.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（法蓮町）／近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約8〜10分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small bakery in Nara's quiet Kitamachi (Horen-cho) district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station along the Saho River. It was founded by the Murakami family to be a neighborhood bread shop rooted in their home city of Nara.

chef_bio.background: A small bakery in Nara's quiet Kitamachi (Horen-cho) district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station along the Saho River. It was founded by the Murakami family to be a neighborhood bread shop rooted in their home city of Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: To make gentle, everyday bread that children and elderly customers alike can enjoy, kept at approachable prices and convenient hours, using domestic wheat and local Nara ingredients such as Yamato tea and Nara-grown rice so the bakery stays tied to its hometown.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature 'Hibi no Shokupan' (daily milk-style loaf) made from a proprietary blend of domestic wheat and Nara rice, with a soft, chewy crumb that is faintly sweet eaten plain and crisp-edged when toasted. Other beloved items include 'Yamato-cha to Azuki' (organic Nara green tea and sweet adzuki swirl), a baked-not-fried vegetable curry pan, the almond-and-sugar 'Sunrise' melon pan, and an anko-and-butter roll.

anecdotes.text: The owner is described talking about bread with sparkling eyes, wanting the shop to walk alongside the community's daily life, which is the spirit behind the name 'Hibi' (everyday).
The curry pan is filled with house-made curry of onion and carrot and is baked rather than deep-fried so it appeals to all ages.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a flour-based bakery where every product is made with domestic wheat, so there is pervasive wheat/gluten throughout the kitchen and no gluten-free options exist.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Documented breads include dairy and egg items (milk-style shokupan, almond melon pan, anko-and-butter), so animal ingredients and shared surfaces are common; no vegan-specific handling is described.

positives.text: The bakery emphasizes domestic wheat and local Nara ingredients (Nara rice, Yamato tea), so ingredient sourcing is transparent and locally focused.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want and pay at the counter, so a foreign student can shop comfortably with little or no Japanese, though staff are unlikely to speak English.

places.name: ユーズ・キッチン (U's kitchen)
places.gf_detail: This is a fresh-pasta specialist where essentially every dish is wheat-based (tagliatelle, linguine, pappardelle, spaghetti, penne). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no gluten-free pasta, and no celiac accommodation. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: An Italian pasta house built around cream sauces, meat sauce, bacon, baby scallop, smoked chicken and shrimp dishes. A plain tomato-sauce pasta or basil pesto (ジェノベーゼ) may be the only meatless option, but no dish is confirmed dairy-free or vegan, and there is no documented vegan menu. Vegans would need to call ahead and likely find very little.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 17:00-20:30 (last entry); closed Sundays; dinner closed on the third Monday.
places.neighborhood: Kitamachi (near Higashimuki-kita shopping street), about a 5
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A snug pasta room tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north from the Kintetsu station amid the nostalgic streetscape near the Higashimuki shopping arcade. It has been a quiet local favorite for years rather than a tourist stop.

chef_bio.background: A snug pasta room tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north from the Kintetsu station amid the nostalgic streetscape near the Higashimuki shopping arcade. It has been a quiet local favorite for years rather than a tourist stop.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner keeps the focus deliberately narrow, stating that this is a pasta specialist that serves pasta and nothing else, letting the noodles and sauces carry the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh house-made pasta is the heart of the menu, built around three carefully chosen shapes: tagliatelle (an egg-rich ribbon a bit like kishimen), linguine with a firm bite and faint sweetness, and wide pappardelle. The kitchen turns out a long list of sauces, and the baby-scallop cream pasta is a standout regulars come back for.

anecdotes.text: A longtime regular writes that they stumbled on this pasta specialist years ago and it became their go-to, praising the chewy tagliatelle in baby-scallop cream sauce.
The restaurant's own site sums up its identity bluntly: as a pasta specialty shop, there is nothing on the menu but pasta.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built on house-made wheat pasta, so flour is central to the kitchen; there is no gluten-free preparation or separate workflow documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sauces lean heavily on cream, meat sauce, bacon, scallop, chicken and shrimp, so meat- and dairy-free cooking is not a focus and shared pots are likely.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally loved neighborhood pasta room where menus and service are in Japanese, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 奈良きたまち食堂 イル・ソッリーゾ・デッラ・ファミーリア (Nara Kitamachi Shokudo Il Sorriso Della Fami)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is an Italian/Western canteen built around pasta, fried items (フリット), and rice plates, so wheat is pervasive and there is no celiac-safe handling. The chef is known for adjusting dishes to customer requests, so you could ask, but cross-contamination cannot be assured in a kitchen this small.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian destination — the menu centers on meat and fish mains (fried chicken breast, grilled pork, deep-fried horse mackerel). The owner does customize dishes and asks diners about dislikes, so a vegetable-forward plate might be possible by request, but no documented vegan offering exists. House-made non-alcoholic sangria is one plant-based item noted.
places.neighborhood: きたまち／近鉄奈良駅
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, home-style Italian and Western canteen tucked on the second floor of a building in Nara's historic Kitamachi district, near Nara Women's University. Run by an owner who goes by Sadayan with one part-time helper, it sits along the route east from Kintetsu Nara Station thr

chef_bio.background: A small, home-style Italian and Western canteen tucked on the second floor of a building in Nara's historic Kitamachi district, near Nara Women's University. Run by an owner who goes by Sadayan with one part-time helper, it sits along the route east from Kintetsu Nara Station through the Higashimuki and Hanashiba shopping streets, marked by a wooden sign painted with black cats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The Italian name means 'the family's smile,' reflecting the owner's stated wish to meet many customers' happy, satisfied faces each day. The kitchen embraces flexibility, adjusting sauces and ingredients to individual tastes and inviting requests.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-changing one-plate lunch sets (around 850-900 yen) with a rotating main such as fried chicken breast, grilled pork shoulder, or deep-fried horse mackerel, served with a generous portion of rice, assorted sides, and fruit; pasta dishes and a teppan Neapolitan are also signatures, alongside house-made non-alcoholic sangria.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps the spirit of a relaxed 'home drinking' spot where you can come alone for a single drink or gather with friends and family, and it is known for keeping cats around, adding to its casual, family feel.
A diner came away with the strong impression that the chef genuinely loves cooking, noting the kitchen will tweak dishes to suit the customer.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff ask diners about dislikes and will adjust sauces and ingredients to suit individual tastes, and the menu can be customized on request.

positives.text: The owner welcomes customer requests and can accommodate dietary preferences or specific dish requests.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, student-friendly owner-run canteen near Nara Women's University where ordering and chatting will go most smoothly in Japanese, though the relaxed family atmosphere is welcoming to all.

places.name: ボカロス (BOCARROZ)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is craft hamburgers on house-made brioche/wheat buns, and there is no documented gluten-free option or celiac handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The concept centers on hand-chopped beef patties, house-made bacon and smoked brisket, plus cheese; Spanish tapas also lean meat/seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
places.hours_raw: 火〜日・祝 11:30-15:00, 18:00-20:00（ランチは15:00まで、ディナーは17:00または18:00開始の記載あり）。売り切れ次第終了。月曜定休。
places.neighborhood: きたまち／近鉄奈良駅
places.cuisine: クラフトハンバーガー＆スペインのタパス
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] ボカロスは、奈良市内でスペイン料理店『Gentry（ジェントリー）』を営んでいた店主が、2021年3月に同店を閉め、2021年4月にきたまち・花芝町で独立開業したクラフトバーガー＆タパスバルです。元スペイン料理シェフの経歴を生かし、ハンバーガーにスペインの小皿料理を組み合わせた独自のスタイルを打ち出しています。

chef_bio.origin: 店名『ボカロス』は、スペイン語で『ボカディージョ（サンドイッチ）』のボカと、米料理を意味する『アロス（arroz）』を組み合わせた造語です。
chef_bio.background: ボカロスは、奈良市内でスペイン料理店『Gentry（ジェントリー）』を営んでいた店主が、2021年3月に同店を閉め、2021年4月にきたまち・花芝町で独立開業したクラフトバーガー＆タパスバルです。元スペイン料理シェフの経歴を生かし、ハンバーガーにスペインの小皿料理を組み合わせた独自のスタイルを打ち出しています。
chef_bio.philosophy: バンズを毎朝店で焼き、ベーコン・ブリスケット・ソースまで自家製にこだわる、手作り主体のクラフト志向。夜はハンバーガーに加えてスペインのタパスやワイン・カクテルが楽しめ、ランチとは異なるバル的な雰囲気になります。
