chef_bio.specialty: 看板はカルニータスバーガー。豚肩肉をオレンジとスパイスで低温コンフィ風にじっくり火入れし、ほろほろと柔らかく仕上げた一品で、丼スタイルでも提供されます。ほかにベーコンチーズバーガー、ダブルチーズバーガー、BBQチーズバーガーなど。バンズは自家製ブリオッシュ、パティは手切り（ハンドチョップ）のビーフ、ベーコンやスモークブリスケット、各種ソースまで店で手作りしています。自家製コーラやジンジャーエールも提供。

anecdotes.text: 店名『ボカロス（BOCARROZ）』はスペイン語のボカディージョ（サンドイッチ）の『ボカ』と、米料理を意味する『アロス（arroz）』を掛け合わせた造語。
店主は元スペイン料理店『Gentry』のオーナーシェフで、2021年3月に同店を閉店、同年4月にボカロスを開業した。
看板のカルニータスバーガーは豚肩肉をオレンジとスパイスで低温コンフィ風に火入れし、丼でも提供される。

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 18-seat chef-run burger and tapas bar where a few Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably, though staff may not speak much English.

places.name: SAHHA（サッハ） (SAHHA)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. The signature dish is couscous, which is wheat-based semolina pasta and unsafe for celiacs. However, some naturally gluten-free items exist: falafel (chickpea), shakshuka (a tomato/vegetable stew), and rice-based kebsa/biryani. Cross-contamination is not addressed in any source, and harissa/spice blends and sauces have unverified ingredients. A gluten-free diner would need to ask the owner directly about each dish.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant explicitly offers some vegan-appropriate and vegetarian dishes. Documented plant-based options include falafel (chickpea, ~¥1,300) and shakshuka (Tunisian vegetable/tomato stew, ~¥1,200), and the kitchen emphasizes fresh vegetables (cabbage, chickpeas, dates, radish, zucchini, onion, carrot). It is not a fully vegan restaurant — couscous and kebsa dishes are prepared with lamb or chicken. Confirm egg-free preparation of shakshuka, which can sometimes include egg, directly with the owner.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–20:00 (some sources list split hours of 11:30–15:00 / 17:00–20:30). Closed Fridays; some sources also note Mondays and irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（法蓮町）／近鉄奈良駅
places.notes: SAHHA is a Tunisian and North African restaurant tucked inside a renovated traditional Japanese house (kominka) in the Kitamachi district of Nara, near Nara Women's University. It opened in August 2021 and is one of very few places in Nara — and rare even nationwide in Japan — se

chef_bio.background: SAHHA is a Tunisian and North African restaurant tucked inside a renovated traditional Japanese house (kominka) in the Kitamachi district of Nara, near Nara Women's University. It opened in August 2021 and is one of very few places in Nara — and rare even nationwide in Japan — serving authentic Tunisian home cooking. The owner, originally from Tunisia, runs the place largely on his own, and family cooking is central to the experience.
chef_bio.philosophy: Every dish on the menu is halal, so Muslim travelers can eat freely, and the kitchen leans heavily on fresh vegetables — cabbage, chickpeas, dates, radish, zucchini, onion and carrot — giving vegetarians and vegans some honest options too.
chef_bio.specialty: Couscous is the signature: the traditional couscous (around ¥1,400–¥1,800) is built on the world's smallest pasta soaking up a tomato-based broth simmered with root vegetables and lamb, finished with a hint of raisin sweetness. Other beloved dishes include kebsa (an Arab-style spiced long-grain rice biryani with lamb or chicken, described as milder than its Indian counterpart), shakshuka (a Tunisian tomato-and-vegetable stew), and falafel made from chickpeas. Homemade harissa chili paste, baklava, and Arab coffee round out the table.

anecdotes.text: The traditional couscous is said to taste richer the more you chew it, the tiny pasta grains soaking up a lamb-and-root-vegetable broth with a faint sweetness of raisin.
The dining space is a restored kominka with several tatami rooms, dressed in Middle Eastern rugs and cushions so guests sit cross-legged in a North African mood inside an old Nara house.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The entire menu is prepared to halal standards, which means strict attention to what goes into each dish, and the owner runs the kitchen personally — making it practical to ask about ingredients directly.

positives.text: Documented vegetable-forward and chickpea-based options exist, including falafel and shakshuka, alongside an emphasis on fresh produce.
Naturally gluten-free choices such as falafel, shakshuka and rice-based kebsa are on the menu, though cross-contamination is not documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run place where the Tunisian proprietor is used to international guests and Muslim diners, so a friendly greeting and a few words about your dietary needs will be warmly received.

places.name: KAYA cafe ならまち店 (Kaya Cafe Naramachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are documented. The signature tofu tiramisu and other sweets (pancakes, parfaits) typically contain wheat-based components (sponge/biscuit, flour), and savory items like omelette rice are not GF. There is no published allergen information or dedicated gluten-free preparation, so a celiac traveler must ask directly and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: Although the desserts are tofu-based and marketed as healthy, they are not described as vegan. The tiramisu is "creamy" in a mascarpone style and the menu includes items like strawberry milk and omelette rice, indicating dairy and egg use. No vegan labeling is published, so vegans must confirm ingredients in person.
places.neighborhood: ならまち（鶴福院町）／近鉄奈良駅徒歩約10分
places.cuisine: 豆腐スイーツ専門カフェ
places.notes: A specialty tofu-sweets café in the historic Naramachi neighborhood, about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and minutes from Sarusawa Pond. The Naramachi location opened in early 2023 (pre-opening in late January, with a February 1, 2023 start), joining the brand's pre

chef_bio.background: A specialty tofu-sweets café in the historic Naramachi neighborhood, about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and minutes from Sarusawa Pond. The Naramachi location opened in early 2023 (pre-opening in late January, with a February 1, 2023 start), joining the brand's presence that also includes shops associated with Kobe and Osaka's Nakazakicho area.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café centers on 'guilt-free,' health-conscious sweets made with tofu that are described as creamy and indulgent without tasting of tofu.
chef_bio.specialty: Handmade tofu tiramisu served in a small wooden box, offered in flavors such as cocoa, matcha, hojicha, black sesame, mango, and a seasonal fresh-strawberry version ('Ichigo kaoru tofu tiramisu'). The menu also features parfaits, pancakes, an 'Angel Strawberry Milk' drink, and savory omelette rice.

anecdotes.text: The tofu tiramisu is presented in a stylish wooden box, making it both photogenic and healthy, which has made it especially popular.
Local coverage framed the shop around saying goodbye to guilt by eating seasonal tiramisu made from tofu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly local sweets café where a foreign student can comfortably order with a few basic Japanese words and pointing at the photo menu, though staff may have limited English.

places.name: 大和自家焙煎 桜座珈琲 (Yamato Jika Baisen Ouza Coffee)
places.gf_detail: This is a coffee shop, not a kitchen geared to dietary needs. Black coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food offered (hot dogs) is wheat-based bread, and no gluten-free food or celiac-safe handling is documented. Treat it as a place for a drink rather than a safe gluten-free meal.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee is the core offering and is plant-based, but no plant milk or vegan menu is documented; food items such as the hot dog are not vegan. Suitable only if you stick to black coffee; confirm milk options on site.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–19:00. Closed Mondays (also closed during Obon, year-end/New Year, and some holidays).
places.neighborhood: 恋の窪／新大宮エリア（佐保川桜並木近く）
places.cuisine: Self-roasting specialty coffee shop and café
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by Mr. Shirai, who took up coffee roasting after retiring and became absorbed in its depth and complexity, eventually opening this self-roasting coffee shop in the quiet Koi-no-kubo residential district of Nara.

chef_bio.background: The shop is run by Mr. Shirai, who took up coffee roasting after retiring and became absorbed in its depth and complexity, eventually opening this self-roasting coffee shop in the quiet Koi-no-kubo residential district of Nara.
chef_bio.philosophy: To help each customer find the coffee truly suited to their taste and to enjoy fresh, aromatic specialty coffee, while creating a relaxed gathering place for the neighborhood. Green beans are checked before roasting and hand-picked afterward for quality.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee, with a signature 'Sakura Blend' that is balanced with fruity notes and named for the Saho River cherry blossoms nearby; beans are roasted daily in-house on a 1kg roaster and sold by weight, and food includes a hot dog.

anecdotes.text: First-time visitors are offered a complimentary tasting, and buying 200g or more of beans comes with a free cup of coffee — small touches reflecting the shop's wish to be welcoming.
The shop sits near the cherry-tree-lined banks of the Saho River, a peaceful setting that inspired its 'Sakura' blend.

positives.text: Black self-roasted coffee is the focus, brewed cup by cup, so a plain cup of coffee is naturally free of gluten and animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood roastery where Japanese is the working language; a friendly point-and-smile spot for a coffee, but do not expect English menus or service.

places.name: うな菊 奈良本店 (Unakiku Nara Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature unagi is grilled and finished with a tare made from wheat-based soy sauce (Tenno Shoyu) and mirin, so the core dish contains gluten. No dedicated gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. This is a dedicated freshwater-eel (unagi) specialist; the entire menu centers on grilled live eel. No plant-based dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:45 (last entry), Dinner 17:00-19:00 (last entry); last order around 18:30. Closed Tuesdays. Year-end/New Year closures and a modified New Year's menu apply.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake / in front of Kasuga Taisha first torii gate, wit
places.notes: Unakiku is a dedicated eel restaurant born from Kikusuiro, a celebrated ryotei founded in 1891 (Meiji 24) and now a registered tangible cultural property, set directly in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha shrine. For more than 130 years the house has welcomed disting

chef_bio.background: Unakiku is a dedicated eel restaurant born from Kikusuiro, a celebrated ryotei founded in 1891 (Meiji 24) and now a registered tangible cultural property, set directly in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha shrine. For more than 130 years the house has welcomed distinguished guests, and Unakiku carries that hospitality into a focused eel specialty.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames eating eel as part of a pilgrimage ritual, building on the local custom of enjoying unagi after visiting Kasuga Taisha, and aims to connect people through eel cuisine.
chef_bio.specialty: Edo-style unagi: live eels are filleted to order, white-grilled, thoroughly steamed, then charcoal-grilled with the house tare. Signature dishes include the classic unagi-don and the ainose-don, which pairs plain white-grilled eel (shirayaki) with tare-glazed eel in one bowl. The tare blends pure additive-free Tenno Shoyu soy sauce (brewed with Fuji spring water) and traditional hon-mirin, and the rice is exclusively Niigata Uonuma Koshihikari grown for the restaurant by a partner farmer.

anecdotes.text: Unakiku grew out of the long-standing local custom of eating eel after worshipping at Kasuga Taisha, positioning itself in front of the shrine's first torii gate as part of Nara's food-culture pilgrimage experience.
Its parent house, Kikusuiro, has served eel cuisine for over 130 years and is a registered tangible cultural property dating to 1891.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is made by blending pure Tenno Shoyu soy sauce with hon-mirin; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the eel glaze contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a dedicated eel restaurant; effectively every dish is built around grilled freshwater eel, so there is no plant-based mainline and shared grill/tare make it unsuitable for vegans.

cultural_comfort.note: An upscale eel restaurant inside a historic ryotei property where reservations and basic Japanese dining etiquette help; a foreign student can manage with a polite "konnichiwa," a reservation, and a translation app, but it is a special-occasion splurge rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: オーセンティックテーブル タバーン (Authentic Table Tavern)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a casual French/yoshoku restaurant whose menu includes sauces, homemade ham, soup curry and charcoal-grilled dishes; flour-based sauces and wheat are common in this cuisine, so it is not celiac-safe without direct confirmation. A celiac diner must ask the kitchen about every dish.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The kitchen does source seasonal Yamato vegetables from Nara and grills vegetables such as broccoli, but the menu centers on charcoal-grilled meats, fresh fish, homemade Yamato-pork ham and dairy-based French preparations. There may be vegetable side dishes, but no confirmed plant-only mains; a vegan diner should call ahead and ask.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Sun: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) & 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Fri, Sat & day before public holiday: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) & 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Wednesday (subject to change).
places.neighborhood: 下三条町 (Shimosanjo-cho), on Sanjo-dori near Kintetsu Nara & JR
places.notes: A casual French dining room set on Sanjo-dori, the historic street running between Nara Station and Kasuga Taisha Shrine, in a warm interior of natural-wood tones meant for relaxed special occasions.

chef_bio.background: A casual French dining room set on Sanjo-dori, the historic street running between Nara Station and Kasuga Taisha Shrine, in a warm interior of natural-wood tones meant for relaxed special occasions.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to honor the natural flavor of its ingredients, sourcing seasonal Yamato vegetables and premium produce and using special sauces and spices only as refined accents rather than to mask the food.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled cooking is the heart of the kitchen, built around carefully chosen ingredients from Nara and Hokkaido. Signature plates include charcoal-grilled steak and vegetables, fresh charcoal-grilled fish, a house specialty soup curry, and homemade ham made from Yamato pork, all paired with an extensive sommelier-curated wine list.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant hosts wine-pairing evenings and occasional live guitar performances, doubling as a relaxed evening venue.

positives.text: Sources seasonal Yamato vegetables from Nara and offers charcoal-grilled vegetable dishes such as broccoli, so some plant-forward options exist on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A multilingual (English) menu is available and the staff are used to guests, so a foreign student can order comfortably; web and phone reservations are accepted.

places.name: BAKERY GEN（ベーカリー ゲン） (BAKERY GEN)
places.gf_detail: This is a conventional wheat-flour bakery; the owners blend several types of wheat flour for their breads, and signature items (cream pan, milk bread, focaccia, kouign-amann) are all wheat-based. There is no gluten-free offering and no measures against cross-contamination documented. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: Breads documented include custard-cream pan (eggs/dairy), butter-rich kouign-amann, and milk bread, so dairy/eggs/butter are common. One item noted is an egg-free milk bread, but it is not described as vegan (likely still contains dairy/butter), and no vegan range is advertised. Not a vegan destination; a vegan traveler would need to ask about specific loaves and assume most contain animal products.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (closes early when bread sells out). 定休日: 毎週月曜日・金曜日 (Mondays and Fridays).
places.neighborhood: Sakyō (Heijō New Town / near Kintetsu Takanohara Station), n
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] BAKERY GEN is a small neighborhood bakery run by a husband-and-wife team in the Sakyo residential district of northern Nara, set among the Heijo New Town housing estates. The couple bake roughly 40 kinds of bread by hand each day and pride themselves on being close to their custo

chef_bio.background: BAKERY GEN is a small neighborhood bakery run by a husband-and-wife team in the Sakyo residential district of northern Nara, set among the Heijo New Town housing estates. The couple bake roughly 40 kinds of bread by hand each day and pride themselves on being close to their customers, often turning conversations at the counter into new products on the shelves.
chef_bio.philosophy: They describe themselves as a bakery that stays close to the community, adjusting the blend of several wheat flours to suit each bread and recipe, drawing on years of accumulated technique, and developing new breads from customer feedback.
chef_bio.specialty: Their best-known item is the cream pan, filled with rich custard cream made fresh by hand every morning to pair with their dough. Other favorites include a salted potato focaccia, an egg-free fluffy milk bread, and a buttery kouign-amann.

anecdotes.text: The name GEN comes from a beloved family dog the owner lived with as a child; the shop carries the wish that the family can always stay together.
The bakery is woven into the life of the surrounding Heijo-Sakyo housing estate, profiled as part of a feature on the relationship between bread shops and danchi communities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Conventional bakery that blends multiple wheat flours for its breads; flour is used throughout, so cross-contamination is unavoidable and there is no gluten-free production.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads commonly use eggs, dairy, custard cream, and butter; no vegan line is advertised and shared equipment/surfaces would contact animal-derived ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood bakery in a residential danchi far from the tourist center; little to no English, so point at what you want and pay at the counter — straightforward even with no Japanese.

places.name: 梛の木 (Nagi no Ki)
places.gf_detail: This is an Italian kitchen built around pasta and Western dishes, so wheat is central and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation; not suitable for celiacs. The kakigori may be naturally wheat-light but no gluten-free handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. It is a creative Italian restaurant likely using meat, fish, dairy and eggs, and the signature kakigori use syrups, umeboshi paste and a tomato-and-sour-cream version that is not vegan. A vegan diner would need to ask directly about any plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Italian: 11:30- (L.O. 14:00), 18:00- (L.O. 20:30), dinner by reservation. Kakigori served irregularly (mainly summer) on days when dinner reservations are not taken; check Instagram for dates/times.
places.neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, central Nara (near Nara Hotel / old Daijoin
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef trained at prestigious Tokyo hotels and restaurants before opening this small Italian cafe in 2017 in Takabatake-cho, his wife's hometown, in a quiet corner of central Nara near the Nara Hotel.

chef_bio.origin: Tokyo fine-dining background; settled in Nara.
chef_bio.background: The chef trained at prestigious Tokyo hotels and restaurants before opening this small Italian cafe in 2017 in Takabatake-cho, his wife's hometown, in a quiet corner of central Nara near the Nara Hotel.
chef_bio.philosophy: He frames the shaved ice in light of Nara's long history of worshipping the guardian deity of ice since the Heijo-kyo era, and treats kakigori as a dish rather than a sweet — building flavors with techniques borrowed from Western cooking. He offers it only irregularly, woven into the restaurant's schedule, valuing quality over convenience.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative Italian cooking using local Nara ingredients, plus a celebrated summer shaved ice. The signature is a Garden Plum Soda kakigori made from syrup pressed from the large plum tree growing on the property, layered with umeboshi paste for a sweet-tart-salty profile and a fizzing carbonated jelly at the bottom. His fine-dining background also produces savory, Italian-style kakigori such as a tomato version finished with basil and sour cream.

anecdotes.text: He wanted to help bring vitality to the neighborhood and took up shaved ice after receiving guidance from popular local kakigori shops, then developed his own approach using soup-like bases, salt and pepper alongside gentle sweetness.

The Garden Plum Soda kakigori is built from syrup made from the fruit of the large plum tree in the shop's own garden, with umeboshi paste adding a salty seasoning note said to help guard against summer heat exhaustion.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run hideaway where dinner is reservation-only and the kakigori schedule is posted irregularly on Instagram, so a foreign student will do best with some Japanese and a willingness to plan ahead.

places.name: お庭 シェイブアイス ミラコロ (Oniwa Shaveice Miracolo)
places.gf_detail: A shaved ice specialist where the base is ice and house-made fruit/sugar syrups, which are inherently wheat-free. However, the shop makes no documented gluten-free claim, toppings such as konpeito candy and seasonal extras have not been verified as GF, and there is no information on a gluten-free kitchen protocol. Coeliac visitors should ask directly about each topping. Not a dedicated gluten-free establishment.
places.vegan_detail: Many bowls rely on dairy elements such as crème d'ange (an egg/cream sauce) and milk-based toppings, so the menu is not vegan by default. Some fruit-syrup bowls (for example the salted coconut-pineapple) may be closer to plant-based, but ingredients change daily (about five varieties offered) and no vegan labelling is documented. Vegans should ask which of the day's bowls omit dairy and egg.
places.hours_raw: 水・木 10:00-16:00（L.O. 15:30）。他の曜日に臨時営業の日もあり。
places.neighborhood: ならまち／旧大乗院庭園周辺（高畑町）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner left a career as a securities trader to pursue a long-held passion for kakigori, spending roughly a year of preparation before opening the shop in 2019 rather than launching before the quality met his standards.

chef_bio.background: The owner left a career as a securities trader to pursue a long-held passion for kakigori, spending roughly a year of preparation before opening the shop in 2019 rather than launching before the quality met his standards.
chef_bio.philosophy: Relentless quality control, with the owner describing himself as the harshest critic of his own shaved ice; the ice is shaved for a fluffy texture and served in vessels chosen to slow melting.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made shaved ice with from-scratch syrups: a salted coconut-pineapple bowl finished with Nara konpeito candy from a confectioner founded in 1861, a 'MAX Ginger / 本気の生姜' bowl built for intense ginger umami, and a seasonal 'ミラコロいちご' using Nara's premium Koto-ka (古都華) strawberries with crème d'ange.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits just south of the historic Daijoin Garden in the Naramachi district, marked by a 'NARAMACHI GARDEN' sign, with a shoes-off interior of raw wood and tatami plus a small garden-facing terrace.

Opening on essentially only Wednesdays and Thursdays, the limited schedule has helped build a devoted following among local 'gōrā' (shaved-ice enthusiasts), with about five varieties shown by photo at the counter each day.

positives.text: Syrups are made in-house from fruit and sugar, and the core product is shaved ice rather than wheat- or batter-based, so several bowls may suit avoiders of gluten if toppings are checked.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny shoes-off, garden-side shop run by one passionate owner with no English documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps for choosing among the daily bowls.

places.name: ラトリエ スリジェ (Latelier Cerisier)
places.gf_detail: This is a French-style patisserie whose core products are wheat-based: cakes, galette bretonne, financiers and other baked goods all contain flour. No gluten-free items or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs. The shaved ice (kakigori) may use wheat-free syrups, but it is typically plated with cake/custard/baked elements, so cross-contact with wheat is very likely. Confirm directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: A pastry shop centered on butter, eggs, cream and dairy-rich custards (galette bretonne, financiers, fresh cakes, custard-and-cream shaved ice). No vegan items or plant-based options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri 11:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); closes early when sold out (売り切れ次第終了). Closed Wed & Thu.
places.neighborhood: ならまち・興福寺周辺（近鉄奈良駅圏内）
places.cuisine: 個人経営のパティスリー
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Latelier Cerisier opened in July 2023, founded by pastry chef Masayoshi Yamaguchi, who previously honed his craft as a pâtissier at the hotel The Hilltop Terrace Nara before striking out on his own just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station.

chef_bio.background: Latelier Cerisier opened in July 2023, founded by pastry chef Masayoshi Yamaguchi, who previously honed his craft as a pâtissier at the hotel The Hilltop Terrace Nara before striking out on his own just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated aim is to make 'sweets that bring smiles and emotion,' pairing refined pâtisserie technique with careful, personal presentation.
chef_bio.specialty: French-style baked sweets and fresh cakes — signatures include galette bretonne and financiers — and, in the warmer months, elaborate dessert-style shaved ice (kakigori). The flagship 'Puin à la Mode' shaved ice is built like a composed dessert, layering caramel and custard sauce over baked custard, house-made berry ice, and pistachio and strawberry syrups that shift in flavor as you eat.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 'cerisier' is French for cherry tree, and cherry-themed creations — including a shaved ice using two kinds of cherries with a vanilla anglaise sauce — are part of its identity.
To counter the chill of eating shaved ice, the shop offers guests complimentary roasted barley tea.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood sweets shop where pointing at the display case and a few polite Japanese words go a long way; staff likely speak limited English, so a translation app helps for the kakigori menu.

places.name: ルアン NIPPONIA HOTEL 奈良 ならまち (LE UN NIPPONIA HOTEL Nara Naramachi)
places.gf_detail: A French-based innovative restaurant; bread, flour, and dashi feature heavily in the cuisine, so it is not a dedicated or celiac-safe gluten-free venue. Allergies (which would include gluten/wheat) must be communicated at booking via the comments field or by phone at least 3 days in advance, as same-day accommodation is not possible. No documented dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer.
places.vegan_detail: A French restaurant centered on Yamato pork, Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, fresh fish, and dairy; documented signature dishes are meat- and fish-based, so this is not a vegan or vegetarian destination. No vegan/vegetarian menu is documented. Dietary requests must be made at booking or by phone at least 3 days ahead; verify directly whether a plant-based course is possible.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 20:00). Tuesday is dinner only (no lunch).
places.neighborhood: ならまち
places.cuisine: フレンチ／大和野菜・大和牛・大和豚を使うイノベーティブ料理
places.notes: LE UN occupies a converted former sake brewery storehouse of the Toyosawa Sake Brewery within NIPPONIA HOTEL Nara Naramachi, set among the Edo-to-Taisho-era townscapes of Naramachi. The dining room is an intimate counter-style space carved out of a renovated traditional house.

roles: A Swiss-trained head chef leads the kitchen, described as bringing technique and refined sensibility.
chef_bio.background: LE UN occupies a converted former sake brewery storehouse of the Toyosawa Sake Brewery within NIPPONIA HOTEL Nara Naramachi, set among the Edo-to-Taisho-era townscapes of Naramachi. The dining room is an intimate counter-style space carved out of a renovated traditional house.
chef_bio.philosophy: A local-production, local-consumption ethos: French technique used to draw out the true flavor of Nara's land and its traditional Yamato ingredients, with deliberate sake pairing.
chef_bio.specialty: Yamato French courses built on regional Nara ingredients: Yamato pork (served as confit and with young spring vegetables), Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, and seasonal local vegetables and fruit, alongside dishes such as steamed fresh fish with zucchini and mint, conger eel carpione, and new onion gratin. Sake lees from the on-site Toyosawa brewery are woven into the cooking, with courses designed to pair with local Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant and hotel are housed in the renovated storehouses and former residence of the Toyosawa Sake Brewery, and the kitchen incorporates that brewery's sake lees into its dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with allergies must inform the restaurant either in the comments field at the time of online booking or by phone at least 3 days in advance; due to ingredient preparation, same-day accommodation is not possible.

positives.text: The restaurant has a stated process for handling allergies when notified at least 3 days ahead of the reservation.

cultural_comfort.note: An English-language reservation site (TableCheck) and English menu descriptions exist, so a foreign student can book and dine comfortably, though this is a refined, higher-priced course restaurant rather than a casual spot.

places.name: 奈良めし 板焚屋 三条店 (Narameshi Itadakiya (Sanjo))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program or labeling found on any page. This is a soy-sauce, sake, and tempura-heavy izakaya (fried items, grilled skewers with tare, simmered dishes), so wheat exposure is pervasive and it is not suitable for celiacs. No allergy or gluten information is published.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish izakaya centered on Yamato beef, pork, and chicken. It does feature Yamato heirloom vegetables and vegetable tempura, but no dedicated vegan menu exists and dashi/soy seasonings make most dishes non-vegan. A flexible vegan might find a few plain vegetable plates but should ask in advance; no vegan info is published.
places.hours_raw: Sources differ. Tabelog: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30), Sat/Sun/holidays also lunch 11:30-14:00 plus 17:00-24:00; Gnavi: daily 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30); one aggregator: Tue-Sun 17:00-23:00, closed Mon. Closed days reported inconsistently (年中無休 on Tabelog vs Monday elsewhere).
places.neighborhood: Sanjo-dori / near Kintetsu Nara Station
places.notes: Itadakiya sits on Sanjo-dori, the historic approach toward Kasuga Taisha that has served pilgrims into Nara for centuries, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by Ing, a Nara-based restaurant group, under the easygoing concept of a 'teishoku eatery where you

chef_bio.background: Itadakiya sits on Sanjo-dori, the historic approach toward Kasuga Taisha that has served pilgrims into Nara for centuries, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by Ing, a Nara-based restaurant group, under the easygoing concept of a 'teishoku eatery where you can also drink' — comfortable enough for a quiet dinner or a group banquet.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Nara's terroir — the kitchen selects vegetables daily and leans on nationally known Yamato heirloom varieties alongside local meats and sake, so a meal doubles as a tour of regional produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative 'Nara-meshi' cooking that puts local Yamato ingredients front and center: Yamato chicken and Yamato pork grilled as skewers and over the robata, Yamato heirloom vegetables in tempura, clay-pot rice, and regional dishes like Asuka-nabe (a milk-based hot pot). A signature is a green-tea-broth shabu hot pot with Yamato pork, paired with sake brought in from Nara-area breweries.

anecdotes.text: The house describes its food as creative cooking that makes the most of Nara ingredients, sourcing fresh local vegetables daily and building dishes around Yamato heirloom vegetables, Yamato chicken, and Yamato pork.
The owner's recommended course pairs the regional ingredients with two hours of free-flowing local sake, positioned as the kitchen's confidence pick for both quantity and quality.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling or fryer information is published; the menu is built around soy-sauce-based grilling, tempura, and simmered dishes, so wheat cross-contact should be assumed throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori/grilled and simmered izakaya dishes typically use standard wheat-based soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared in a meat-and-fish kitchen and most use dashi or soy seasoning, so vegetable items are not reliably vegan; no separation is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, making it easier for a non-Japanese-speaking diner to identify and ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: An English (and Chinese) menu is available and the place is used to both tourists and locals, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with limited Japanese.

places.name: 天迦久茶寮 (Amenokaku Saryo)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature items are wheat-based: house dorayaki (made from wheat-flour pancake batter) and freshly baked toast. Dango are rice-based but are served with mitarashi/sweet tare typically built on wheat-containing soy sauce, and there is no documented gluten-free or allergy program. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Drinks include Yamato tea (matcha/sencha/houjicha), specialty coffee, and non-alcoholic mocktails, several of which may be plant-based, and lattes can presumably be made with plant milk if available, but this is unconfirmed. Sweets such as dorayaki and dango commonly contain egg/dairy and the tare may contain non-vegan elements, so a vegan visitor must ask item by item. No vegan claims are made by the shop.
places.hours_raw: Sun-Thu: around midday to 17:00 (last order 16:30); Fri-Sat: around midday to 21:00 (last order 20:30), with Nara wine and alcoholic drinks served on Fri/Sat nights. Opening time was still being finalized at the time of the April 2026 report.
places.neighborhood: きたまち（奈良市押上町・近鉄奈良駅北側）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 天迦久茶寮 opened in spring 2026 in a preserved merchant-house setting in Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, on the old Kyoto highway just north of Kintetsu Nara Station near Todai-ji's Tegai Gate and Kaidan-in. It is the latest venue from the Nara coffee-and-tea group behind Amenokak

chef_bio.background: 天迦久茶寮 opened in spring 2026 in a preserved merchant-house setting in Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, on the old Kyoto highway just north of Kintetsu Nara Station near Todai-ji's Tegai Gate and Kaidan-in. It is the latest venue from the Nara coffee-and-tea group behind Amenokaku Coffee, conceived as a grown-up retreat where guests slow down over carefully chosen drinks and sweets in a tranquil Japanese space.
chef_bio.philosophy: A tea house where adults can relax in a Japanese space, pairing locally rooted ingredients and craft with an unhurried, considered hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee and Yamato tea (matcha, sencha, houjicha) served alongside house-made wagashi and Western sweets baked to order, including the signature dorayaki and dango, plus freshly baked toast. Hot tea is poured into French cups and coffee and lattes are served in Akahada-yaki (Nara pottery) bowls with Nara motifs.

anecdotes.text: The interior spreads over two floors, with a modern ground floor and a tatami-mat second floor that looks out over neighboring shrines, while a small tsubo-niwa courtyard garden is tended by a landscaper who also maintains gardens at Kyoto's Taizo-in.
On Friday and Saturday evenings the cafe extends its hours and serves Nara wine and other alcoholic drinks, shifting from a daytime tea stop to an evening lounge.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dorayaki are made from wheat-flour batter and toast is baked on-site, so wheat is handled throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dango are typically served with mitarashi/sweet tare based on Japanese soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; the shop does not document wheat-free tare.

positives.text: Drinks lean toward tea and coffee, including Yamato tea and non-alcoholic mocktails, giving plant-based visitors some beverage options to ask about.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, design-forward Japanese tea cafe in the quiet Kitamachi district; a foreign student would be comfortable but should expect a Japanese-language menu and may want a translation app or a Japanese-speaking companion to navigate the tea and sweets options.

places.name: おはなさんのおむすび屋さん (Ohana-san no Omusubi-yasan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The single set meal includes miso soup (most miso/dashi seasonings are not declared gluten-free) plus grilled fish and side dishes; takeout items include tsukudani and seasoned kombu that typically use wheat-based soy sauce. While plain salted onigiri rice is naturally gluten-free, there is no gluten-free protocol, no documented separate prep, and the kitchen is not set up for celiac needs. No published allergen handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian/vegan destination. The one set (はな結び定食) comes with grilled fish, and several onigiri fillings are fish-based (salmon, mentaiko). The morning miso soup is made with dashi (not specified as kombu-only), so it is likely not vegan. A vegan could potentially eat plain rice onigiri or homemade kombu onigiri à la carte, but this is not confirmed as animal-free and would need to be asked about in person. There is no documented vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: 水・木・金・土曜日、時々日曜 7:30-10:00（ラストオーダー9:30）、テイクアウトは12:00頃まで
places.neighborhood: Naramachi (Higashi-jōdo-chō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Originally from Wajima in Ishikawa Prefecture, the owner known as Ohana-san worked as a sales representative in Osaka before moving to Nara in 2013 out of deep affection for the city. She spent about seven years working at a traditional Japanese restaurant in the Gakuen-mae area,

chef_bio.background: Originally from Wajima in Ishikawa Prefecture, the owner known as Ohana-san worked as a sales representative in Osaka before moving to Nara in 2013 out of deep affection for the city. She spent about seven years working at a traditional Japanese restaurant in the Gakuen-mae area, then was invited to take over the unmanned bookstore Fuusenkazura, where she opened her onigiri shop in its kitchen in December 2020.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made carefully, one item at a time. The onigiri are formed gently so the grains loosen easily on the tongue, and the miso soup is made fresh each morning from freshly drawn dashi, aiming for a simple, gentle, refined plate.
chef_bio.specialty: A single set meal, the Hana-musubi teishoku (around 700 yen): two freshly hand-formed onigiri using Koshihikari rice from the Yamato Highlands, plus grilled fish, small side dishes, and miso soup. Fillings rotate among several options including homemade kombu, salmon, mentaiko, and pickled-greens combinations; takeout onigiri and homemade kombu/tsukudani are also sold.

anecdotes.text: She moved to Nara in 2013 simply because she loved the city so much, after a career in Osaka sales and seven years at a Japanese restaurant in Gakuen-mae.
She opened the onigiri shop in December 2020 inside the kitchen of the unmanned bookstore Fuusenkazura after being invited to become its proprietor.
Each onigiri is served freshly made to order, one at a time, with the second piece brought out after an interval so it is eaten warm.
The miso soup is made every morning from freshly taken dashi, and the set is praised as a simple yet gentle and refined one-plate breakfast.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Takeout offerings include homemade kombu and tsukudani, which are typically simmered in wheat-based soy sauce; soy sauce content is not declared gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The set comes with grilled fish and the miso soup is made with morning dashi (not specified as kombu-only), so vegan separation is not assured.

positives.text: Rice is the focus: Koshihikari from the Yamato Highlands, with onigiri formed gently to order, so plain salted or kombu onigiri may be requestable à la carte.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny solo-run morning counter inside a Japanese bookstore where little to no English is likely, so a few Japanese phrases and patience will make for a smooth, warm visit.

places.name: おむすび処 和が家 (Omusubi-dokoro Wagaya)
places.gf_detail: Plain rice balls (especially the shio-musubi / salted onigiri) are naturally based on rice, but this is not a gluten-free destination and there is no documented allergen handling. The signature 一汁三菜 set is built around miso soup and assorted side dishes; miso typically contains barley or wheat, the soup uses dashi, and side dishes and certain fillings can contain wheat-based soy sauce. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail, and no dedicated gluten-free practices are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Several fillings are animal-based (salmon, etc.) and the popular sets include miso soup that almost certainly uses fish dashi, plus a rolled-omelet (dashimaki) option made with egg. A few items such as the plain salted rice ball or umboshi (plum) onigiri may be plant-based, but vegan suitability is not documented and would need to be confirmed directly.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30, closes when sold out)
places.neighborhood: Funabashi shopping street / Shibatsuji-cho, about a 7-minute
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, hand-made-onigiri shop that opened in autumn 2023 on Nara's retro Funabashi shopping street. It is run single-handedly by a young proprietor who first opened the shop while still a fourth-year university student and has since graduated holding childcare and kindergarten-

chef_bio.background: A small, hand-made-onigiri shop that opened in autumn 2023 on Nara's retro Funabashi shopping street. It is run single-handedly by a young proprietor who first opened the shop while still a fourth-year university student and has since graduated holding childcare and kindergarten-teaching qualifications, with an aspiration to one day work in child- and family-support services. The shop name Wagaya draws on the owner's own name and carries the wish that guests will treat the place like their own home.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner chose this calm corner of the old shopping street specifically to give guests a peaceful, restful experience, reflecting the neighborhood spirit of being kind to both heart and body; a small raised seating area stocked with toys and picture books welcomes families with young children.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-formed rice balls made from carefully chosen Hokkaido-grown Yumepirika rice, prized for its natural sweetness and for keeping its flavor even after it cools. The signature 一汁三菜 (one-soup, three-side) set pairs two rice balls of your choice with a miso soup and three small side dishes, and there is an equally popular set that swaps in a fluffy rolled-omelet (dashimaki). Roughly ten regular fillings rotate with the seasons, alongside a simple salted rice ball.

anecdotes.text: The owner tasted many rice varieties before settling on Hokkaido Yumepirika because its grains stay sweet and flavorful even once cooled, making it ideal for both dine-in and takeout rice balls.
Within months of opening, 30 to 40 percent of customers had become repeat visitors, a sign of how the cozy, home-like atmosphere resonated with locals.
The shop opened while its proprietor was still a university student, who has since graduated with childcare and kindergarten-teaching licenses and hopes to move into child and family support work.

positives.text: The owner is selective about ingredients, choosing a specific Hokkaido rice variety along with particular salt and miso, and works alone, so it is feasible to ask directly about how a dish is made.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, warm solo-run neighborhood shop where ordering is simple but interaction is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 和処 よしの (Washokoro Yoshino)
places.gf_detail: A traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant. Course menus rely heavily on soy sauce and dashi (typically fish-based), and dishes commonly include wheat-containing seasonings and items, so it is not a celiac-safe environment without direct confirmation. The hotel publishes an allergen-information document, so it is best to call ahead (0742-35-5819) and discuss gluten needs in advance.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore kaiseki restaurant; most courses feature fish, fish-based dashi, and other animal products. While Yamato/Nara vegetables and Nara-pickle (奈良漬) feature in the cooking, no documented vegan menu exists. Vegetarian/vegan needs should be arranged in advance by phone, ideally when reserving.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 / Dinner 17:30-19:30 (L.O.); closed Mondays.
places.notes: A full Japanese-cuisine dining room serving seasonal washoku and kaiseki built around carefully chosen ingredients. The kitchen leans into the local larder, featuring Yamato/Nara vegetables, Nara-pickle (nara-zuke), and the regional rice and produce that define Nara's table.

chef_bio.origin: Central Nara, on the third floor of the hotel that sits directly at the west exit of JR Nara Station.
chef_bio.background: A full Japanese-cuisine dining room serving seasonal washoku and kaiseki built around carefully chosen ingredients. The kitchen leans into the local larder, featuring Yamato/Nara vegetables, Nara-pickle (nara-zuke), and the regional rice and produce that define Nara's table.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house describes its cooking as dishes woven from carefully examined, dependable ingredients and a spirit of hospitality that spares no effort (吟味した確かな食材と、手間を惜しまぬおもてなしの心で綴る逸品).
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki and course menus that change with the four seasons, paired with a curated flight of Nara sake, offered as a three-type tasting up to a six-type selection in a nod to Nara being regarded as a birthplace of sake brewing.

anecdotes.text: Diners come to experience the four seasons through Japanese cuisine, with monthly-changing courses and a tasting line-up of Nara local sake.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel provides an allergen-information document, indicating that allergy inquiries are handled, though specific gluten/vegan handling details were not published.

cultural_comfort.note: As a polished hotel restaurant directly connected to JR Nara Station, staff are used to guests and reservations can be made online, though a few words of Japanese and an advance call about any dietary needs will make the visit smoother.
